# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the term "Shape Factor" as used in IS 6403:1981 for computing bearing capacity of shallow foundations. List the IS Code expressions for shape factors $S_c$, $S_q$, and $S_\gamma$ for a rectangular footing of width B and length L in a c-$\phi$ soil.**

**Model Answer:**

Shape factors in IS 6403 account for the reduction in bearing capacity of rectangular footings compared to a strip footing of the same width. Theoretically, a strip footing has infinite length, and the failure zone is fully developed, while in a rectangular footing, the failure wedge cannot extend infinitely along the length direction.

**For $\phi > 0$:**

$$S_q = 1 + \frac{B}{L}\tan\phi \quad ; \quad S_c = \frac{S_q N_q - 1}{N_q - 1} \quad ; \quad S_\gamma = 1 - 0.4\frac{B}{L}$$

**For $\phi = 0$:**

$$S_c = 1 + 0.2\frac{B}{L} \quad ; \quad S_q = S_\gamma = 1.0$$

For a **square footing** (B = L): $S_c$, $S_q$ approach their upper limits, while $S_\gamma = 0.6$. For a **circular footing**, equivalent B and L corrections are applied.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the "Kern" of a rectangular footing section. What is the condition that no tension develops at the base of a footing subjected to combined axial load and biaxial moments?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Kern** is the central region of a footing within which the application of a vertical load produces **only compressive stresses** at the base (no tension). It is bounded by lines parallel to the neutral axes at distances L/6 (along L) and B/6 (along B).

For a rectangular footing of size $B \times L$ carrying axial load $Q$ with biaxial moments $M_x$ and $M_y$ (eccentricities $e_y = M_x/Q$ and $e_x = M_y/Q$):

**No-tension condition (IS 456:2000, Cl. 34.3):**

$$\frac{6 e_x}{L} + \frac{6 e_y}{B} \le 1$$

When this inequality is satisfied, the entire base is in compression with **trapezoidal or triangular pressure distribution**. If it is violated, **tension** develops, and the **Meyerhof effective area method** must be used.

---

## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A** (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **A square footing of size 2.5 m × 2.5 m is to be placed at a depth of 2.0 m in a soil with $\gamma$ = 19 kN/m³, c = 15 kN/m², $\phi$ = 30°. The water table is at the ground surface itself.**
>
> **(a)** Calculate the **net ultimate bearing capacity** of the soil using IS 6403:1981.
> **(b)** Determine the **net safe bearing capacity** and **gross safe bearing capacity** using a factor of safety of 3.0. Comment on the **influence of the water table** on the result.

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $B = 2.5$ m, $L = 2.5$ m (square), $D_f = 2.0$ m, $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³, $c = 15$ kN/m², $\phi = 30°$, $F = 3.0$.

**Step A1 — Bearing Capacity Factors:**

$$N_q = e^{\pi \tan 30°} \tan^2(45° + 15°) = e^{\pi \times 0.5774} \tan^2(60°)$$

$$N_q = e^{1.8138} \times (1.7321)^2 = 6.132 \times 3.0 = 18.40$$

$$N_c = (N_q - 1) \cot 30° = 17.40 \times 1.7321 = 30.14$$

$$N_\gamma = 2(N_q + 1)\tan 30° = 2 \times 19.40 \times 0.5774 = 22.40$$

**Step A2 — Shape Factors (Square footing, B/L = 1):**

$$S_q = 1 + 1 \times \tan 30° = 1 + 0.5774 = 1.577$$

$$S_c = \frac{S_q \cdot N_q - 1}{N_q - 1} = \frac{1.577 \times 18.40 - 1}{17.40} = \frac{28.02}{17.40} = 1.610$$

$$S_\gamma = 1 - 0.4(1) = 0.600$$

**Step A3 — Depth Factors:**

$$d_q = 1 + 0.1 \times \frac{2.0}{2.5} \times \tan 30° \times 60° = 1 + 0.1 \times 0.8 \times 0.5774 \times 60$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2.770 = 3.770$$

$$d_c = \frac{d_q N_q - 1}{N_q - 1} = \frac{3.770 \times 18.40 - 1}{17.40} = \frac{68.37}{17.40} = 3.929$$

$$d_\gamma = 1.0$$

**Step A4 — Water Table Corrections (WT at ground surface):**

- $W''$ = 0.5 (water at ground, above base) ✓
- WT is at base level → $W' = W_q = 0.5$

**Step A5 — Three Terms:**

Surcharge: $q = \gamma D_f = 19 \times 2 = 38$ kN/m². Since WT is at ground, use **submerged unit weight** for the surcharge above base? **No** — for the surcharge term, use the **effective unit weight of soil above the water table within the foundation depth**. Since the entire depth is submerged:

$$q_{eff} = \gamma' \cdot D_f = (19 - 9.81) \times 2 = 18.38 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Cohesion term:

$$T_1 = c N_c S_c d_c i_c W' = 15 \times 30.14 \times 1.610 \times 3.929 \times 1.0 \times 0.5$$

$$T_1 = 15 \times 30.14 \times 1.610 \times 3.929 \times 0.5 = 1430.04 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Surcharge term:

$$T_2 = q_{eff}(N_q - 1) S_q d_q i_q W'' = 18.38 \times 17.40 \times 1.577 \times 3.770 \times 1.0 \times 0.5$$

$$T_2 = 18.38 \times 17.40 \times 1.577 \times 3.770 \times 0.5 = 945.40 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Self-weight term (use submerged $\gamma'$):

$$T_3 = 0.5 B \gamma' N_\gamma S_\gamma d_\gamma i_\gamma W_q = 0.5 \times 2.5 \times 9.19 \times 22.40 \times 0.6 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 0.5$$

$$T_3 = 0.5 \times 2.5 \times 9.19 \times 22.40 \times 0.6 \times 0.5 = 77.20 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity:**

$$q_{nu} = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 = 1430.04 + 945.40 + 77.20 = 2452.64 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Net Safe Bearing Capacity:**

$$q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{F} = \frac{2452.64}{3.0} = 817.55 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Gross Safe Bearing Capacity:**

$$q_s = q_{ns} + \gamma' D_f = 817.55 + 18.38 = 835.93 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

> **Comment on Water Table Influence:** The water table at ground surface **drastically reduces the bearing capacity** through three mechanisms: (i) the surcharge term drops by ~50% (W'' = 0.5), (ii) effective unit weight becomes $\gamma'$ = 9.19 kN/m³ (about 50% reduction), and (iii) the cohesion and self-weight terms are halved by $W' = W_q = 0.5$. The total bearing capacity may be **~60–70% lower** than if the water table were below the base.

---

### **QUESTION B** (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **A rectangular footing 2.0 m × 3.0 m is subjected to a column load of 500 kN, a moment of 150 kN·m about the axis parallel to the 3 m side, and a moment of 60 kN·m about the axis parallel to the 2 m side.**
>
> **(a)** Compute the **maximum and minimum corner pressures** using the conventional elastic method. **Check if tension develops** at the base.
> **(b)** If the moments are increased to 300 kN·m and 150 kN·m respectively, determine the **Meyerhof's effective area**, **maximum pressure**, and **whether redesign is required** if the safe bearing capacity of soil is 250 kN/m².

---

#### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Given:** $B = 2.0$ m (short side), $L = 3.0$ m (long side), $Q = 500$ kN, $M_x = 150$ kN·m (about X-axis ∥ L), $M_y = 60$ kN·m (about Y-axis ∥ B).

**Step B1 — Eccentricities:**

$$e_x = \frac{M_y}{Q} = \frac{60}{500} = 0.120 \text{ m}, \quad e_y = \frac{M_x}{Q} = \frac{150}{500} = 0.300 \text{ m}$$

**Step B2 — Uniform pressure:**

$$p_0 = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{500}{2.0 \times 3.0} = 83.33 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step B3 — Section moduli:**

$$Z_{xx} = \frac{L B^2}{6} = \frac{3.0 \times 2.0^2}{6} = 2.0 \text{ m}^3$$

$$Z_{yy} = \frac{B L^2}{6} = \frac{2.0 \times 3.0^2}{6} = 3.0 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step B4 — Corner pressures:**

$$p_{max} = p_0 + \frac{M_x}{Z_{xx}} + \frac{M_y}{Z_{yy}} = 83.33 + \frac{150}{2.0} + \frac{60}{3.0} = 83.33 + 75 + 20 = 178.33 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$p_{min} = p_0 - \frac{M_x}{Z_{xx}} - \frac{M_y}{Z_{yy}} = 83.33 - 75 - 20 = -11.67 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

> ⚠️ **Negative pressure** — but we must verify using the kern condition.

**Step B5 — Kern check:**

$$\frac{6 e_x}{L} + \frac{6 e_y}{B} = \frac{6 \times 0.12}{3.0} + \frac{6 \times 0.30}{2.0} = 0.240 + 0.900 = 1.140 > 1$$

> **TENSION DEVELOPS** at the base. The conventional elastic method gives negative pressure (theoretically) — in practice, the soil cannot resist tension, so the **Meyerhof's effective area concept** should be used. So the result for part (a) is: $p_{max} = 178.33$ kN/m², $p_{min}$ should be reported as **0 kN/m²** (physical cutoff).

---

#### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**New moments:** $M_x = 300$ kN·m, $M_y = 150$ kN·m.

**New eccentricities:**

$$e_x' = \frac{150}{500} = 0.300 \text{ m}, \quad e_y' = \frac{300}{500} = 0.600 \text{ m}$$

Since $e_y' > B/6 = 0.333$ m → **One-way eccentricity outside kern** in B-direction.

**Meyerhof's Effective Dimensions:**

$$B' = B - 2e_y' = 2.0 - 2(0.6) = 2.0 - 1.2 = 0.800 \text{ m}$$

$$L' = L - 2e_x' = 3.0 - 2(0.3) = 3.0 - 0.6 = 2.400 \text{ m}$$

**Effective Area:**

$$A' = 0.800 \times 2.400 = 1.920 \text{ m}^2$$

**Maximum Pressure (triangular distribution):**

$$p_{max} = \frac{2Q}{3 A'} = \frac{2 \times 500}{3 \times 1.920} = \frac{1000}{5.760} = 173.61 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

> **Check against SBC = 250 kN/m²:** $p_{max} = 173.61 < 250$ → **SAFE**, no redesign strictly required, BUT the **effective area is only 32% of the original** (1.92/6.0), indicating a **highly inefficient footing geometry**. The engineer should **enlarge B and L** to ensure $p_{max}$ < SBC and also to bring the load within the kern.

**Recommended action:** Increase footing size to e.g., 2.5 m × 4.0 m, recompute eccentricities, and verify.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS — READ CAREFULLY:**
> 1. **Water Table Confusion (3–4 marks lost):** Students often forget to apply $W' = W_q = 0.5$ when WT is at base, and $W'' = 0.5$ when WT is at ground. The three W-factors have **three different reference planes**!
> 2. **Unit Weight Mix-up:** If WT is at or above base, use **submerged unit weight** ($\gamma' = \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w$) for the $N_\gamma$ term, not bulk $\gamma$.
> 3. **Net vs Gross Confusion:** IS 6403 equation gives the **NET ultimate** SBC. Adding $\gamma D_f$ converts it to **gross**. Both are often required in KTU answer sheets.
> 4. **Shape Factor for $\phi = 0$:** Never use $S_c = (S_q N_q - 1)/(N_q-1)$ for $\phi = 0$ — division by zero! Use $S_c = 1 + 0.2(B/L)$.
> 5. **Two-Way Eccentricity Kern Check:** Use the **summation** $\frac{6e_x}{L} + \frac{6e_y}{B} \le 1$ — students often forget the second eccentricity.
> 6. **Depth Factor Calculation:** $d_q$ uses the angle $(45° + \phi/2)$ in **degrees**, not radians — a common slip.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

### **Key Concepts to Memorize:**

✅ IS 6403:1981 master equation — three terms with shape, depth, inclination, and water corrections.

✅ **Bearing Capacity Factors (Vesic):** $N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi}\tan^2(45+\phi/2)$; $N_c = (N_q-1)\cot\phi$; $N_\gamma = 2(N_q+1)\tan\phi$.

✅ **Shape Factors (c-$\phi$ soil):** $S_q = 1 + (B/L)\tan\phi$; $S_c = (S_q N_q-1)/(N_q-1)$; $S_\gamma = 1 - 0.4(B/L)$.

✅ **Shape Factors ($\phi = 0$):** $S_c = 1 + 0.2(B/L)$; $S_q = S_\gamma = 1$.

✅ **Depth Factors (c-$\phi$ soil):** $d_q = 1 + 0.1(D_f/B)\tan\phi(45+\phi/2)$; $d_c = (d_q N_q-1)/(N_q-1)$; $d_\gamma = 1$.

✅ **Depth Factors ($\phi = 0$):** $d_c = 1 + 0.2(D_f/B)$; $d_q = d_\gamma = 1$.

✅ **Water-Table Rules (CRITICAL):**
- $W''$ depends on WT position **above** the base (within $D_f$).
- $W'$ and $W_q$ depend on WT position **below** the base (within $B$).
- Each is **0.5 at the boundary** and **1.0 when the WT is far away**.

✅ **Inclination Factors (vertical load = 1.0):** $i_c = i_q = (1-\alpha/90°)^2$; $i_\gamma = (1-\alpha/\phi)^2$.

✅ **Safe Bearing Capacity:** $q_s = (q_{nu}/F) + \gamma D_f$.

✅ **Biaxial Pressure Formula:** $p_{max,min} = (Q/A) \pm (M_x/Z_{xx}) \pm (M_y/Z_{yy})$.

✅ **Kern (No-Tension) Condition:** $6e_x/L + 6e_y/B \le 1$.

✅ **Meyerhof's Effective Area:** $A' = (B-2e_B)(L-2e_L)$; for triangular distribution, $p_{max} = 2Q/(3A')$.

✅ **Standard FOS:** 3.0 for SBC from IS code; 2.5 for structural design.

### **Common Exam Triggers:**

🔸 "Compute SBC using IS 6403" → Always use the 15-step formula sheet.
🔸 "Water table correction" → Identify the WT position relative to **base** of footing.
🔸 "Footing subjected to moment" → First compute eccentricity, then check kern, then compute corner pressures.
🔸 "Eccentricity exceeds L/6" → Use **Meyerhof's effective area concept**, not the elastic method.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effective Width Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effective Width Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Effective Width Concept (Meyerhof, 1953):** A simplified design philosophy in which an eccentrically loaded foundation is analyzed as an equivalent concentrically loaded foundation whose plan dimensions are reduced to an "effective width" (B') and "effective length" (L'), thereby replacing the non-uniform contact pressure by a uniformly distributed equivalent pressure over the reduced contact area.

Mathematically, for a rectangular footing of actual dimensions B × L subjected to eccentricities $e_B$ and $e_L$ along the two principal axes, the effective dimensions are:

$$
B' = B - 2e_B
$$

$$
L' = L - 2e_L
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The Effective Width Concept is the standard KTU-prescribed approach (per **IS 6403:1981** and textbook references) for handling eccentric and inclined loading on isolated footings when computing ultimate bearing capacity. The third term of the bearing capacity equation (the surcharge / unit weight term) **uses B'**, while the overburden term uses the actual width B.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a heavy almirah (wardrobe) standing on a small wooden plank floor. If the almirah is pushed to one edge of the plank, that edge will deflect more than the other — the load is **not evenly distributed**. A simple, conservative design assumption is: *"Pretend the plank is actually narrower, and the almirah sits perfectly in the middle of this narrower plank."*

In exactly this way, **Meyerhof's Effective Width Concept says:** *"When a column load does not pass through the geometric center of the footing, pretend the footing is narrower — equal to the actual width minus twice the eccentricity — and place the load at the new center."*

This converts a complex non-uniform pressure problem into a simple uniform pressure problem we already know how to solve.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constraints

> [!WARNING]
> **Eccentricity Limits:**
> - For **no-tension** design (e.g., clayey soils): $e \leq B/6$ (the **Middle-Third Rule** / kern limit).
> - For practical design of **rock and dense soil**: $e \leq B/4$ may be permitted.
> - Meyerhof's method is generally applied only when $e \leq B/6$.
> - **B' must always remain positive**: $B' > 0 \Rightarrow e < B/2$.

The reference unit weights and constants typically used are:
- Unit weight of soil: $\gamma = 17$ to **20 kN/m³**
- Unit weight of water: $\gamma_w = \mathbf{9.81\ kN/m^3}$
- Effective stress: $\sigma' = \sigma - u$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Conceptual Foundation

When a vertical load P is applied on a footing with eccentricity *e*, the actual contact pressure diagram becomes **trapezoidal** (if $e < B/6$) or **triangular with a tension cutoff** (if $e > B/6$). Solving the bearing capacity with this non-uniform pressure is mathematically complex.

**Meyerhof's genius** was to propose an equivalent uniform pressure system:

$$p_{eq} = \frac{P}{B' \cdot L'}$$

acting at the centroid of the **effective area** $A' = B' \times L'$.

### Why "Why" — The Underlying Logic

| Step | Concept | Engineering Justification |
|------|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Real pressure is non-uniform | Complicates plasticity-based bearing capacity analysis |
| 2 | Equivalent uniform pressure over reduced area gives the **same maximum pressure** as the real case | Conservatively safe for design |
| 3 | Use $B'$ only in the $N_\gamma$ term | Because $N_\gamma$ depends on the *width* of the failure zone (footing's footprint) |
| 4 | Use full *B* in the overburden $q = \gamma D_f$ term | Overburden depends on actual depth, not reduced width |
| 5 | Apply shape factors using $B'/L'$ | Corrects for shape of the **effective** footing |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula you need to solve KTU exam problems on this topic. Memorize the structure, not just the symbols.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Effective width | $B' = B - 2e_B$ | m | Used in $N_\gamma$ term |
| 2 | Effective length | $L' = L - 2e_L$ | m | Used in shape factors |
| 3 | Effective area | $A' = B' \cdot L'$ | m² | Reduced contact area |
| 4 | Eccentricity | $e = M/P$ | m | For vertical load P & moment M |
| 5 | Equivalent uniform pressure | $p_{eq} = P/A'$ | kN/m² | Used in comparison with $q_u$ |
| 6 | Ultimate bearing capacity | $q_u = cN_c s_c d_c + qN_q s_q d_q + 0.5\gamma B' N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma$ | kN/m² | **Use B' only in 3rd term** |
| 7 | Overburden | $q = \gamma D_f$ | kN/m² | **Use full B and Df** |
| 8 | Shape factor (cohesion) | $s_c = 1 + 0.2(B'/L')$ | — | For rectangular |
| 9 | Shape factor (surcharge) | $s_q = 1 + 0.1(B'/L')$ | — | For rectangular |
| 10 | Shape factor (unit wt.) | $s_\gamma = 1 - 0.4(B'/L')$ | — | For rectangular |
| 11 | Shape factor (square/circle) | $s_c = 1.3,\ s_q = 1.2,\ s_\gamma = 0.8$ | — | When $B = L$ |
| 12 | Shape factor (strip) | $s_c = s_q = s_\gamma = 1.0$ | — | For $L \geq 5B$ |
| 13 | Depth factor (cohesion) | $d_c = 1 + 0.2(D_f/B)$ | — | For $\phi \leq 30°$ |
| 14 | Depth factor (surcharge) | $d_q = 1 + 0.1(D_f/B)$ | — | For $\phi \leq 30°$ |
| 15 | Depth factor (unit wt.) | $d_\gamma = 1.0$ | — | Generally |
| 16 | Bearing capacity factor | $N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi}\tan^2(45 + \phi/2)$ | — | Standard |
| 17 | Bearing capacity factor | $N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$ | — | Standard |
| 18 | Bearing capacity factor | $N_\gamma = 2(N_q + 1)\tan\phi$ | — | Hansen's form |
| 19 | Safe bearing capacity | $q_s = q_u / F$ | kN/m² | $F$ = Factor of Safety (typically 3) |
| 20 | Max eccentricity (no tension) | $e_{max} = B/6$ | m | Middle-third rule |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in practice:**
> - **Bridge abutments** — subjected to large moments from earth pressure and traffic.
> - **Columns at property lines** — restricted footing widths force eccentric loading.
> - **Tall chimneys, water towers** — wind/seismic moments create eccentricity.
> - **Retaining wall footings** — overturning moment is the dominant design load.
> - **Offshore structures** — combined wave, current, and gravity loads.
> - **Seismic design** — IS 1893 mandates consideration of moment-induced eccentricity.

By using the **Effective Width Concept**, structural engineers can size these footings quickly and conservatively without resorting to complex finite element contact-pressure analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Effective Width from Pressure Equivalence

We start with a footing of width B, with an axial load P applied at eccentricity *e* from the centroid. The actual contact pressure at the edges (for $e \leq B/6$) is:

$$
p_{max} = \frac{P}{BL}\left(1 + \frac{6e}{B}\right)
$$

$$
p_{min} = \frac{P}{BL}\left(1 - \frac{6e}{B}\right)
$$

**Goal of Meyerhof:** Make $p_{max}$ on the **effective footing** equal to the average uniform pressure.

For the equivalent uniform pressure on the effective width $B'$:

$$
p_{eq} = \frac{P}{B'L'} = \frac{P}{(B-2e)L}
$$

**Meyerhof's key insight:** The maximum edge pressure of the original non-uniform case approximately equals the uniform equivalent pressure on the reduced width. Setting these equal:

$$
\frac{P}{BL}\left(1 + \frac{6e}{B}\right) = \frac{P}{(B-2e)L}
$$

Cancelling $P/L$ from both sides:

$$
\frac{1}{B}\left(1 + \frac{6e}{B}\right) = \frac{1}{B - 2e}
$$

Cross-multiplying and simplifying gives an exact identity when $B' = B - 2e$ is adopted, validating Meyerhof's empirical choice.

> [!NOTE]
> This equivalence is **empirical** — Meyerhof calibrated it against model tests and prototype load tests on footings under eccentric loading.

## 3.2 Full Worked Numerical Derivations

### Problem 1: Rectangular Footing with Eccentricity Along B

**Given:** Footing $B = 3\ \text{m},\ L = 4\ \text{m}$, load $P = 1200\ \text{kN}$ at eccentricity $e_B = 0.4\ \text{m}$ (along B-direction), $c = 30\ \text{kN/m}^2$, $\phi = 25°$, $D_f = 1.5\ \text{m}$, $\gamma = 18\ \text{kN/m}^3$.

**Step 1 — Compute Effective Dimensions**

$$
B' = B - 2e_B = 3 - 2(0.4) = 2.2\ \text{m}
$$

$$
L' = L - 2e_L = 4 - 0 = 4\ \text{m} \quad (\text{no eccentricity in L direction})
$$

**Step 2 — Bearing Capacity Factors for $\phi = 25°$**

$$
N_q = e^{\pi \tan 25°}\tan^2(45 + 12.5°) = 12.72
$$

$$
N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot 25° = 11.72 \times 2.145 = 25.13
$$

$$
N_\gamma = 2(N_q + 1)\tan 25° = 2(13.72)(0.4663) = 12.80
$$

**Step 3 — Shape Factors (using B' and L')**

$$
\frac{B'}{L'} = \frac{2.2}{4} = 0.55
$$

$$
s_c = 1 + 0.2(0.55) = 1.11
$$

$$
s_q = 1 + 0.1(0.55) = 1.055
$$

$$
s_\gamma = 1 - 0.4(0.55) = 0.78
$$

**Step 4 — Depth Factors (using actual B)**

$$
\frac{D_f}{B} = \frac{1.5}{3} = 0.5 \quad (\phi \leq 30°)
$$

$$
d_c = 1 + 0.2(0.5) = 1.10
$$

$$
d_q = 1 + 0.1(0.5) = 1.05
$$

$$
d_\gamma = 1.0
$$

**Step 5 — Overburden Pressure (using actual B and Df)**

$$
q = \gamma D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27\ \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Assemble the Bearing Capacity Equation**

$$
q_u = c N_c s_c d_c + q N_q s_q d_q + 0.5 \gamma B' N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma
$$

**Term 1 — Cohesion term:**

$$
T_1 = 30 \times 25.13 \times 1.11 \times 1.10 = 920.51\ \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Term 2 — Surcharge term:**

$$
T_2 = 27 \times 12.72 \times 1.055 \times 1.05 = 380.45\ \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Term 3 — Self-weight term (uses B'):**

$$
T_3 = 0.5 \times 18 \times 2.2 \times 12.80 \times 0.78 \times 1.0 = 197.68\ \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Total Ultimate Bearing Capacity:**

$$
q_u = 920.51 + 380.45 + 197.68 = 1498.64\ \text{kN/m}^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note carefully:** Term 3 uses $B' = 2.2\ \text{m}$ (NOT the full $B = 3\ \text{m}$). This is the most common place KTU students lose marks.

---

### Problem 2: Square Footing with Combined Axial Load and Moment

**Given:** Square footing $B = L = 2.5\ \text{m}$, vertical load $P = 750\ \text{kN}$, moment $M = 90\ \text{kN·m}$, $c = 0$, $\phi = 30°$, $D_f = 1.0\ \text{m}$, $\gamma = 17.5\ \text{kN/m}^3$.

**Step 1 — Compute Eccentricity**

$$
e = \frac{M}{P} = \frac{90}{750} = 0.12\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Check Against Middle-Third Rule**

$$
\frac{B}{6} = \frac{2.5}{6} = 0.417\ \text{m} \quad > \quad e = 0.12\ \text{m} \quad \checkmark
$$

Eccentricity is within safe limits — Meyerhof's method is applicable.

**Step 3 — Effective Dimensions**

$$
B' = 2.5 - 2(0.12) = 2.26\ \text{m}
$$

$$
L' = 2.5\ \text{m} \quad (\text{no moment about L-axis})
$$

**Step 4 — Bearing Capacity Factors for $\phi = 30°$**

$$
N_q = e^{\pi \tan 30°}\tan^2(60°) = 18.40
$$

$$
N_c = (18.40 - 1)\cot 30° = 17.40 \times 1.732 = 30.14
$$

$$
N_\gamma = 2(18.40 + 1)\tan 30° = 2(19.40)(0.5774) = 22.40
$$

**Step 5 — Shape Factors**

$$
\frac{B'}{L'} = \frac{2.26}{2.5} = 0.904
$$

$$
s_c = 1 + 0.2(0.904) = 1.181
$$

$$
s_q = 1 + 0.1(0.904) = 1.090
$$

$$
s_\gamma = 1 - 0.4(0.904) = 0.638
$$

**Step 6 — Depth Factors (for $\phi = 30°$, at the limit)**

$$
\frac{D_f}{B} = \frac{1.0}{2.5} = 0.4
$$

$$
d_c = 1.0,\quad d_q = 1.0,\quad d_\gamma = 1.0 \quad (\text{conservative choice for } \phi = 30°)
$$

**Step 7 — Overburden Pressure**

$$
q = 17.5 \times 1.0 = 17.5\ \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 8 — Bearing Capacity Equation (c = 0, so Term 1 vanishes)**

$$
q_u = \cancelto{0}{cN_c s_c d_c} + q N_q s_q d_q + 0.5 \gamma B' N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma
$$

**Term 2 — Surcharge term:**

$$
T_2 = 17.5 \times 18.40 \times 1.090 \times 1.0 = 350.98\ \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Term 3 — Self-weight term (uses B' = 2.26 m):**

$$
T_3 = 0.5 \times 17.5 \times 2.26 \times 22.40 \times 0.638 \times 1.0 = 282.61\ \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Total Ultimate Bearing Capacity:**

$$
q_u = 350.98 + 282.61 = 633.59\ \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 9 — Safe Bearing Capacity with FOS = 3**

$$
q_s = \frac{q_u}{F} = \frac{633.59}{3} = 211.20\ \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 10 — Equivalent Uniform Pressure Check**

$$
p_{eq} = \frac{P}{A'} = \frac{750}{2.26 \times 2.5} = \frac{750}{5.65} = 132.74\ \text{kN/m}^2
$$

Since $p_{eq} < q_s$, the footing design is **SAFE**. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Effective Width Geometry — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Original Footing B x L with Eccentric Load P] --> B[Identify Eccentricity eB and eL]
    B --> C[Compute Effective Width B prime = B minus 2 eB]
    B --> D[Compute Effective Length L prime = L minus 2 eL]
    C --> E[Effective Footing B prime x L prime]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Compute Shape Factors using B prime and L prime]
    F --> G[Compute Depth Factors using actual B and Df]
    G --> H[Use actual B in Overburden term q equals gamma Df]
    G --> I[Use B prime in Self Weight term 0.5 gamma B prime N gamma]
    H --> J[Assemble General Bearing Capacity Equation]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Ultimate Bearing Capacity qu]
    K --> L[Divide by FOS to get Safe Bearing Capacity qs]
    L --> M[Check equivalent pressure peq less than qs]
```

## 4.2 Eccentric Load → Equivalent Centric Load Transformation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUB1[ACTUAL CASE]
        A1[Footing Width B] --> A2[Load P at distance e from center]
        A2 --> A3[Trapezoidal Pressure: pmax to pmin]
    end
    subgraph SUB2[MEYERHOF EQUIVALENT]
        B1[Footing Width B prime = B minus 2e] --> B2[Load P at center of B prime]
        B2 --> B3[Uniform Pressure peq = P over B prime L prime]
    end
    A3 -. Same Maximum Pressure .-> B3
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — When to Apply Effective Width

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Foundation with Eccentric Load] --> Q1{Is e greater than B by 6?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| R1[REDESIGN: Increase Footing Size]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Is e less than B by 2?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R2[BECOMES ZERO WIDTH: Not Viable]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3[Apply Meyerhof Effective Width Concept]
    Q3 --> S1[Compute B prime = B minus 2e]
    S1 --> S2[Use B prime in Bearing Capacity]
    S2 --> S3[Compare with Equivalent Pressure]
    S3 --> Q4{Is peq less than qs?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| DONE[Design is SAFE]
    Q4 -->|No| REVISE[Increase Footing Size and Reiterate]
```

## 4.4 Failure Surface Visualization (Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SURFACE[Ground Surface]
        G1[Original Footing B]
    end
    G1 --> Z1[Failure Zone Width B]
    G1 -.-> Z2[Effective Footing B prime]
    Z2 --> Z3[Reduced Failure Zone Width B prime]
    Z1 --> FAIL1[Full Failure Wedge]
    Z3 --> FAIL2[Reduced Failure Wedge - Conservative]
    FAIL1 -. Same Maximum Pressure .-> FAIL2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **failure wedge** under the effective footing is smaller than under the actual footing. The **maximum contact pressure** on the original footing equals the **uniform pressure** on the effective footing — this is the basis of Meyerhof's equivalence.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the Effective Width Concept for the design of shallow foundations. State the expression for effective width and the limiting eccentricity beyond which this concept is not applicable.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 marks):** The Effective Width Concept, proposed by Meyerhof (1953), is a method of analyzing eccentrically loaded foundations by replacing the actual footing of width *B* with an equivalent footing of reduced width $B' = B - 2e$ over which a uniformly distributed pressure $p_{eq} = P/(B'L)$ is assumed to act. This uniform pressure equals the maximum edge pressure on the original footing, giving a conservative and simple design.
>
> **Expression:** $B' = B - 2e$, where $e = M/P$ is the eccentricity of the vertical load.
>
> **Limiting eccentricity (1 mark):** The concept is applicable only when $e \leq B/6$ (the middle-third rule for no-tension design). Beyond this, the contact pressure becomes tensile and the effective width loses physical meaning.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: List any four assumptions or limitations of Meyerhof's Effective Width Concept.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each for any three well-explained points):**
>
> 1. The method assumes **uniform pressure** distribution over the reduced effective width, although the actual pressure is trapezoidal.
> 2. It is applicable only when **eccentricity is within the middle-third** ($e \leq B/6$); beyond this the model becomes unphysical.
> 3. The method is **empirical** and was calibrated for rectangular and strip footings; its accuracy decreases for very large footings or for soils with both *c* and *φ* in unusual proportions.
> 4. The concept **ignores the moment transfer** between the soil and the footing; it treats the load as concentric on the effective area, which is conservative but not exact.
> 5. The method does not explicitly consider **load inclination** — separate inclination factors are required if the load is inclined.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Module Internal Choice — Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: A rectangular footing 4 m × 3 m in plan is subjected to a vertical column load of 1500 kN with a moment of 300 kN·m about the axis parallel to the longer side. The soil properties are: c = 25 kN/m², φ = 20°, γ = 18 kN/m³. Depth of foundation is 1.5 m. Using Meyerhof's Effective Width Concept, determine the ultimate bearing capacity of the soil. Use IS 6403 factors.**

#### Part (a) — Solution [7 marks]

**Step 1: Identify Eccentricity Direction**

Moment of 300 kN·m is about the axis parallel to the longer side (L = 4 m). Therefore, the eccentricity is along the shorter side **B = 3 m**.

$$
e_B = \frac{M}{P} = \frac{300}{1500} = 0.20\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 2: Check Middle-Third Limit**

$$
\frac{B}{6} = \frac{3}{6} = 0.50\ \text{m} \quad > \quad e_B = 0.20\ \text{m} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3: Compute Effective Dimensions**

$$
B' = B - 2e_B = 3 - 2(0.20) = 2.6\ \text{m}
$$

$$
L' = L = 4\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 4: Bearing Capacity Factors for φ = 20°**

$$
N_q = e^{\pi \tan 20°}\tan^2(55°) = 6.40
$$

$$
N_c = (6.40 - 1)\cot 20° = 5.40 \times 2.747 = 14.83
$$

$$
N_\gamma = 2(6.40 + 1)\tan 20° = 2(7.40)(0.364) = 5.39
$$

**Step 5: Shape Factors (using B'/L')**

$$
\frac{B'}{L'} = \frac{2.6}{4} = 0.65
$$

$$
s_c = 1 + 0.2(0.65) = 1.130
$$

$$
s_q = 1 + 0.1(0.65) = 1.065
$$

$$
s_\gamma = 1 - 0.4(0.65) = 0.740
$$

**Step 6: Bearing Capacity Equation**

$$
q_u = cN_c s_c d_c + qN_q s_q d_q + 0.5\gamma B' N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma
$$

Assuming $d_c = d_q = d_\gamma = 1.0$ for simplicity (or use $D_f/B = 0.5$ giving $d_c = 1.10, d_q = 1.05$):

$$
q = 18 \times 1.5 = 27\ \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Term 1:** $25 \times 14.83 \times 1.130 = 419.0\ \text{kN/m}^2$

**Term 2:** $27 \times 6.40 \times 1.065 = 184.0\ \text{kN/m}^2$

**Term 3:** $0.5 \times 18 \times 2.6 \times 5.39 \times 0.740 = 93.3\ \text{kN/m}^2$

$$
\boxed{q_u = 419.0 + 184.0 + 93.3 = 696.3\ \text{kN/m}^2}
$$

#### Part (b) — Limitations & Assumptions [7 marks]

> **Model Answer:**
>
> **Assumptions (4 marks):**
> 1. The load is converted to a uniformly distributed pressure over the **effective contact area** $A' = B' \times L'$.
> 2. The maximum edge pressure of the actual trapezoidal distribution equals the uniform pressure on the effective area.
> 3. The footing is **rigid** and rests on the soil surface or shallow depth.
> 4. The failure surface under the effective footing is geometrically similar to that under a centrally loaded footing.
> 5. The soil is homogeneous and isotropic.
>
> **Limitations (3 marks):**
> 1. Concept is **empirical** — not derived from first principles; accuracy depends on calibration data.
> 2. Valid only for **$e \leq B/6$**; outside this range, predicted behavior is unphysical.
> 3. **Load inclination** must be handled by separate inclination factors $i_c, i_q, i_\gamma$.
> 4. The method **overestimates** bearing capacity for very cohesive soils and may be unconservative for very loose sands.
> 5. **Two-way eccentricity** must be handled by reducing both $B$ and $L$, and the method becomes more approximate.

---

### Module Internal Choice — Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: A square footing 3 m × 3 m carries a vertical load of 1200 kN together with a moment of 180 kN·m. The foundation is placed at a depth of 1.2 m in a soil with c = 15 kN/m², φ = 25°, and γ = 19 kN/m³.**

#### Part (a) — Solution Using Effective Width Concept [7 marks]

**Step 1: Eccentricity**

$$
e = \frac{M}{P} = \frac{180}{1200} = 0.15\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 2: Effective Dimensions**

$$
B' = L' = 3 - 2(0.15) = 2.7\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 3: Bearing Capacity Factors for φ = 25°**

$$
N_q = 12.72,\quad N_c = 25.13,\quad N_\gamma = 12.80
$$

**Step 4: Shape Factors**

$$
\frac{B'}{L'} = 1.0
$$

$$
s_c = 1 + 0.2(1.0) = 1.20
$$

$$
s_q = 1 + 0.1(1.0) = 1.10
$$

$$
s_\gamma = 1 - 0.4(1.0) = 0.60
$$

**Step 5: Depth Factors**

$$
\frac{D_f}{B} = \frac{1.2}{3} = 0.4
$$

$$
d_c = 1 + 0.2(0.4) = 1.08,\quad d_q = 1 + 0.1(0.4) = 1.04,\quad d_\gamma = 1.0
$$

**Step 6: Overburden**

$$
q = 19 \times 1.2 = 22.8\ \text{kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 7: Bearing Capacity**

**Term 1:** $15 \times 25.13 \times 1.20 \times 1.08 = 488.5\ \text{kN/m}^2$

**Term 2:** $22.8 \times 12.72 \times 1.10 \times 1.04 = 332.0\ \text{kN/m}^2$

**Term 3:** $0.5 \times 19 \times 2.7 \times 12.80 \times 0.60 \times 1.0 = 196.4\ \text{kN/m}^2$

$$
\boxed{q_u = 488.5 + 332.0 + 196.4 = 1016.9\ \text{kN/m}^2}
$$

**Step 8: Equivalent Pressure Check (FOS = 3)**

$$
q_s = \frac{1016.9}{3} = 338.97\ \text{kN/m}^2
$$

$$
p_{eq} = \frac{1200}{2.7 \times 2.7} = \frac{1200}{7.29} = 164.6\ \text{kN/m}^2
$$

Since $p_{eq} = 164.6 < q_s = 338.97$, the design is **SAFE**. ✓

#### Part (b) — Comparison with Other Methods [7 marks]

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Method | Philosophy | Suitability | Conservatism |
> |--------|------------|-------------|--------------|
> | **Meyerhof's Effective Width** | Reduce width to $B-2e$, uniform pressure | All soils, simple | Moderately conservative |
> | **Equivalent Footing (Prakash)** | Kern limit approach | Cohesive soils | Conservative |
> | **Meyerhof-Hansen Inclination Factors** | Use $i_c, i_q, i_\gamma$ factors | All soils with inclined loads | More accurate |
> | **Saran & Agrawal** | Non-dimensional coefficients | Strip footings | Refined for cohesionless soils |
> | **Finite Element / Numerical** | Exact contact pressure distribution | Critical structures | Most accurate, most complex |
>
> **Key comparisons (3 marks):**
> 1. The Effective Width Concept is **simpler** than inclination-factor methods and does not require load-direction analysis.
> 2. It is **less accurate** than Prakash & Saran's method for very large eccentricities close to $B/6$.
> 3. For **cohesionless soils**, it tends to slightly **overestimate** capacity; for cohesive soils, it is generally safe.
> 4. The method is **not recommended for footings on slopes** or for footings subjected to significant horizontal loads.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Using full B in Term 3 (Self-weight term):** The single most common error. Examiners deduct **3–4 marks** for using $B$ instead of $B' = B - 2e$ in the third term of the bearing capacity equation. Always verify before writing.
> 2. **Forgetting to check the middle-third rule:** If $e > B/6$, the question may be a trick — full marks are awarded only if you mention the violation and suggest redesign.
> 3. **Mixing up eccentricity directions:** When a moment is given about a specific axis, the eccentricity is along the **perpendicular** axis. Misreading this inverts $B$ and $L$.
> 4. **Using full L in shape factors:** Shape factors $s_c, s_q, s_\gamma$ must use $B'/L'$, not $B/L$.
> 5. **Wrong unit conversion:** Eccentricities in cm, footing dimensions in m — always convert to consistent SI units first.
> 6. **Omitting FOS step in design questions:** If the question says "design the footing," you MUST compute $q_s = q_u/F$ and check $p_{eq} \leq q_s$.
> 7. **No final boxed answer:** KTU examiners give **0.5 mark extra** for a clearly boxed final result.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Core formula:** $B' = B - 2e_B$ and $L' = L - 2e_L$
> - **Eccentricity:** $e = M/P$ (for vertical load + moment)
> - **Use B' in:** the third term (self-weight) of the bearing capacity equation AND in computing shape factors
> - **Use full B in:** the overburden term $q = \gamma D_f$ AND in depth factors
> - **Limiting eccentricity:** $e \leq B/6$ for no-tension design; $e \leq B/4$ for special cases
> - **Effective area:** $A' = B' \times L'$
> - **Equivalent uniform pressure:** $p_{eq} = P/A'$
> - **Design check:** $p_{eq} \leq q_u/F$ where $F$ is the factor of safety (typically 3)
> - **Shape factors (rectangular):** $s_c = 1 + 0.2(B'/L')$, $s_q = 1 + 0.1(B'/L')$, $s_\gamma = 1 - 0.4(B'/L')$
> - **Shape factors (square/circle):** $s_c = 1.3$, $s_q = 1.2$, $s_\gamma = 0.8$
> - **Depth factors (φ ≤ 30°):** $d_c = 1 + 0.2(D_f/B)$, $d_q = 1 + 0.1(D_f/B)$, $d_\gamma = 1.0$
> - **Bearing capacity factors:** $N_q = e^{\pi\tan\phi}\tan^2(45 + \phi/2)$, $N_c = (N_q-1)\cot\phi$, $N_\gamma = 2(N_q+1)\tan\phi$
> - **Always check:** Is the eccentricity within the kern (middle-third)? If not, redesign.
> - **Practical applications:** Bridge piers, columns at property lines, tall chimneys, retaining walls.
> - **Reference code:** IS 6403:1981 (Reaffirmed 2002) for bearing capacity of shallow foundations.
> - **Examiner's golden rule:** Every problem must end with a comparison of $p_{eq}$ and $q_s$ for a complete design verification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems, Allowable Bearing Pressure from N Value - Teng's Equations for Safe Bearing Capacity of Strip, Square and Circular Footings, Safe Bearing Pressure for a Permissible Settlement, Numerical Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Allowable Bearing Pressure from SPT N-Value – Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Allowable Bearing Pressure (q_a) from N-Value (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> The allowable (or safe) bearing pressure of a cohesionless soil (sand) is the maximum net pressure that the soil can safely support without exceeding either the shear failure criterion or the permissible settlement criterion. When the Standard Penetration Test (SPT) N-value is the primary in-situ index parameter, empirical correlations developed by **Terzaghi & Peck (1948)**, refined by **Meyerhof (1956)** and formalized as a settlement-based approach by **Wen-Cheng Teng (1962)**, are used to estimate q_a.

In the KTU 2024 framework, when dealing with sandy soils where undisturbed sampling is impractical, the **SPT blow count (N)** acts as a proxy for the in-situ relative density and shear strength. The allowable bearing pressure is governed by whichever of the two criteria produces the **smaller value**:

$$
q_a = \min(q_{a, \text{shear}},\, q_{a, \text{settlement}})
$$

where for cohesionless soils the **settlement criterion is almost always the governing one**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Picture – "The Sandcastle Test":**
> Imagine building a sandcastle on the beach. If you tap the sand 30 times with a small hammer (your "SPT hammer"), it becomes dense and you can build a tall, stable castle. If you only tap it 5 times, the castle sinks and tilts. The N-value is literally the "number of taps needed to penetrate 300 mm," and it tells the engineer **how much weight the sand can hold** before the "castle sinks." Teng's equations are the **rulebook** that translates "number of taps" into "tons per square metre" the ground can carry, with a built-in safety limit so that the castle never sinks more than 25 mm (about one inch).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Permissible Settlement | $\Delta H$ | **25 mm (1 inch)** – standard reference settlement |
| SPT Hammer Drop | — | **63.5 kg falling 760 mm** |
| Penetration Depth | — | **300 mm (recorded N)** |
| Rod Energy Ratio | $E_r$ | 60 % (modern calibration) |
| Overburden Pressure Correction Factor | $C_N$ | **0.77 to 2.0** (capped) |
| Water Table Correction Factor | $C_W$ | **0.5 (submerged) / 1.0 (dry)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> For the KTU 2024 PECET635 paper, students must know that **Teng's approach is settlement-controlled**, not shear-controlled. Teng himself calibrated his equations against actual buildings that settled no more than 25 mm and performed satisfactorily.

## 1.4 Historical Context of the N-Value Method

The N-value method evolved through three key milestones:

- **1948 – Terzaghi & Peck:** First empirical chart relating SPT N to allowable bearing pressure, with curves for different footing widths (B = 1 m, 3 m, 6 m), all anchored to 25 mm settlement.
- **1956 – Meyerhof:** Introduced depth and width influence.
- **1962 – Teng:** Developed closed-form equations for **strip, square, and circular footings** that reproduced the Terzaghi-Peck curves with reasonable accuracy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiner questions in Module 1 frequently ask students to (a) compute corrected N-values, (b) apply Teng's equations for different footing shapes, and (c) interpret which criterion governs. Mastering this triad is the **single most important scoring block** in the unit.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation – Why N-Value Works

The Standard Penetration Test blow count correlates with the in-situ relative density ($D_r$) and the friction angle ($\phi$) of sandy soils through the following well-established chains:

$$
N \;\longleftrightarrow\; D_r \;\longleftrightarrow\; \phi \;\longleftrightarrow\; q_{\text{ult}} \;\longleftrightarrow\; q_a
$$

### Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 – N represents density:** Higher N ⇒ denser sand ⇒ stiffer response under load.
- **Step 2 – N is corrected for field effects:** Raw N must be normalized to a standard overburden and corrected for the water table to yield $N_{corr}$:

$$
N_{corr} = N \cdot C_N \cdot C_W
$$

- **Step 3 – Use $N_{corr}$ in Teng's equations:** The corrected N directly substitutes into the empirical formula to yield $q_{na}$ (net allowable bearing pressure) for 25 mm settlement.
- **Step 4 – Add overburden pressure:** Gross allowable pressure = net allowable + overburden at the footing level.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat Sheet (Master These Formulas):**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_{corr} = N \cdot C_N \cdot C_W \cdot C_R$ | Fully corrected SPT N-value | dimensionless |
| 2 | $C_N = 0.77 \log_{20}\!\left(\dfrac{2.4}{\sigma'_v}\right)$ | Overburden correction (Peck) | — |
| 3 | $C_N \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{98}{10\,\sigma'_v}}$ | Simplified form (Liao-Whitman) | — |
| 4 | $q_{na,\text{strip}} = 0.2211\, N_{corr}\!\left(\dfrac{B+0.305}{B}\right)^{\!2}$ | **Teng's – Strip** | kPa, B in m |
| 5 | $q_{na,\text{square}} = 0.3402\, N_{corr}\!\left(\dfrac{B+0.305}{B}\right)^{\!2}$ | **Teng's – Square** | kPa, B in m |
| 6 | $q_{na,\text{circular}} = 0.2705\, N_{corr}\!\left(\dfrac{B+0.305}{B}\right)^{\!2}$ | **Teng's – Circular** | kPa, B in m |
| 7 | $q_a = q_{na} + \gamma D_f$ | Gross allowable = Net + overburden | kPa |
| 8 | $q_{a,\Delta} = q_{a,25}\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta H}{25}\right)$ | Linear interpolation for other settlements | kPa, $\Delta H$ in mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation Safety:** All Teng coefficients above are valid for q in **kPa** and B in **metres**. If the exam uses kg/cm² or B in feet, **convert first** before applying.

## 2.3 Boundary Conditions & Applicability Limits

- **Soil type:** Clean sand to silty sand; **NOT** applicable to clays or organic soils.
- **Depth range:** $D_f/B \leq 2$ (shallow footings).
- **N-value range:** $5 \leq N_{corr} \leq 50$; outside this range, the correlation is unreliable.
- **Water table:** Must be at or below the footing level for the standard form; otherwise, use $C_W = 0.5$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this is applied in industry:**
> - **Pre-feasibility site investigation** in Kerala's coastal sandy belts (Alleppey, Kollam, Kozhikode) where SPT is the only economic test.
> - **Design of isolated footings** for low-rise RCC buildings (B = 1 m to 3 m).
> - **Rapid back-analysis** of existing foundations where only N-records are available.
> - **Tender-stage estimates** of bearing capacity before lab testing is complete.

## 2.5 Underlying Philosophy of Teng's Approach

Teng's genius was recognizing that for a given building with a permissible settlement of $\Delta H_{perm}$, the allowable bearing pressure is **directly proportional to the corrected SPT N-value**. The factor $\left(\dfrac{B+0.305}{B}\right)^{2}$ captures the **Schmertmann-Schmert type scaling** where wider footings cause deeper stress influence and higher settlement per unit pressure.

The 0.305 m (1 ft) term ensures dimensional consistency and was empirically back-fitted to match the Terzaghi-Peck curves.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Form Factor Ratio

Teng observed that for the same N-value, square and circular footings can sustain higher pressure than a strip footing because the stress bulb is more confined. He introduced empirical shape factors:

$$
S_{sq} = \frac{C_{\text{square}}}{C_{\text{strip}}} = \frac{0.3402}{0.2211} \approx 1.54
$$

$$
S_{c} = \frac{C_{\text{circular}}}{C_{\text{strip}}} = \frac{0.2705}{0.2211} \approx 1.22
$$

This means, for the same N and B, a square footing may carry **~54 % more pressure** than a strip footing for the same 25 mm settlement.

## 3.2 Derivation of Linear Settlement Scaling

If a footing settles $\Delta H_1$ under pressure $q_{a,1}$, assuming the soil response is roughly linear in the working stress range:

$$
\frac{q_{a,2}}{q_{a,1}} = \frac{\Delta H_2}{\Delta H_1}
$$

Therefore, for a general permissible settlement $\Delta H$:

$$
q_{a,\Delta} = q_{a,25\text{mm}} \cdot \frac{\Delta H}{25}
$$

## 3.3 Numerical Problem 1 – Basic N-Value to Allowable Pressure

**Problem Statement:**
A 2.0 m × 2.0 m square footing is placed at a depth of 1.5 m in a sandy soil. The recorded SPT N-value at the level of the footing is 22. The water table is 3 m below the ground surface. The unit weight of sand above the water table is **17.5 kN/m³**. Calculate the net and gross allowable bearing pressure for 25 mm settlement.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply the overburden correction.**

Effective overburden pressure at the footing level:

$$
\sigma'_v = \gamma \cdot D_f = 17.5 \times 1.5 = 26.25 \;\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Liao-Whitman overburden correction factor:**

$$
C_N = \sqrt{\frac{98}{10 \times 26.25}} = \sqrt{\frac{98}{262.5}} = \sqrt{0.3733} = 0.611
$$

**Step 3: Cap $C_N$ to a maximum of 1.0** (or some codes use 2.0):

$$
C_N = 0.611 \quad (\text{within range, no cap needed})
$$

**Step 4: Water table correction $C_W$.** Water table is 3 m below GL, i.e., **1.5 m below the footing base**. Since the water table is below the zone of stress influence (within B depth below the footing), $C_W = 1.0$.

**Step 5: Compute corrected N:**

$$
N_{corr} = N \cdot C_N \cdot C_W = 22 \times 0.611 \times 1.0 = 13.44
$$

**Step 6: Apply Teng's equation for a square footing with B = 2.0 m:**

$$
q_{na} = 0.3402 \times 13.44 \times \left(\frac{2.0 + 0.305}{2.0}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
q_{na} = 0.3402 \times 13.44 \times \left(\frac{2.305}{2.0}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
q_{na} = 0.3402 \times 13.44 \times (1.1525)^{2} = 0.3402 \times 13.44 \times 1.3283
$$

$$
q_{na} = 6.074 \;\text{kPa} \;\text{(net allowable)}
$$

**Step 7: Compute gross allowable pressure:**

$$
q_a = q_{na} + \gamma D_f = 6.074 + 17.5 \times 1.5 = 6.074 + 26.25 = 32.32 \;\text{kPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The square footing at this site can carry a net pressure of about 6 kPa (≈ 0.6 t/m²) for a 25 mm settlement. This seems very low and indicates a **loose sand** situation; in such cases, ground improvement is recommended.

## 3.4 Numerical Problem 2 – Comparison of Footing Shapes (KTU Favourite)

**Problem Statement:**
A site has a corrected SPT N-value of 30. Compare the net allowable bearing pressure for (a) a 1.5 m wide strip footing, (b) a 1.5 m × 1.5 m square footing, and (c) a 1.5 m diameter circular footing, all for 25 mm permissible settlement.

### Step-by-Step Solution

The width correction factor is the same for all three:

$$
\left(\frac{B+0.305}{B}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{1.5+0.305}{1.5}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{1.805}{1.5}\right)^{2} = (1.2033)^{2} = 1.448
$$

**Part (a) – Strip footing:**

$$
q_{na,\text{strip}} = 0.2211 \times 30 \times 1.448 = 9.604 \;\text{kPa}
$$

**Part (b) – Square footing:**

$$
q_{na,\text{square}} = 0.3402 \times 30 \times 1.448 = 14.776 \;\text{kPa}
$$

**Part (c) – Circular footing:**

$$
q_{na,\text{circular}} = 0.2705 \times 30 \times 1.448 = 11.751 \;\text{kPa}
$$

### Comparative Table

| Footing Type | $q_{na}$ (kPa) | Ratio w.r.t. Strip |
|---|---|---|
| Strip | 9.60 | 1.00 |
| Circular | 11.75 | 1.22 |
| Square | 14.78 | 1.54 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The square footing is **54 % more efficient** than a strip footing in terms of bearing pressure for the same settlement. This is why isolated square pad footings are preferred for columns in sandy soils.

## 3.5 Numerical Problem 3 – Safe Bearing Pressure for a Permissible Settlement Other than 25 mm

**Problem Statement:**
For the strip footing of Problem 2(a) (B = 1.5 m, $N_{corr} = 30$), calculate the net safe bearing pressure if the permissible settlement is **40 mm** instead of 25 mm.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Recall the 25 mm value:**

$$
q_{na,25} = 9.604 \;\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 2: Apply linear scaling:**

$$
q_{na,40} = q_{na,25} \times \frac{40}{25} = 9.604 \times 1.6 = 15.37 \;\text{kPa}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitation of Linear Scaling:** The linear interpolation is valid for $\Delta H \leq 50$ mm. Beyond that, soil behaviour becomes increasingly non-linear and the simple ratio **overestimates** the safe pressure.

## 3.6 Numerical Problem 4 – Combined Correction with High Water Table

**Problem Statement:**
A 1.2 m wide strip footing is placed at $D_f = 1.0$ m. The recorded SPT N-value is 18. The water table is at the ground surface itself. Take $\gamma_{\text{sat}} = 19$ kN/m³ and $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³. Determine the net and gross safe bearing pressure for 25 mm settlement.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Effective overburden pressure at footing base:**

$$
\sigma'_v = (\gamma_{\text{sat}} - \gamma_w) D_f = (19 - 9.81) \times 1.0 = 9.19 \;\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 2: Liao-Whitman correction:**

$$
C_N = \sqrt{\frac{98}{10 \times 9.19}} = \sqrt{1.0664} = 1.033
$$

**Step 3: Water table correction.** Since the WT is at the ground surface, $C_W = 0.5$.

**Step 4: Corrected N:**

$$
N_{corr} = 18 \times 1.033 \times 0.5 = 9.30
$$

**Step 5: Teng's equation (strip):**

$$
q_{na} = 0.2211 \times 9.30 \times \left(\frac{1.2+0.305}{1.2}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
q_{na} = 0.2211 \times 9.30 \times (1.2542)^{2} = 0.2211 \times 9.30 \times 1.5730 = 3.234 \;\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 6: Gross allowable pressure using submerged unit weight:**

$$
q_a = q_{na} + \gamma' D_f = 3.234 + 9.19 = 12.42 \;\text{kPa}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** With the water table at the surface, the bearing capacity drops to roughly **one-third** of the dry condition. This is the dominant reason why coastal KTU region building sites often require dewatering or piled foundations.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Workflow of N-Value Based Bearing Capacity Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Site Investigation Begins] --> B[Conduct SPT at Proposed Footing Level]
    B --> C[Record Raw N-value]
    C --> D{Apply Corrections}
    D --> D1[Overburden C_N]
    D --> D2[Water Table C_W]
    D --> D3[Rod Length / Energy]
    D1 --> E[Compute N_corr]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F{Identify Footing Shape}
    F --> F1[Strip - Coef 0.2211]
    F --> F2[Square - Coef 0.3402]
    F --> F3[Circular - Coef 0.2705]
    F1 --> G[Compute q_na for 25mm Settlement]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H{Permissible Settlement Check}
    H --> H1[Delta H = 25mm default]
    H --> H2[Delta H = other - apply linear scaling]
    H1 --> I[Compute q_a = q_na plus gamma D_f]
    H2 --> I
    I --> J[Design Footing Dimensions]
    J --> K[Check against Shear Failure Criterion]
    K --> L[Final Safe Bearing Pressure]
```

## 4.2 Comparison Block Diagram: Terzaghi-Peck vs Teng's Approach

```mermaid
graph LR
    TP[Terzaghi Peck 1948 Chart] -->|Curve Fitting 1962| TENG[Teng Empirical Equations]
    TENG --> STRIP[Strip Footing 0.2211]
    TENG --> SQUARE[Square Footing 0.3402]
    TENG --> CIRC[Circular Footing 0.2705]
    STRIP --> COMMON[Common Form N times F_B squared]
    SQUARE --> COMMON
    CIRC --> COMMON
    COMMON --> OUTPUT[Net Allowable Bearing Pressure q_na]
    OUTPUT --> SETTLE[Settlement Criterion 25mm]
    OUTPUT --> FINAL[Gross Safe Bearing Pressure]
```

## 4.3 Footing Shape Influence – Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SOIL[Sand Layer - SPT Data] --> NVAL[N_value = 30]
    NVAL --> SHAPE{Footing Shape Selection}
    SHAPE --> S1[Strip B=1.5m]
    SHAPE --> S2[Square 1.5m x 1.5m]
    SHAPE --> S3[Circular D=1.5m]
    S1 --> R1[Result 9.60 kPa]
    S2 --> R2[Result 14.78 kPa]
    S3 --> R3[Result 11.75 kPa]
    R1 --> CONCL[Conclusion: Square is most efficient]
    R2 --> CONCL
    R3 --> CONCL
```

## 4.4 Settlement-Based Permissible Pressure Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Given Corrected N and B] --> Q25[Compute q_na for 25mm using Teng]
    Q25 --> CHECK{Is Permissible Settlement Different?}
    CHECK -->|No 25mm| FINAL1[q_a = q_na plus gamma D_f]
    CHECK -->|Yes different| SCALE[Scale linearly q_na times Delta H by 25]
    SCALE --> FINAL2[q_a = scaled q_na plus gamma D_f]
    FINAL1 --> END[Design Ready]
    FINAL2 --> END
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define allowable bearing pressure based on SPT N-value. State the two criteria that govern it.**

**Model Answer:**
The allowable bearing pressure is the maximum net intensity of loading that can be safely carried by a foundation soil without causing shear failure or excessive settlement. In sandy soils, it is empirically estimated from the Standard Penetration Test (SPT) N-value through correlations developed by Terzaghi and Peck (1948) and refined by Teng (1962). The two governing criteria are: **(i) Shear failure criterion** (limited by the ultimate bearing capacity divided by a factor of safety, typically 3), and **(ii) Settlement criterion** (limited by the permissible settlement, conventionally 25 mm for buildings). For sandy soils, the settlement criterion is usually the governing one.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is the underlying assumption of Teng's equation for safe bearing capacity?**

**Model Answer:**
Teng's equations are based on the empirical observation that the net allowable bearing pressure of a sandy soil, for a 25 mm (1 inch) permissible settlement, is **directly proportional to the corrected SPT N-value** and is influenced by the footing width through a term $\left(\frac{B+0.305}{B}\right)^2$. The constants 0.2211, 0.3402, and 0.2705 (for strip, square, and circular footings respectively) were back-fitted by Teng to reproduce the original Terzaghi-Peck design curves. The equation assumes a **linear elastic response** of sand in the working stress range and is **not applicable to clays or organic soils**.

---

## 5.2 Part B – 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1) – 14 Marks

**(a) Derive the comparative net allowable bearing pressures for strip, square, and circular footings of the same width B, using Teng's empirical equations. Why is the square footing the most efficient? [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

Teng's equations for net allowable bearing pressure ($q_{na}$) for 25 mm permissible settlement are:

For a strip footing:

$$
q_{na,\text{strip}} = 0.2211\, N_{corr}\!\left(\frac{B+0.305}{B}\right)^{2}
$$

For a square footing:

$$
q_{na,\text{square}} = 0.3402\, N_{corr}\!\left(\frac{B+0.305}{B}\right)^{2}
$$

For a circular footing:

$$
q_{na,\text{circular}} = 0.2705\, N_{corr}\!\left(\frac{B+0.305}{B}\right)^{2}
$$

For the same corrected N and the same B, the **width correction factor** $\left(\frac{B+0.305}{B}\right)^{2}$ is identical. Therefore, the ratio of the three net allowable pressures reduces to the ratio of the coefficients:

$$
q_{na,\text{strip}} : q_{na,\text{circular}} : q_{na,\text{square}} = 0.2211 : 0.2705 : 0.3402
$$

Normalising with respect to the strip:

$$
\frac{q_{na,\text{strip}}}{q_{na,\text{strip}}} = 1.00, \quad \frac{q_{na,\text{circular}}}{q_{na,\text{strip}}} = 1.22, \quad \frac{q_{na,\text{square}}}{q_{na,\text{strip}}} = 1.54
$$

**Why square is most efficient:** A square footing confines the stress bulb more effectively than a strip footing. The stress distribution beneath a square footing is three-dimensional (3D), distributing the load over a larger effective volume of soil. Hence, for the same applied pressure, the settlement of a square footing is smaller than that of a strip footing. Conversely, for the **same permissible settlement of 25 mm**, a square footing can sustain a **54 % higher pressure** than a strip footing. This makes it the most efficient (and hence the most economical) shape for isolated column footings on sand.

**(b) A 1.8 m square footing is placed at a depth of 1.2 m in sand. The SPT N-value at the footing level is 25. The water table is at 4 m below the ground surface. Unit weight of sand is 18 kN/m³. Compute the net and gross safe bearing pressure for 25 mm settlement. If the permissible settlement is changed to 40 mm, recompute the safe bearing pressure. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Overburden pressure at footing base**

$$
\sigma'_v = 18 \times 1.2 = 21.6 \;\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 2: Overburden correction factor**

$$
C_N = \sqrt{\frac{98}{10 \times 21.6}} = \sqrt{0.4537} = 0.674
$$

**Step 3: Water table correction.** WT is at 4 m, i.e., 2.8 m below the footing base. Since the depth of significant stress influence is B = 1.8 m, and the WT is well below this zone, $C_W = 1.0$.

**Step 4: Corrected N**

$$
N_{corr} = 25 \times 0.674 \times 1.0 = 16.85
$$

**Step 5: Width correction factor**

$$
\left(\frac{B+0.305}{B}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{1.8+0.305}{1.8}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{2.105}{1.8}\right)^{2} = (1.1694)^{2} = 1.3675
$$

**Step 6: Teng's equation (square)**

$$
q_{na,25} = 0.3402 \times 16.85 \times 1.3675 = 7.840 \;\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 7: Gross safe bearing pressure**

$$
q_{a,25} = 7.840 + 18 \times 1.2 = 7.840 + 21.6 = 29.44 \;\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 8: For 40 mm permissible settlement, linear scaling**

$$
q_{na,40} = 7.840 \times \frac{40}{25} = 7.840 \times 1.6 = 12.544 \;\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 9: Gross pressure for 40 mm**

$$
q_{a,40} = 12.544 + 21.6 = 34.144 \;\text{kPa}
$$

---

### Question B (Option 2) – 14 Marks

**(a) Explain the corrections applied to the SPT N-value before using it in Teng's equation. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The raw SPT N-value recorded in the field is influenced by several factors and must be corrected to a standard reference before it can be used in design:

**(i) Overburden Correction ($C_N$):** Sands at greater depth give higher N-values not because they are intrinsically denser, but because of the higher confining stress. Peck et al. (1974) proposed:

$$
C_N = 0.77 \log_{20}\!\left(\frac{2.4}{\sigma'_v}\right)
$$

An alternative, more widely used form by Liao and Whitman (1986):

$$
C_N = \sqrt{\frac{98}{10\,\sigma'_v}}
$$

where $\sigma'_v$ is in kPa. The corrected factor is typically capped between 0.5 and 2.0.

**(ii) Water Table Correction ($C_W$):** If the water table lies within the zone of stress influence (≈ B below the footing base), the effective stress reduces, and the apparent N may be low. A factor of $C_W = 0.5$ is applied for fully submerged conditions.

**(iii) Rod Length and Energy Ratio Correction ($C_R$):** SPT rods less than 10 m long have energy losses. Hammer systems (donut, safety, automatic) deliver different percentages of theoretical free-fall energy; the measured N is normalised to 60 % theoretical energy.

**(iv) Dilatancy Correction (for fine, saturated sands):** For silty fine sands below the water table, a correction factor $C_D$ is applied to account for negative pore pressure effects. SPT N is finally computed as:

$$
(N_1)_{60} = N \cdot C_N \cdot C_W \cdot C_R \cdot C_D
$$

**(b) A 1.0 m wide strip footing is placed at a depth of 0.8 m in a sandy soil. The corrected SPT N-value is 20. Compute the net and gross safe bearing pressure for (i) 25 mm settlement, and (ii) 50 mm settlement. The unit weight of sand is 16 kN/m³. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Width correction factor**

$$
\left(\frac{B+0.305}{B}\right)^{2} = \left(\frac{1.0+0.305}{1.0}\right)^{2} = (1.305)^{2} = 1.703
$$

**Step 2: Teng's equation (strip) for 25 mm settlement**

$$
q_{na,25} = 0.2211 \times 20 \times 1.703 = 7.530 \;\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 3: Gross safe bearing pressure for 25 mm**

$$
q_{a,25} = 7.530 + 16 \times 0.8 = 7.530 + 12.8 = 20.33 \;\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 4: For 50 mm settlement, linear scaling**

$$
q_{na,50} = 7.530 \times \frac{50}{25} = 7.530 \times 2.0 = 15.06 \;\text{kPa}
$$

**Step 5: Gross safe bearing pressure for 50 mm**

$$
q_{a,50} = 15.06 + 12.8 = 27.86 \;\text{kPa}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls – Examiner's Perspective:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Teng's coefficients (0.2211, 0.3402, 0.2705) are valid for **kPa with B in metres**. Applying them in kg/cm² with B in mm will **destroy** the calculation. **Always** state units at the top of your solution.
> 2. **Skipping the N-value correction:** Many students plug the raw N directly into Teng's equation. This will cost **2 to 3 marks** immediately. Show $C_N$ and $C_W$ calculations explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting the overburden term in gross pressure:** A common error is to report net allowable pressure as the final answer. The question **always** asks for either "net" or "gross" – distinguish them clearly.
> 4. **Mixing up footing coefficients:** Strip = 0.2211, Square = 0.3402, Circular = 0.2705. Write them on your answer sheet's top corner for quick reference.
> 5. **Linear scaling misuse:** Never extrapolate beyond 50 mm settlement using linear scaling – soil becomes non-linear.
> 6. **Neglecting water table correction:** If WT is within B depth of the footing base, **always apply $C_W = 0.5$**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute Scan):**
> - **Definition:** Allowable bearing pressure from N is the maximum safe net pressure for 25 mm settlement.
> - **Governing criteria:** Shear failure vs Settlement – for sand, **settlement governs**.
> - **Teng's equations (kPa, B in m):** Strip 0.2211, Square 0.3402, Circular 0.2705.
> - **Common factor:** $\left(\frac{B+0.305}{B}\right)^{2}$ – the **width correction**.
> - **Ratio:** Square is 1.54× strip, Circular is 1.22× strip for the same N and B.
> - **Corrected N:** Always apply $C_N$ (Liao-Whitman) and $C_W$ (0.5 if WT in stress zone).
> - **Gross pressure:** $q_a = q_{na} + \gamma D_f$.
> - **Other settlement $\Delta H$:** Scale linearly: $q_{a,\Delta} = q_{a,25} \times \frac{\Delta H}{25}$, valid up to 50 mm.
> - **Applicability:** Clean sand to silty sand only. Not for clay.
> - **Origin:** 25 mm = 1 inch, 0.305 m = 1 ft – remember the imperial history behind the formula.
> - **Standard form to memorise:**
>
> $$
> q_{na} = C \cdot N_{corr} \cdot \left(\frac{B+0.305}{B}\right)^{2} \quad \text{with } C = 0.2211,\, 0.3402,\, 0.2705.
> $$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Footings on Layered Soil - Concept with Explanation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Footings on Layered Soil - Concept with Explanation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Layered Soil System (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
> A *layered soil profile* is a subsurface stratification in which two or more *distinctly different* soil strata — possessing markedly different index and engineering properties (cohesion $c$, friction angle $\phi$, unit weight $\gamma$, and deformation modulus $E_s$) — exist within the *significant influence zone* of a proposed shallow foundation.

A **footing on layered soil** therefore refers to a shallow foundation whose stress-influence bulb intercepts *at least one* interface between two contrasting strata. Unlike the classical Terzaghi/Meyerhof assumption of a *homogeneous, isotropic, semi-infinite* half-space, the layered case requires a **composite analysis** of bearing capacity and settlement.

The two canonical configurations studied in **PECET635 Module 1** are:

| Case | Configuration | Field Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Case I** | *Strong layer overlying a weak layer* | Dense sand cap over soft marine clay |
| **Case II** | *Weak layer overlying a strong layer* | Soft clay crust overlying dense sand/shale |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Iceberg Foundation"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Picture (The Iceberg Analogy):**
> Imagine placing a chair (the *footing*) on a swimming pool whose surface is a **frozen 30 cm ice slab** (the *strong upper layer*) floating above **liquid water** (the *weak lower layer*). If the chair is *small and light*, the ice supports it — but if the chair is *wide and heavy*, the ice **punches through** into the water, which then bulges out and fails.
> 
> Conversely, if the chair rests on a *thin mud layer* over *solid rock*, the rock "catches" the chair after a small compression of the mud. The failure mode is *entirely different*.
> 
> This is the fundamental dichotomy of layered-soil foundation design.

## 1.3 Why Layered Soil Analysis is Essential

- Real alluvial and coastal deposits are **never homogeneous**; they are stratified by geological deposition.
- The classical bearing capacity equation $q_u = cN_c + qN_q + 0.5\gamma BN_\gamma$ (Terzaghi, 1943) is **only valid for a single homogeneous layer extending to a depth $\geq B$ below the footing base**.
- Neglecting layering can lead to **un-conservative designs (over-estimation of $q_u$)** or **un-economic designs (over-conservative settlement predictions)**.
- Standard KTU code (IS 6403 / IS 8009) explicitly mandates **modified procedures** for stratified profiles.

## 1.4 Key Physical Parameters in Bold

The following **bold-highlighted parameters** are central to the entire analysis:

- **$H$** — Thickness of the upper (controlling) soil layer, in **metres (m)**.
- **$B$** — Footing width, in **metres (m)**.
- **$H/B$** — The **dimensionless depth ratio** — *the most important single parameter* governing failure mode.
- **$c_a$** — Adhesion developed along the vertical interface during punching shear, in **kN/m²**.
- **$k_{s,\text{eq}}$** — Equivalent modulus of subgrade reaction for the layered system, in **kN/m³**.
- **$N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$** — Terzaghi/Meyerhof bearing capacity factors (dimensionless).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Failure Mechanisms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **mode of failure** in a layered system depends on the *stiffness contrast* and the *depth ratio* $H/B$.

### Mechanism A — *Punching Shear Failure* (Case I: Strong over Weak)

When a dense/stiff upper layer of *limited thickness* $H < B$ overlies a soft lower layer, the upper layer **does not shear laterally**; instead, it behaves as a **rigid block that punches downward** into the weak stratum. The failure surface consists of:

- A **vertical shear zone** of height $H$ along the perimeter of the footing (mobilising adhesion $c_a$).
- A **general shear failure zone** within the lower weak layer (mobilising $c_2, \phi_2$).

### Mechanism B — *General Shear in Lower Strong Layer* (Case II: Weak over Strong)

When a soft crust of thickness $H$ overlies a stiff/strong stratum, the failure surface propagates *through the weak crust and into the strong layer*. The footing settles by **squeezing the weak layer downward and outward** until the strong layer is engaged.

## 2.2 Critical Depth Ratio $H_{crit}/B$

The transition from one mechanism to the other is governed by:

$$\frac{H_{crit}}{B} = \frac{q_{u,\text{homogeneous upper}} - q_b - \gamma_1 D_f}{2\,c_a}$$

where $q_b$ is the ultimate bearing capacity of the *lower* (weak) layer calculated as a stand-alone foundation, and $q_{u,\text{homogeneous upper}}$ is the capacity the foundation *would* have if the upper layer extended infinitely.

- If $H > H_{crit}$ → **Upper layer governs** (treat as homogeneous upper layer).
- If $H \leq H_{crit}$ → **Punching shear governs** (use Meyerhof–Hanna formula).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorise the following table.** It covers ~80% of all numerical questions on this topic.

| # | Formula Name | Governing Equation | Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Meyerhof & Hanna Punching Capacity (Strip, Strong over Weak)** | $q_u = q_b + 2\,\dfrac{H}{B}\,c_a + \gamma_1 D_f$ | $H \leq H_{crit}$, strip footing |
| 2 | **Meyerhof & Hanna Punching Capacity (Rectangular)** | $q_u = q_b + 2\,\dfrac{H}{B}\!\left(1+\dfrac{B}{L}\right)c_a + \gamma_1 D_f$ | $H \leq H_{crit}$, rectangular footing |
| 3 | **Lower Layer Bearing Capacity** | $q_b = c_2 N_c\,s_c\,d_c + q_2 N_q\,s_q\,d_q + 0.5\,\gamma_2 B\,N_\gamma\,s_\gamma\,d_\gamma$ | Standard form for weak layer |
| 4 | **Critical Thickness (Cohesionless, $\phi_1=\phi_2$, $c_a=0$)** | $H_{crit} = B\left[\dfrac{3\,N_{\gamma,2}(\gamma_2/\gamma_1) - (N_{q,2}-1)}{N_{q,2}+1}\right]$ | Meyerhof–Hanna closed form |
| 5 | **Critical Thickness (Pure Cohesive, $\phi=0$)** | $\dfrac{H_{crit}}{B} = \dfrac{3\,c_2 N_c}{\gamma_1 B}$ | Soft clay below |
| 6 | **Equivalent Subgrade Modulus (Hanna & Meyerhof, 1980)** | $k_{s,eq} = k_{s,2}\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{k_{s,1}}{k_{s,2}} - 1\right)\dfrac{H}{B}\right]$ | Two-layer with $H \leq B/2$ |
| 7 | **Equivalent Subgrade Modulus (Weighted, Influence Zone $B$)** | $k_{s,eq} = \dfrac{k_{s,1}H_1 + k_{s,2}H_2 + k_{s,3}H_3}{H_1+H_2+H_3}$ | $H_i \leq B$ each |
| 8 | **Equivalent Elastic Modulus (Boussinesq–Steinbrenner)** | $E_{s,eq} = \dfrac{\sum H_i}{\sum (H_i / E_{s,i})}$ | Harmonic mean, layered profile |
| 9 | **Meyerhof Equivalent $N_c$ (Weak over Strong)** | $N_{c,eq} = \dfrac{N_{c,1} \cdot H + N_{c,2}(B-H)}{B}$ | $H < B$, weighted |
| 10 | **Modified Surcharge on Lower Layer** | $q_2 = \gamma_1 D_f + \gamma_1 H$ | Stress transferred to weak layer |

> ⚠️ **Symbol Disambiguation (no pipe characters used):** All absolute-value bars in this document are written as $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ (LaTeX), never as a literal keyboard `\|` that would break markdown tables.

## 2.4 The Adhesion Parameter $c_a$

A common KTU confusion is the value of $c_a$. Standard recommendations:

- For **dense sand over soft clay**: $c_a \approx c_2$ (cohesion of the *lower* weak clay), because slip occurs in the clay at the interface.
- For **stiff clay over soft clay**: $c_a \approx (0.5 \text{ to } 1.0)\,c_2$.
- For **cohesionless over cohesionless**: $c_a = 0$ (no adhesion; only friction acts).

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore wind turbine foundations** in layered seabed clays over dense tills.
- **Bridge abutments** in riverine deposits (sand over soft alluvial clay).
- **Tank farms** on reclaimed coastal land (fill over compressible marine clay).
- **High-rise raft foundations** in deltaic profiles (stiff crust over deep soft clay).
