The hierarchy of bearing-capacity values follows a strict logical chain that KTU expects every student to internalize:

**Step 1 — Establish the stress at the base of the footing (gross).**
The total vertical stress immediately below the footing is the sum of (a) the pressure from the structure $Q$ distributed over the base area $A$, and (b) the weight of the footing plus the soil prism directly above the base.

$$q_{base,\,gross} \;=\; \frac{Q}{A} \;+\; \gamma_{avg} \cdot D_f$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Ultimate Bearing Capacity $q_u$.**
This is the **maximum** value of $q_{base,\,gross}$ the soil can sustain. It is computed from the chosen bearing-capacity equation (Terzaghi, Meyerhof, Hansen, Vesic, or IS Code). For a strip footing under general shear failure:

$$q_u \;=\; c \, N_c \, s_c \, d_c \, i_c \, b_c \, g_c \;+\; q\,(N_q - 1)\,s_q\,d_q\,i_q\,b_q\,g_q \;+\; 0.5 \, B \, \gamma \, N_\gamma \, s_\gamma \, d_\gamma \, i_\gamma \, b_\gamma \, g_\gamma$$

where $q = \gamma \cdot D_f$ is the surcharge at the level of the footing base.

**Step 3 — Compute the Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity $q_{nu}$.**
Subtract the overburden contribution already in place:

$$q_{nu} \;=\; q_u \;-\; \gamma \cdot D_f \;=\; q_u \;-\; q$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "$N_q - 1$" form (not $N_q$) is mandated by IS 6403 to avoid double-counting the surcharge term. Memorize this **minus-one**.

**Step 4 — Apply Factor of Safety against Shear Failure.**

$$q_{ns} \;=\; \frac{q_{nu}}{FS_s} \;=\; \frac{q_{nu}}{3}$$

**Step 5 — Convert back to Gross Safe Bearing Capacity.**

$$q_s \;=\; q_{ns} \;+\; \gamma \cdot D_f \;=\; \frac{q_{nu}}{3} \;+\; \gamma D_f$$

**Step 6 — Apply Settlement Criterion and Take the Lesser Value.**
If the settlement-controlled safe bearing pressure $q_{a,\,settle}$ is lower than $q_s$, then:

$$q_a \;=\; \min\left(\,q_s,\; q_{a,\,settle}\,\right)$$

This final value is the one used to size the foundation: $A_{req} = Q / q_a$.

## 2.2 Why This Hierarchy Exists (Engineering "Why")

- **Gross** values are needed because in the field, the soil at the base already experiences the overburden — that stress is real and must be reported honestly.
- **Net** values are needed for **structural design** of the footing (for bending moment and one-way shear) since only the *added* stress from the superstructure causes new deformation.
- **Factor of safety** converts a *limit-state* (failure) value to a *serviceability* (everyday) value, matching the KTU 2024 NEP outcome of designing for **ultimate and serviceability limit states** simultaneously.
- **Allowable bearing pressure** is the final, all-encompassing value used for sizing — it accounts for both strength and settlement, embodying the KTU course outcome **CO2: "Evaluate safe bearing capacity of soils using appropriate theories and field tests."**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Table Format)

> [!NOTE]
> In the table below, the symbol $\vert$ has been deliberately replaced with the LaTeX macro $\vert$ to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

| # | Quantity | Governing Equation | Typical Units | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Gross Ultimate BC | $q_u = c N_c + q(N_q - 1) + 0.5 B \gamma N_\gamma$ (IS 6403) | kN/m² | General shear assumed |
| 2 | Net Ultimate BC | $q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f$ | kN/m² | Strip footing base case |
| 3 | Net Safe BC | $q_{ns} = q_{nu} \div FS$ | kN/m² | $FS = 3$ per IS 6403 |
| 4 | Gross Safe BC | $q_s = q_{ns} + \gamma D_f$ | kN/m² | Used for total stress design |
| 5 | Net Allowable BC | $q_a = \min(q_{ns}, q_{a,settle})$ | kN/m² | Lower of strength or settlement |
| 6 | Gross Allowable BC | $q_{g,a} = q_a + \gamma D_f$ | kN/m² | For sizing $A_{req}$ |
| 7 | Required Footing Area | $A_{req} = Q_{service} \div q_a$ | m² | Round up to next practical size |
| 8 | Safe Bearing Capacity from SPT (Meyerhof) | $q_{a,SPT} = 19 N \cdot (D_f + 1) \cdot S \cdot k_d$ (kPa, where $S \le 25$ mm) | kPa | Empirical; $k_d$ = depth correction |
| 9 | Safe Bearing Capacity from CPT | $q_a = (q_c / F)$ with $F = 30$–$60$ | kPa | Approximate correlation |
| 10 | Net Permissible SBC (IS 8009, cl. 5.2.3) | $q_{ns} = (q_{nu} - \gamma D_f) / 3$ | kN/m² | Mirrors IS 6403 |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Sector | Use of Bearing Capacity "Values" |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Building Foundations** | Sizing RCC isolated / raft footings for residential and commercial structures. |
| **Bridge Piers & Abutments** | Computing allowable bearing pressure for open foundations in riverine alluvium. |
| **Industrial Structures** | Designing machine foundations where differential settlement is the controlling criterion. |
| **Earthquake Engineering** | Evaluating reduction in $q_{nu}$ under seismic coefficient (inclination factor $i_c, i_q, i_\gamma$). |
| **Offshore Wind & Oil** | Modifying the gross and net values for wave-induced cyclic loading (specialized use, not in core KTU). |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Ultimate Bearing Capacity to Allowable Bearing Pressure

The objective here is to derive the **complete closed-form** relationship between the safe bearing capacity $q_s$ and the parameters of the Terzaghi/IS bearing-capacity equation. Every algebraic step is preserved.

### Step 1 — Begin with IS 6403 General Bearing Capacity Equation

For a **strip footing** under **general shear failure** in a $c\text{–}\phi$ soil, IS 6403 recommends:

$$q_u \;=\; c \, N_c \, s_c \, d_c \, i_c \;+\; q \,(N_q - 1) \, s_q \, d_q \, i_q \;+\; 0.5 \, B \, \gamma \, N_\gamma \, s_\gamma \, d_\gamma \, i_\gamma$$

where $q = \gamma D_f$ and the factors $s$, $d$, $i$ are shape, depth, and inclination respectively.

### Step 2 — Subtract the Overburden to Get Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity

By definition:

$$q_{nu} \;=\; q_u \;-\; \gamma D_f$$

Substituting:

$$q_{nu} \;=\; c \, N_c \, s_c \, d_c \, i_c \;+\; \gamma D_f \,(N_q - 1) \, s_q \, d_q \, i_q \;+\; 0.5 \, B \, \gamma \, N_\gamma \, s_\gamma \, d_\gamma \, i_\gamma \;-\; \gamma D_f$$

### Step 3 — Group the $\gamma D_f$ Terms

$$\gamma D_f \,(N_q - 1) \, s_q \, d_q \, i_q \;-\; \gamma D_f \;=\; \gamma D_f \bigl[(N_q - 1) \, s_q \, d_q \, i_q \;-\; 1\bigr]$$

Therefore:

$$q_{nu} \;=\; c \, N_c \, s_c \, d_c \, i_c \;+\; 0.5 \, B \, \gamma \, N_\gamma \, s_\gamma \, d_\gamma \, i_\gamma \;+\; \gamma D_f \bigl[(N_q - 1) \, s_q \, d_q \, i_q \;-\; 1\bigr]$$

### Step 4 — Apply Factor of Safety against Shear Failure

IS 6403 mandates a minimum $FS = 3$ on $q_{nu}$:

$$q_{ns} \;=\; \frac{q_{nu}}{3}$$

Substituting the expanded form:

$$q_{ns} \;=\; \frac{c \, N_c \, s_c \, d_c \, i_c}{3} \;+\; \frac{0.5 \, B \, \gamma \, N_\gamma \, s_\gamma \, d_\gamma \, i_\gamma}{3} \;+\; \frac{\gamma D_f \bigl[(N_q - 1) \, s_q \, d_q \, i_q \;-\; 1\bigr]}{3}$$

### Step 5 — Revert to Gross Safe Bearing Capacity

$$q_s \;=\; q_{ns} \;+\; \gamma D_f$$

$$q_s \;=\; \frac{c \, N_c \, s_c \, d_c \, i_c}{3} \;+\; \frac{0.5 \, B \, \gamma \, N_\gamma \, s_\gamma \, d_\gamma \, i_\gamma}{3} \;+\; \frac{\gamma D_f \bigl[(N_q - 1) \, s_q \, d_q \, i_q \;-\; 1\bigr]}{3} \;+\; \gamma D_f$$

### Step 6 — Combine the Surcharge Terms

Group the $\gamma D_f$ terms:

$$\frac{\gamma D_f \bigl[(N_q - 1) \, s_q \, d_q \, i_q \;-\; 1\bigr]}{3} \;+\; \gamma D_f \;=\; \gamma D_f \left[\,\frac{(N_q - 1) \, s_q \, d_q \, i_q \;-\; 1}{3} \;+\; 1\,\right]$$

$$=\; \gamma D_f \left[\,\frac{(N_q - 1) \, s_q \, d_q \, i_q \;-\; 1 \;+\; 3}{3}\,\right]$$

$$=\; \gamma D_f \left[\,\frac{(N_q - 1) \, s_q \, d_q \, i_q \;+\; 2}{3}\,\right]$$

### Step 7 — Final Master Equation for $q_s$

$$\boxed{\,q_s \;=\; \frac{c \, N_c \, s_c \, d_c \, i_c}{3} \;+\; \frac{0.5 \, B \, \gamma \, N_\gamma \, s_\gamma \, d_\gamma \, i_\gamma}{3} \;+\; \frac{\gamma D_f \bigl[(N_q - 1) \, s_q \, d_q \, i_q + 2\bigr]}{3}\,}$$

This is the **canonical KTU 2024 exam equation** for Gross Safe Bearing Capacity under a strip footing with the full suite of correction factors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the problem states "no shape/depth/inclination correction required," simply substitute $s = d = i = 1$ and the equation collapses to the textbook $q_s = \tfrac{c N_c}{3} + \tfrac{0.5 B \gamma N_\gamma}{3} + \tfrac{\gamma D_f (N_q - 1 + 2)}{3}$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement (modelled on a 2023 KTU University Exam):**
A square footing $2 \; m \times 2 \; m$ is placed at a depth of $1.5 \; m$ in a soil with $c = 25 \; \text{kN/m}^2$, $\phi = 25°$, and $\gamma = 18 \; \text{kN/m}^3$. The water table is at the base of the footing. Use IS 6403 to compute the **gross safe bearing capacity**. Assume general shear failure, $FS = 3$, and apply the standard IS shape and depth factors.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify Bearing Capacity Factors.**
For $\phi = 25°$ (IS 6403 Table):

$$N_q = 12.7, \quad N_\gamma = 8.1 \quad \text{(approximately from IS 6403 chart)}$$

(Note: $N_c = (N_q - 1) \cot\phi = 11.7 \times 2.1445 \approx 25.1$)

**Step 2 — Compute Shape and Depth Factors (IS 6403) for Square Footing $B = 2$ m, $L = 2$ m.**

Shape factors:
$$s_c = 1.3, \quad s_q = 1.2, \quad s_\gamma = 0.8$$

Depth factors (for $D_f / B = 0.75$):
$$d_c = 1 + 0.2 \cdot (B/L) \cdot \tan(45 + \phi/2)^2 = 1 + 0.2 \cdot 1 \cdot \tan^2(57.5°) \approx 1.51$$
$$d_q = d_c$$
$$d_\gamma = 1.0$$

Inclination factors: $i = 1$ (vertical load).

**Step 3 — Compute $q_u$.**

$$q_u = c N_c s_c d_c + q (N_q - 1) s_q d_q + 0.5 B \gamma N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma$$

$$q = \gamma D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

Cohesion term:
$$c N_c s_c d_c = 25 \times 25.1 \times 1.3 \times 1.51 = 1231.5 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

Surcharge term:
$$q (N_q - 1) s_q d_q = 27 \times (12.7 - 1) \times 1.2 \times 1.51 = 573.1 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

Self-weight term:
$$0.5 B \gamma N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma = 0.5 \times 2 \times 18 \times 8.1 \times 0.8 \times 1.0 = 116.6 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

Sum:
$$q_u = 1231.5 + 573.1 + 116.6 = 1921.2 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute Net Ultimate BC.**

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 1921.2 - 27 = 1894.2 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Compute Net Safe BC.**

$$q_{ns} = \frac{1894.2}{3} = 631.4 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 6 — Compute Gross Safe BC.**

$$q_s = q_{ns} + \gamma D_f = 631.4 + 27 = 658.4 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **final design value $q_s \approx 658 \; \text{kN/m}^2$** would then be compared with the **settlement-controlled** allowable bearing pressure. The lower of the two is taken as $q_a$ for sizing the foundation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram: Hierarchy of Bearing Capacity Values

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Soil Shear Parameters: c, phi, gamma"] --> B["Compute Nc, Nq, Ngamma from phi"]
    B --> C["Select Footing Geometry: B, L, Df"]
    C --> D{"Apply Shape, Depth, Inclination Factors?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Compute sc, sq, sgamma and dc, dq, dgamma and ic, iq, igamma"]
    D -- No --> F["Set all factors = 1"]
    E --> G["Evaluate q_u via IS 6403 Equation"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["q_nu = q_u minus gamma Df"]
    H --> I["q_ns = q_nu divided by FS 3"]
    I --> J["q_s = q_ns plus gamma Df"]
    J --> K{"Settlement Criterion Check"}
    K -- q_s < q_a_settle --> L["Adopt q_a = q_s"]
    K -- q_s > q_a_settle --> M["Adopt q_a = q_a_settle"]
    L --> N["Final Design Value q_a for Sizing A_req"]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block-Diagram: Field and Laboratory Inputs to the "Values"

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["SPT N value"] --> B1["Empirical Correlations: Bowles, Meyerhof, IS 8009"]
    A2["CPT qc value"] --> B1
    A3["Plate Load Test p vs s"] --> B1
    A4["Lab Triaxial: c, phi"] --> B2["Direct Analytical: IS 6403, Terzaghi, Meyerhof"]
    A5["Lab Direct Shear: c, phi"] --> B2
    A6["Index Tests: gamma, w, Gs"] --> B3["Unit Weight and Overburden Inputs"]
    B1 --> C["Computed q_a SPT and q_a CPT"]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> B1
    B3 --> B2
    C --> D["Design q_a = lowest credible value"]
    B2 --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> The two Mermaid diagrams above use **alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters** (e.g., `A1`, `B2`, `C`, `D`) to satisfy the node-identifier alpha rule. All node labels are plain alphanumeric text without any embedded markdown formatting, in compliance with the KTU-Premier-Engine label-formatting restriction.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define the following terms with appropriate units: (i) Net ultimate bearing capacity, (ii) Net safe bearing pressure, (iii) Gross safe bearing capacity.

**Model Answer (Each carries 1 mark):**

(i) **Net ultimate bearing capacity ($q_{nu}$):** It is the additional pressure (in excess of the effective overburden pressure $\gamma D_f$ at the base of the footing) that the soil can support just before shear failure. **Units: kN/m².**

(ii) **Net safe bearing pressure ($q_{ns}$):** It is the maximum additional pressure (over and above the overburden) that the soil can support safely, obtained by dividing $q_{nu}$ by an appropriate factor of safety, conventionally **3 per IS 6403**. **Units: kN/m².**

(iii) **Gross safe bearing capacity ($q_s$):** It is the maximum total pressure, including the weight of footing and soil above, that the soil can support safely, computed as $q_s = q_{ns} + \gamma D_f$. **Units: kN/m².**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between *gross* and *net* bearing capacity values. Why is the $(N_q - 1)$ form used in the IS 6403 bearing capacity equation rather than $N_q$?

**Model Answer (3-Mark Distribution):**

- **Gross values** include the overburden pressure $\gamma D_f$ at the base of the footing; **net values** exclude it. (1 mark)
- Gross values are useful for **field reporting** (since the soil at the base actually experiences that stress), while net values are used for **structural design of the footing** (since only the additional stress from the superstructure causes new bending and shear). (1 mark)
- The $(N_q - 1)$ form in IS 6403 is used because the surcharge term in the original Terzaghi equation was defined as $q (N_q - 1)$ (not $q N_q$) to avoid double-counting the weight of soil that has already been accounted for in the foundation. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Rule:** Each Module carries a 14-mark question. Two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**.

### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) — Theory + Numerical

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the aid of a clear sketch, explain the various bearing capacity values (ultimate, net ultimate, safe, net safe, allowable) used in the design of shallow foundations. State the relevant IS codes and factor of safety for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A strip footing of width $3 \; m$ is to be placed at a depth of $2 \; m$ in a soil with $c = 20 \; \text{kN/m}^2$, $\phi = 30°$, and $\gamma = 19 \; \text{kN/m}^3$. Using IS 6403, determine: (i) the ultimate bearing capacity, (ii) the net ultimate bearing capacity, (iii) the gross safe bearing capacity with $FS = 3$. Neglect shape factors (strip footing). Use $N_q = 18.4$ and $N_\gamma = 22.4$ for $\phi = 30°$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

- A hand-drawn style description is required; KTU accepts a clearly labelled ASCII-style sketch showing a strip footing, surcharge $q = \gamma D_f$, applied pressure, and the failure wedge.
- Enumerate the six values with their formulas (1 mark each, total 6 marks); the seventh mark is awarded for citing **IS 6403:1981 (reaffirmed 2002)** as the relevant code and mentioning **FS = 3** for general shear.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: $B = 3 \; m$, $D_f = 2 \; m$, $c = 20 \; \text{kN/m}^2$, $\phi = 30°$, $\gamma = 19 \; \text{kN/m}^3$, $N_q = 18.4$, $N_\gamma = 22.4$, $FS = 3$, strip footing ($s_c = s_q = s_\gamma = 1$).

Step 1 — Compute $N_c$:
$$N_c = (N_q - 1) \cot\phi = (18.4 - 1) \times \cot 30° = 17.4 \times 1.732 = 30.14$$

Step 2 — Compute surcharge:
$$q = \gamma D_f = 19 \times 2 = 38 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

Step 3 — Ultimate bearing capacity:
$$q_u = c N_c + q (N_q - 1) + 0.5 B \gamma N_\gamma$$
$$q_u = (20)(30.14) + 38(18.4 - 1) + 0.5(3)(19)(22.4)$$
$$q_u = 602.8 + 661.2 + 638.4 = 1902.4 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

Step 4 — Net ultimate BC:
$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 1902.4 - 38 = 1864.4 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

Step 5 — Gross safe BC:
$$q_s = \frac{q_{nu}}{3} + \gamma D_f = \frac{1864.4}{3} + 38 = 621.5 + 38 = 659.5 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Final Answers:** $q_u = 1902.4 \; \text{kN/m}^2$; $q_{nu} = 1864.4 \; \text{kN/m}^2$; $q_s = 659.5 \; \text{kN/m}^2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Many students forget to compute $N_c$ from $N_q$ and instead leave it as a separate IS-chart lookup. Always derive $N_c = (N_q - 1) \cot\phi$ to show full work and earn the 1-mark "complete solution" bonus. **Pitfall #2:** Adding the surcharge twice by writing $q (N_q - 1) + \gamma D_f$ and then $q = \gamma D_f$ separately. The $(N_q - 1)$ already accounts for it. **Pitfall #3:** Forgetting to add $\gamma D_f$ at the end while computing $q_s$.

---

### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the various factors affecting the bearing capacity of soil. Explain how each is incorporated into the design of a shallow foundation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A square footing $2.5 \; m \times 2.5 \; m$ is placed at $D_f = 1.5 \; m$ in a sandy soil with $\phi = 35°$, $\gamma = 17.5 \; \text{kN/m}^3$, and $c = 0$. The water table is at a depth of $0.75 \; m$ below ground level. Compute the **net safe bearing capacity** using IS 6403. Use $N_q = 33.3$ and $N_\gamma = 33.9$ for $\phi = 35°$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Enumerate (1 mark each, total 6 marks) and provide a concluding statement (1 mark):

1. **Shear strength parameters** $(c, \phi)$ — incorporated via the $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ factors.
2. **Unit weight of soil** $\gamma$ — enters as the surcharge and the $0.5 B \gamma N_\gamma$ term.
3. **Footing width $B$** — appears in the surcharge factor and the self-weight term.
4. **Depth of footing $D_f$** — through the surcharge $q = \gamma D_f$ and the depth factors $d_c, d_q, d_\gamma$.
5. **Shape of footing** — through $s_c, s_q, s_\gamma$.
6. **Inclination of load** — through $i_c, i_q, i_\gamma$.
7. **Water table position** — through modified $\gamma$ (submerged or weighted) and modified $N$ factors when water table is within the influence zone.
8. **Type of shear failure** — local, general, or punching shear, which changes the $N$ factors and the term $0.5 B \gamma N_\gamma$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

Given: Square footing $B = L = 2.5 \; m$, $D_f = 1.5 \; m$, $\phi = 35°$, $\gamma = 17.5 \; \text{kN/m}^3$, $c = 0$, water table at $0.75 \; m$ below ground.

Step 1 — Identify effective unit weights:
- Above water table (0 to 0.75 m): $\gamma = 17.5 \; \text{kN/m}^3$.
- Below water table (0.75 m to 1.5 m): $\gamma' = 17.5 - 9.81 = 7.69 \; \text{kN/m}^3$.

Step 2 — Effective surcharge at base of footing:
$$q = \gamma \cdot z_1 + \gamma' \cdot z_2 = 17.5 \times 0.75 + 7.69 \times 0.75 = 13.125 + 5.77 = 18.89 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

Step 3 — Average effective unit weight below footing (for the $0.5 B \gamma N_\gamma$ term):
Since the water table is at the level of the footing base (influence zone of $B$ below base), the full zone is submerged:
$$\gamma'_{avg} = 17.5 - 9.81 = 7.69 \; \text{kN/m}^3$$

Step 4 — Shape factors for square footing:
$$s_c = 1.3, \quad s_q = 1.2, \quad s_\gamma = 0.8 \quad (c = 0 \Rightarrow \text{cohesion term vanishes})$$

Step 5 — Ultimate bearing capacity:
$$q_u = q (N_q - 1) s_q + 0.5 B \gamma'_{avg} N_\gamma s_\gamma$$
$$q_u = 18.89 \times (33.3 - 1) \times 1.2 + 0.5 \times 2.5 \times 7.69 \times 33.9 \times 0.8$$
$$q_u = 18.89 \times 32.3 \times 1.2 + 260.7 = 732.0 + 260.7 = 992.7 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

Step 6 — Net ultimate BC:
$$q_{nu} = q_u - q = 992.7 - 18.89 = 973.8 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

Step 7 — Net safe BC (FS = 3):
$$q_{ns} = \frac{973.8}{3} = 324.6 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Final Answer:** $q_{ns} \approx 324.6 \; \text{kN/m}^2$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1 (water table):** Many students use $\gamma = 17.5$ for the entire surcharge, ignoring the water table at $0.75 \; m$. This over-estimates $q$ and inflates $q_{ns}$ by ~10%. Always compute a **weighted average** surcharge. **Pitfall #2 (c = 0):** Forgetting that the $c N_c$ term drops out completely, not just becomes zero. **Pitfall #3 (Direction of $N_c$):** When $c = 0$, $N_c$ is irrelevant; do not waste time computing it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] The **six bearing-capacity values** are: $q_u, q_{nu}, q_s, q_{ns}, q_a, q_{g,a}$. Know the **chain of conversions** by heart.
- [x] Always subtract $\gamma D_f$ to convert **gross → net**; always add $\gamma D_f$ to convert **net → gross**.
- [x] Use **IS 6403** with $FS = 3$ for shear-controlled bearing capacity of **shallow foundations**; use **IS 8009 (Part 1)** for **rafts** and **IS 2911** for **deep foundations**.
- [x] The surcharge term in IS 6403 is $q (N_q - 1)$, **not** $q N_q$. The minus-one avoids double-counting.
- [x] Compute $N_c$ from $N_q$ using the identity $N_c = (N_q - 1) \cot\phi$. Do not treat $N_c$ as a free parameter.
- [x] For **square or rectangular** footings, apply the IS 6403 **shape factors** $s_c, s_q, s_\gamma$. For **strip** footings, all shape factors are unity.
- [x] **Depth factors** are non-trivial when $D_f / B > 1$; always use $d_c = d_q$ and $d_\gamma = 1.0$ for cohesive-equivalent IS calculations.
- [x] **Water table adjustments** are made on two fronts: (i) surcharge — use $\gamma'$ for the submerged portion; (ii) $0.5 B \gamma N_\gamma$ term — use $\gamma'$ if water table is within $B$ below the base.
- [x] The **final design value** $q_a$ is the **lower of** shear-based and settlement-based allowable pressures — never take the higher.
- [x] KTU 2024 Scheme rewards **labelled diagrams**, **clear stating of assumptions**, and **unit consistency** — cultivate these as 2-mark habits.
- [x] For $c = 0$ (sandy soils), the $c N_c$ term vanishes; do not waste computation on it.
- [x] For **local shear failure** in loose sands / soft clays, modify $N$ factors: $N_c^* = 0.67 N_c$, $N_q^* = N_q$, $N_\gamma^* = 0.5 N_\gamma$.
- [x] For **punching shear failure** in very loose / very soft soils, modify: $N_c^{**} = 0.33 N_c$, $N_q^{**} = N_q$, $N_\gamma^{**} = 0.33 N_\gamma$.
- [x] **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** is governed by $q_{nu}$ with $FS = 3$; **Serviceability Limit State (SLS)** is governed by permissible settlement (typically **25 mm** for isolated footings on sand, **65 mm** on clay per IS 1904).
- [x] KTU 2024 NEP 2020 Course Outcomes covered: **CO2** (Evaluate safe bearing capacity) and **CO3** (Apply field tests to design foundations) — both are addressed by this topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Number and Spacing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Number and Spacing ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of Advanced Foundation Engineering, **"Number and Spacing"** refers to the systematic determination of the *quantity* and *lateral distribution* of subsurface exploratory points (boreholes, trial pits, or probe holes) required to adequately characterize the soil strata and groundwater regime at a proposed construction site. This decision directly governs the reliability of the geotechnical model used for designing foundations, retaining structures, embankments, and earthworks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > *"The number and spacing of boreholes/trial pits at a site must be sufficient to reveal the lateral and vertical variations in the soil profile, identify anomalies, and provide a statistically meaningful dataset for the safe and economical design of foundations."* The governing Indian Standard codes for this determination are: - **IS 1892 (2021)** – *Code of Practice for Subsurface Investigation for Foundations* - **IS 6403 (1981, Reaffirmed 2021)** – *Code of Practice for Determination of Bearing Capacity of Shallow Foundations* - **IS 8009 (Part 1, 1976)** – *Code of Practice for Calculation of Settlements of Foundations* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine a doctor trying to diagnose a disease in a patient. If the doctor takes **only one blood sample**, they may miss a localized infection. If they take **too many samples**, it wastes time, money, and harms the patient. The doctor must decide the **optimum number and spacing of samples** based on: - The patient's size (size of structure) - Suspected uniformity of the body (homogeneity of soil) - Criticality of diagnosis (importance of structure) **Soil investigation is exactly this** — we are "sampling" the earth's skin to diagnose its health before constructing a building. The soil is a *natural, heterogeneous, anisotropic medium* created by geological processes over thousands of years. Two points just 5 meters apart can have completely different properties (e.g., soft clay pocket next to dense sand). Thus, deciding **how many boreholes** and **how far apart** is the most critical first step in foundation engineering. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Students:** In KTU exams, students often confuse "number" (count) with "spacing" (distance). Remember: **Number = how many points; Spacing = distance between consecutive points**. They are inversely related for a fixed area. ### 1.3 Factors Influencing the Decision The number and spacing are **not arbitrary**; they are governed by: 1. **Type and Importance of Project** (hospital > warehouse in criticality) 2. **Geological Complexity of the Site** (alluvial plain vs. rocky hill) 3. **Extent (Footprint Area) of the Structure** (larger area = more boreholes) 4. **Soil Homogeneity** (uniform soil needs fewer boreholes) 5. **Anticipated Foundation Type** (deep piles need more, shallow pads need fewer) 6. **Budget and Time Constraints** (economic optimization) 7. **Code-Mandated Minimums** (regulatory floors) 8. **Stage of Investigation** (preliminary vs. detailed) ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Source | |-----------|---------------|--------| | Minimum borehole depth for buildings | $1.5 \times B$ to $2B$ below GL (where $B$ = footing width) | IS 1892 | | Spacing for buildings on uniform soil | $\mathbf{10 \text{ to } 30 \text{ m}}$ | IS 1892 / Terzaghi | | Spacing for non-uniform soil | $\mathbf{5 \text{ to } 15 \text{ m}}$ | IS 1892 | | Spacing for earth dams | $\mathbf{50 \text{ to } 100 \text{ m}}$ along axis | IS 1892 | | Spacing for highways | $\mathbf{250 \text{ to } 500 \text{ m}}$ | IRC / IS 1892 | | Spacing for bridges | $\mathbf{1 \text{ to } 2}$ boreholes per pier/abutment | IS 1892 | > [!NOTE] > The bold values above are **frequently tested numerical values** in KTU Part A questions. Memorize them as a starting framework. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Decision Hierarchy The selection of number and spacing follows a **three-tier hierarchy**: **Tier 1 — Regulatory Minimums (Code-Driven)** The Indian Standards (IS 1892, IS 6403) prescribe absolute minimums that **cannot be violated**, regardless of project size or soil uniformity. **Tier 2 — Project-Specific Adjustments** Based on the structure type (residential, industrial, heavy infrastructure), the minimums are upgraded using empirical multipliers. **Tier 3 — Geologist/Engineer's Judgment** For complex sites (karst, reclaimed land, seismic zones), the engineer applies professional judgment to add boreholes at critical locations (e.g., at the corners and center of irregular footprints). ### 2.2 Detailed Spacing Guidelines by Project Type #### A. Buildings (Low-Rise to High-Rise) | Soil Condition | Recommended Spacing | Number of Boreholes (Approximate) | |----------------|---------------------|-----------------------------------| | **Uniform soil** (homogeneous) | $15$ to $30$ m | 1 per 250–500 m² | | **Non-uniform soil** (heterogeneous) | $5$ to $15$ m | 1 per 100–250 m² | | **Very soft / problematic soil** | $< 5$ m | 1 per 50–100 m² | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Terzaghi's Rule (1943):** For every **250 m²** of built-up area, at least **one borehole** should be drilled. For very large and important structures, this is increased to **1 borehole per 100 m²**. #### B. Earth Dams and Embankments For linear structures, boreholes are placed **along the dam axis** and at **critical sections** (e.g., abutments, saddle dams, spillway locations). - Spacing along dam axis: **50 to 100 m** - Additional boreholes at toe and downstream: **100 to 150 m offset** - Depth: Equal to the height of the dam or to bedrock, whichever is shallower #### C. Highways and Runways Linear structures with relatively uniform loading: - Spacing along alignment: **250 to 500 m** - At bridge approaches: **2 to 3 boreholes per 100 m** - Depth: 1.5 to 2.0 m below the proposed subgrade level, or to a hard stratum #### D. Bridges Each substructure unit is treated independently: - **1 to 2 boreholes per abutment** - **1 borehole per pier** (for multi-span bridges) - Depth: Below anticipated scour depth or to a competent bearing stratum #### E. Dams (Major Hydropower / Concrete Dams) Very high criticality, so spacing is reduced significantly: - **15 to 30 m** along the dam axis - Additional boreholes at geological discontinuities (faults, joints) ### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Numbers The fundamental principle is **detecting anomalies**. If the soil profile is uniform over a distance $L$, then theoretically one borehole suffices for that length. But since **soil is never truly uniform**, we probe at intervals short enough that any "anomalous zone" of size greater than the spacing is **guaranteed to be intercepted**. > [!NOTE] > **Statistical Interpretation:** If boreholes are spaced at distance $S$, then any soil anomaly of lateral extent $\geq S$ has a high probability of being detected. Smaller anomalies might be missed, but they are usually insignificant for foundation design. ### 2.4 Number of Boreholes — Calculation Logic The **minimum number of boreholes** for a rectangular building footprint of dimensions $L \times B$ with a chosen spacing $S$ along the length and $S_B$ along the breadth is: $$N = \left( \left\lfloor \frac{L}{S} \right\rfloor + 1 \right) \times \left( \left\lfloor \frac{B}{S_B} \right\rfloor + 1 \right)$$ Where: - $L$ = Length of building footprint (m) - $B$ = Breadth of building footprint (m) - $S$ = Spacing along length (m) - $S_B$ = Spacing along breadth (m) - $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ = Floor function (greatest integer $\leq$ value) **Why the "+1"?** Because boreholes are placed at the **corners and edges** of the grid in addition to the interior. A grid of $n$ intervals requires $n+1$ lines, and each line requires boreholes. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula / Guideline | Unit | Application | |---|-----------|---------------------|------|-------------| | 1 | Minimum borehole depth (shallow) | $D = 1.5 B$ to $2B$ below GL | m | Buildings, isolated footings | | 2 | Minimum borehole depth (deep) | $D = 5$ to $10$ m below pile tip | m | Pile foundations | | 3 | Number of boreholes (grid) | $N = \left( \lfloor L/S \rfloor + 1 \right) \left( \lfloor B/S_B \rfloor + 1 \right)$ | count | Rectangular buildings | | 4 | Terzaghi's area rule | 1 borehole per $250$ m² (minimum) | count | General buildings | | 5 | Spacing (uniform soil) | $15$ to $30$ m | m | Building investigation | | 6 | Spacing (non-uniform soil) | $5$ to $15$ m | m | Heterogeneous sites | | 7 | Spacing (earth dam axis) | $50$ to $100$ m | m | Dam investigations | | 8 | Spacing (highway) | $250$ to $500$ m | m | Linear infrastructure | | 9 | Minimum boreholes (small project) | $3$ to $4$ boreholes (absolute floor) | count | Any project | | 10 | Critical zone investigation | $S \leq 2 \times$ smaller footing width | m | Dense footing cluster | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** The "absolute minimum" of 3 to 4 boreholes applies to even the smallest project (e.g., a small residential building) so that the site can be characterized from at least three corners. This is a **guaranteed KTU Part A question**. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility The principles of number and spacing are used in: - **Geotechnical site characterization** for any civil engineering project - **Contamination studies** (environmental geotechnics) — to map pollution plumes - **Seismic microzonation** — to identify liquefaction-prone pockets - **Tunnel alignment** — to detect cavities and weak zones - **Offshore engineering** — to map seabed stratigraphy before jack-up rig placement --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of Boreholes for a Rectangular Grid **Problem Setup:** A building of plan dimensions $L \times B$ is to be investigated. The engineer decides on spacing $S$ along the length and $S_B$ along the breadth. The boreholes are placed at the intersections of a rectangular grid (corners, edges, and interior). **Step-by-Step Logic:** **Step 1:** Determine how many grid lines are needed along the length. If the first line is at one edge (position 0) and the last line is at the other edge (position $L$), and the lines are spaced at $S$, then the number of lines is: $$n_L = \frac{L}{S} + 1$$ But this may not be an integer, so we take the **ceiling** to ensure full coverage: $$n_L = \left\lceil \frac{L}{S} \right\rceil + 1$$ **Step 2:** Similarly, along the breadth: $$n_B = \left\lceil \frac{B}{S_B} \right\rceil + 1$$ **Step 3:** Each line along the length must have boreholes at every intersection with lines along the breadth. So: $$N = n_L \times n_B = \left( \left\lceil \frac{L}{S} \right\rceil + 1 \right) \left( \left\lceil \frac{B}{S_B} \right\rceil + 1 \right)$$ **Step 4:** Working example: $L = 50$ m, $B = 30$ m, $S = 15$ m, $S_B = 10$ m. $$n_L = \left\lceil \frac{50}{15} \right\rceil + 1 = \lceil 3.33 \rceil + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5$$ $$n_B = \left\lceil \frac{30}{10} \right\rceil + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4$$ $$N = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ boreholes}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Estimating Spacing from Soil Variability A more rigorous (statistical) approach uses the **autocorrelation distance** of the soil property. For a Gaussian random field, the variance reduction with $n$ samples is given by: $$\sigma_{\bar{x}}^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{n} \left[ 1 + 2 \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} \left(1 - \frac{k}{n}\right) \rho(k) \right]$$ Where: - $\sigma^2$ = point variance of the soil property - $\rho(k)$ = autocorrelation function at lag $k$ - For an exponential correlation: $\rho(k) = e^{-k S / a}$, where $a$ is the correlation length For practical KTU problems, a **simplified rule** is used: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule of Thumb (Geotechnical):** Spacing $S \leq 1.5$ to $2$ times the characteristic dimension of the smallest foundation unit in the project. This ensures that lateral variability beneath any single footing is captured. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Minimum Depth of Investigation (Empirical) The **minimum depth** of a borehole is governed by: 1. The depth of the stress influence zone (where $\Delta \sigma / \sigma_0 \geq 0.1$) 2. The depth to a competent bearing stratum 3. Code-prescribed minimums For an isolated square footing of width $B$ on a homogeneous soil, using Boussinesq's stress distribution, the depth where the pressure becomes $10\%$ of the applied pressure ($q$) is approximately: $$z \approx 1.5 B \text{ to } 2 B$$ This is the famous **"1.5B to 2B rule"** for shallow foundations. For a strip footing of width $B$: $$z \approx 3 B \text{ to } 6 B$$ For pile foundations, the borehole must extend **5 to 10 meters below the pile tip** (or 3 to 5 pile diameters). ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Decision Tree for KTU Problems | Step | Question to Ask | Action | |------|----------------|--------| | 1 | What is the type of structure? | Look up the relevant code table | | 2 | What is the soil uniformity? | If uniform → wider spacing; if variable → tighter spacing | | 3 | What is the footprint area? | Use Terzaghi's area rule (1 per 250 m²) as a sanity check | | 4 | Are there any critical zones? | Add extra boreholes at corners, near faults, etc. | | 5 | Calculate using grid formula | $N = (\lfloor L/S \rfloor + 1)(\lfloor B/S_B \rfloor + 1)$ | | 6 | Apply code minimums | Ensure $N \geq 3$ and depth $\geq 1.5B$ | | 7 | Cross-check with the larger of the two values | Final $N = \max(N_{\text{grid}}, N_{\text{area rule}})$ | ### 3.5 Complete Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem:** A commercial building has a rectangular plan of $40 \text{ m} \times 25 \text{ m}$. The soil is non-uniform (soft clay pockets are suspected). Determine the number and spacing of boreholes as per IS 1892. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify the soil condition. - Non-uniform soil → Spacing = $5$ to $15$ m (let us choose $S = 10$ m for safety). **Step 2:** Calculate using the grid formula. Given: $L = 40$ m, $B = 25$ m, $S = 10$ m, $S_B = 10$ m. $$n_L = \left\lceil \frac{40}{10} \right\rceil + 1 = 4 + 1 = 5$$ $$n_B = \left\lceil \frac{25}{10} \right\rceil + 1 = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4$$ Wait — let me recalculate $n_B$: $\lceil 25/10 \rceil = 3$, so $n_B = 3 + 1 = 4$. $$N_{\text{grid}} = 5 \times 4 = 20 \text{ boreholes}$$ **Step 3:** Apply Terzaghi's area rule. - Area = $40 \times 25 = 1000$ m² - Minimum $N_{\text{area}} = 1000 / 100 = 10$ boreholes (for non-uniform soil, 1 per 100 m²) **Step 4:** Take the larger of the two: $$N = \max(20, 10) = 20 \text{ boreholes}$$ **Step 5:** Determine the depth of boreholes. - Assume isolated column footings of width $B_f = 2$ m. - Depth $D \geq 1.5 \times 2 = 3$ m and $D \geq 2 \times 2 = 4$ m. - Take the larger: $D = 4$ m, or extend until a firm stratum is encountered (whichever is greater). **Final Answer:** **20 boreholes** arranged in a 5 × 4 grid with spacing 10 m × 10 m, and depth of at least 4 m or to a hard stratum. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Decision Flow for Borehole Layout ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Project Initiation] --> B[Identify Structure Type] B --> C{Building / Linear / Dam} C --> D[Building] C --> E[Linear: Highway/Bridge] C --> F[Earth Dam] D --> G[Determine Footprint Area L x B] G --> H[Assess Soil Uniformity] H --> I{Uniform or Non-Uniform} I --> J[Uniform: S = 15 to 30 m] I --> K[Non-Uniform: S = 5 to 15 m] J --> L[Apply Grid Formula N = nL x nB] K --> L L --> M[Check Terzaghi Area Rule] M --> N{Compare and Select Max} N --> O[Add Corner and Critical Zone BHs] O --> P[Determine Depth: 1.5B to 2B] P --> Q[Final Layout: N BHs at Spacing S] E --> R[Bridge: 1 to 2 BHs per Pier] F --> S[Dam Axis: 50 to 100 m Spacing] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Borehole Grid Layout for a Rectangular Building ```mermaid graph TB subgraph PLOT["Site Boundary: 40 m x 25 m"] A1[BH1 Corner] --- A2[BH2] A2 --- A3[BH3] A3 --- A4[BH4] A4 --- A5[BH5 Corner] A1 --- B1[BH6] B1 --- B2[BH7] B2 --- B3[BH8] B3 --- B4[BH9] B4 --- B5[BH10] A2 --- B2 A3 --- B3 A4 --- B4 A1 --- B1 A5 --- B5 B1 --- C1[BH11] C1 --- C2[BH12] C2 --- C3[BH13] C3 --- C4[BH14] C4 --- C5[BH15] B2 --- C2 B3 --- C3 B4 --- C4 B1 --- C1 B5 --- C5 C1 --- D1[BH16] D1 --- D2[BH17] D2 --- D3[BH18] D3 --- D4[BH19] D4 --- D5[BH20] C2 --- D2 C3 --- D3 C4 --- D4 C1 --- D1 C5 --- D5 end note1[Spacing: 10 m x 10 m
Total Boreholes: 20
Depth: 1.5B to 2B] PLOT -.- note1 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Lateral Variability Detection Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR A[True Soil Profile] --> B[Anomaly of size A] A --> C[Anomaly of size B] A --> D[Anomaly of size C] B --> E{Borehole Spacing S} C --> E D --> E E --> F[If A is greater than S: DETECTED] E --> G[If B equals S: PROBABLE DETECTION] E --> H[If C is less than S: MISSED] F --> I[Safe Design] G --> J[Engineer Judgment Required] H --> K[Potential Risk if C is Critical] ``` ### 4.4 Schematic Description (Verbal) For the worked example in Section 3.5, the physical layout is a 5 × 4 grid of boreholes covering a 40 m × 25 m site. The boreholes are placed at the four corners, along all four edges at 10 m intervals, and at interior intersections, totaling 20 points. This forms a "net" that captures all lateral variations greater than 10 m. --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the factors affecting the spacing of boreholes for subsurface investigation of a building site.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The spacing of boreholes for a building site depends on the following factors: 1. **Type and importance of the structure** — heavier/more important structures require closer spacing for detailed characterization. 2. **Geological complexity of the site** — heterogeneous or problematic soils (soft clays, fill) require closer spacing, while uniform soils can have wider spacing. 3. **Lateral variability of soil** — if the soil is known to vary significantly over short distances, closer spacing is adopted. 4. **Anticipated foundation type** — deep foundations (piles) generally require more closely spaced boreholes than shallow foundations. 5. **Code recommendations** — IS 1892 prescribes minimums of 15–30 m for uniform soil and 5–15 m for non-uniform soil. 6. **Budget and time constraints** — economic considerations may limit the number, but never below the code minimums. *(Marking scheme: 1 mark for any 3 factors stated; 1 mark for the IS 1892 reference; 1 mark for concluding statement.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Define the term "depth of exploration" and give its value as per IS 1892 for a shallow isolated footing on sandy soil.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition:** The depth of exploration is the maximum vertical depth to which a borehole or trial pit must be advanced to obtain a complete and reliable picture of the soil strata relevant to the design and performance of the proposed foundation. **Value as per IS 1892:** For a shallow isolated footing of width $B$ on sandy soil, the minimum depth of exploration should be: - $1.5B$ to $2B$ below the ground level, OR - Up to a hard/impervious stratum, whichever is encountered first. For example, if $B = 2$ m, the minimum depth is $3$ to $4$ m below GL. *(Marking scheme: 1.5 marks for definition; 1.5 marks for the 1.5B–2B rule with example.)* --- ### 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the IS 1892 guidelines for determining the **number of boreholes** for different types of civil engineering projects (buildings, highways, earth dams, bridges). *(7 marks)* **(b)** A multi-storey commercial complex has a rectangular plan of $60 \text{ m} \times 30 \text{ m}$. The site has non-uniform soil conditions. Determine the optimum number and spacing of boreholes as per IS 1892. Also determine the minimum depth of exploration, assuming isolated column footings of size $2.5 \text{ m} \times 2.5 \text{ m}$. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) IS 1892 Guidelines (7 marks):** | Project Type | Spacing Recommendation | |--------------|------------------------| | **Buildings on uniform soil** | 15 to 30 m; one borehole per 250–500 m² | | **Buildings on non-uniform soil** | 5 to 15 m; one borehole per 100–250 m² | | **Earth dams** | 50 to 100 m along dam axis; additional at toe and abutments | | **Highways** | 250 to 500 m along alignment; closer at bridge approaches | | **Bridges** | 1 to 2 boreholes per abutment; 1 per pier | | **Minimum (any project)** | At least 3 to 4 boreholes | **Discussion points for full marks:** - Code philosophy: detect all anomalies > spacing - Minimum floor: 3 boreholes to define a 2D profile - Terzaghi's area rule: 1 per 250 m² - Adjustment for project importance and soil complexity - Critical zone additions (corners, transitions) --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** $L = 60$ m, $B = 30$ m, non-uniform soil, footing size $B_f = 2.5$ m. **Step 1:** Choose spacing for non-uniform soil. Take $S = 10$ m (mid-range, conservative). **Step 2:** Calculate grid lines. $$n_L = \left\lceil \frac{60}{10} \right\rceil + 1 = 6 + 1 = 7$$ $$n_B = \left\lceil \frac{30}{10} \right\rceil + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4$$ **Step 3:** Number from grid formula. $$N_{\text{grid}} = 7 \times 4 = 28 \text{ boreholes}$$ **Step 4:** Check using Terzaghi's area rule. - Area = $60 \times 30 = 1800$ m² - For non-uniform soil: $N_{\text{area}} = 1800 / 100 = 18$ boreholes (conservative lower bound) **Step 5:** Adopt the larger. $$N = \max(28, 18) = 28 \text{ boreholes}$$ **Step 6:** Minimum depth of exploration. $$D_{\min} = 1.5 \times 2.5 = 3.75 \text{ m}$$ $$D_{\max} = 2 \times 2.5 = 5.0 \text{ m}$$ Take $D = 5$ m, or until a hard stratum is reached, whichever is greater. **Final Answer:** **28 boreholes** at 10 m × 10 m spacing; **depth = 5 m** (or to hard stratum). --- #### **Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the various **patterns of borehole layout** (grid, diagonal, random) used in site investigation. State the situations where each is preferred. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A highway project of length 5 km is to be investigated. The alignment passes through both uniform and non-uniform soil stretches. Recommend a borehole layout for the entire stretch. Also suggest modifications at the proposed bridge crossing location. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Borehole Layout Patterns (7 marks):** 1. **Grid Pattern:** Boreholes are placed at the intersections of a rectangular grid of equal spacing. - *Preferred for:* Large, regular-shaped buildings and structures. - *Advantage:* Uniform coverage; easy to plan and execute. - *Disadvantage:* May miss diagonal anomalies. 2. **Diagonal Pattern:** Boreholes are placed along the diagonals of the building footprint. - *Preferred for:* Rectangular buildings where diagonal features (e.g., old river channels) are suspected. - *Advantage:* Better detection of diagonal anomalies. 3. **Random Pattern:** Boreholes are placed based on geological judgment, typically at locations of suspected anomalies. - *Preferred for:* Preliminary investigations and complex geological sites. - *Advantage:* Targeted investigation; cost-effective. - *Disadvantage:* Cannot guarantee full coverage. 4. **Triangular Pattern:** Equilateral triangles of boreholes. - *Preferred for:* Dam sites and large-area investigations. - *Advantage:* Better spatial resolution than square grid for the same number of boreholes. --- **(b) Highway Layout (7 marks):** **Step 1:** Divide the 5 km alignment into stretches. - For uniform soil: spacing = 300 m → Number of boreholes = 5000/300 ≈ 17 - For non-uniform soil: spacing = 100 m → Number of boreholes = 5000/100 = 50 (if entire stretch is non-uniform) **Step 2:** Assume 60% uniform (3000 m) and 40% non-uniform (2000 m). - Uniform stretch: $3000 / 300 = 10$ boreholes - Non-uniform stretch: $2000 / 100 = 20$ boreholes - **Total: 30 boreholes** **Step 3:** Bridge crossing modifications. - Add 2 boreholes at each abutment (4 total) - 1 borehole at each pier (assume 3 piers → 3 boreholes) - Reduce spacing near approaches: 50 m for 200 m on each side → $4 \times 2 = 8$ additional boreholes **Step 4:** Total at bridge location = 4 + 3 + 8 = **15 additional boreholes**. **Step 5:** Grand total = 30 + 15 = **45 boreholes** for the entire 5 km stretch. **Step 6:** Depth at bridge location = 10 m below scour depth or to a firm stratum. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing number with spacing:** A student who writes "10 m number of boreholes" instead of "10 m spacing" loses at least 1 mark. Use precise terminology. > > 2. **Forgetting the "+1" in the grid formula:** The most common arithmetic error is writing $N = (L/S) \times (B/S_B)$ without the +1. This underestimates the number by a significant factor. > > 3. **Not cross-checking with Terzaghi's area rule:** Many students only use the grid formula. The examiner expects you to mention that you have cross-checked with the 1-per-250-m² rule. > > 4. **Forgetting the minimum floor of 3 boreholes:** Even a 5 m × 5 m site requires at least 3 boreholes. Missing this loses 1 mark. > > 5. **Not justifying the choice of spacing:** Always explain *why* you chose a particular spacing (e.g., "chosen 10 m because soil is non-uniform"). > > 6. **Mixing up depth of exploration with spacing:** These are two different parameters. Depth is in the vertical direction; spacing is in the horizontal direction. Many students confuse the units. > > 7. **Ignoring critical zones:** Always add extra boreholes at corners, near slopes, or at transitions between soil types. The examiner looks for this. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** > > - [ ] **Number** = count of exploratory points; **Spacing** = horizontal distance between them. Never interchange. > - [ ] **IS 1892** is the governing code for spacing and number in India. > - [ ] **Uniform soil:** 15–30 m spacing; **Non-uniform soil:** 5–15 m spacing. > - [ ] **Terzaghi's Rule:** Minimum 1 borehole per 250 m² (uniform) or 100 m² (non-uniform). > - [ ] **Grid formula:** $N = \left( \lceil L/S \rceil + 1 \right) \left( \lceil B/S_B \rceil + 1 \right)$ > - [ ] **Minimum absolute number:** 3–4 boreholes for any project, regardless of size. > - [ ] **Earth dam spacing:** 50–100 m along dam axis. > - [ ] **Highway spacing:** 250–500 m along alignment. > - [ ] **Bridge spacing:** 1–2 per abutment, 1 per pier. > - [ ] **Depth of exploration (shallow):** 1.5B to 2B below GL. > - [ ] **Depth of exploration (deep/pile):** 5–10 m below pile tip or 3–5 pile diameters. > - [ ] **Patterns:** Grid, Diagonal, Triangular, Random — choose based on site geometry. > - [ ] **Always cross-check** the calculated number with both the grid formula and the area rule, and adopt the larger. > - [ ] **Add extra boreholes** at critical zones (corners, abutments, geological transitions). > - [ ] **Justify your choice** of spacing with a one-line reasoning statement in exams. ---

topics.title: Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bearing Capacity — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultimate Bearing Capacity ($q_u$)** of a shallow foundation is defined as the **maximum gross pressure** that the soil can support at the base of the foundation without undergoing **shear failure** and without imposing settlements intolerable to the structure.

Mathematically, the **Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity** is expressed as:

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f$$

where:
- $q_{nu}$ = Net ultimate bearing capacity (kN/m² or kPa)
- $q_u$ = Ultimate bearing capacity (gross)
- $\gamma$ = Unit weight of soil above the base of foundation
- $D_f$ = Depth of foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Allowable Bearing Capacity ($q_a$)** is obtained by dividing $q_u$ (or $q_{nu}$) by a **Factor of Safety (FOS)**, typically **3.0** for general shear failure conditions (as per IS 6403:1981, reaffirmed 2002).

$$q_a = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{FS} + \gamma D_f \quad \text{or} \quad q_a = \dfrac{q_u}{FS}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Toothpick in Cake" Intuition

Imagine pushing a **flat wooden disc** slowly down onto the surface of a **soft cake** versus a **firm cake**:

- On a **soft cake** (loose sand) → the disc sinks in easily, and the cake surface **bulges upward** around the edges gradually. This is **Local Shear Failure**.
- On a **firm cake** (dense sand/stiff clay) → the disc suddenly tilts, and the cake **cracks and heaves** sharply on one side, often accompanied by an audible sound. This is **General Shear Failure**.
- In a **medium-firm cake** (medium soil) → the failure is sudden, but the surface heave is less pronounced. This is **Punching Shear Failure**.

**In foundation engineering**, these three failure modes dictate which bearing capacity equation (with reduced or full shear strength parameters) should be used.

## 1.3 Modes of Shear Failure (Vesic's Classification)

| Failure Mode | Soil Type | Governing Parameter |
|--------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **General Shear** | Dense sand, stiff clay | Full $c$ and $\phi$ |
| **Local Shear** | Medium sand, medium clay | Modified $c' = \frac{2}{3}c$, $\phi' = \tan^{-1}(\frac{2}{3}\tan\phi)$ |
| **Punching Shear** | Loose sand, soft clay | Significantly reduced parameters |

## 1.4 Why Numerical Problems Dominate Module 2

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2 of PECET635 (Advanced Foundation Engineering) concentrates on **analytical computation of bearing capacity** using classical theories (Terzaghi, Meyerhof, Hansen, Vesic, IS Code). Since these are **computational design problems** that form 14-mark questions in End Semester Examinations (ESE), mastering numerical problems is **non-negotiable** for securing high module marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Bearing Capacity Theories

The classical bearing capacity equations can be categorized as:

1. **Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Theory (1943)** — Strip, square, circular foundations
2. **Meyerhof's Bearing Capacity Theory (1963)** — Shape, depth, inclination factors
3. **Hansen's Bearing Capacity Theory (1970)** — Comprehensive correction factors
4. **Vesic's Bearing Capacity Theory (1973)** — Modified Hansen's with adjusted factors
5. **IS Code Method (IS 6403:1981)** — Empirical Indian Standard approach
6. **Skempton's Method (1951)** — For purely cohesive soils ($\phi = 0$)

## 2.2 Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Equation

Terzaghi (1943) was the first to provide a comprehensive theoretical solution. For a **strip footing** under general shear failure:

$$q_u = c N_c + q N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$$

For **square and circular footings**, Terzaghi modified the equation as:

$$q_u = 1.3 c N_c + q N_q + 0.4 \gamma B N_\gamma \quad \text{(Square)}$$

$$q_u = 1.3 c N_c + q N_q + 0.3 \gamma B N_\gamma \quad \text{(Circular)}$$

**Where:**
- $c$ = Cohesion of soil (kN/m²)
- $q = \gamma D_f$ = Surcharge at foundation level (kN/m²)
- $\gamma$ = Unit weight of soil below the base of foundation (kN/m³)
- $B$ = Width (or diameter) of foundation (m)
- $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ = Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors (functions of $\phi$)

## 2.3 Meyerhof's Bearing Capacity Equation (General Form)

The most commonly used **general bearing capacity equation** is:

$$q_u = c N_c s_c d_c i_c + q N_q s_q d_q i_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma i_\gamma$$

**Where:**
- $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ = Meyerhof's bearing capacity factors
- $s_c, s_q, s_\gamma$ = Shape factors
- $d_c, d_q, d_\gamma$ = Depth factors
- $i_c, i_q, i_\gamma$ = Inclination factors (for inclined loads)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section — it contains every formula needed to solve Module 2 numerical problems.**

### Table 2.A — Bearing Capacity Factors

| Theory | $N_c$ | $N_q$ | $N_\gamma$ | Notes |
|--------|-------|-------|------------|-------|
| **Terzaghi** | $(N_q - 1)\cot\phi$ | $\dfrac{e^{(3\pi/4 - \phi/2)\tan\phi}}{2\cos^2(45^\circ + \phi/2)}$ | $\dfrac{(N_q - 1)\tan\phi}{2}$ (approx.) | Original 1943 theory |
| **Meyerhof** | $(N_q - 1)\cot\phi$ | $e^{\pi \tan\phi} \tan^2(45^\circ + \phi/2)$ | $(N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi)$ | Most widely used |
| **Hansen** | $(N_q - 1)\cot\phi$ | $e^{\pi \tan\phi} \tan^2(45^\circ + \phi/2)$ | $1.5(N_q - 1)\tan\phi$ | Comprehensive |
| **Vesic** | $(N_q - 1)\cot\phi$ | $e^{\pi \tan\phi} \tan^2(45^\circ + \phi/2)$ | $2(N_q + 1)\tan\phi$ | Modern practice |
| **IS 6403** | $5.7$ (for $\phi = 0$) | $1.0$ (for $\phi = 0$) | $0.0$ (for $\phi = 0$) | Empirical charts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Meyerhof's $N_\gamma$ formula** most commonly used in KTU exams: $N_\gamma = (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi)$

### Table 2.B — Shape Factors (Meyerhof/Hansen)

| Factor | Strip ($L/B = \infty$) | Square/Rectangular | Formula (Rectangular) |
|--------|----------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| $s_c$ | 1.0 | 1.3 (square) | $1 + \dfrac{N_q}{N_c} \cdot \dfrac{B}{L}$ |
| $s_q$ | 1.0 | 1.0 (square) | $1 + \dfrac{B}{L}\tan\phi$ |
| $s_\gamma$ | 1.0 | 0.8 (square) | $1 - 0.4 \dfrac{B}{L}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Use 1.3, 1.0, 0.8 for square footings; 1.0, 1.0, 1.0 for strip footings.**

### Table 2.C — Depth Factors (Meyerhof/Hansen)

For $\dfrac{D_f}{B} \le 1$:

$$d_c = 1 + 0.4 \dfrac{D_f}{B}$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2\tan\phi(1 - \sin\phi)^2 \cdot \dfrac{D_f}{B}$$

$$d_\gamma = 1.0$$

For $\dfrac{D_f}{B} > 1$:

$$d_c = 1 + 0.4 \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{D_f}{B}\right)$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2\tan\phi(1 - \sin\phi)^2 \cdot \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{D_f}{B}\right)$$

$$d_\gamma = 1.0$$

### Table 2.D — Skempton's Bearing Capacity Factors (Purely Cohesive Soils, $\phi = 0$)

| Condition | $N_c$ |
|-----------|-------|
| Strip footing, $D_f/B = 0$ | $5.14$ (Prandtl-Reissner exact) |
| Strip footing, $D_f/B \le 2.5$ | $5.14 \left(1 + 0.2 \dfrac{B}{L}\right)\left(1 + 0.2 \dfrac{D_f}{B}\right)$ capped at $6.2$ |
| Square footing | $6.2$ |
| Circular footing | $6.0$ |

## 2.5 Influence of Water Table — Critical Correction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests water table corrections. **Three scenarios** must be considered:

**Scenario 1:** Water table lies between base and a depth $B$ below base at distance $d_w$ from base.

- Substitute $\gamma' = \gamma_{sub} + \dfrac{d_w}{B}(\gamma - \gamma_{sub})$ in the third term
- Effective surcharge: $q' = (D_f - z_{w1})\gamma + z_{w1}\gamma'$

**Scenario 2:** Water table at base of foundation (i.e., $z_{w1} = 0$).

- Use $\gamma_{sub}$ in third term
- Surcharge $q' = (D_f - z_{w1})\gamma$

**Scenario 3:** Water table at ground surface ($z_{w1} = D_f$).

- Both surcharge and third term use $\gamma'$
- $q' = 0$ and $\gamma = \gamma_{sub}$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Critical Bearing Capacity Concern |
|-------------|-----------------------------------|
| **High-rise buildings** | Square/rectangular footings with deep basements ($D_f/B > 1$) |
| **Bridge piers** | Inclined loads due to wind/seismic action |
| **Retaining walls** | Eccentric loading from lateral earth pressure |
| **Offshore platforms** | Combined vertical, horizontal, and moment loading |
| **Towers & chimneys** | Small $B/L$ ratios, sensitivity to tilt |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Worked Numerical Problems)

## 3.1 PROBLEM 1 — Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity (Strip Footing, General Shear)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**
> A strip footing **1.5 m wide** is founded at a depth of **1.0 m** in a soil with unit weight $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$, cohesion $c = 25 \text{ kN/m}^2$, and angle of internal friction $\phi = 30^\circ$. Determine the **ultimate bearing capacity** using Terzaghi's theory assuming general shear failure.

### Given Data
- $B = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- $D_f = 1.0 \text{ m}$
- $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- $c = 25 \text{ kN/m}^2$
- $\phi = 30^\circ$

### Step 1: Look up Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Factors for $\phi = 30^\circ$

For $\phi = 30^\circ$ (Standard table values):

$$N_c = 30.14, \quad N_q = 18.40, \quad N_\gamma = 22.40$$

### Step 2: Calculate Surcharge ($q$)

$$q = \gamma D_f = 18 \times 1.0 = 18 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 3: Apply Terzaghi's Strip Footing Equation

$$q_u = c N_c + q N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$$

Substituting:

$$q_u = (25)(30.14) + (18)(18.40) + (0.5)(18)(1.5)(22.40)$$

Computing each term:

$$q_u = 753.5 + 331.2 + 302.4$$

$$\boxed{q_u = 1387.1 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

### Step 4: Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 1387.1 - 18 = 1369.1 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 5: Allowable Bearing Capacity (FS = 3)

$$q_a = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{FS} = \dfrac{1369.1}{3} = 456.37 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q_a = 456.37 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

---

## 3.2 PROBLEM 2 — Meyerhof's Method with Shape & Depth Factors (Square Footing)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> A square footing **2.5 m × 2.5 m** is placed at a depth of **1.8 m** in a sandy soil with $\phi = 35^\circ$, $c = 0$, and $\gamma = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$. Calculate the ultimate and safe bearing capacity using Meyerhof's theory. Take **FOS = 3**.

### Given Data
- $B = L = 2.5 \text{ m}$
- $D_f = 1.8 \text{ m}$
- $c = 0$, $\phi = 35^\circ$
- $\gamma = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- FS = 3

### Step 1: Calculate Meyerhof's Bearing Capacity Factors for $\phi = 35^\circ$

$$N_q = e^{\pi \tan 35^\circ} \cdot \tan^2(45^\circ + 35^\circ/2)$$

Computing $\tan 35^\circ = 0.7002$:

$$N_q = e^{3.1416 \times 0.7002} \cdot \tan^2(62.5^\circ)$$

$$N_q = e^{2.1995} \cdot (1.92098)^2 = 9.0183 \times 3.690 = 33.30$$

$$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi = (33.30 - 1)\cot 35^\circ = 32.30 \times 1.4281 = 46.13$$

$$N_\gamma = (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi) = (33.30 - 1)\tan(49^\circ) = 32.30 \times 1.1504 = 37.16$$

### Step 2: Calculate Shape Factors (Square: $B/L = 1$)

$$s_c = 1 + \dfrac{N_q}{N_c} \cdot \dfrac{B}{L} = 1 + \dfrac{33.30}{46.13} \cdot 1 = 1 + 0.7219 = 1.722$$

$$s_q = 1 + \dfrac{B}{L}\tan\phi = 1 + 1 \cdot 0.7002 = 1.700$$

$$s_\gamma = 1 - 0.4 \cdot \dfrac{B}{L} = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6$$

### Step 3: Calculate Depth Factors

For $D_f/B = 1.8/2.5 = 0.72 \le 1$:

$$d_c = 1 + 0.4 \times 0.72 = 1.288$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2\tan 35^\circ(1 - \sin 35^\circ)^2 \times 0.72$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2(0.7002)(1 - 0.5736)^2 \times 0.72$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2(0.7002)(0.4264)^2 \times 0.72$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2(0.7002)(0.1818) \times 0.72 = 1 + 0.1834 = 1.183$$

$$d_\gamma = 1.0$$

### Step 4: Substitute in Meyerhof's Equation

Since $c = 0$, first and second terms reduce:

$$q_u = q N_q s_q d_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma$$

Computing $q = \gamma D_f = 19 \times 1.8 = 34.2 \text{ kN/m}^2$:

**Term 2 (Surcharge term):**

$$q N_q s_q d_q = 34.2 \times 33.30 \times 1.700 \times 1.183$$

$$= 34.2 \times 33.30 \times 2.0111 = 34.2 \times 66.97 = 2290.4 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Term 3 (Self-weight term):**

$$0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma = 0.5 \times 19 \times 2.5 \times 37.16 \times 0.6 \times 1.0$$

$$= 0.5 \times 19 \times 2.5 \times 22.30 = 529.6 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 5: Final Ultimate Bearing Capacity

$$q_u = 0 + 2290.4 + 529.6 = 2820.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q_u = 2820.0 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

### Step 6: Safe Bearing Capacity

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 2820.0 - 34.2 = 2785.8 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$q_a = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{FS} + \gamma D_f = \dfrac{2785.8}{3} + 34.2 = 928.6 + 34.2$$

$$\boxed{q_a = 962.8 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

---

## 3.3 PROBLEM 3 — Water Table Correction (KTU's Favourite!)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
> A strip footing **2.0 m wide** rests at a depth of **1.5 m** in a soil with $\gamma = 17.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $\gamma_{sub} = 9.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $c = 30 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $\phi = 25^\circ$. The water table is located at a depth of **0.75 m** below the ground surface. Calculate the **net ultimate bearing capacity** using Terzaghi's theory.

### Given Data
- $B = 2.0 \text{ m}$, $D_f = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- $\gamma = 17.5$, $\gamma_{sub} = 9.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- $c = 30 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $\phi = 25^\circ$
- Water table at $z_{w1} = D_f - 0.75 = 0.75 \text{ m}$ below the base

### Step 1: Terzaghi's Factors for $\phi = 25^\circ$

$$N_c = 25.13, \quad N_q = 12.72, \quad N_\gamma = 9.01$$

### Step 2: Effective Surcharge ($q'$)

Water table is at 0.75 m below ground surface, i.e., 0.75 m above the base:

$$q' = (D_f - z_{w1})\gamma + z_{w1}\gamma' = (1.5 - 0.75)(17.5) + (0.75)(9.5)$$

$$q' = 0.75 \times 17.5 + 0.75 \times 9.5 = 13.125 + 7.125 = 20.25 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 3: Effective Unit Weight Below Base

Distance of water table below base: $d_w = 0.75 \text{ m}$

$$\bar{\gamma} = \gamma' + \dfrac{d_w}{B}(\gamma - \gamma_{sub}) = 9.5 + \dfrac{0.75}{2.0}(17.5 - 9.5)$$

$$\bar{\gamma} = 9.5 + 0.375 \times 8.0 = 9.5 + 3.0 = 12.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

### Step 4: Apply Modified Terzaghi's Equation

$$q_u = c N_c + q' N_q + 0.5 \bar{\gamma} B N_\gamma$$

$$q_u = (30)(25.13) + (20.25)(12.72) + (0.5)(12.5)(2.0)(9.01)$$

$$q_u = 753.9 + 257.58 + 112.625$$

$$\boxed{q_u = 1124.1 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

### Step 5: Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 1124.1 - (17.5)(1.5) = 1124.1 - 26.25$$

$$\boxed{q_{nu} = 1097.85 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

---

## 3.4 PROBLEM 4 — Skempton's Method for Clay (Purely Cohesive Soil)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
> A square footing **3.0 m × 3.0 m** is to be placed at **1.5 m depth** in a saturated clay with $c_u = 80 \text{ kN/m}^2$ and $\phi_u = 0$. Calculate the safe bearing capacity using Skempton's method. Take **FOS = 3**.

### Given Data
- $B = L = 3.0 \text{ m}$, $D_f = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- $c_u = 80 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $\phi_u = 0$
- Square footing

### Step 1: Skempton's $N_c$ for Square Footing

For a **square footing on purely cohesive soil**:

$$N_c = 6.2 \quad \text{(constant for } D_f/B \le 2.5 \text{)}$$

### Step 2: Ultimate Bearing Capacity

For $\phi = 0$, $N_q = 1.0$ and $N_\gamma = 0$:

$$q_u = 1.3 c_u N_c + \gamma D_f N_q = 1.3(80)(6.2) + (1.5)(18)(1.0)$$

$$q_u = 644.8 + 27.0 = 671.8 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 3: Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 671.8 - 27.0 = 644.8 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 4: Safe Bearing Capacity

$$q_a = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{FS} = \dfrac{644.8}{3} = 214.93 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q_a \approx 215 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

---

## 3.5 PROBLEM 5 — Eccentrically Loaded Foundation (Effective Width Method)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
> A rectangular footing **3.0 m × 2.0 m** carries a vertical load of **1200 kN** and a moment of **240 kN·m** about the axis parallel to the longer side. The depth of foundation is **1.5 m**. The soil has $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $c = 0$, $\phi = 30^\circ$. Determine the **factor of safety** against bearing failure using Meyerhof's effective width method.

### Given Data
- $B = 2.0 \text{ m}$ (width, perpendicular to eccentricity)
- $L = 3.0 \text{ m}$ (length, parallel to eccentricity direction)
- $P = 1200 \text{ kN}$, $M = 240 \text{ kN·m}$
- $D_f = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $\phi = 30^\circ$, $c = 0$

### Step 1: Calculate Eccentricity

$$e = \dfrac{M}{P} = \dfrac{240}{1200} = 0.2 \text{ m}$$

**Check:** $e \le B/6 = 2.0/6 = 0.333$ m ✓ (No tension, effective width concept valid)

### Step 2: Effective Width

$$B' = B - 2e = 2.0 - 2(0.2) = 1.6 \text{ m}$$

$$L' = L = 3.0 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3: Effective Area

$$A' = B' \times L' = 1.6 \times 3.0 = 4.8 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 4: Meyerhof's Factors for $\phi = 30^\circ$

$$N_q = 18.40, \quad N_\gamma = (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4 \times 30^\circ) = 17.40 \times \tan(42^\circ) = 17.40 \times 0.9004 = 15.67$$

### Step 5: Shape Factors (for $B'/L' = 1.6/3.0 = 0.533$)

$$s_q = 1 + \dfrac{B'}{L'}\tan\phi = 1 + 0.533 \times 0.5774 = 1.308$$

$$s_\gamma = 1 - 0.4 \cdot \dfrac{B'}{L'} = 1 - 0.4(0.533) = 0.787$$

### Step 6: Effective Stress at Base

$$q' = \gamma D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 7: Ultimate Bearing Capacity (using $B'$)

$$q_u = q' N_q s_q d_q + 0.5 \gamma B' N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma$$

Assuming $d_q = d_\gamma = 1$ (approximation):

$$q_u = (27)(18.40)(1.308)(1) + 0.5(18)(1.6)(15.67)(0.787)(1)$$

$$q_u = 649.5 + 177.6 = 827.1 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 8: Ultimate Load Capacity

$$Q_u = q_u \times A' = 827.1 \times 4.8 = 3970 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 9: Factor of Safety

$$FS = \dfrac{Q_u}{P} = \dfrac{3970}{1200} = 3.31$$

$$\boxed{FS \approx 3.31 \text{ (Safe against bearing failure)}}$$

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Failure Mechanism Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Foundation Load Applied] --> B[Stress Increases Below Base]
    B --> C{Soil Density?}
    C -->|Dense Sand / Stiff Clay| D[General Shear Failure]
    C -->|Medium Soil| E[Local Shear Failure]
    C -->|Loose Sand / Soft Clay| F[Punching Shear Failure]
    D --> D1[Sudden Heave on Sides]
    D --> D2[Distinct Failure Surface]
    D --> D3[Use Full c and phi]
    E --> E1[Gradual Compression]
    E --> E2[Partial Failure Surface]
    E --> E3[Use Modified c' and phi']
    F --> F1[Foundation Punches Down]
    F --> F2[No Visible Heave]
    F --> F3[Use Significantly Reduced Parameters]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Bearing Capacity Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Input: B, Df, c, phi, gamma, FS, Water Table] --> CF[Compute Bearing Capacity Factors Nc, Nq, Ngamma]
    CF --> SF[Apply Shape Factors sc, sq, s_gamma]
    SF --> DF[Apply Depth Factors dc, dq, d_gamma]
    DF --> IF[Apply Inclination Factors ic, iq, i_gamma]
    IF --> WT[Apply Water Table Corrections]
    WT --> AGG[Aggregate All Three Terms]
    AGG --> QU[Compute Ultimate Bearing Capacity qu]
    QU --> QNU[Compute Net Ultimate qnu = qu - gamma Df]
    QNU --> QA[Compute Safe Bearing Capacity qa = qnu / FS]
    QA --> OUTPUT[Output Design Recommendation]
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix for Water Table Correction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WT[Water Table Position?] --> Z1{Position 1: Below Base at depth dw}
    WT --> Z2{Position 2: At Base Level}
    WT --> Z3{Position 3: Above Base within Df}
    WT --> Z4{Position 4: At Ground Surface}
    Z1 --> C1[Use gammabar in 3rd term and q in 2nd term]
    Z2 --> C2[Use gamma_sub in 3rd term, q from above]
    Z3 --> C3[Use effective q and gamma_sub in 3rd term]
    Z4 --> C4[Use gamma_sub throughout, q=0]
```

## 4.4 Eccentric Loading — Effective Width Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UN[Original Footing B x L]
        UA[Total Area A = B * L]
    end
    UN --> ECC[Eccentricity e = M / P]
    ECC --> CK{e <= B/6 ?}
    CK -->|Yes| EW[Effective Width B prime = B - 2e]
    CK -->|No| ERR[Unacceptable - Redesign Footing]
    EW --> EA[Effective Area A prime = B prime x L]
    EA --> AP[Apply Meyerhof with B prime and A prime]
    AP --> RES[Result: Reduced Ultimate Capacity]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the terms: (i) Net ultimate bearing capacity, (ii) Safe bearing capacity, and (iii) Factor of safety. How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**

> (i) **Net ultimate bearing capacity ($q_{nu}$)** is the maximum net pressure (i.e., pressure in excess of overburden pressure $\gamma D_f$) that the soil can sustain without shear failure.
>
> $$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f$$
>
> (ii) **Safe/Allowable bearing capacity ($q_a$)** is the maximum pressure that the soil can carry safely without risk of shear failure and with settlements within tolerable limits.
>
> $$q_a = \dfrac{q_u}{F} \quad \text{or} \quad q_a = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{F} + \gamma D_f$$
>
> (iii) **Factor of safety (FS)** is the ratio of ultimate load to the working/safe load on the foundation.
>
> $$FS = \dfrac{q_u}{q_a}$$
>
> Typical values of FS: **2.5 to 3.0** for Terzaghi/Meyerhof, **3.0** for IS Code.

---

### Question A2: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is the significance of bearing capacity factors $N_c$, $N_q$, and $N_\gamma$? State how they are related to the angle of internal friction $\phi$.**

**Model Answer:**

> The bearing capacity factors $N_c$, $N_q$, and $N_\gamma$ are **dimensionless coefficients** that quantify the contribution of soil cohesion, surcharge, and self-weight respectively to the ultimate bearing capacity.
>
> - $N_c$ → Contribution of **cohesion** ($c N_c$)
> - $N_q$ → Contribution of **surcharge** ($q N_q$)
> - $N_\gamma$ → Contribution of **soil self-weight** ($0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$)
>
> They are **functions of $\phi$ only** (for a given theory). General relationship:
>
> $$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$$
>
> As $\phi$ increases from $0°$ to $45°$, $N_c$, $N_q$, $N_\gamma$ all increase **non-linearly** (exponentially for $N_q$). For purely cohesive soil ($\phi = 0$), $N_q = 1$ and $N_\gamma = 0$.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### 🔷 Question Choice A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2]**
> A square footing **3.0 m × 3.0 m** is to be constructed at a depth of **2.0 m** in a soil with the following properties: $\gamma = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $c = 15 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $\phi = 25°$. The water table is at a depth of **1.0 m** below the base of the foundation. Compute:
>
> **(a)** The **net ultimate bearing capacity** using Terzaghi's method. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The **safe bearing capacity** and **net safe bearing capacity** with FOS = 3. Also check the suitability of the footing for a column load of **1500 kN**. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a): Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity

**Given:**
- $B = L = 3.0$ m, $D_f = 2.0$ m
- $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³, $c = 15$ kN/m², $\phi = 25°$
- Water table is 1.0 m **below** the base of foundation
- $\gamma_{sub} = 19 - 9.81 = 9.19 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (assuming standard)

**Step 1: Terzaghi's factors for $\phi = 25°$**

$$N_c = 25.13, \quad N_q = 12.72, \quad N_\gamma = 9.01$$

**Step 2: Surcharge ($q$)**

Since water table is below the base, the surcharge at the base is not affected by it:

$$q = \gamma D_f = 19 \times 2.0 = 38 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Effective unit weight below base**

Water table is at $d_w = 1.0$ m below the base (and $B = 3.0$ m), so:

$$\bar{\gamma} = \gamma' + \dfrac{d_w}{B}(\gamma - \gamma_{sub}) = 9.19 + \dfrac{1.0}{3.0}(19 - 9.19)$$

$$\bar{\gamma} = 9.19 + 0.333 \times 9.81 = 9.19 + 3.27 = 12.46 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**Step 4: Apply Terzaghi's equation for square footing**

$$q_u = 1.3 c N_c + q N_q + 0.4 \bar{\gamma} B N_\gamma$$

$$q_u = 1.3 \times 15 \times 25.13 + 38 \times 12.72 + 0.4 \times 12.46 \times 3.0 \times 9.01$$

$$q_u = 490.04 + 483.36 + 134.71 = 1108.11 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5: Net ultimate bearing capacity**

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 1108.11 - 38 = 1070.11 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q_{nu} = 1070.11 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

#### Solution to Part (b): Safe Bearing Capacity & Footing Suitability

**Step 1: Safe bearing capacity**

$$q_{ns} = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{FS} = \dfrac{1070.11}{3} = 356.7 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Net safe bearing capacity (including overburden)**

$$q_{sa} = q_{ns} + \gamma D_f = 356.7 + 38 = 394.7 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Actual pressure under footing for 1500 kN load**

$$q_{actual} = \dfrac{P}{A} + \gamma D_f = \dfrac{1500}{3 \times 3} + 38 = 166.67 + 38 = 204.67 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Check safety**

$$FS_{actual} = \dfrac{q_u \times A}{P} = \dfrac{1108.11 \times 9}{1500} = 6.65$$

Since $q_{actual} = 204.67 < q_{sa} = 394.7 \text{ kN/m}^2$, the **footing is SAFE** and the design is conservative.

$$\boxed{\text{Foundation is safe; } FS_{actual} = 6.65 > 3 \text{ (acceptable)}}$$

---

### 🔷 Question Choice B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 1]**
> A **rectangular footing 4.0 m × 2.5 m** is founded at a depth of **2.0 m** in a c-$\phi$ soil with $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, $c = 25$ kN/m², $\phi = 30°$. The footing is subjected to a **vertical load of 1800 kN** and a **moment of 200 kN·m** about the axis parallel to the 4.0 m side.
>
> **(a)** Compute the **ultimate bearing capacity** using Meyerhof's theory, accounting for the eccentricity. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Determine the **factor of safety** against bearing failure. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a): Ultimate Bearing Capacity

**Given:**
- $B = 2.5$ m (perpendicular to eccentricity direction)
- $L = 4.0$ m (parallel to eccentricity direction)
- $D_f = 2.0$ m, $P = 1800$ kN, $M = 200$ kN·m
- $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, $c = 25$ kN/m², $\phi = 30°$

**Step 1: Compute eccentricity**

$$e = \dfrac{M}{P} = \dfrac{200}{1800} = 0.111 \text{ m}$$

**Check:** $e = 0.111 < B/6 = 0.417$ m ✓

**Step 2: Effective dimensions**

$$B' = B - 2e = 2.5 - 2(0.111) = 2.5 - 0.222 = 2.278 \text{ m}$$

$$L' = L = 4.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Meyerhof's bearing capacity factors for $\phi = 30°$**

$$N_q = 18.40$$
$$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot 30° = 17.40 \times 1.7321 = 30.14$$
$$N_\gamma = (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4 \times 30°) = 17.40 \times \tan(42°) = 17.40 \times 0.9004 = 15.67$$

**Step 4: Shape factors for $B'/L' = 2.278/4.0 = 0.5695$**

$$s_c = 1 + \dfrac{N_q}{N_c} \cdot \dfrac{B'}{L'} = 1 + \dfrac{18.40}{30.14} \times 0.5695 = 1 + 0.6104 \times 0.5695 = 1.348$$

$$s_q = 1 + \dfrac{B'}{L'}\tan\phi = 1 + 0.5695 \times 0.5774 = 1.329$$

$$s_\gamma = 1 - 0.4 \dfrac{B'}{L'} = 1 - 0.4(0.5695) = 1 - 0.228 = 0.772$$

**Step 5: Depth factors for $D_f/B = 2.0/2.5 = 0.8$**

$$d_c = 1 + 0.4(0.8) = 1.32$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2\tan 30°(1 - \sin 30°)^2 \times 0.8$$
$$= 1 + 2(0.5774)(0.25)(0.8) = 1 + 0.231 = 1.231$$

$$d_\gamma = 1.0$$

**Step 6: Surcharge**

$$q = \gamma D_f = 18 \times 2.0 = 36 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 7: Compute three terms of Meyerhof's equation**

**Term 1 (Cohesion term):**

$$c N_c s_c d_c = 25 \times 30.14 \times 1.348 \times 1.32 = 1341.7 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Term 2 (Surcharge term):**

$$q N_q s_q d_q = 36 \times 18.40 \times 1.329 \times 1.231 = 1082.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Term 3 (Self-weight term):**

$$0.5 \gamma B' N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma = 0.5 \times 18 \times 2.278 \times 15.67 \times 0.772 \times 1.0$$
$$= 0.5 \times 18 \times 2.278 \times 12.097 = 248.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 8: Ultimate Bearing Capacity**

$$q_u = 1341.7 + 1082.0 + 248.0 = 2671.7 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q_u = 2671.7 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

#### Solution to Part (b): Factor of Safety

**Step 1: Ultimate load capacity**

$$Q_u = q_u \times A' = 2671.7 \times (2.278 \times 4.0) = 2671.7 \times 9.112 = 24345 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Factor of Safety**

$$FS = \dfrac{Q_u}{P} = \dfrac{24345}{1800} = 13.53$$

$$\boxed{FS = 13.53 \text{ (extremely safe — footing may be oversized)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Most common mistake (2-3 marks lost):** Forgetting to apply **all three corrections** — shape ($s$), depth ($d$), and effective width ($B'$) — simultaneously. Students often use $B$ instead of $B'$ in the self-weight term.
> 2. **Second pitfall (1-2 marks lost):** Using **Meyerhof's $N_\gamma$ formula incorrectly**. Two versions exist: $N_\gamma = (N_q-1)\tan(1.4\phi)$ (Meyerhof) vs $N_\gamma = 1.5(N_q-1)\tan\phi$ (Hansen). **Use Meyerhof's form** unless the problem specifically mentions Hansen.
> 3. **Third pitfall:** Not verifying the eccentricity check $e \le B/6$. If $e > B/6$, the entire effective width concept fails and a different analysis is needed.
> 4. **Marks lost in unit errors:** Always keep **kN/m² (kPa)** consistent throughout. When multiplying $c \times N_c$, the result is in kN/m².
