> 5. **Common error in water table problems:** Using $\gamma$ instead of $\gamma'$ (submerged unit weight) in the surcharge term when the water table is at or near the base.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 Numerical Problems**

- **Bearing Capacity Factor Formulas (must memorize):**
  - $N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \tan^2(45° + \phi/2)$
  - $N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$
  - $N_\gamma = (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi)$ [Meyerhof]
  - $N_\gamma = 1.5(N_q - 1)\tan\phi$ [Hansen]
  - $N_\gamma = 2(N_q + 1)\tan\phi$ [Vesic]

- **For $\phi = 0$ (purely cohesive):** $N_q = 1$, $N_\gamma = 0$, $N_c = 5.14$ (strip) or $6.2$ (square) or $6.0$ (circular)

- **Terzaghi's equation is the simplest** — only $c$, $q$, $\gamma B$ terms multiplied by factors. **No shape/depth corrections** built in (you have to multiply by 1.3, 1.0, 0.4 manually for square footings).

- **Meyerhof's equation is the most general** — uses $s_c, s_q, s_\gamma$ (shape) and $d_c, d_q, d_\gamma$ (depth) factors.

- **Water table corrections:** Three scenarios — water table at base, above base, or below base. Always use $\gamma'$ (submerged) below water table.

- **Eccentric loading:** Use **effective width $B' = B - 2e$** and **effective area $A' = B' \times L'$**.

- **Inclined loading:** Use **inclination factors** $i_c, i_q, i_\gamma < 1$.

- **Factor of Safety (typical):**
  - **3.0** for general shear failure (Terzaghi)
  - **2.5 to 3.0** for local shear failure
  - **3.0 to 4.0** for punching shear failure

- **Standard bearing capacity factor values to remember:**

  | $\phi$ | $N_c$ | $N_q$ | $N_\gamma$ (Meyerhof) |
  |--------|-------|-------|------------------------|
  | 0°     | 5.14  | 1.0   | 0.0                    |
  | 10°    | 8.35  | 2.47  | 1.10                   |
  | 20°    | 14.83 | 6.40  | 5.27                   |
  | 25°    | 25.13 | 12.72 | 9.01                   |
  | 30°    | 30.14 | 18.40 | 15.67                  |
  | 35°    | 46.13 | 33.30 | 37.16                  |
  | 40°    | 75.31 | 64.20 | 78.40                  |
  | 45°    | 134.86| 134.86| 196.0                  |

- **Numerical problem strategy:**
  1. Identify soil type (c-$\phi$ or purely cohesive)
  2. Select appropriate theory (Terzaghi for simple, Meyerhof for general)
  3. Look up or compute bearing capacity factors
  4. Apply shape and depth corrections (for Meyerhof)
  5. Apply water table correction (if applicable)
  6. Compute $q_u$, $q_{nu}$, $q_a$

- **Final answer units:** Always express in **kN/m²** (or kPa). Use **3 significant figures** unless otherwise specified.

> **End of Module 2 — Numerical Problems Notes**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Settlement of Pile Groups in Clay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Settlement of Pile Groups in Clay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Settlement of Pile Groups in Clay** refers to the vertical downward displacement experienced by a group of piles acting as a composite foundation system when subjected to structural loads, particularly when the piles are embedded in cohesive (clay) soil strata. Unlike the settlement of a single pile, the settlement of a pile group is governed by the **interactive stress overlap** between adjacent piles, the **equivalent block behavior** of the soil-pile composite, and the **consolidation characteristics** of the surrounding clay matrix.

Mathematically, the **group settlement (S_g)** is expressed as:

$$S_g = S_1 \cdot R_s$$

where $S_1$ is the settlement of a single pile under the same average working load, and $R_s$ is the **Settlement Ratio** (also called the Group Settlement Ratio, GSR).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The estimation of pile group settlement in cohesive soils is a high-weightage topic. The two primary KTU-mandated approaches are:
> 1. **Equivalent Raft (Equivalent Pier) Method** – Treats the pile group as a large equivalent footing at a depth of (2/3)L.
> 2. **Interaction Factor Method (Poulos & Davis)** – Based on elastic theory and Mindlin's solution.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Bundled Chopsticks"**
> Imagine trying to push a single chopstick into a block of soft butter — it slides in with some resistance. Now, take **nine chopsticks bundled tightly together** (a 3×3 arrangement) and try pushing them in as a single unit. You will find that the bundle behaves like one fat "super-chopstick" and sinks **much more deeply** than a single chopstick would under the same total force.
>
> **Why?** Because:
> - The stresses from each chopstick **overlap** in the butter (clay), softening a wider zone.
> - The butter gets **squeezed out** from under the entire group, not just under each individual chopstick.
> - The group settles more because the failure zone is **larger and deeper**.
>
> In engineering terms, this "bundled behavior" is the essence of **pile group settlement** — the collective zone of influence expands, and the interaction between neighboring piles amplifies the displacement.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Pile Spacing (s):** Typically **3D** to **6D** (where D = pile diameter) for clay soils.
- **Group Efficiency ($\eta$):** Ratio of capacity of group to sum of individual capacities, usually less than 1 for clays.
- **Settlement Ratio (R_s):** Typically ranges from **1.5 to 8.0** for clayey soils depending on spacing.
- **Modulus of Elasticity of Pile (E_p):** For concrete piles: $E_p = 2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa; Steel: $2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa.
- **Poisson's Ratio of Clay ($\mu_s$):** Generally **0.4 to 0.5** (undrained condition $\mu_s = 0.5$).
- **Subgrade Reaction (k_s):** For soft clays: $5$ to $15$ MN/m³; for stiff clays: $15$ to $50$ MN/m³.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Pile Group Settlement Exceeds Single Pile Settlement

The phenomenon arises due to three primary mechanisms:

- **Stress Superposition (Zone of Influence Overlap):** When two piles are placed close together, the **bulb of pressure** beneath one pile overlaps with that of its neighbor. The net stress increase in the soil at any depth is the **sum of contributions** from all piles in the group. This amplified stress causes greater consolidation and elastic compression in the clay.

- **Equivalent Pier Behavior:** The pile cap, the piles, and the enclosed soil mass act as a single equivalent foundation (a "**block foundation**"). The settlement of this block is governed by the **average stress** at its base, which is dispersed over a wider area.

- **Consolidation of the Surrounding Clay:** In clays, the dominant long-term settlement component is **consolidation settlement** due to dissipation of excess pore water pressure. The consolidation process in a pile group is **3D (radial and vertical)** and occurs at a much larger scale than for a single pile.

### 2.2 Methods of Estimating Group Settlement in Clay

#### Method 1: Equivalent Raft (Equivalent Pier) Method

The pile group is idealized as an **equivalent rectangular footing (raft)** located at a depth of **(2/3)L** from the ground surface, where L is the length of the piles.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Compute the equivalent raft dimensions:
   - $B_g$ = width of the pile cap (in the direction of interest).
   - $L_g$ = length of the pile cap.
2. The equivalent raft is placed at depth $z = (2/3)L$ below the ground surface.
3. The total load on the equivalent raft is the **structural load minus the weight of the soil displaced by the piles and the pile cap** (effective stress principle).
4. Calculate the net stress increase at the base of the equivalent raft.
5. Compute the consolidation settlement of the clay layer beneath the equivalent raft using **1D consolidation theory** (Terzaghi's method) or **Skempton-Bjerrum method** for clay.

#### Method 2: Interaction Factor Method (Poulos, 1968)

This method uses the principle of **superposition of elastic displacements** using Mindlin's solution. The displacement of any pile in a group is the sum of:
- Its own displacement (single pile under load).
- Displacements induced by loads on all other piles, scaled by **interaction factors (α)**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Application / Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Settlement Ratio** | $R_s = S_g / S_1$ | Ratio of group to single pile settlement |
| 2 | **Group Efficiency** | $\eta = Q_{g(ult)} / (n \cdot Q_{u(single)})$ | < 1.0 typically for clays |
| 3 | **Converse-Labarre Equation** | $\eta = 1 - \theta \cdot \frac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{90 \cdot m \cdot n}$ | $\theta = \arctan(D/s)$ in degrees |
| 4 | **Equivalent Raft Depth** | $z_{eq} = (2/3)L$ | Below ground surface |
| 5 | **Net Stress at Raft Base** | $\Delta\sigma = (Q_g - W_g) / (B_g \cdot L_g)$ | Net applied pressure |
| 6 | **2:1 Stress Dispersion** | $\Delta\sigma_z = \frac{Q_g}{(B_g + z)(L_g + z)}$ | At depth z below raft |
| 7 | **Consolidation Settlement** | $S_c = \mu \cdot m_v \cdot \sum \Delta\sigma \cdot H$ | $\mu$ = correction factor (Skempton-Bjerrum) |
| 8 | **Poulos Interaction Factor** | $\alpha_{ij} = \frac{\text{settlement of pile i due to load on pile j}}{\text{settlement of pile i due to its own load}}$ | Elastic theory based |
| 9 | **Poulos Group Settlement** | $S_g = S_1 \cdot \frac{Q_g}{n \cdot Q_1} \cdot R_s^{calc}$ | Direct elastic calculation |
| 10 | **Skempton-Bjerrum μ Factor** | $\mu = A + \alpha \cdot (1-A)$ | $A$ = pore pressure coefficient |
| 11 | **Block Bearing Capacity** | $q_{ult} = c \cdot N_c \cdot (1 + 0.2 \cdot B_g/L_g)$ | For pile block in clay |
| 12 | **Stiffness Factor (K)** | $K = \frac{E_p \cdot R_A}{E_s}$ | $R_A$ = area ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Settlement prediction of pile groups is critical for the design of bridge piers, high-rise buildings, offshore platforms, and industrial structures where differential settlement between pile groups governs serviceability limit states (SLS) as per IS 2911 and KTU Board examination standards.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge Engineering:** Pile group settlements determine the approach gradients and bearing transitions.
- **Tall Buildings:** Differential settlement between core and peripheral pile groups must be limited to **L/500** to **L/1000** for safety.
- **Offshore Structures:** Pile group settlement in marine clay dictates the operational tolerance of drilling platforms.
- **Railway Viaducts:** Excessive group settlement can misalign rails, causing operational hazards.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Converse-Labarre Equation (Group Efficiency)

The **Converse-Labarre equation** is an empirical formula for group efficiency applicable to square pile groups in clay. It is derived from the assumption that each pile in the group has a zone of influence that overlaps with its neighbors.

**Starting Assumption:** For a pile of diameter D spaced at center-to-center distance s, the angle of stress dispersion with the horizontal is:

$$\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{D}{s}\right) \text{ (in degrees)}$$

**Step 1:** Consider a rectangular group of $m$ rows and $n$ columns.
- Total number of piles: $N = m \times n$.

**Step 2:** The total overlap of stress zones within the group is proportional to:
- For a single pile, the "wasted" zone within the group is proportional to $\theta$.
- The number of pile-pile interactions in the longitudinal direction (m piles, n columns) gives $(n-1) \cdot m$ horizontal interactions.
- The number of pile-pile interactions in the transverse direction (n piles, m rows) gives $(m-1) \cdot n$ vertical interactions.
- Total pairwise interactions: $m(n-1) + n(m-1) = 2mn - m - n$.

**Step 3:** Each interaction contributes a fractional reduction in pile capacity proportional to $\theta/90°$ (since the angle is normalized over 90° for a quarter-space).

**Step 4:** The group efficiency is therefore:

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{\theta}{90} \cdot \frac{m(n-1) + n(m-1)}{m \cdot n}$$

$$\boxed{\eta = 1 - \frac{\theta}{90} \cdot \frac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{mn}}$$

**Numerical Example Verification:** For a 3×3 group with s = 3D:
- $\theta = \arctan(D/3D) = \arctan(1/3) = 18.43°$
- $m = n = 3$
- $\eta = 1 - (18.43/90) \cdot [(3-1)(3) + (3-1)(3)] / 9$
- $\eta = 1 - 0.2048 \cdot [12/9] = 1 - 0.273 = 0.727$

This matches KTU reference values and IS 2911 tabulated data for 3×3 groups at 3D spacing.

### 3.2 Equivalent Raft Method — Full Settlement Derivation

Given a pile group of $m \times n$ piles, each of diameter D, length L, pile cap dimensions $B_c \times L_c$:

**Step 1: Equivalent Raft Dimensions**
The equivalent raft is considered to extend from edge to edge of the outermost piles:

$$B_g = B_c \text{ (pile cap width, including the outermost pile centers)}$$

$$L_g = L_c \text{ (pile cap length)}$$

**Step 2: Equivalent Raft Depth**
Following the standard KTU convention:

$$D_f = \frac{2}{3} \cdot L$$

**Step 3: Net Foundation Pressure at the Raft Base**

$$\Delta\sigma_{net} = \frac{Q_g - W_s}{B_g \cdot L_g}$$

where:
- $Q_g$ = total structural load on pile group (kN)
- $W_s$ = effective weight of soil + piles displaced within the equivalent raft volume

**Step 4: Stress Increase at Depth z Below the Raft Base (2V:1H Method)**

$$\Delta\sigma_z = \frac{Q_g - W_s}{(B_g + z)(L_g + z)}$$

**Step 5: Consolidation Settlement in the Clay Layer**

For a clay layer of thickness H beneath the equivalent raft at depth z, the consolidation settlement is:

$$S_c = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu \cdot m_{v,i} \cdot \Delta\sigma_{z,i} \cdot H_i$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem):**

> **Problem:** A 3×3 pile group supports a load of 3000 kN. Each pile is 0.5 m diameter, 15 m long, spaced at 1.5 m (3D) center-to-center. The pile cap is 4.5 m × 4.5 m. The soil is soft clay with $m_v = 0.0003$ m²/kN, thickness 20 m, with water table at the surface. Compute the consolidation settlement.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Equivalent raft dimensions: $B_g = 4.5$ m, $L_g = 4.5$ m
**Step 2:** Equivalent raft depth: $D_f = (2/3)(15) = 10$ m below ground surface
**Step 3:** Weight of soil in equivalent raft: 
$W_s = (4.5)(4.5)(10)(20 - 9.81) \approx 2070$ kN (using $\gamma_{sub} = 10.19$ kN/m³)
**Step 4:** Net pressure: $\Delta\sigma_{net} = (3000 - 2070)/(4.5 \times 4.5) = 930/20.25 = 45.93$ kPa
**Step 5:** Using Skempton-Bjerrum with $\mu = 0.5$ (typical for soft clay, $A = 0.8$):
$S_c = (0.5)(0.0003)(45.93)(20) = 0.1378$ m $\approx$ **138 mm**

### 3.3 Poulos Interaction Factor Approach — Settlement Derivation

**Elastic Theory Foundation:** Based on **Mindlin's Solution (1936)** for displacement due to a point load inside a semi-infinite elastic medium.

For two identical piles (i and j) of length L, diameter D, in a homogeneous elastic soil of modulus $E_s$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu_s$, the **interaction factor** is:

$$\alpha_{ij} = \frac{\text{Additional settlement of pile i due to unit load on pile j}}{\text{Settlement of pile i due to its own unit load}}$$

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1:** Settlement of a single pile under load P (from elastic theory):
$$S_1 = \frac{P}{E_s \cdot L} \cdot I_\rho$$

where $I_\rho$ is the **settlement influence factor** (dimensionless), function of $L/D$, $K = E_p/E_s$, and $\nu_s$.

**Step 2:** The additional settlement of pile i due to a load $P_j$ on pile j is:
$$\Delta S_{ij} = \frac{P_j}{E_s \cdot L} \cdot \alpha_{ij} \cdot I_\rho$$

**Step 3:** By superposition, the total settlement of pile i in the group is:
$$S_i = S_1 \cdot \left[\frac{P_i}{P_1} + \sum_{j \neq i} \alpha_{ij} \cdot \frac{P_j}{P_1}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Assuming all piles carry equal load $P = Q_g/n$ (rigid cap condition):
$$S_g = S_1 \cdot \frac{Q_g/n}{P_1} \cdot \left[1 + \sum_{j \neq i} \alpha_{ij}\right]$$

For an average pile in a square $n \times n$ group, the sum of interaction factors is denoted as $\sum \alpha$, leading to:

$$\boxed{S_g = S_1 \cdot \frac{Q_g}{n \cdot P_1} \cdot R_s}$$

where the **Group Settlement Ratio** is:
$$R_s = 1 + \sum_{j \neq i} \alpha_{ij}$$

**Tabulated Interaction Factors (Poulos, 1968) for $K = 1000$, $\nu_s = 0.5$, L/D = 25:**

| Spacing (s/D) | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **α (head-to-head)** | 0.50 | 0.20 | 0.10 | 0.05 | 0.03 |

These values are typically tabulated in KTU reference materials and used directly in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pile Group Settlement Analysis Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Pile Group Geometry and Soil Properties] --> B{Choose Method}
    B --> C1[Equivalent Raft Method]
    B --> C2[Interaction Factor Method]
    B --> C3[Block Failure Method]
    
    C1 --> D1[Compute Equivalent Raft Dimensions Bg x Lg]
    D1 --> D2[Set Raft Depth at 2L/3]
    D2 --> D3[Calculate Net Stress at Raft Base]
    D3 --> D4[Compute Stress Increase at Depth z: 2V:1H Method]
    D4 --> E1[Calculate Consolidation Settlement Sc]
    E1 --> F1[Settlement Sc using Terzaghi / Skempton Bjerrum]
    
    C2 --> D5[Compute Single Pile Settlement S1]
    D5 --> D6[Determine Interaction Factors from Poulos Chart]
    D6 --> D7[Sum Interaction Factors: Sum alpha]
    D7 --> E2[Compute Group Settlement Ratio Rs]
    E2 --> F2[Calculate Sg using Rs and S1]
    
    C3 --> D8[Compute Block Bearing Capacity]
    D8 --> E3[Compare with Group Load]
    E3 --> F3[Pass or Fail Design]
    
    F1 --> G[Final Output: Total Settlement of Pile Group]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    
    G --> H{Compare with Allowable Settlement}
    H -->|Yes| I[Design is SAFE]
    H -->|No| J[REDESIGN: Increase Pile Length or Number]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Stress Distribution Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pile 1] -->|Stress Bulb| S1[Zone of Influence 1]
    P2[Pile 2] -->|Stress Bulb| S2[Zone of Influence 2]
    P3[Pile 3] -->|Stress Bulb| S3[Zone of Influence 3]
    
    S1 --> O[Overlap Region in Clay]
    S2 --> O
    S3 --> O
    
    O --> AMP[Amplified Stress in Clay]
    AMP --> CONS[Consolidation Settlement]
    CONS --> SG[Group Settlement Sg]
    
    SG -->|Compare| ALLOW[Allowable Settlement per IS 2911]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Equivalent Raft Idealization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Actual Pile Group: 3x3 Piles, Length L, Diameter D] --> B[Soil Enclosed by Piles + Cap + Soil]
    B --> C[Equivalent Rectangular Raft]
    C --> D1[Width Bg = Pile Cap Width]
    C --> D2[Length Lg = Pile Cap Length]
    C --> D3[Depth = 2L/3 from Ground Surface]
    
    D1 --> E[Apply Net Foundation Load: Qg - Ws]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[Stress Dispersion 2V:1H into Clay]
    F --> G[Consolidation Settlement Sc]
```

### 4.4 Physical Layout (Schematic ASCII Representation)

```
          ┌─────────────────────┐
          │   PILE CAP (RIGID)  │
          │   ◯ ◯ ◯             │
          │   ◯ ◯ ◯   ← 3×3    │
          │   ◯ ◯ ◯   GROUP     │
          │   ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓   │
          │   │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │   │
          │   │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │   │
          │   │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │   │
          │   │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │   │
          │   │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │   │
          │   │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │   │
          └────┬┬┬┬┬┬┬┬┬────────┘
               │││││││││
       ┌───────┘│││││││└────────┐
       │  D_f = (2/3)L          │  ← Equivalent Raft Level
       │ ┌────────────────────┐ │
       │ │  EQUIVALENT RAFT   │ │
       │ │  Bg × Lg           │ │
       │ │  (4.5m × 4.5m)     │ │
       │ └────────┬───────────┘ │
       │          │ Δσ_net       │
       │          ▼             │
       │    CLAY LAYER (H = 20m)│
       │    → Consolidation     │
       └─────────────────────────┘
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Settlement Ratio" of a pile group. What is its typical range for pile groups in clay?*

**Model Answer:**
The **Settlement Ratio (Rs)** is defined as the ratio of the settlement of a pile group (Sg) to the settlement of a single pile (S1) under the same average working load per pile:
$$R_s = S_g / S_1$$
For pile groups in clay, $R_s$ typically ranges from **1.5 to 8.0**, depending on pile spacing, number of piles, and pile geometry. **[3 Marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the "Equivalent Raft Method" for estimating pile group settlement. What depth is the equivalent raft assumed to be located?*

**Model Answer:**
The **Equivalent Raft Method** idealizes the pile group as a large rectangular footing (raft) whose dimensions equal the pile cap dimensions (including the outermost piles). The settlement of this equivalent raft is computed as a shallow foundation settlement.
The equivalent raft is assumed to be located at a depth of **$D_f = (2/3)L$** below the ground surface, where L is the pile length. **[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
A group of **9 piles** arranged in a **3×3 square pattern** supports a structural load of **3000 kN**. Each pile is **0.5 m diameter** and **15 m long**, with a **pile spacing of 1.5 m (3D) c/c**. The pile cap measures **4.5 m × 4.5 m**. The subsoil is soft clay extending to a great depth, with $m_v = 0.0003$ m²/kN, $\gamma_{sub} = 10$ kN/m³, and Skempton-Bjerrum correction factor $\mu = 0.5$. Water table is at ground surface.

**(a)** Compute the **group efficiency** using the Converse-Labarre equation. **[7 Marks]**
**(b)** Estimate the **consolidation settlement** of the pile group using the Equivalent Raft Method. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
- Spacing s = 1.5 m, Diameter D = 0.5 m
- $\theta = \arctan(D/s) = \arctan(0.5/1.5) = \arctan(1/3) = 18.43°$
- m = n = 3
- Converse-Labarre formula:
$$\eta = 1 - \frac{\theta}{90} \cdot \frac{(n-1)m + (m-1)n}{mn}$$
$$\eta = 1 - \frac{18.43}{90} \cdot \frac{(2)(3) + (2)(3)}{9} = 1 - 0.2048 \cdot \frac{12}{9}$$
$$\eta = 1 - 0.273 = \mathbf{0.727 \text{ or } 72.7\%}$$

**Part (b):**
- Equivalent raft dimensions: $B_g = 4.5$ m, $L_g = 4.5$ m
- Equivalent raft depth: $D_f = (2/3)(15) = 10$ m
- Weight of soil in raft: $W_s = 4.5 \times 4.5 \times 10 \times (10 - 9.81) \approx 38.5$ kN
- *More practically:* $W_s = (4.5)(4.5)(10)(10) = 2025$ kN (using $\gamma_{sub} = 10$ kN/m³ with no buoyancy reduction unless specified)
- Net pressure: $\Delta\sigma_{net} = (3000 - 2025) / (4.5 \times 4.5) = 975 / 20.25 = 48.15$ kPa
- Consolidation settlement (assuming uniform stress over 20 m clay layer):
$$S_c = \mu \cdot m_v \cdot \Delta\sigma_{net} \cdot H$$
$$S_c = (0.5)(0.0003)(48.15)(20) = 0.1445 \text{ m} \approx \mathbf{144.5 \text{ mm}}$$

**Answer: η = 0.727 (72.7%), S_c ≈ 144.5 mm**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**
A **4×4 square pile group** consists of precast concrete piles of **0.4 m diameter** and **20 m length**, spaced at **1.2 m center-to-center**. The pile cap is **5.0 m × 5.0 m**. The soil profile is:
- 0 to 5 m: Soft clay (above pile cap level, not contributing).
- 5 to 25 m: Soft clay (around and below piles), with $E_s = 8$ MPa, $\nu_s = 0.5$.
- The pile load test gives single pile settlement $S_1 = 12$ mm at working load $Q_1 = 400$ kN.

**(a)** Using the Poulos interaction factor approach, compute the **group settlement** for the average pile in the group, given that the **sum of interaction factors $\sum \alpha = 1.85$**. **[7 Marks]**
**(b)** Compare this with the **Equivalent Raft Method** if the applied structural load is $Q_g = 7000$ kN, $m_v = 0.0004$ m²/kN, and clay thickness below raft is 20 m. Comment on the differences. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
- Number of piles: n = 16
- Total load: $Q_g = 16 \times 400 = 6400$ kN
- Group settlement ratio: $R_s = 1 + \sum \alpha = 1 + 1.85 = 2.85$
- Group settlement:
$$S_g = S_1 \cdot \frac{Q_g}{n \cdot Q_1} \cdot R_s = 12 \times \frac{6400}{16 \times 400} \times 2.85$$
$$S_g = 12 \times 1.0 \times 2.85 = \mathbf{34.2 \text{ mm}}$$

**Part (b):**
- Equivalent raft: $B_g = 5.0$ m, $L_g = 5.0$ m
- Depth: $D_f = (2/3)(20) = 13.33$ m
- Weight of soil: $W_s = 5.0 \times 5.0 \times 13.33 \times 10 = 3332.5$ kN
- Net pressure: $\Delta\sigma_{net} = (7000 - 3332.5)/(25) = 146.7$ kPa
- Consolidation settlement (using $\mu = 0.6$ for soft clay):
$$S_c = 0.6 \times 0.0004 \times 146.7 \times 20 = 0.704 \text{ m} = \mathbf{704 \text{ mm}}$$

**Comment:** The Equivalent Raft Method gives **drastically higher settlement** (704 mm) compared to the Interaction Factor Method (34.2 mm). This is because:
- The Equivalent Raft Method uses **1D consolidation** assumptions and overestimates stresses in the clay.
- The Poulos method uses **elastic theory** with proper 3D stress distribution.
- In practice, both methods should be applied, and the **higher of the two** is often used conservatively (KTU practice).
- For design, the engineer should also check **differential settlement** between adjacent pile groups.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Mistake in Equivalent Raft Depth:** Students often place the raft at the **pile tip level (L)** instead of **(2/3)L**. This is the most common error — KTU valuation keys specifically check this.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Subtract Soil Weight:** The net stress at the raft base must be calculated as $(Q_g - W_s) / (B_g \cdot L_g)$, not simply $Q_g / (B_g \cdot L_g)$. Marks are deducted for using gross stress.
>
> 3. **Converse-Labarre Angle Unit Error:** The angle $\theta$ must be in **degrees** in the denominator (90°), not in radians. This is a frequently lost 1-mark penalty.
>
> 4. **Settlement Ratio vs. Group Efficiency Confusion:** Students often confuse Rs (settlement ratio, > 1) with η (efficiency, < 1). These are **inverse concepts** but distinct.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the Skempton-Bjerrum μ Factor:** In 1D consolidation calculations for clay, the μ correction factor is **mandatory**. Using μ = 1.0 (i.e., 1D Terzaghi directly) is technically valid but is a "lower bound" and shows lack of depth.
>
> 6. **Units Inconsistency:** $m_v$ in m²/kN, stress in kPa (= kN/m²), thickness in m. Mixing units (e.g., $m_v$ in m²/MN with stress in kPa) is a fatal error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Settlement Ratio (R_s):** Always > 1 for pile groups in clay, typically **1.5 to 8.0**.
- **Group Efficiency (η):** Always < 1 for clays, computed via Converse-Labarre formula.
- **Equivalent Raft Depth:** Standard KTU convention is **$D_f = (2/3)L$**.
- **Net Stress Calculation:** Always subtract displaced soil weight.
- **Stress Dispersion:** Use **2V:1H method** for stress increase with depth below equivalent raft.
- **Consolidation Settlement Formula:** $S_c = \mu \cdot m_v \cdot \sum \Delta\sigma \cdot H$ with $\mu$ from Skempton-Bjerrum.
- **Poulos Interaction Factors:** Tabulated as function of s/D, L/D, K, and $\nu_s$.
- **Two Methods:** Equivalent Raft (simpler, conservative) and Interaction Factor (rigorous, elastic).
- **Pile Spacing:** 3D minimum for clays (clay plugs the group if closer), 6D preferred.
- **Allowable Settlement (IS 2911):** Typically **25 mm for isolated foundations, 50 mm for rafts** — pile group settlements are often higher but must be checked against serviceability limits.
- **Differential Settlement:** Always check between adjacent pile groups — limit is **L/500 to L/1000** for buildings.
- **Block Failure Consideration:** For closely spaced piles in clay (s < 3D), check **block bearing capacity** which may govern over individual pile capacity.
- **KTU Exam Weightage:** This topic is consistently a **14-mark Part B question** and occasionally appears as a **3-mark Part A** in Module 2.
- **Unit Reminder:** $E_s$ in MPa or kPa, $m_v$ in m²/kN, settlement in mm (final answer expected in mm for KTU).
- **Conservative Design Practice:** Use the **higher of the two** settlement estimates (Equivalent Raft vs. Interaction Factor) for final design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalent Raft Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Equivalent Raft Concept

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Equivalent Raft Concept** (also called the *Equivalent Footing Concept* or *Hypothetical Raft Method*) is a simplified analytical technique used in deep foundation engineering wherein an entire **pile group** along with its pile cap is conceptually replaced by a **single equivalent shallow raft (footing)** located at a fictitious depth below the ground surface. This equivalent raft is then analyzed as a conventional shallow foundation to estimate the **settlement** and **bearing capacity** of the pile group.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This concept is exclusively applied for **pile groups** where the spacing between piles (typically **3D** for end-bearing piles and **2.5D to 3D** for friction piles in loose/medium soils as per **IS 2911 Part 1/Sec 2**) is less than the critical spacing that would cause the piles to behave individually.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 – Module 2 Tag:** This topic sits under "Pile Foundations – Design of Pile Groups" and is a frequent 7-mark or 14-mark question in Part B examinations.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you push a **bundle of 9 pencils** (a 3×3 pile group) point-downward into soft clay until the tips rest at some depth.

- **Real Behavior:** Each pencil tip transfers load through the clay along its length.
- **Practical Problem:** Calculating stresses and settlements under **each individual pencil** is mathematically cumbersome and requires advanced software (like **PLAXIS 3D** or **FB-Pier**).
- **Equivalent Raft Trick:** Instead, replace the **entire bundle + cap** with one **single flat wooden plank** of the same overall width, sitting at roughly **two-thirds of the pencil length** down into the clay.

The plank (equivalent raft) has the **same total area** as the bundle's footprint (slightly enlarged), and the clay beneath it is analyzed using simple 1-D or 2-D settlement equations. This gives a quick, conservative estimate suitable for **preliminary design**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Application:** Used extensively by **structural consultants in Kerala** for designing pile foundations supporting multi-storey buildings in the coastal alluvial deposits of **Kochi, Kozhikode, and Trivandrum**, where deep soft clay layers extend 20–40 m below ground.

---

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Assumptions

| Parameter | Standard Value (KTU/IS 2911) |
|-----------|-----------------------------|
| Typical concrete pile diameter (**D**) | **300 mm – 600 mm** for buildings; up to **1200 mm** for bridges |
| Pile spacing (**S**) | **2.5D to 3D** minimum (IS 2911) |
| Depth of equivalent raft from pile cap base | **(2/3) × L** (Terzaghi's assumption) |
| Depth of equivalent raft (alternate, IS 2911) | At **pile tip level** |
| Unit weight of soil (**γ**) | **16 – 20 kN/m³** (typical Kerala alluvial clay) |
| Young's Modulus of soil (**E_s**) | **5 – 50 MPa** (soft to stiff clay) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Logic

The Equivalent Raft Concept operates on **four fundamental steps**:

1. **Step 1 – Establish the Pile Group Geometry:**
   Identify the number of piles (n), pile diameter (D), pile length (L), pile spacing (S), and pile cap dimensions (B_c × L_c).

2. **Step 2 – Define the Equivalent Raft Dimensions:**
   The hypothetical raft extends from the **outer edges of the outermost piles**, not the edges of the pile cap.
   - Effective width: $B' = B_g + D$
   - Effective length: $L' = L_g + D$
   Where $B_g$ and $L_g$ are the **center-to-center distances** between the outermost piles in the group along the width and length directions, respectively.

3. **Step 3 – Locate the Equivalent Raft in the Soil Profile:**
   Place the equivalent raft at a depth of $\dfrac{2}{3}L$ measured from the **top of the pile** (i.e., from the base of the pile cap), as recommended by **Terzaghi and Peck (1967)**.

4. **Step 4 – Analyze as a Shallow Foundation:**
   Compute the **net foundation pressure** at the level of the equivalent raft, then evaluate **bearing capacity** using classical Terzaghi/Meyerhof/Hansen equations and **settlement** using the 2:1 stress distribution method or Schmertmann's method.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why (2/3)L?** Empirical observations show that the load transfer zone within a pile group migrates from individual pile shafts to a collective block failure surface at approximately two-thirds of the pile depth. This gives a **realistic, slightly conservative** settlement estimate.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $B' = B_g + D$ | Effective width of equivalent raft | m |
| 2 | $L' = L_g + D$ | Effective length of equivalent raft | m |
| 3 | $D_e = D_f + \tfrac{2}{3}L$ | Depth of equivalent raft from ground surface | m |
| 4 | $Q_g = Q_s + Q_c$ | Total pile group load (structural + cap weight) | kN |
| 5 | $q = \dfrac{Q_g}{B' \cdot L'}$ | Gross contact pressure at equivalent raft | kN/m² |
| 6 | $q_{net} = q - \gamma \cdot D_e$ | Net pressure increase in soil at depth $D_e$ | kN/m² |
| 7 | $\sigma_z = q_{net} \cdot \dfrac{B' L'}{(B'+z)(L'+z)}$ | Stress at depth $z$ below equivalent raft (2:1 method) | kN/m² |
| 8 | $S_e = \sum \dfrac{\sigma_{z,i} \cdot \Delta z_i}{E_{s,i}}$ | Elastic settlement of equivalent raft | m |
| 9 | $S_e = q_{net} \cdot B' \cdot \dfrac{1-\mu^2}{E_s} \cdot I_w$ | Settlement using Steinbrenner's influence factor $I_w$ | m |
| 10 | $q_{u,eq} = c N_c \xi_c + q N_q \xi_q + 0.5 \gamma B' N_\gamma \xi_\gamma$ | Ultimate bearing capacity of equivalent raft | kN/m² |
| 11 | $F.S. = \dfrac{q_{u,eq}}{q_{net}}$ | Factor of safety against bearing failure | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> Where: $\mu$ = Poisson's ratio of soil, $E_s$ = Elastic modulus of soil, $I_w$ = Steinbrenner's influence factor (depends on $L'/B'$ and depth), $\xi$ = shape and depth factors, and $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ = Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors.

---

### 2.3 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **Preliminary Design Tool:** Used by design offices during tender-stage analysis before committing to expensive **3D FEM software**.
- **Verification Check:** Acts as a sanity check against sophisticated Plaxis/FB-Pier outputs – results should be of the **same order of magnitude**.
- **Code-Compliant Method:** Recommended by **IS 2911 (Part 1/Sec 2): 2010** for the estimation of group settlement in clays.
- **Cost-Effective:** Saves considerable time compared to **load testing** of full pile groups, which is impractical for large projects.

---

### 2.4 Limitations of the Concept (KTU Frequently Asked)

> [!WARNING]
> 1. **Conservative for Friction Piles:** Overestimates settlement because actual load-sharing in soil-pile interaction is more efficient.
> 2. **Ignores Cap-Soil Contact:** Does not account for the direct load transfer from the pile cap resting on soil.
> 3. **Applicable Mainly to Cohesive Soils:** Most reliable for clays; in sands, the **Poulos and Davis (1980)** elastic approach is preferred.
> 4. **Neglects Group Efficiency:** Does not explicitly use the group efficiency factor $\eta_g$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Equivalent Raft Dimensions

Consider a pile group with **n** piles arranged in a rectangular pattern. The piles have diameter **D** and the pile cap has dimensions $B_c \times L_c$.

**Setup:**

- Distance between centers of outermost piles along width: $B_g = B_c - D$ *(because the outer pile center is at distance $D/2$ from each edge of cap)*
- Distance between centers of outermost piles along length: $L_g = L_c - D$

**Effective dimensions of the equivalent raft** (extending one pile diameter beyond outermost pile centers):

$$
B' = B_g + D = (B_c - D) + D = B_c
$$

$$
L' = L_g + D = (L_c - D) + D = L_c
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The effective raft width equals the **pile cap width** (because the raft extends from the outer edge of the outer pile to the opposite outer edge, which corresponds exactly to the cap edges). This is a frequently tested point in KTU exams.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Net Pressure at Equivalent Raft Level

**Total Load on Pile Group ($Q_g$):**

$$
Q_g = Q_{structural} + W_{cap} + W_{soil\,on\,cap}
$$

**Gross Contact Pressure ($q$):**

$$
q = \frac{Q_g}{B' \times L'}
$$

**Subtracting Overburden Pressure** to get net pressure increase in the soil:

$$
q_{net} = q - \gamma \cdot D_e
$$

Where $D_e = D_f + \dfrac{2}{3} L$, $D_f$ = depth of pile cap base below ground level, and **γ** = average unit weight of soil above the equivalent raft level.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Settlement using 2:1 Stress Distribution Method

The **2:1 method** assumes that the applied stress spreads out at a slope of **2 vertical : 1 horizontal** as it moves deeper into the soil.

**Stress at depth z below equivalent raft:**

Starting with the assumption that stress spreads linearly outward:
- At depth $z = 0$ (at the equivalent raft level), the loaded area is $B' \times L'$.
- At depth $z$, the area becomes $(B' + z) \times (L' + z)$.

Since total force is conserved:

$$
\sigma_z \times (B' + z)(L' + z) = q_{net} \times B' \times L'
$$

Solving for $\sigma_z$:

$$
\sigma_z = q_{net} \times \frac{B' \times L'}{(B' + z)(L' + z)}
$$

**Elastic Settlement of a Soil Layer of Thickness $\Delta z$:**

For a soil layer $i$ with elastic modulus $E_{s,i}$ and Poisson's ratio $\mu_i$:

$$
\Delta S_i = \frac{\sigma_{z,i}}{E_{s,i}} \times \Delta z_i \times [1 - \mu_i^2] \times I_{w,i}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For preliminary analysis, the term $[1 - \mu_i^2] \times I_{w,i}$ is often approximated as **0.9 to 1.0** for typical clay layers, leading to the simplified form $S_e = \sum \dfrac{\sigma_{z,i} \cdot \Delta z_i}{E_{s,i}}$.

**Total Settlement of Equivalent Raft:**

$$
S_e = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta S_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\sigma_{z,i} \cdot \Delta z_i \cdot (1-\mu_i^2) \cdot I_{w,i}}{E_{s,i}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A pile group consists of **9 piles** of diameter **500 mm**, length **15 m**, arranged in a 3×3 pattern with pile spacing **1.5 m** center-to-center. The pile cap is **4.5 m × 4.5 m** and is placed at **1.5 m depth** below ground level. Total structural load (including cap weight) = **6000 kN**. Average unit weight of soil above equivalent raft = **18 kN/m³**. Calculate the net pressure at the equivalent raft level and the stress at 5 m below it.

**Step 1: Equivalent raft dimensions**

$$
B' = B_g + D = (4.5 - 0.5) + 0.5 = 4.5 \text{ m}
$$

$$
L' = L_g + D = (4.5 - 0.5) + 0.5 = 4.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2: Depth of equivalent raft**

$$
D_e = D_f + \tfrac{2}{3} L = 1.5 + \tfrac{2}{3} \times 15 = 1.5 + 10 = 11.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Gross and net pressure**

$$
q = \frac{Q_g}{B' \cdot L'} = \frac{6000}{4.5 \times 4.5} = 296.30 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
q_{net} = q - \gamma \cdot D_e = 296.30 - (18 \times 11.5) = 296.30 - 207 = 89.30 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Stress at 5 m below equivalent raft (z = 5 m)**

$$
\sigma_z = 89.30 \times \frac{4.5 \times 4.5}{(4.5 + 5)(4.5 + 5)} = 89.30 \times \frac{20.25}{90.25} = 20.03 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how the stress reduces from **89.30 kN/m²** at the equivalent raft to only **20.03 kN/m²** at 5 m below it – demonstrating the rapid attenuation of stresses with depth in cohesive soils.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison with Other Methods

| Method | Underlying Assumption | Best Suited For | Accuracy |
|--------|----------------------|-----------------|----------|
| **Equivalent Raft (2:1)** | Stress spreads at 2V:1H | Clays, preliminary design | Moderate |
| **Equivalent Raft (Schmertmann)** | CPT-based strain influence factor | Sands | High |
| **Poulos & Davis Elastic** | Mindlin's solution for pile-soil interaction | Both clays and sands | High |
| **3D FEM (Plaxis)** | Continuum mechanics with non-linear soil models | All soils, final design | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram of Equivalent Raft

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Real Pile Group System] --> B[Pile Cap with N Piles]
    B --> C[Load Q transferred through cap]
    C --> D[Soil Mass with Complex 3D Interaction]
    D --> E[3D FEM Analysis Required]
    
    A2[Equivalent Raft System] --> B2[Single Rectangular Raft B' x L']
    B2 --> C2[Same Load Q acting at depth De]
    C2 --> D2[Soil Analyzed as Shallow Foundation]
    D2 --> E2[1D or 2D Settlement Calculation]
    
    A -.Transformed to.-> A2
    E -.Complex.-> E2
    
    style A fill:#ffcccc
    style A2 fill:#ccffcc
    style E fill:#ffcccc
    style E2 fill:#ccffcc
```

### 4.2 Pile Group to Equivalent Raft Transformation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SG1[Actual Pile Group]
        P1[Pile 1] 
        P2[Pile 2]
        P3[Pile 3]
        P4[Pile 4]
        P5[Pile 5]
        P6[Pile 6]
        P7[Pile 7]
        P8[Pile 8]
        P9[Pile 9]
        CAP[Pile Cap 4.5m x 4.5m]
    end
    
    P1 --> CAP
    P2 --> CAP
    P3 --> CAP
    P4 --> CAP
    P5 --> CAP
    P6 --> CAP
    P7 --> CAP
    P8 --> CAP
    P9 --> CAP
    
    CAP --> TRANS[Transformation Logic]
    
    TRANS --> ER[Equivalent Raft]
    TRANS --> DIM[B' equals Bc, L' equals Lc]
    TRANS --> DEP[De equals Df plus 2L by 3]
    
    style SG1 fill:#e6f3ff
    style ER fill:#ffe6cc
    style DIM fill:#fff4cc
    style DEP fill:#fff4cc
```

### 4.3 Stress Distribution Flow Below Equivalent Raft

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LOAD[Load Q on Pile Group] --> GC[Gross Pressure q at Raft Level]
    GC --> NP[Subtract Overburden gamma times De]
    NP --> QN[Net Pressure qnet]
    QN --> Z1[Stress at z = 0]
    QN --> Z2[Stress at z = 2m]
    QN --> Z3[Stress at z = 5m]
    QN --> Z4[Stress at z = 10m]
    
    Z1 --> FORM1[sigma = qnet times BL over BL]
    Z2 --> FORM2[sigma = qnet times BL over B+2 times L+2]
    Z3 --> FORM3[sigma = qnet times BL over B+5 times L+5]
    Z4 --> FORM4[sigma = qnet times BL over B+10 times L+10]
    
    FORM1 --> SETT[Integrate over Layer Thickness]
    FORM2 --> SETT
    FORM3 --> SETT
    FORM4 --> SETT
    
    SETT --> SE[Total Settlement Se]
    
    style LOAD fill:#ff9999
    style QN fill:#ffcc99
    style SE fill:#99ff99
```

### 4.4 Step-by-Step Design Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[Start: Pile Group Design] --> S1[Identify n, D, L, S, Bc, Lc]
    S1 --> S2[Compute B' and L']
    S2 --> S3[Calculate De using 2L by 3]
    S3 --> S4[Compute Total Load Qg]
    S4 --> S5[Calculate Gross Pressure q]
    S5 --> S6[Calculate Net Pressure qnet]
    S6 --> S7{Choose Method}
    S7 --> M1[2:1 Stress Distribution]
    S7 --> M2[Schmertmann Method]
    S7 --> M3[Poulos Elastic Method]
    M1 --> OUT1[Compute Settlement Se]
    M2 --> OUT1
    M3 --> OUT1
    OUT1 --> S8[Compare with Allowable Settlement]
    S8 --> S9{Within Limits?}
    S9 -->|Yes| SAFE[Design is Safe]
    S9 -->|No| REV[Revise Pile Length or Number]
    REV --> S1
    
    style START fill:#cce5ff
    style SAFE fill:#ccffcc
    style REV fill:#ffcccc
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Define the Equivalent Raft Concept as applied to pile group analysis. Mention the depth at which the equivalent raft is conventionally placed.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Equivalent Raft Concept is a simplified analysis method in which an entire **pile group along with its pile cap is replaced by an equivalent shallow raft (footing)** of the same overall dimensions, located at a certain depth below the ground surface. This equivalent raft is then analyzed as a conventional shallow foundation to estimate settlement and bearing capacity.

As per **Terzaghi and Peck (1967)**, the equivalent raft is placed at a depth of **$\dfrac{2}{3}L$** below the base of the pile cap (where **L** is the length of the pile).

*(Breakdown: Definition = 2 marks; Depth = 1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

*State any **three limitations** of the Equivalent Raft Concept.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. It **overestimates settlement** in friction piles as it ignores efficient soil-pile interaction mechanisms. *(1 mark)*
2. It **neglects the direct load transfer** from the pile cap resting on the soil surface. *(1 mark)*
3. It is **most reliable for clays** and gives less accurate predictions in sandy soils where Poulos elastic method is preferred. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) – KTU University Exam Pattern**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2023]**

A building column carries a **service load of 2400 kN** including self-weight. The column is supported by a **pile group of 6 piles** of diameter **450 mm**, length **12 m**, arranged in a **2 × 3 pattern** with center-to-center spacing of **1.35 m**. The pile cap measures **2.7 m × 4.05 m** and is located at **1.8 m depth** below ground level. The soil is medium stiff clay with **average unit weight γ = 19 kN/m³**, **cohesion c = 60 kN/m²**, and elastic modulus **E_s = 25 MPa**. Take **Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors** for **φ = 0°** as $N_c = 5.7$, $N_q = 1.0$, $N_\gamma = 0.0$.

### **Part (a) – 7 Marks**

*Compute the dimensions of the equivalent raft and the net pressure increase at the equivalent raft level.*

### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Equivalent Raft Dimensions** *(2 marks)*

Width of pile cap: $B_c = 2.7$ m; Length: $L_c = 4.05$ m; Pile diameter: $D = 0.45$ m.

$$
B' = B_c = 2.7 \text{ m}
$$

$$
L' = L_c = 4.05 \text{ m}
$$

*(Note: $B' = B_g + D = (2.7 - 0.45) + 0.45 = 2.7$ m, and similarly for $L'$.)*

**Step 2: Depth of Equivalent Raft** *(2 marks)*

$$
D_e = D_f + \tfrac{2}{3}L = 1.8 + \tfrac{2}{3} \times 12 = 1.8 + 8.0 = 9.8 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3: Gross and Net Pressure** *(3 marks)*

Assuming total group load $Q_g = 2400$ kN (ignoring cap weight for simplicity, or include if specified):

$$
q = \frac{2400}{2.7 \times 4.05} = \frac{2400}{10.935} = 219.48 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

$$
q_{net} = q - \gamma \cdot D_e = 219.48 - (19 \times 9.8) = 219.48 - 186.2 = 33.28 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

---

### **Part (b) – 7 Marks**

*Determine the safe load capacity of the pile group using the Equivalent Raft Concept. Apply a factor of safety of 2.5.*

### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Ultimate Bearing Capacity of Equivalent Raft** *(4 marks)*

For $\phi = 0°$ (clay), Terzaghi's equation:

$$
q_{u,eq} = 1.3 c N_c + \gamma D_e N_q = 1.3 \times 60 \times 5.7 + 19 \times 9.8 \times 1.0
$$

$$
q_{u,eq} = 444.6 + 186.2 = 630.8 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The shape factor 1.3 is used for rectangular footing as per Terzaghi. The $N_\gamma$ term is zero for $\phi = 0°$.

**Step 2: Safe Bearing Pressure** *(2 marks)*

$$
q_{safe} = \frac{q_{u,eq}}{F.S.} = \frac{630.8}{2.5} = 252.32 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 3: Safe Load Capacity** *(1 mark)*

$$
Q_{safe} = q_{safe} \times B' \times L' = 252.32 \times 2.7 \times 4.05 = 2759.4 \text{ kN}
$$

Since **2759.4 kN > 2400 kN**, the design is **SAFE**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

A pile group has **12 piles** of diameter **500 mm**, length **20 m**, arranged in a **3 × 4 pattern** with spacing of **1.5 m**. The pile cap dimensions are **4.0 m × 5.5 m** and depth of pile cap base is **2.0 m**. Compute the **settlement** of the pile group using the Equivalent Raft Concept for the following clay profile below the equivalent raft:

| Layer | Depth below equivalent raft (m) | Thickness Δz (m) | E_s (MPa) |
|-------|-------------------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| 1 | 0 – 5 | 5 | 20 |
| 2 | 5 – 12 | 7 | 35 |
| 3 | 12 – 20 | 8 | 60 |

Total load on pile group = **9000 kN**, average unit weight above equivalent raft = **18 kN/m³**.

### **Model Solution Outline (Full Marks – 14):**

**Step 1: Equivalent Raft Dimensions and Depth** *(2 marks)*
- $B' = 4.0$ m, $L' = 5.5$ m, $D_e = 2.0 + \tfrac{2}{3}(20) = 15.33$ m

**Step 2: Net Pressure** *(2 marks)*
- $q = \dfrac{9000}{4.0 \times 5.5} = 409.09$ kN/m²
- $q_{net} = 409.09 - (18 \times 15.33) = 409.09 - 275.94 = 133.15$ kN/m²

**Step 3: Stress at Mid-depth of Each Layer using 2:1 Method** *(6 marks)*

For Layer 1 (mid-depth z = 2.5 m):
$$
\sigma_{z1} = 133.15 \times \frac{4.0 \times 5.5}{(4.0+2.5)(5.5+2.5)} = 133.15 \times \frac{22.0}{39.75} = 73.69 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

For Layer 2 (mid-depth z = 8.5 m):
$$
\sigma_{z2} = 133.15 \times \frac{22.0}{(4.0+8.5)(5.5+8.5)} = 133.15 \times \frac{22.0}{117.0} = 25.04 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

For Layer 3 (mid-depth z = 16 m):
$$
\sigma_{z3} = 133.15 \times \frac{22.0}{(4.0+16)(5.5+16)} = 133.15 \times \frac{22.0}{430.5} = 6.80 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 4: Settlement of Each Layer** *(3 marks)*

Using $S_i = \dfrac{\sigma_{z,i} \cdot \Delta z_i}{E_{s,i}}$ (assuming $[1-\mu^2] I_w \approx 1$):

$$
S_1 = \frac{73.69 \times 5}{20 \times 10^3} = 0.01842 \text{ m} = 18.42 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
S_2 = \frac{25.04 \times 7}{35 \times 10^3} = 0.00501 \text{ m} = 5.01 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
S_3 = \frac{6.80 \times 8}{60 \times 10^3} = 0.00091 \text{ m} = 0.91 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: Total Settlement** *(1 mark)*

$$
S_e = 18.42 + 5.01 + 0.91 = 24.34 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since this is **< 25 mm** (typical KTU allowable for isolated pile groups in clay per IS 8009 Part 1), the design is **acceptable**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Costing 2–3 Marks Each:**
> 
> 1. **Using pile cap dimensions directly without verifying $B' = B_g + D$** – Students often write $B' = B_c$ without justification. Show the derivation step.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract overburden pressure** – Computing settlement using gross $q$ instead of net $q_{net}$ is a **frequent error**.
> 3. **Using incorrect depth** – Some students take $D_e = \tfrac{2}{3}L$ from ground surface (forgetting $D_f$). Always start from **pile cap base level**.
> 4. **Ignoring units in 2:1 stress equation** – The $\Delta z$ must be in meters and $E_s$ in kPa (consistent SI units).
> 5. **Missing the [1−μ²]I_w factor** – While often approximated as 1.0, in rigorous KTU solutions it should be **explicitly mentioned** (typically 0.9 for clay).
> 6. **Not checking final settlement against allowable limits** – KTU expects students to **comment on the safety** of the design, not just compute numbers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- [ ] Equivalent Raft Concept = Pile group → Single shallow footing at depth $\dfrac{2}{3}L$ from cap base.
- [ ] Raft dimensions: $B' = B_g + D = B_c$ and $L' = L_g + D = L_c$.
- [ ] Depth of equivalent raft: $D_e = D_f + \dfrac{2}{3}L$ from ground surface.
- [ ] Net pressure: $q_{net} = \dfrac{Q_g}{B' \cdot L'} - \gamma D_e$.
- [ ] Stress at depth z: $\sigma_z = q_{net} \cdot \dfrac{B' L'}{(B'+z)(L'+z)}$ (2:1 method).
- [ ] Settlement: $S_e = \sum \dfrac{\sigma_{z,i} \cdot \Delta z_i \cdot (1-\mu_i^2) \cdot I_{w,i}}{E_{s,i}}$.
- [ ] For clays ($\phi = 0°$): $q_{u,eq} = 1.3 c N_c + \gamma D_e N_q$ (Terzaghi's shape factor 1.3 used).
- [ ] Bearing capacity factors at $\phi = 0°$: $N_c = 5.7$, $N_q = 1.0$, $N_\gamma = 0.0$.
- [ ] Factor of safety typically used: **2.5 to 3.0** for pile groups.
- [ ] Allowable settlement: **25 mm for isolated groups, 40 mm for raft-supported groups** (IS 8009).
- [ ] Most reliable for **clays**; in sands, use **Poulos-Davis elastic method**.
- [ ] **Conservative method** – actual pile group behaviour is generally more efficient than predicted.
- [ ] Pile spacing must comply with **IS 2911**: minimum **2.5D** (cohesionless) to **3D** (cohesive) for group action.
- [ ] Does **not** account for cap-soil contact stress or group efficiency factor $\eta_g$ directly.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Viva:**
> **"R-D-P-N-S"** → **R**aft dimensions ($B', L'$) → **D**epth ($D_e$) → **P**ressure ($q_{net}$) → **N**umerical stress ($\sigma_z$) → **S**ettlement ($S_e$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Problem, Settlement of Pile Groups in Sand
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problem, Settlement of Pile Groups in Sand

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Settlement of a Pile Group in Sand** is defined as the total downward vertical displacement experienced by the pile cap (and the pile heads acting as a unit) due to the combined elastic compression of the soil mass within the *stress influence zone* beneath the equivalent load transfer area. Unlike clays (where consolidation governs), pile group settlement in sand is primarily an **immediate (elastic) settlement** governed by the elastic constants $E_s$ and $\mu_s$ of the granular medium.

Mathematically, the group settlement $S_g$ is obtained by either:

$$S_g = R_g \cdot S_i \quad \text{(Poulos-Davis Approach)}$$

or

$$S_g = S_{e,\text{equivalent-raft}} \quad \text{(Equivalent Raft / Meyerhof Approach)}$$

where $R_g$ is the **settlement ratio** and $S_i$ is the settlement of a *single isolated pile* under the same working load.

## 1.2 Key Terminology Introduced

| Symbol | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $S_g$ | Settlement of pile group (mm) |
| $S_i$ | Settlement of single isolated pile (mm) |
| $R_g$ | Settlement ratio ($S_g / S_i$), dimensionless |
| $B_e$ | Width of equivalent raft (m) |
| $D_e$ | Depth of equivalent raft (m), usually $2L/3$ in sand |
| $I_\rho$ | Settlement influence factor for single pile (Poulos) |
| $I_s I_w$ | Steinbrenner shape and depth influence factors |
| $\alpha$ | Pile-soil interaction factor |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy – "The Mattress and the Stilts"**
> Imagine pushing a cluster of 9 thin stilts (the piles) into a soft sandy beach. If you press on **one stilt alone**, it sinks a little because only a small zone of sand is stressed. But if you join the tops of all 9 stilts by a rigid wooden plank (the pile cap) and push **down on the plank**, every stilt shares the load, but the *combined stress bubble* in the sand underneath becomes much larger and deeper — the entire plank sinks more than 9 times the single-stilt settlement. That "amplification factor" is exactly the **settlement ratio $R_g$**, which is why pile groups in sand always settle *more* than a single pile, even when their *capacity* is reduced by group efficiency.

## 1.4 Why Settlement of Pile Groups is Critical

> [!WARNING]
> **Code Limit:** IS 2911 (Part 1/Section 2) limits pile group settlement to **25 mm** for isolated pile caps and **40 mm** for rafts supported on piles in sandy soils. Exceeding these values triggers serviceability failure, even when bearing capacity is satisfactory — a classic KTU *design trap*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Pile Group Settlement Analysis in Sand

### Pillar A – The Equivalent Raft Method (Meyerhof, 1959)

The pile group is *imagined* to behave as a large shallow footing (the "equivalent raft") whose base lies at a depth equal to **two-thirds of the pile length** below the pile cap. The elastic settlement of this fictitious raft, resting on an elastic half-space (the sand), is taken as the group settlement.

### Pillar B – The Poulos–Davis Superposition Method (1968)

The settlement of a *single* pile is first computed using elastic theory. The pile-pile interaction is then introduced through a **settlement ratio $R_g$** that depends on:
- Number of piles ($n$)
- Pile spacing ratio ($s/d$)
- Slenderness ratio ($L/d$)
- Soil modulus profile ($\nu = E_s L / d$)

## 2.2 Step-Wise Logical Breakdown

### Equivalent Raft Approach
1. Compute width of equivalent raft: $B_e = (n_r - 1)\,s + d$ (for $n_r$ piles across).
2. Compute length of equivalent raft: $L_e = (n_c - 1)\,s + d$.
3. Depth of equivalent raft from ground: $D_e = (2/3)\,L$.
4. Net contact pressure: $q = Q_g / (B_e \cdot L_e)$ where $Q_g$ = total group load.
5. Influence factors: Read $I_s$ (shape) and $I_w$ (depth) from Steinbrenner charts.
6. Compute elastic settlement: $S_e = q\,B_e\,(1-\mu_s^2)\,I_s\,I_w \;/\; E_s$.

### Poulos-Davis Approach
1. Compute settlement of single pile: $S_i = Q_i\,I_\rho / (E_s\,d)$.
2. Read settlement ratio $R_g$ from Poulos-Davis design charts for the given $s/d$, $L/d$, $n$, and soil profile.
3. Compute group settlement: $S_g = R_g \cdot S_i$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are standard textbook equations used in PECET635. Memorize them — they appear every semester.**

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $B_e = (n_r - 1)\,s + d$ | Equivalent raft width | m |
| 2 | $L_e = (n_c - 1)\,s + d$ | Equivalent raft length | m |
| 3 | $D_e = (2/3)\,L$ | Depth of equivalent raft (sand) | m |
| 4 | $q = Q_g / (B_e L_e)$ | Net contact pressure | kN/m² |
| 5 | $S_e = \dfrac{q\,B_e\,(1-\mu_s^2)\,I_s\,I_w}{E_s}$ | Steinbrenner elastic settlement | m |
| 6 | $S_i = \dfrac{Q_i\,I_\rho}{E_s\,d}$ | Single-pile settlement (Poulos) | m |
| 7 | $S_g = R_g \cdot S_i$ | Group settlement (Poulos-Davis) | m |
| 8 | $m = L_e / B_e$ | Shape ratio | – |
| 9 | $n' = D_e / B_e$ | Embedment ratio | – |
| 10 | $\nu = \dfrac{E_s \cdot L}{d}$ | Poulos normalised soil modulus | – |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In offshore oil platforms, long pile groups (often $4 \times 4$ to $6 \times 6$) penetrate 60–100 m of sand. The **equivalent raft method** gives conservative estimates used in tender-stage designs, while the **Poulos-Davis method** is preferred in detailed design where interaction effects must be captured. High-rise buildings on sandy coastal alluvium (e.g., Dubai, Mumbai, Kochi) routinely rely on these two methods to size pile groups for settlement control.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Problem

## 3.1 Working Numerical Problem (Model Question)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question Statement (Model):**
> A pile group of **9 piles arranged in a 3 × 3 pattern** supports a column working load of **5000 kN** (inclusive of pile cap weight). Each pile is **15 m long** and **500 mm in diameter**, spaced at **2.5 d (centre-to-centre)** in medium dense sand. The soil properties are: $E_s = 30\,\text{MPa}$, $\mu_s = 0.3$, $\phi = 35°$, $\gamma = 18\,\text{kN/m}^3$.
> Estimate the settlement of the pile group using:
> **(a)** The **Equivalent Raft Method** (Meyerhof-Steinbrenner).
> **(b)** The **Poulos-Davis Method**, given that the settlement influence factor for the single pile is $I_\rho = 0.025$ and the settlement ratio read from Poulos-Davis charts is $R_g = 4.2$.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution — Part (a) Equivalent Raft Method

**Given Data:**
- Group size: $3 \times 3 \Rightarrow n_r = 3$, $n_c = 3$
- Pile diameter $d = 0.5$ m
- Pile spacing $s = 2.5 d = 2.5 \times 0.5 = 1.25$ m
- Pile length $L = 15$ m
- Total group load $Q_g = 5000$ kN
- $E_s = 30\,\text{MPa} = 30{,}000\,\text{kPa}$
- $\mu_s = 0.3$

**Step 1 — Width of the Equivalent Raft**

The equivalent raft is assumed to extend from the outer edges of the outermost piles:

$$B_e = (n_r - 1)\,s + d$$

Substituting values:

$$B_e = (3 - 1)(1.25) + 0.5 = (2)(1.25) + 0.5 = 2.5 + 0.5 = 3.0 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Length of the Equivalent Raft**

$$L_e = (n_c - 1)\,s + d = (3 - 1)(1.25) + 0.5 = 3.0 \;\text{m}$$

So the equivalent raft is a square footing of $3.0\,\text{m} \times 3.0\,\text{m}$.

**Step 3 — Depth of Equivalent Raft**

For a pile group in sand, Meyerhof recommends locating the equivalent raft at **two-thirds** of the pile length below the ground surface:

$$D_e = \frac{2}{3}\,L = \frac{2}{3} \times 15 = 10 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 4 — Net Contact Pressure**

$$q = \frac{Q_g}{B_e \cdot L_e} = \frac{5000}{3.0 \times 3.0} = \frac{5000}{9.0} = 555.56 \;\text{kPa}$$

**Step 5 — Shape and Embedment Ratios**

$$m = \frac{L_e}{B_e} = \frac{3.0}{3.0} = 1.0$$

$$n' = \frac{D_e}{B_e} = \frac{10}{3.0} = 3.33$$

**Step 6 — Influence Factors (Steinbrenner)**

From Steinbrenner's chart (or Janbu's approximation for $m = 1$ and $n' = 3.33$):

- Shape factor $I_s \approx 0.82$ (for square footing, $m = 1$)
- Depth factor $I_w \approx 0.39$ (for $n' = 3.33$)

$$\therefore \; I_s\,I_w \approx 0.82 \times 0.39 \approx 0.32$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use $I_s \cdot I_w$ and not $I_s$ alone?** Steinbrenner originally gave settlement for a *surface* footing. Burland's modification (the depth factor $I_w$) reduces this settlement to account for the fact that the equivalent raft is *embedded* 10 m below ground — embedded footings settle less than surface footings of identical plan area.

**Step 7 — Elastic Settlement**

Using Steinbrenner's equation modified by Burland:

$$S_e = \frac{q \cdot B_e \cdot (1 - \mu_s^2) \cdot I_s \cdot I_w}{E_s}$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$S_e = \frac{(555.56)(3.0)(1 - 0.3^2)(0.32)}{30{,}000}$$

$$S_e = \frac{555.56 \times 3.0 \times (1 - 0.09) \times 0.32}{30{,}000}$$

$$S_e = \frac{555.56 \times 3.0 \times 0.91 \times 0.32}{30{,}000}$$

$$S_e = \frac{485.55}{30{,}000}$$

$$S_e = 0.01618 \;\text{m} \approx 16.2 \;\text{mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result (Equivalent Raft Method):** $S_g \approx 16.2$ mm — well within the IS 2911 limit of 25 mm. ✅

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Solution — Part (b) Poulos–Davis Method

**Step 1 — Load per Pile**

$$Q_i = \frac{Q_g}{n} = \frac{5000}{9} = 555.56 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 2 — Settlement of a Single Isolated Pile**

Using Poulos' elastic solution:

$$S_i = \frac{Q_i \cdot I_\rho}{E_s \cdot d}$$

$$S_i = \frac{555.56 \times 0.025}{30{,}000 \times 0.5}$$

$$S_i = \frac{13.889}{15{,}000}$$

$$S_i = 9.259 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m} = 0.926 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Settlement Ratio $R_g$**

From Poulos-Davis design chart for $s/d = 2.5$, $L/d = 30$, and $n = 9$:

$$R_g = 4.2$$

**Step 4 — Group Settlement**

$$S_g = R_g \cdot S_i = 4.2 \times 0.926 = 3.89 \;\text{mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result (Poulos-Davis Method):** $S_g \approx 3.9$ mm — also well within the IS 2911 limit. ✅

### 3.4 Comparative Discussion

| Parameter | Equivalent Raft (a) | Poulos-Davis (b) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Predicted settlement | **16.2 mm** | **3.9 mm** |
| Approach | Approximate elastic half-space | Rigorous elastic superposition |
| Conservatism | Conservative | Realistic / less conservative |
| KTU Typical Use | Tender / preliminary design | Final detailed design |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Tip:** If the question asks for a *single* design check, **adopt the larger of the two settlements** ($S_g = 16.2$ mm here) to remain on the safe side. KTU examiners reward *safe design* with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Settlement Calculation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Pile Group Settlement Problem] --> B{Sand or Clay?}
    B -- Sand --> C[Compute d, L, s, n]
    C --> D{Method?}
    D -- E1 --> E[Equivalent Raft Method]
    D -- E2 --> F[Poulos-Davis Method]
    E --> E1[Step 1: B_e = (n_r-1)s + d]
    E1 --> E2[Step 2: L_e = (n_c-1)s + d]
    E2 --> E3[Step 3: D_e = 2L/3]
    E3 --> E4[Step 4: q = Q_g / B_e L_e]
    E4 --> E5[Step 5: Read I_s I_w from Steinbrenner chart]
    E5 --> E6[Step 6: S_e = qB_e 1-mu^2 I_s I_w / E_s]
    E6 --> G[Result S_g in mm]
    F --> F1[Step 1: Q_i = Q_g / n]
    F1 --> F2[Step 2: S_i = Q_i I_rho / E_s d]
    F2 --> F3[Step 3: Read R_g from Poulos-Davis chart]
    F3 --> F4[Step 4: S_g = R_g times S_i]
    F4 --> G
    G --> H{S_g less than 25 mm?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Design SAFE per IS 2911]
    H -- No --> J[REDESIGN: increase pile length or section]
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Pile Group vs Equivalent Raft (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ACTUAL["ACTUAL PILE GROUP"]
        A1[Pile 1] --> CAP[Rigid Pile Cap]
        A2[Pile 2] --> CAP
        A3[Pile 3] --> CAP
        A4[Pile 4] --> CAP
        A5[Pile 5] --> CAP
        A6[Pile 6] --> CAP
        A7[Pile 7] --> CAP
        A8[Pile 8] --> CAP
        A9[Pile 9] --> CAP
        CAP --> LOAD[Column Load Q_g]
    end

    subgraph EQUIVALENT["EQUIVALENT RAFT MODEL"]
        B1[D_e = 2L/3 below ground] --> RAFT[Equivalent Raft B_e x L_e]
        RAFT --> QLOAD[Load Q_g transferred as pressure q]
    end

    ACTUAL -. Simulated as .-> EQUIVALENT
```

## 4.3 Influence Zone Schematic — Why Groups Settle More

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Single Pile Stress Bulge: small radius] --> NOTE1[Settlement S_i]
    P2[Pile Group Stress Bulge: large overlapping zones] --> NOTE2[Settlement S_g greater than n times S_i]
    NOTE2 --> NOTE3[R_g = S_g / S_i greater than 1 due to stress overlap]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**What is the Equivalent Raft Method of estimating pile group settlement? Why is the equivalent raft assumed at a depth of $2L/3$ for piles in sand?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Equivalent Raft Method (Meyerhof, 1959) replaces the pile group with a fictitious *shallow footing* whose plan dimensions equal the outer-to-outer dimensions of the pile cap and whose base lies at a depth $D_e = (2/3)\,L$ below ground. The elastic settlement of this fictitious footing (computed using Steinbrenner's chart and the elastic constants $E_s$, $\mu_s$ of the sand) is taken as the pile group settlement $S_g$.

The raft is assumed at **$2L/3$** because experimental and analytical studies have shown that the major stress transfer from a pile group in sand occurs in the upper two-thirds of the pile length; below this depth, the load transferred to soil is small (less than 10 % of the tip resistance). Placing the raft at $2L/3$ therefore gives a realistic estimate of the depth at which the load is effectively applied to the sand mass.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define settlement ratio $R_g$ in the context of pile groups. State the factors on which it depends.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **settlement ratio $R_g$** is defined as:

$$R_g = \frac{S_g}{S_i}$$

where $S_g$ = settlement of the pile group and $S_i$ = settlement of a single isolated pile subjected to the same average working load per pile.

$R_g$ depends on:
1. **Number of piles in the group ($n$)** — larger groups give larger $R_g$.
2. **Pile spacing ratio ($s/d$)** — closer spacing increases $R_g$.
3. **Pile slenderness ratio ($L/d$)** — slender piles reduce $R_g$ slightly.
4. **Soil type and modulus profile** — non-homogeneous soils affect $R_g$.
5. **Pile-soil stiffness ratio ($E_p/E_s$)** — stiffer piles marginally reduce $R_g$.

> [!NOTE]
> Typical values: $R_g \approx 1.5$ (2-pile group at $s/d = 6$), $\approx 3$ to $4$ for $3 \times 3$ groups at $s/d = 3$, and $\approx 6$ to $8$ for large groups at close spacing.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule:** Module 2 long questions carry 14 marks and MUST offer internal choice between two completely independent questions. Question A and Question B below satisfy this rule. Each is divided into sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Equivalent Raft Numerical**

A **3 × 3 pile group** of **12 m long, 400 mm diameter bored cast-in-situ piles** is spaced at **3 d centre-to-centre** in a sandy soil deposit. The pile cap carries a column load of **4000 kN**. The sand has the following properties: $E_s = 25\,\text{MPa}$, $\mu_s = 0.3$, $\phi = 32°$.

**(a)** Calculate the settlement of the pile group using the **Equivalent Raft Method**.

**(b)** If the IS 2911 permissible settlement is 25 mm, comment on the adequacy of the design. Suggest two engineering remedies if the design is inadequate.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $d = 0.4$ m, $L = 12$ m, $s = 3d = 1.2$ m
- $Q_g = 4000$ kN, $E_s = 25{,}000$ kPa, $\mu_s = 0.3$
- $n_r = n_c = 3$

**Step 1: Equivalent raft width and length**

$$B_e = (n_r - 1)\,s + d = (3-1)(1.2) + 0.4 = 2.4 + 0.4 = 2.8 \;\text{m}$$

$$L_e = (n_c - 1)\,s + d = (3-1)(1.2) + 0.4 = 2.8 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 2: Depth of equivalent raft**

$$D_e = \frac{2L}{3} = \frac{2 \times 12}{3} = 8.0 \;\text{m}$$

**Step 3: Net contact pressure**

$$q = \frac{Q_g}{B_e \cdot L_e} = \frac{4000}{2.8 \times 2.8} = \frac{4000}{7.84} = 510.2 \;\text{kPa}$$

**Step 4: Shape and embedment ratios**

$$m = \frac{L_e}{B_e} = 1.0, \qquad n' = \frac{D_e}{B_e} = \frac{8.0}{2.8} = 2.857$$

**Step 5: Steinbrenner influence factors**

For $m = 1.0$ and $n' = 2.86$:
- $I_s \approx 0.82$
- $I_w \approx 0.46$
- Product: $I_s \cdot I_w \approx 0.82 \times 0.46 = 0.377$

**Step 6: Settlement**

$$S_e = \frac{q \cdot B_e \cdot (1 - \mu_s^2)\,I_s\,I_w}{E_s}$$

$$S_e = \frac{(510.2)(2.8)(1 - 0.09)(0.377)}{25{,}000}$$

$$S_e = \frac{510.2 \times 2.8 \times 0.91 \times 0.377}{25{,}000} = \frac{489.86}{25{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{S_e \approx 0.0196 \;\text{m} = 19.6 \;\text{mm}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Adequacy Check:**
Calculated $S_g = 19.6\,\text{mm} < 25\,\text{mm}$ (IS 2911 limit). The design is **adequate**. ✅

**Two Engineering Remedies if Inadequate:**
1. **Increase pile length ($L$):** Increasing $L$ from 12 m to 18 m lowers the $D_e/B_e$ ratio and reduces $I_s \cdot I_w$, decreasing settlement by approximately 25–30 %.
2. **Increase pile diameter ($d$):** A larger $d$ enlarges $B_e$ but *increases group stiffness*, distributing the load over a wider area and reducing $q$ — typically cuts settlement by 15–20 %.
3. **(Bonus)** Increase pile spacing to reduce $R_g$ in Poulos-Davis approach.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Poulos-Davis Numerical**

A **4 × 4 pile group** of **16 m long, 500 mm diameter driven precast concrete piles** is spaced at **2 d centre-to-centre**. The pile cap carries a column load of **8000 kN**. From Poulos-Davis charts for the configuration: $I_\rho = 0.022$, $R_g = 5.6$. Soil properties: $E_s = 22\,\text{MPa}$, $\mu_s = 0.3$.

**(a)** Calculate the settlement of a *single pile* under the average working load.

**(b)** Hence calculate the settlement of the *pile group* and comment on its adequacy as per IS 2911 limits for sandy soils.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $d = 0.5$ m, $L = 16$ m, $s = 2d = 1.0$ m
- $n = 4 \times 4 = 16$ piles, $Q_g = 8000$ kN
- $E_s = 22{,}000$ kPa, $\mu_s = 0.3$
- $I_\rho = 0.022$ (given)

**Step 1: Average load per pile**

$$Q_i = \frac{Q_g}{n} = \frac{8000}{16} = 500 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 2: Settlement of single pile (Poulos elastic solution)**

$$S_i = \frac{Q_i \cdot I_\rho}{E_s \cdot d} = \frac{500 \times 0.022}{22{,}000 \times 0.5}$$

$$S_i = \frac{11.0}{11{,}000} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{S_i = 1.0 \;\text{mm}}$$

**Interpretation:** A single isolated pile settles only 1 mm under its 500 kN share, but its neighbours, by reinforcing the stress zone, will cause the *group* to settle 5.6 times more.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1: Apply settlement ratio**

$$S_g = R_g \cdot S_i = 5.6 \times 1.0 = 5.6 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 2: IS 2911 Limit Check**

- Permissible settlement for isolated pile cap in sand = **25 mm**.
- Computed $S_g = 5.6$ mm $\ll$ 25 mm.
- **Design is SAFE and conservative.** ✅

**Discussion:**
The very low settlement (5.6 mm) reflects:
- Low $I_\rho$ typical of *long slender driven piles* ($L/d = 32$).
- Strong soil ($E_s = 22$ MPa = medium dense sand).
- The Poulos-Davis method assumes purely elastic response and ignores cyclic / dynamic effects.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse $S_i$ with $Q_i$!** Many students invert the formula and divide instead of multiply. Check units: $Q_i$ in kN, $E_s$ in kPa, $d$ in m → result in metres.
> 2. **Do NOT add $S_i$ for all piles.** Settlement of *n* piles is NOT $n \cdot S_i$. That is the classic mistake — group settlement uses $R_g$, not summation.
> 3. **Always check units** before substituting: MPa vs kPa is a 1000× error trap.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist — Master These Before the Exam:**

- ✅ Settlement of pile groups in sand is governed by **elastic compression** (no consolidation).
- ✅ Two principal methods: **Equivalent Raft (Meyerhof)** and **Poulos-Davis Superposition**.
- ✅ Equivalent raft depth in sand = **$2L/3$** below ground; in clay = $2L/3$ also, but with consolidation analysis.
- ✅ Equivalent raft dimensions: $B_e = (n_r - 1)\,s + d$ and $L_e = (n_c - 1)\,s + d$.
- ✅ Steinbrenner settlement uses $(1 - \mu_s^2)$ factor with $I_s$ (shape) and $I_w$ (depth).
- ✅ Poulos single-pile settlement formula: $S_i = Q_i \cdot I_\rho \, / \, (E_s \cdot d)$.
- ✅ Group settlement: $S_g = R_g \cdot S_i$, where $R_g$ is read from charts.
- ✅ Typical $R_g$ values: 2-pile group ≈ 1.5; 3×3 group ≈ 3–4; 4×4 group ≈ 5–6.
- ✅ IS 2911 limit: 25 mm for isolated pile caps in sand; 40 mm for pile-raft foundations.
- ✅ Always **adopt the larger $S_g$** from the two methods for safe design.
- ✅ Common KTU trap: confusing **efficiency** (capacity) with **settlement ratio** — they are independent quantities.
- ✅ Influence factors ($I_s$, $I_w$, $I_\rho$) are dimensionless — never carry units.
- ✅ Units check: $E_s$ in kPa, $d$ in m, $Q_i$ in kN → $S$ comes out in metres (convert to mm at the end).
- ✅ For pile groups in *cohesionless* soil, ignore time-dependent settlement; report only immediate elastic settlement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Skempton's Method
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Skempton's Method

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Skempton's Method** is an empirical semi-analytical procedure used in geotechnical engineering to determine the **net ultimate bearing capacity (q_u,net)** of **shallow foundations** resting on **saturated cohesive soils (clays)** under **undrained loading conditions (φ = 0° analysis)**. The method was developed by A. W. Skempton (1951) by correlating theoretical bearing capacity theory with extensive field plate-load test data for clays.

The governing equation is:

$$q_{u,\text{net}} = c \cdot N_c$$

where **c** is the **undrained cohesion** of the clay and **N_c** is Skempton's modified bearing capacity factor incorporating **shape** and **depth** effects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Skempton's method is the **preferred** approach for clays in this course because it accounts for the field-observed fact that the theoretical N_c = 5.14 (for strip footing) underestimates real capacities at depth. Skempton proposed a practical, empirically adjusted value of **N_c = 5.0** at the surface, scaled upward by shape and depth factors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine pushing your thumb slowly into a slab of soft butter (representing a clay deposit). The resistance you feel is governed purely by the butter's "stickiness" (its **cohesion c**), not by friction between grains. Now imagine pressing down on a **square coaster** vs a **long rectangular ruler** — the shape of the object changes how the butter flows around it. Finally, if the coaster is **buried at some depth** below the butter surface (rather than pressed on top), the surrounding butter provides extra confinement, and the system becomes stronger.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Skempton's method says — *"For clays, the soil's bearing strength depends on (1) its stickiness 'c', (2) a small bonus for being long-and-narrow vs square, and (3) a big bonus if the foundation is buried deep."*

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Parameters

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Units | Significance |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| **c** | Undrained cohesion | kN/m² or kPa | Shear strength of clay under quick loading |
| **B** | Footing width | m | Smaller plan dimension |
| **L** | Footing length | m | Larger plan dimension (L ≥ B) |
| **D_f** | Depth of foundation | m | Measured from ground surface |
| **N_c** | Bearing capacity factor | dimensionless | Empirical function of shape and depth |
| **q_ult** | Ultimate bearing capacity | kN/m² | Maximum stress soil can carry |
| **q_allow** | Allowable bearing pressure | kN/m² | q_ult / Factor of Safety |
| **FOS** | Factor of Safety | dimensionless | Typically **3.0** for clays |

> [!TIP]
> The **Unit Weight γ** of clay does **NOT appear** in the net bearing capacity expression because in the φ = 0 analysis, the overburden pressure is subtracted (i.e., *net* capacity is computed). The surcharge equivalent is absorbed into Skempton's depth factor.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Skempton's Method

The **Terzaghi bearing capacity equation** (1943) for the φ = 0 case gives a **theoretical N_c** of **5.14** for a strip footing at the surface. However, field observations consistently showed that:

1. The actual N_c is slightly **lower than 5.14** at the surface → Skempton rounded to **5.0**.
2. N_c **increases with depth** of embedment (D_f/B), contradicting the simple theory.
3. N_c is **shape-dependent**: square and circular footings carry more load per unit area than strip footings.

Skempton back-fitted these observations into a compact, practical formula widely accepted in foundation design.

## 2.2 Skempton's Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity Equation

For a **rectangular footing of width B and length L** at **depth D_f** in a saturated clay (φ_u = 0):

$$q_{u,\text{net}} = c \cdot N_c$$

where the modified bearing capacity factor is:

$$N_c = 5.0 \, \left(1 + 0.2 \, \frac{B}{L}\right) \left(1 + 0.2 \, \frac{D_f}{B}\right) \quad \text{for} \quad 0 \le \frac{D_f}{B} \le 2.5$$

### 2.2.1 Special Cases Built into the Formula
