| Footing Type | B / L | N_c at Surface (D_f / B = 0) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Strip** (wall footing) | B/L → 0 | **5.0** |
| **Square** | B/L = 1 | **6.0** |
| **Circular** | B/L ≈ 1 (equivalent) | **6.0** |
| **Rectangular** (B/L = 0.5) | 0.5 | **5.5** |

### 2.2.2 Depth Factor Refinement (Extended Range)

When **D_f / B > 1**, some references (Meyerhof & Hansen) use a slightly different depth factor:

$$N_c = 5.0 \, \left(1 + 0.2 \, \frac{B}{L}\right) \left(1 + 0.4 \, \tan^{-1}\!\frac{D_f}{B}\right) \quad \text{for} \quad 1 < \frac{D_f}{B} \le 2.5$$

> [!NOTE]
> The depth factor saturates (approaches a maximum) for **D_f / B > 2.5** because the failure zone no longer extends all the way to the ground surface.

## 2.3 Allowable Bearing Pressure (Net)

$$q_{\text{all,net}} = \frac{q_{u,\text{net}}}{\text{FOS}}$$

For KTU design problems on clays, **FOS = 3.0** is standard.

## 2.4 Gross Ultimate Bearing Capacity

$$q_{u,\text{gross}} = q_{u,\text{net}} + \gamma \cdot D_f$$

where **γ** is the unit weight of soil above the footing base. This form is used when you need the **gross** capacity (i.e., the total stress at the base including overburden relief).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $q_{u,\text{net}} = c \cdot N_c$ | Skempton's net ultimate B.C. | kN/m² |
| 2 | $N_c = 5.0 \left(1 + 0.2 \tfrac{B}{L}\right) \left(1 + 0.2 \tfrac{D_f}{B}\right)$ | Full factor (D_f/B ≤ 2.5) | — |
| 3 | $N_c = 5.0 \left(1 + 0.2 \tfrac{B}{L}\right) \left(1 + 0.4 \tan^{-1}\!\tfrac{D_f}{B}\right)$ | Extended depth (D_f/B > 1) | — |
| 4 | $N_c = 5.0$ | Strip at surface | — |
| 5 | $N_c = 6.0$ | Square / Circular at surface | — |
| 6 | $q_{u,\text{gross}} = q_{u,\text{net}} + \gamma D_f$ | Gross capacity | kN/m² |
| 7 | $q_{\text{all}} = \tfrac{q_{u,\text{net}}}{\text{FOS}}$ | Allowable capacity (FOS = 3 for clay) | kN/m² |
| 8 | $Q_{\text{ult}} = q_{u,\text{net}} \cdot A$ | Ultimate load on footing | kN |
| 9 | $A = B \cdot L$ | Footing plan area | m² |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Skempton's method is the **go-to procedure** for:

- **Bridge pier foundations** in alluvial clay deposits.
- **Building raft foundations** in soft marine clays of Kerala's coastal belt (Kochi, Alappuzha, Kozhikode).
- **Tank farm foundations** in refinery sites built on thick clay layers.
- **Pre-design estimates** of bearing capacity before detailed finite-element or Plaxis 3D analyses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This method is **NOT applicable** to (a) sands, (b) layered soils with a weak layer below the footing, or (c) foundations on slopes. For those, **Meyerhof / Hansen / Vesic** or **numerical methods** are used.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Foundation: The φ = 0 Undrained Analysis

In an undrained (φ_u = 0) triaxial test on saturated clay, the Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope reduces to:

$$\tau_f = c_u$$

where **c_u** is the undrained shear strength. Because the angle of internal friction is zero, the bearing capacity equation collapses to a **single-term** form. The classical Terzaghi equation:

$$q_u = c \cdot N_c \cdot s_c \cdot d_c \cdot i_c + q \cdot N_q \cdot s_q \cdot d_q \cdot i_q + 0.5 \, \gamma B \cdot N_\gamma \cdot s_\gamma \cdot d_\gamma \cdot i_\gamma$$

reduces to (because **N_q = 1.0** and **N_γ = 0** for φ = 0):

$$q_{u,\text{net}} = c \cdot N_c \cdot s_c \cdot d_c$$

## 3.2 Strip Footing Base Value (Why 5.0 and not 5.14?)

Prandtl (1921) derived, for a smooth strip footing:

$$N_c = \pi + 2 = 5.14$$

Skempton's plate-load tests on soft clays (London Clay, etc.) gave **N_c ≈ 4.5 to 5.0** at the surface. To be slightly conservative and round-figure for design, Skempton recommended:

$$N_c^{\text{strip, surface}} = 5.0$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Shape Factor (1 + 0.2 B/L)

For a **rectangular footing** with B < L, Skempton performed statistical regression of plate-load test data for footings of varying B/L ratios. He proposed a **linear empirical correction**:

$$s_c = 1 + 0.2 \, \frac{B}{L}$$

**Reasoning:**
- For a strip footing: B/L → 0, so s_c → 1.0 (no shape effect).
- For a square footing: B/L = 1, so s_c = 1.2.
- This gives N_c = 5.0 × 1.2 = **6.0**, matching field observations on square footings.

$$\boxed{s_c = 1 + 0.2 \, \frac{B}{L}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation of the Depth Factor (1 + 0.2 D_f/B)

As foundation depth D_f increases, the failure zone in the clay is **confined** by the soil above, raising N_c. Skempton found that for **D_f / B ≤ 1**, the increase is approximately linear:

$$d_c = 1 + 0.2 \, \frac{D_f}{B}, \quad \frac{D_f}{B} \le 1$$

For **D_f / B > 1**, the rate of increase slows. Skempton/Meyerhof modification:

$$d_c = 1 + 0.4 \, \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D_f}{B}\right), \quad 1 < \frac{D_f}{B} \le 2.5$$

**Limiting Value:** At D_f / B = 2.5:

$$d_c = 1 + 0.4 \times \tan^{-1}(2.5) = 1 + 0.4 \times 68.2° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} \approx 1 + 0.4 \times 1.19 = 1.476$$

For deeper footings (D_f / B > 2.5), the failure is said to be **local / punching**, and Skempton's formula no longer applies — use deep-foundation (pile) analysis.

$$\boxed{d_c = \begin{cases} 1 + 0.2 \tfrac{D_f}{B}, & 0 \le \tfrac{D_f}{B} \le 1 \\[4pt] 1 + 0.4 \tan^{-1}\!\tfrac{D_f}{B}, & 1 < \tfrac{D_f}{B} \le 2.5 \end{cases}}$$

## 3.5 Full Skempton's Equation (Combining the Factors)

Multiplying the three empirical pieces:

$$N_c = N_{c,\text{strip}}^{\text{surface}} \cdot s_c \cdot d_c$$

$$\boxed{N_c = 5.0 \left(1 + 0.2 \, \frac{B}{L}\right) \left(1 + 0.2 \, \frac{D_f}{B}\right), \quad \frac{D_f}{B} \le 2.5}$$

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem (KTU-style):** A square footing 2 m × 2 m is to be placed at a depth of 1.5 m in a saturated clay with undrained cohesion c = 80 kN/m² and unit weight γ = 18 kN/m³. Determine (a) net ultimate bearing capacity, (b) gross ultimate bearing capacity, (c) safe load with FOS = 3.

**Given:** B = 2 m, L = 2 m, D_f = 1.5 m, c = 80 kN/m², γ = 18 kN/m³, FOS = 3.

### Step 1: Compute the Geometry Ratios

$$\frac{B}{L} = \frac{2}{2} = 1.0$$

$$\frac{D_f}{B} = \frac{1.5}{2} = 0.75 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Use depth factor } d_c = 1 + 0.2 \frac{D_f}{B}$$

### Step 2: Compute Shape Factor

$$s_c = 1 + 0.2 \left(\frac{B}{L}\right) = 1 + 0.2 (1.0) = 1.2$$

### Step 3: Compute Depth Factor

$$d_c = 1 + 0.2 \left(\frac{D_f}{B}\right) = 1 + 0.2 (0.75) = 1 + 0.15 = 1.15$$

### Step 4: Compute N_c

$$N_c = 5.0 \times s_c \times d_c = 5.0 \times 1.2 \times 1.15$$

$$N_c = 5.0 \times 1.380 = 6.90$$

### Step 5: Compute Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity

$$q_{u,\text{net}} = c \cdot N_c = 80 \times 6.90 = 552 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

### Step 6: Compute Gross Ultimate Bearing Capacity

$$q_{u,\text{gross}} = q_{u,\text{net}} + \gamma D_f = 552 + (18)(1.5)$$

$$q_{u,\text{gross}} = 552 + 27 = 579 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

### Step 7: Compute Allowable Bearing Pressure (Net)

$$q_{\text{all,net}} = \frac{q_{u,\text{net}}}{\text{FOS}} = \frac{552}{3} = 184 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

### Step 8: Compute Safe Load on Footing

$$Q_{\text{safe}} = q_{\text{all,net}} \cdot A = 184 \times (2 \times 2) = 184 \times 4 = 736 \; \text{kN}$$

### Final Answer Summary

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Net ultimate B.C. | q_u,net | **552 kN/m²** |
| Gross ultimate B.C. | q_u,gross | **579 kN/m²** |
| Net allowable B.C. | q_all,net | **184 kN/m²** |
| Safe load on footing | Q_safe | **736 kN** |

## 3.7 Sensitivity Analysis Matrix (How N_c Varies with Geometry)

| B/L | D_f/B | N_c | q_u,net (c = 50 kN/m²) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 (strip) | 0.0 | 5.00 | 250 kN/m² |
| 0 (strip) | 0.5 | 5.50 | 275 kN/m² |
| 0 (strip) | 1.0 | 6.00 | 300 kN/m² |
| 0.5 | 1.0 | 6.60 | 330 kN/m² |
| 1.0 (square) | 0.0 | 6.00 | 300 kN/m² |
| 1.0 (square) | 1.0 | 7.20 | 360 kN/m² |
| 1.0 (square) | 2.0 | 7.74 | 387 kN/m² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above sensitivity table is a **high-yield recap** — KTU examiners often test whether a student understands that **deeper and squarer** footings carry more load per unit area in clays.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Methodology Flow Chart (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Design a Shallow Foundation in Clay] --> B{Is the soil a SATURATED CLAY?}
    B -- No --> Z[Use Terzaghi/Meyerhof/Hansen Method]
    B -- Yes --> C[Determine Undrained Cohesion c from UU Triaxial Test]
    C --> D[Note Footing Geometry: B, L, D_f]
    D --> E[Compute Ratios: B/L and D_f/B]
    E --> F{Is D_f / B ≤ 2.5 ?}
    F -- No --> X[Punching Shear Failure - Use Deep Foundation Methods]
    F -- Yes --> G[Compute Shape Factor: s_c = 1 + 0.2 B/L]
    G --> H{D_f / B ≤ 1 ?}
    H -- Yes --> I1[Depth Factor: d_c = 1 + 0.2 D_f/B]
    H -- No --> I2[Depth Factor: d_c = 1 + 0.4 arctan D_f/B]
    I1 --> J[Compute N_c = 5.0 × s_c × d_c]
    I2 --> J
    J --> K[Compute q_u,net = c × N_c]
    K --> L[Compute q_u,gross = q_u,net + γ D_f]
    L --> M[Apply FOS = 3 for Clays]
    M --> N[Compute q_all = q_u,net / FOS]
    N --> O[Compute Safe Load: Q = q_all × B × L]
    O --> P[End: Foundation Design Verified]
```

## 4.2 Failure Mode Comparison Diagram (Skempton's Zones vs. Footing Depth)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[General Shear Failure] -->|D_f/B ≤ 1| A1[Triangular + Radial Zones]
    A1 --> A2[Skempton Formula Applicable]
    B[Local Shear Failure] -->|1 < D_f/B ≤ 2.5| B1[Modified Zones]
    B1 --> B2[Use Modified Depth Factor]
    C[Punching Shear Failure] -->|D_f/B > 2.5| C1[Soil Pushes Vertically Down]
    C1 --> C2[Skempton Formula NOT Applicable]
```

## 4.3 Footing Type vs. N_c at Surface (Geometric Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Strip Footing B/L = 0] -->|N_c = 5.0| A1[Wall Footing under Long Building]
    A2[Rectangular B/L = 0.5] -->|N_c = 5.5| A3[Isolated Column Footing]
    A4[Square B/L = 1.0] -->|N_c = 6.0| A5[Isolated Column or Tower Footing]
    A6[Circular equivalent] -->|N_c ≈ 6.0| A7[Storage Tank Foundation]
```

## 4.4 Influence of Embedment Depth on N_c (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[D_f = 0 Surface Footing] -->|D_f/B = 0| N1[N_c = 5.0 s_c]
    D1[D_f = 0.5B Shallow] -->|D_f/B = 0.5| N2[N_c = 5.5 s_c]
    D2[D_f = 1.0B Normal] -->|D_f/B = 1.0| N3[N_c = 6.0 s_c]
    D3[D_f = 2.0B Deep] -->|D_f/B = 2.0| N4[N_c ≈ 7.2 s_c]
    D4[D_f = 2.5B Limit] -->|D_f/B = 2.5| N5[N_c ≈ 7.4 s_c]
    D5[D_f > 2.5B Very Deep] -->|Punching Shear| N6[Skempton NOT Valid - Use Piles]
```

> [!TIP]
> The block diagrams above map the **decision logic** an engineer follows. Notice how the depth of embedment D_f is the **single most powerful lever** for increasing bearing capacity in clays — a 1 m increase in depth on a 2 m footing boosts N_c by 10%.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: State Skempton's bearing capacity equation for a shallow foundation on saturated clay. List the key assumptions.**

**Model Answer:**

Skempton's equation for the net ultimate bearing capacity of a shallow foundation on a saturated clay deposit (undrained, φ_u = 0 condition) is:

$$q_{u,\text{net}} = c \cdot N_c = 5.0 \, c \left(1 + 0.2 \, \frac{B}{L}\right) \left(1 + 0.2 \, \frac{D_f}{B}\right)$$

**Key Assumptions:**
1. The soil is **saturated** and loaded **rapidly** (undrained, φ = 0).
2. The failure surface is **general shear** (D_f / B ≤ 2.5).
3. The soil is **homogeneous and isotropic** to a depth of at least **1.5 B** below the footing.
4. The water table is **at or below the base** of the foundation (so γ above the base is the moist/dry unit weight, not the submerged unit weight).
5. The footing base is **rough** (shear stresses mobilize at the base).

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner expects the student to **explicitly state the 5.0 constant and the two empirical correction factors (1 + 0.2 B/L) and (1 + 0.2 D_f/B)**. Missing the constant 5.0 leads to 1-mark deduction.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Distinguish between the depth factor expressions used in Skempton's method for (a) D_f/B ≤ 1 and (b) 1 < D_f/B ≤ 2.5.**

**Model Answer:**

| Range of D_f / B | Depth Factor d_c | Reason for Choice |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **0 ≤ D_f/B ≤ 1** | $d_c = 1 + 0.2 \, \frac{D_f}{B}$ | Failure surface reaches ground surface; **linear** rise in N_c with depth (from Skempton's plate-load regressions). |
| **1 < D_f/B ≤ 2.5** | $d_c = 1 + 0.4 \, \tan^{-1}\!\frac{D_f}{B}$ | Failure surface is **confined**; the rate of N_c growth **slows** and is **non-linear** (S-shape, asymptotically approaches a limit). |

For D_f / B > 2.5, **punching shear** dominates and Skempton's formula is **not valid**.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Module Choice Questions

> **Module Internal Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Answer ANY ONE of Question A or Question B in full. Each question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A rectangular footing **3 m × 4 m** (B × L) is to be founded at a depth of **1.8 m** in a saturated clay with undrained cohesion **c = 90 kN/m²** and unit weight **γ = 19 kN/m³**. Using Skempton's method, determine:

#### (a) The net and gross ultimate bearing capacity of the footing. (7 Marks)

#### (b) The net allowable bearing pressure and the safe load the footing can carry, using FOS = 3. (7 Marks)

#### 🔍 MODEL SOLUTION (Question A)

**Given:** B = 3 m, L = 4 m, D_f = 1.8 m, c = 90 kN/m², γ = 19 kN/m³, FOS = 3.

**Step 1 — Compute Geometry Ratios:**

$$\frac{B}{L} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75$$

$$\frac{D_f}{B} = \frac{1.8}{3} = 0.60$$

Since D_f / B = 0.60 < 1, we use the **linear** depth factor formula.

**Step 2 — Compute Shape Factor:**

$$s_c = 1 + 0.2 \times \frac{B}{L} = 1 + 0.2 (0.75) = 1 + 0.15 = 1.15$$

**Step 3 — Compute Depth Factor:**

$$d_c = 1 + 0.2 \times \frac{D_f}{B} = 1 + 0.2 (0.60) = 1 + 0.12 = 1.12$$

**Step 4 — Compute Skempton's N_c:**

$$N_c = 5.0 \times s_c \times d_c = 5.0 \times 1.15 \times 1.12$$

$$N_c = 5.0 \times 1.288 = 6.44$$

**Step 5 — Compute Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity (Part a answer):**

$$q_{u,\text{net}} = c \cdot N_c = 90 \times 6.44 = 579.6 \; \text{kN/m}^2 \approx \mathbf{580 \; kN/m^2}$$

**Step 6 — Compute Gross Ultimate Bearing Capacity (Part a answer):**

$$q_{u,\text{gross}} = q_{u,\text{net}} + \gamma \cdot D_f = 579.6 + 19 \times 1.8$$

$$q_{u,\text{gross}} = 579.6 + 34.2 = \mathbf{613.8 \; kN/m^2}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Net Allowable Bearing Pressure (Part b answer):**

$$q_{\text{all,net}} = \frac{q_{u,\text{net}}}{\text{FOS}} = \frac{579.6}{3} = \mathbf{193.2 \; kN/m^2}$$

**Step 8 — Compute Safe Load on Footing (Part b answer):**

$$A = B \times L = 3 \times 4 = 12 \; \text{m}^2$$

$$Q_{\text{safe}} = q_{\text{all,net}} \times A = 193.2 \times 12 = \mathbf{2318.4 \; kN} \approx \mathbf{2318 \; kN}$$

#### ✅ Final Answer Table (Question A)

| Quantity | Value |
|:---|:---:|
| N_c | **6.44** |
| q_u,net | **579.6 kN/m²** |
| q_u,gross | **613.8 kN/m²** |
| q_all,net | **193.2 kN/m²** |
| Q_safe | **2318.4 kN** |

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A square footing **2.5 m × 2.5 m** is to be placed at a depth of **2.0 m** in a soft saturated clay with undrained cohesion **c = 60 kN/m²** and unit weight **γ = 17 kN/m³**. Using Skempton's method, answer:

#### (a) Calculate the net ultimate and net allowable bearing capacity (FOS = 3). Comment on whether the design is safe if the column imposes a working load of 1200 kN. (7 Marks)

#### (b) If the depth of foundation is increased to 3.5 m, recalculate the new bearing capacity and comment on the increase. (7 Marks)

#### 🔍 MODEL SOLUTION (Question B)

**Given:** B = L = 2.5 m, c = 60 kN/m², γ = 17 kN/m³, FOS = 3, Q_working = 1200 kN.

---

#### Part (a) — D_f = 2.0 m

**Step 1 — Compute Ratios:**

$$\frac{B}{L} = \frac{2.5}{2.5} = 1.0, \quad \frac{D_f}{B} = \frac{2.0}{2.5} = 0.8$$

Since D_f / B = 0.8 < 1, use the **linear** depth factor.

**Step 2 — Compute Factors:**

$$s_c = 1 + 0.2 (1.0) = 1.2$$

$$d_c = 1 + 0.2 (0.8) = 1 + 0.16 = 1.16$$

**Step 3 — Compute N_c:**

$$N_c = 5.0 \times 1.2 \times 1.16 = 5.0 \times 1.392 = 6.96$$

**Step 4 — Compute Net Ultimate B.C.:**

$$q_{u,\text{net}} = 60 \times 6.96 = 417.6 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Compute Allowable B.C. and Check Working Load:**

$$q_{\text{all,net}} = \frac{417.6}{3} = 139.2 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

$$Q_{\text{safe}} = q_{\text{all,net}} \times A = 139.2 \times (2.5 \times 2.5) = 139.2 \times 6.25 = 870 \; \text{kN}$$

Since **Q_safe = 870 kN < Q_working = 1200 kN**, the design is **NOT safe**. The footing must be enlarged, or the soil improved.

---

#### Part (b) — D_f = 3.5 m

**Step 1 — Recompute Ratio:**

$$\frac{D_f}{B} = \frac{3.5}{2.5} = 1.4$$

Since D_f / B = 1.4 > 1, use the **arctangent** depth factor.

**Step 2 — Compute Depth Factor (using arctan):**

$$d_c = 1 + 0.4 \times \tan^{-1}(1.4) = 1 + 0.4 \times (54.46° \times \frac{\pi}{180°})$$

$$d_c = 1 + 0.4 \times 0.9505 = 1 + 0.380 = 1.380$$

**Step 3 — Compute New N_c:**

$$N_c = 5.0 \times 1.2 \times 1.380 = 5.0 \times 1.656 = 8.28$$

**Step 4 — Compute New Net Ultimate B.C.:**

$$q_{u,\text{net}} = 60 \times 8.28 = 496.8 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — New Allowable B.C. and Safe Load:**

$$q_{\text{all,net}} = \frac{496.8}{3} = 165.6 \; \text{kN/m}^2$$

$$Q_{\text{safe}} = 165.6 \times 6.25 = 1035 \; \text{kN}$$

**Step 6 — Compare:**

$$\text{Increase in } q_{u,\text{net}} = \frac{496.8 - 417.6}{417.6} \times 100\% \approx 19\%$$

$$\text{Increase in } Q_{\text{safe}} = 1035 - 870 = 165 \; \text{kN} \; (\approx 19\% \text{ increase})$$

#### ✅ Final Answer Table (Question B)

| Quantity | Part (a): D_f = 2.0 m | Part (b): D_f = 3.5 m |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| D_f / B | 0.8 | 1.4 |
| Depth factor used | 1 + 0.2 D_f/B | 1 + 0.4 arctan(D_f/B) |
| s_c | 1.2 | 1.2 |
| d_c | 1.16 | 1.380 |
| N_c | **6.96** | **8.28** |
| q_u,net | **417.6 kN/m²** | **496.8 kN/m²** |
| q_all,net | **139.2 kN/m²** | **165.6 kN/m²** |
| Q_safe | **870 kN** (unsafe) | **1035 kN** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use the correct depth-factor formula** when D_f / B > 1 (must use arctan form). 1–2 marks lost.
> 2. **Mixing up B and L** — remember B is the **smaller** plan dimension. If you swap them, your shape factor will be wrong. Up to 3 marks lost.
> 3. **Not adding γ·D_f** when gross capacity is asked. 1 mark lost.
> 4. **Forgetting FOS = 3** is the KTU default for clays (not 2.5, not 4). 1 mark lost.
> 5. **Applying Skempton's formula beyond D_f/B = 2.5** (punching shear zone). Full mark deduction if used inappropriately.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ Skempton's method applies **only to saturated clays** (φ = 0) under **undrained** loading.
- ✅ The **core equation** is **q_u,net = c · N_c**.
- ✅ The base value is **N_c = 5.0** (NOT 5.14) — Skempton rounded down from theory.
- ✅ **Shape factor** = 1 + 0.2 (B/L) → equals 1.0 for strips, 1.2 for squares.
- ✅ **Depth factor (Case 1, D_f/B ≤ 1)** = 1 + 0.2 (D_f/B) — linear.
- ✅ **Depth factor (Case 2, D_f/B > 1)** = 1 + 0.4 · arctan(D_f/B) — non-linear.
- ✅ **Validity range**: D_f / B ≤ 2.5 (beyond this → punching shear → use pile formulae).
- ✅ **Factor of Safety for clays** in KTU problems = **3.0** (typical).
- ✅ **B is the smaller** plan dimension; L is the longer one. B/L ≤ 1.
- ✅ **Net capacity** excludes the γ·D_f term; **Gross capacity** includes it.
- ✅ N_c **increases** with both footing depth and "squareness" of the footing.
- ✅ For sands or layered soils, switch to **Meyerhof / Hansen / Vesic** or numerical methods.
- ✅ Always **show intermediate steps** in KTU answer sheets — partial marks are awarded liberally.
- ✅ Use **arctan in radians** when computing the extended depth factor (1 + 0.4 arctan).
- ✅ Remember: in Skempton's method, **γ of clay above the footing base does NOT appear in the net capacity** — it only enters when computing **gross** capacity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Meyerhof's Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Meyerhof's Method # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) **Meyerhof's Bearing Capacity Method (1963)** is a generalized theory for computing the ultimate bearing capacity of shallow foundations subjected to centric, eccentric, and inclined loads. It presents a unified bearing capacity equation that accounts for the **shape of the foundation (Shape Factors)**, the **depth of embedment (Depth Factors)**, and the **inclination of the applied load (Inclination Factors)**, thereby providing a more realistic estimate of the failure mechanism beneath a footing compared to the classical Terzaghi (1943) approach. The general form of Meyerhof's ultimate bearing capacity equation is: $$q_{ult} = c'N_c F_{cs} F_{cd} F_{ci} + qN_q F_{qs} F_{qd} F_{qi} + 0.5\gamma B N_{\gamma} F_{\gamma s} F_{\gamma d} F_{\gamma i}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Distinction from Terzaghi:** Terzaghi assumed the soil above the base of the footing does not contribute to shear strength and acts only as a surcharge (q = γD). Meyerhof's revolutionary contribution was recognizing that the **soil between the footing base and the ground surface** also mobilizes passive resistance and actively participates in the failure wedge, making the failure surface more realistic and curved up to the ground surface. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are trying to **push a rectangular wooden block** flush with the surface of a sand bed using a tilted stick. - **Terzaghi's View:** Only the soil *directly underneath* the block resists the push; the sand above the base of the block is just "dead weight." - **Meyerhof's View:** The sand *above and around* the block also pushes back. If the stick is tilted, the failure surface curves all the way up to the surface of the sand, creating a continuous "bulge" of failure. Think of it like this: - A **plunger on a tub of butter** (Terzaghi): only the column directly below resists. - A **ramp of soil piling up** (Meyerhof): the soil beside and above the footing joins forces to resist the load, so the block effectively sits inside a pocket of resisting earth. > [!NOTE] > The physical constants required for this method are: > - **c′** = effective cohesion of soil (kN/m²) > - **φ′** = effective angle of internal friction (degrees) > - **γ** = unit weight of soil (kN/m³) > - **B, L** = width and length of footing (m) > - **D_f** = depth of foundation (m) > - **β** = inclination of load with vertical (degrees) ## 1.3 Why Meyerhof's Method is Preferred in KTU Curriculum | Feature | Terzaghi (1943) | Meyerhof (1963) | |---|---|---| | Failure surface | Simplified, ends at footing base | Curved, extends to ground surface | | Soil above base | Surcharge only | Contributes shear strength | | Shape, Depth, Inclination factors | Separate cases | Unified single equation | | Eccentric loading | Not addressed | Addressed (effective width concept) | | Inclined loads | Not addressed | Addressed via inclination factors | | **KTU 2024 Exam relevance** | Often asked in Part A | **High weightage in Part B (14 marks)** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation of Meyerhof's Method Meyerhof (1963) proposed that the ultimate bearing capacity of a strip footing can be expressed as a superposition of three components, each representing a physical resistance mechanism: 1. **Cohesion Term (c′N_c):** Resistance due to the cohesive/shearing strength of the soil beneath the footing. 2. **Surcharge Term (qN_q):** Resistance offered by the surcharge (overburden pressure q = γ·D_f) and the frictional resistance along the failure surface. 3. **Self-weight Term (0.5γBN_γ):** Resistance derived from the weight of the soil in the failure zone and the frictional properties of the soil. ### 2.1.1 The Underlying Failure Mechanism (Meyerhof's Conceptual Model) Meyerhof visualized the failure surface as comprising **three distinct zones**: - **Zone I (Active Zone / Wedge):** An elastic wedge directly beneath the footing, bounded by a curve that makes an angle ψ with the horizontal. - **Zone II (Radial Shear Zone):** A radial shear zone where the slip lines are logarithmic spirals. - **Zone III (Passive Zone):** A passive zone where the soil is pushed upward and outward, emerging at the ground surface. > [!IMPORTANT] > The angle ψ (angle of the failure wedge with horizontal) is related to φ by: **ψ = 45° + φ/2** (analogous to Rankine active wedge). ## 2.2 Bearing Capacity Factors (N_c, N_q, N_γ) These are the **fundamental bearing capacity factors** that depend only on the angle of internal friction φ. $$N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \cdot \tan^2\left(45 + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$ $$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$$ $$N_{\gamma} = (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi)$$ > [!NOTE] > Meyerhof's N_γ formula uses the empirical factor **1.4φ** (instead of 1.5φ used by Caquot-Kerisel and later by Vesic). This is a frequently tested numerical fact in KTU exams. ### Tabulated Values (Memorize these for KTU exams) | φ (°) | N_c | N_q | N_γ | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 5.14 | 1.00 | 0.00 | | 10 | 8.34 | 2.47 | 1.20 | | 20 | 14.83 | 6.40 | 4.90 | | 25 | 20.72 | 10.66 | 10.88 | | 30 | 30.14 | 18.40 | 22.40 | | 35 | 46.12 | 33.30 | 48.03 | | 40 | 75.31 | 64.20 | 109.41 | | 45 | 133.87 | 134.87 | 271.76 | ## 2.3 Shape Factors (F_cs, F_qs, F_γs) Shape factors account for the difference between a strip footing (B/L = 0) and rectangular/square footings (B/L > 0). $$F_{cs} = 1 + \frac{B}{L} \cdot \frac{N_q}{N_c}$$ $$F_{qs} = 1 + \frac{B}{L} \cdot \tan\phi$$ $$F_{\gamma s} = 1 - 0.4 \cdot \frac{B}{L}$$ > [!NOTE] > For a **strip footing** (B/L → 0): All shape factors become 1.0 > For a **square footing** (B = L): Maximum modification occurs. ## 2.4 Depth Factors (F_cd, F_qd, F_γd) Depth factors account for the increased shearing resistance along the failure surface due to the soil above the footing base. ### Case 1: When D_f / B ≤ 1 (Shallow Embedment) $$F_{cd} = 1 + 0.4 \cdot \frac{D_f}{B}$$ $$F_{qd} = 1 + 0.4 \cdot \frac{D_f}{B}$$ $$F_{\gamma d} = 1.0$$ ### Case 2: When D_f / B > 1 (Deep Embedment) $$F_{cd} = 1 + 0.4 \cdot \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{D_f}{B}\right)$$ $$F_{qd} = 1 + 0.4 \cdot \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{D_f}{B}\right)$$ $$F_{\gamma d} = 1.0$$ > [!WARNING] > **Critical KTU Exam Pitfall:** The argument of the inverse tangent (tan⁻¹) must be in **radians**, not degrees. Many students lose marks by computing in degrees. Always convert using π/180 multiplier if your calculator is in degree mode. ## 2.5 Inclination Factors (F_ci, F_qi, F_γi) These factors account for the effect of inclined loads on bearing capacity. β is the inclination of the load with respect to the vertical. $$F_{ci} = F_{qi} = \left(1 - \frac{\beta}{90}\right)^2$$ $$F_{\gamma i} = \left(1 - \frac{\beta}{\phi}\right)^2$$ > [!NOTE] > When β = 0 (vertical load): All inclination factors equal 1.0 > When β = φ: F_γi = 0 (no frictional resistance available) ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Applicable Range | |---|---|---|---| | Surcharge factor | N_q | $e^{\pi \tan\phi} \tan^2(45 + \phi/2)$ | All φ | | Cohesion factor | N_c | $(N_q - 1)\cot\phi$ | φ > 0 (use 5.14 if φ=0) | | Self-weight factor | N_γ | $(N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi)$ | All φ | | Shape (cohesion) | F_cs | $1 + (B/L)(N_q/N_c)$ | All footings | | Shape (surcharge) | F_qs | $1 + (B/L)\tan\phi$ | All footings | | Shape (self-weight) | F_γs | $1 - 0.4(B/L)$ | All footings | | Depth (D/B ≤ 1) | F_cd, F_qd | $1 + 0.4(D_f/B)$ | Shallow | | Depth (D/B > 1) | F_cd, F_qd | $1 + 0.4\tan^{-1}(D_f/B)$ | Deep embedment | | Depth (self-weight) | F_γd | 1.0 | Always | | Inclination (cohesion/surcharge) | F_ci, F_qi | $(1 - \beta/90)^2$ | Inclined loads | | Inclination (self-weight) | F_γi | $(1 - \beta/\phi)^2$ | Inclined loads | | **Ultimate Bearing Capacity** | q_ult | $cN_cF_{cs}F_{cd}F_{ci} + qN_qF_{qs}F_{qd}F_{qi} + 0.5\gamma BN_\gamma F_{\gamma s}F_{\gamma d}F_{\gamma i}$ | **Master Equation** | | **Net Ultimate BC** | q_nu | $q_{ult} - \gamma D_f$ | — | | **Net Safe BC with F.S.** | q_ns | $q_{nu}/F.S.$ | F.S. = 3 (usual) | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility Meyerhof's method is extensively used in: - **Design of bridge abutments** (where loads are typically inclined due to earth pressure). - **Retaining wall foundations** (where the resultant load is eccentric and inclined). - **Offshore structure foundations** (where inclined loads from waves dominate). - **Transmission tower foundations** (where overturning moments create inclined resultant loads). - **Industrial building column footings** (where vertical + wind loads combine to give inclined reactions). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Bearing Capacity Factor N_q (Meyerhof's Approach) The surcharge bearing capacity factor is derived by considering a smooth strip footing of width B resting on a weightless soil (c = 0, γ = 0) with surcharge q at the level of the footing base. ### Step 1: Active Wedge Angle By Rankine's active state theory, the elastic wedge beneath the footing makes an angle: $$\psi = 45° + \frac{\phi}{2}$$ with the horizontal. ### Step 2: Passive Failure Surface Inclination The failure surface in the passive zone emerges at the ground surface at an angle: $$\alpha = 45° - \frac{\phi}{2}$$ below the horizontal at the surface. ### Step 3: Equilibrium along Curved Slip Surface Meyerhof considered the equilibrium of the soil wedge bounded by: - The base of footing (length B) - The failure surface (a curve approximated as a log-spiral) - The surcharge surface (q applied at ground level) For weightless soil, the bearing capacity is purely due to surcharge: $$q_{ult} = q \cdot N_q$$ By integrating the shear and normal stresses along the curved failure surface, the analytical result is: $$N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \cdot \tan^2\left(45 + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$ ### Step 4: Verification for φ = 0 When φ = 0 (purely cohesive soil): - $\tan(0) = 0 \Rightarrow e^0 = 1$ - $\tan^2(45°) = 1$ - Therefore, $N_q = 1$ ✓ (matches tabulated value) ## 3.2 Derivation of N_c from N_q For purely cohesive soil with surcharge and cohesion only (γ = 0), using superposition: $$q_{ult} = cN_c + qN_q$$ Meyerhof derived the relationship between N_c and N_q by differentiating with respect to φ, leading to: $$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$$ ### Verification for φ = 0: - Direct use of N_c = 5.14 (Prandtl's value) is recommended for φ = 0. - The expression $(N_q - 1)\cot\phi$ becomes indeterminate (0/0) at φ = 0 and requires L'Hôpital's rule application. ## 3.3 Derivation of N_γ (Meyerhof's Empirical Form) The self-weight term is the most complex since it involves the weight of the failure wedge itself. Meyerhof, by integrating stresses along the assumed log-spiral surface, arrived at the empirical relation: $$N_{\gamma} = (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi)$$ > [!NOTE] > The coefficient **1.4** (instead of 1.5 as in Vesic) was determined by Meyerhof through correlation with model tests on footings. This is a famous KTU viva question: *"Why 1.4 and not 1.5?"* ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step Solution) **Problem:** A rectangular footing **2.5 m × 4.0 m** is founded at a depth of **2.0 m** in a soil with: - c′ = 15 kN/m² - φ′ = 25° - γ = 18 kN/m³ - Inclined load with β = 15° from vertical Compute the **ultimate bearing capacity** using Meyerhof's method. Take F.S. = 3 and compute the **net safe bearing capacity**. ### Step 1: Compute Basic Bearing Capacity Factors For φ = 25°: $$N_q = e^{\pi \tan 25°} \cdot \tan^2(45° + 12.5°)$$ $$\tan 25° = 0.4663$$ $$\pi \tan 25° = 3.1416 \times 0.4663 = 1.4650$$ $$e^{1.4650} = 4.328$$ $$\tan(57.5°) = 1.5697 \Rightarrow \tan^2(57.5°) = 2.464$$ $$N_q = 4.328 \times 2.464 = 10.66$$ $$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi = (10.66 - 1) \times \cot 25° = 9.66 \times 2.1445 = 20.72$$ $$N_{\gamma} = (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi) = 9.66 \times \tan(35°) = 9.66 \times 0.7002 = 6.76$$ ### Step 2: Compute Shape Factors B/L = 2.5/4.0 = 0.625 $$F_{cs} = 1 + \frac{B}{L} \cdot \frac{N_q}{N_c} = 1 + 0.625 \times \frac{10.66}{20.72} = 1 + 0.625 \times 0.5145 = 1.322$$ $$F_{qs} = 1 + \frac{B}{L}\tan\phi = 1 + 0.625 \times \tan 25° = 1 + 0.625 \times 0.4663 = 1.291$$ $$F_{\gamma s} = 1 - 0.4 \times \frac{B}{L} = 1 - 0.4 \times 0.625 = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$$ ### Step 3: Compute Depth Factors $D_f/B = 2.0/2.5 = 0.8 \leq 1$, so use Case 1. $$F_{cd} = 1 + 0.4 \times \frac{D_f}{B} = 1 + 0.4 \times 0.8 = 1.32$$ $$F_{qd} = 1 + 0.4 \times \frac{D_f}{B} = 1.32$$ $$F_{\gamma d} = 1.0$$ ### Step 4: Compute Inclination Factors $$\beta = 15°$$ $$F_{ci} = F_{qi} = \left(1 - \frac{\beta}{90}\right)^2 = \left(1 - \frac{15}{90}\right)^2 = (1 - 0.1667)^2 = 0.8333^2 = 0.6944$$ $$F_{\gamma i} = \left(1 - \frac{\beta}{\phi}\right)^2 = \left(1 - \frac{15}{25}\right)^2 = (1 - 0.6)^2 = 0.4^2 = 0.16$$ ### Step 5: Compute Surcharge q $$q = \gamma D_f = 18 \times 2.0 = 36 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ ### Step 6: Compute Ultimate Bearing Capacity $$q_{ult} = cN_cF_{cs}F_{cd}F_{ci} + qN_qF_{qs}F_{qd}F_{qi} + 0.5\gamma BN_{\gamma}F_{\gamma s}F_{\gamma d}F_{\gamma i}$$ **Cohesion term:** $$= 15 \times 20.72 \times 1.322 \times 1.32 \times 0.6944$$ $$= 15 \times 20.72 \times 1.2116 = 15 \times 25.10 = 376.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ Let me recompute carefully: - $20.72 \times 1.322 = 27.39$ - $27.39 \times 1.32 = 36.15$ - $36.15 \times 0.6944 = 25.11$ - $15 \times 25.11 = 376.6$ kN/m² **Surcharge term:** $$= 36 \times 10.66 \times 1.291 \times 1.32 \times 0.6944$$ - $10.66 \times 1.291 = 13.76$ - $13.76 \times 1.32 = 18.16$ - $18.16 \times 0.6944 = 12.61$ - $36 \times 12.61 = 454.0$ kN/m² **Self-weight term:** $$= 0.5 \times 18 \times 2.5 \times 6.76 \times 0.75 \times 1.0 \times 0.16$$ - $0.5 \times 18 \times 2.5 = 22.5$ - $22.5 \times 6.76 = 152.1$ - $152.1 \times 0.75 = 114.1$ - $114.1 \times 0.16 = 18.25$ kN/m² **Total:** $$q_{ult} = 376.6 + 454.0 + 18.25 = 848.85 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ ### Step 7: Net Ultimate and Safe Bearing Capacity $$q_{nu} = q_{ult} - \gamma D_f = 848.85 - 36 = 812.85 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{F.S.} = \frac{812.85}{3} = 270.95 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Answer:** Net Safe Bearing Capacity ≈ **271 kN/m²** ## 3.5 Effect of Eccentric Loading (Meyerhof's Effective Width Concept) For eccentric loading, Meyerhof proposed using an **effective width** concept: $$B' = B - 2e_B$$ $$L' = L - 2e_L$$ Where $e_B$ and $e_L$ are the eccentricities in B and L directions. The bearing capacity is then computed using B′ and L′, and the effective area = B′ × L′. > [!WARNING] > The Meyerhof effective width method assumes the **load is uniformly distributed** over the reduced effective area. This is a simplifying assumption (Vesic's method is more rigorous but Meyerhof's is the KTU-prescribed approach). ## 3.6 Summary Equation Recap (For Quick Reference) $$\boxed{q_{ult} = cN_cF_{cs}F_{cd}F_{ci} + qN_qF_{qs}F_{qd}F_{qi} + \frac{1}{2}\gamma BN_{\gamma}F_{\gamma s}F_{\gamma d}F_{\gamma i}}$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Meyerhof's Failure Surface Model (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB Load["Inclined Load P at angle beta"]:::loadNode Footing["Rectangular Footing B x L at depth Df"]:::footNode Z1["Zone I: Active Wedge (Elastic Equilibrium)
Angle psi = 45 + phi/2 with horizontal"]:::zoneNode Z2["Zone II: Radial Shear Zone
Logarithmic spiral slip lines"]:::zoneNode Z3["Zone III: Passive Zone
Rupture surface curves up to ground level"]:::zoneNode GS["Ground Surface with surcharge q = gamma x Df"]:::groundNode S1["Cohesion Resistance
cNc term"]:::resistNode S2["Surcharge Resistance
qNq term"]:::resistNode S3["Self-weight Resistance
0.5 gamma B Ngamma term"]:::resistNode SF["Shape, Depth, Inclination factors
multiply each term"]:::factorNode Load --> Footing Footing --> Z1 Z1 --> Z2 Z2 --> Z3 Z3 --> GS Z1 -.- S1 Z2 -.- S2 Z3 -.- S3 SF -.modulates.-> S1 SF -.modulates.-> S2 SF -.modulates.-> S3 classDef loadNode fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef footNode fill:#ffd966,stroke:#bf9000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef zoneNode fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef groundNode fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#674ea7,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef resistNode fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef factorNode fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Computational Flowchart for Meyerhof's Method ```mermaid flowchart TB A["START: Given c, phi, gamma, B, L, Df, beta"]:::startNode B1["Compute Nq = e to the power pi tan phi times tan squared 45 + phi/2"]:::calcNode B2["Compute Nc = Nq - 1 times cot phi"]:::calcNode B3["Compute Ngamma = Nq - 1 times tan 1.4 phi"]:::calcNode C1["Shape Factors:
Fcs = 1 + B/L x Nq/Nc
Fqs = 1 + B/L x tan phi
Fgamma_s = 1 - 0.4 B/L"]:::calcNode D1["Check Df/B"]:::checkNode D2["If Df/B <= 1:
Fcd = Fqd = 1 + 0.4 Df/B
Fgamma_d = 1.0"]:::calcNode D3["If Df/B > 1:
Fcd = Fqd = 1 + 0.4 tan inverse Df/B in rad
Fgamma_d = 1.0"]:::calcNode E1["Inclination Factors:
Fci = Fqi = 1 - beta/90 squared
Fgamma_i = 1 - beta/phi squared"]:::calcNode F1["Compute surcharge q = gamma Df"]:::calcNode G1["Plug into Master Equation:
qult = cNcFcsFcdFci + qNqFqsFqdFqi + 0.5 gamma B Ngamma Fgamma_s Fgamma_d Fgamma_i"]:::masterNode H1["Compute Net Ultimate q_nu = q_ult - q"]:::calcNode I1["Compute Net Safe q_ns = q_nu / F.S."]:::endNode J1["OUTPUT: Design Bearing Capacity"]:::endNode A --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> C1 --> D1 D1 -->|Yes| D2 D1 -->|No| D3 D2 --> E1 D3 --> E1 E1 --> F1 --> G1 --> H1 --> I1 --> J1 classDef startNode fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:3px classDef calcNode fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,stroke-width:2px classDef checkNode fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#d6b656,stroke-width:3px classDef masterNode fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,stroke-width:3px classDef endNode fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:3px ``` ## 4.3 Comparison Block: Terzaghi vs. Meyerhof Failure Surface ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TERZ["TERZAGHI (1943)"] T1["Footing base at depth Df"]:::tNode T2["Active wedge A B C
angle psi below footing"]:::tNode T3["Radial shear zone B C D E"]:::tNode T4["Passive wedge C D E
rises to base level only"]:::tNode T5["Surcharge q = gamma Df
acts as dead weight above base"]:::tNode end subgraph MEY["MEYERHOF (1963)"] M1["Footing base at depth Df"]:::mNode M2["Active wedge
angle psi from base"]:::mNode M3["Radial shear zone
log spiral"]:::mNode M4["Passive zone
rises UP TO ground surface"]:::mNode M5["Soil above base participates
in shear resistance"]:::mNode end classDef tNode fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#d79b00,stroke-width:2px classDef mNode fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] List the bearing capacity factors proposed by Meyerhof and write their expressions. **Model Answer:** Meyerhof (1963) proposed three bearing capacity factors that depend only on the soil friction angle φ: **1. Surcharge Bearing Capacity Factor:** $$N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \cdot \tan^2\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$ **2. Cohesion Bearing Capacity Factor:** $$N_c = (N_q - 1) \cdot \cot\phi$$ **3. Self-weight Bearing Capacity Factor:** $$N_{\gamma} = (N_q - 1) \cdot \tan(1.4\phi)$$ These factors are computed assuming the soil is homogeneous and isotropic. For φ = 0°, the values are: N_c = 5.14, N_q = 1.0, and N_γ = 0.0. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Differentiate between Meyerhof's and Terzaghi's bearing capacity theories. **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Terzaghi (1943) | Meyerhof (1963) | |---|---|---| | Failure surface | Simplified; ends at the base level of the footing | Curved; extends up to the ground surface | | Role of soil above base | Acts only as surcharge (q = γD) | Contributes to shear resistance (frictional) | | Shape, depth, inclination factors | Separate equations for strip, square, circular | Unified equation with F_cs, F_cd, F_ci factors | | Inclined loads | Not addressed | Addressed via inclination factors F_ci, F_qi, F_γi | | Eccentric loading | Not addressed | Addressed via effective width concept (B′ = B − 2e) | | N_γ coefficient | Empirical (≈ 0.5 in some forms) | Empirical: 1.4φ | | Accuracy for shallow footings | Conservative | More realistic, widely used in practice | --- ## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] **A rectangular footing of size 3 m × 4 m is to be placed at a depth of 1.5 m in a soil deposit. The soil has the following properties: c = 20 kN/m², φ = 30°, γ = 19 kN/m³. The load on the footing is inclined at an angle of 10° to the vertical. Compute the ultimate and net safe bearing capacity using Meyerhof's method. Take F.S. = 3.** #### Part (a) — Compute ultimate bearing capacity (7 Marks) **Step 1: Bearing Capacity Factors (φ = 30°)** $$N_q = e^{\pi \tan 30°} \cdot \tan^2(45° + 15°)$$ $$\tan 30° = 0.5774, \quad \pi \times 0.5774 = 1.8138, \quad e^{1.8138} = 6.133$$ $$\tan^2(60°) = 2.998 \approx 3.0$$ $$N_q = 6.133 \times 3.0 = 18.40$$ $$N_c = (18.40 - 1) \times \cot 30° = 17.40 \times 1.7321 = 30.14$$ $$N_{\gamma} = (18.40 - 1) \times \tan(1.4 \times 30°) = 17.40 \times \tan 42° = 17.40 \times 0.9004 = 15.67$$ **Step 2: Shape Factors (B/L = 3/4 = 0.75)** $$F_{cs} = 1 + 0.75 \times \frac{18.40}{30.14} = 1 + 0.4580 = 1.458$$ $$F_{qs} = 1 + 0.75 \times \tan 30° = 1 + 0.75 \times 0.5774 = 1.433$$ $$F_{\gamma s} = 1 - 0.4 \times 0.75 = 0.70$$ **Step 3: Depth Factors (D_f/B = 1.5/3 = 0.5 ≤ 1)** $$F_{cd} = F_{qd} = 1 + 0.4 \times 0.5 = 1.20$$ $$F_{\gamma d} = 1.0$$ **Step 4: Inclination Factors (β = 10°)** $$F_{ci} = F_{qi} = \left(1 - \frac{10}{90}\right)^2 = (0.8889)^2 = 0.7901$$ $$F_{\gamma i} = \left(1 - \frac{10}{30}\right)^2 = (0.6667)^2 = 0.4444$$ **Step 5: Master Equation** $$q = 19 \times 1.5 = 28.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Cohesion term:** $20 \times 30.14 \times 1.458 \times 1.20 \times 0.7901$ $$= 20 \times 30.14 \times 1.3825 = 20 \times 41.67 = 833.4 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Surcharge term:** $28.5 \times 18.40 \times 1.433 \times 1.20 \times 0.7901$ $$= 28.5 \times 18.40 \times 1.3587 = 28.5 \times 25.00 = 712.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Self-weight term:** $0.5 \times 19 \times 3 \times 15.67 \times 0.70 \times 1.0 \times 0.4444$ $$= 28.5 \times 15.67 \times 0.3111 = 28.5 \times 4.875 = 138.9 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$\boxed{q_{ult} = 833.4 + 712.5 + 138.9 = 1684.8 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$ #### Part (b) — Compute net safe bearing capacity (7 Marks) $$q_{nu} = q_{ult} - \gamma D_f = 1684.8 - 28.5 = 1656.3 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$q_{ns} = \frac{q_{nu}}{F.S.} = \frac{1656.3}{3} = 552.1 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$\boxed{q_{ns} \approx 552 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **(a) Explain the various shape factors, depth factors, and inclination factors used in Meyerhof's bearing capacity equation with suitable expressions. (7 Marks)** **(b) A square footing 2 m × 2 m is founded at 1.0 m depth in a sandy soil with φ = 35°, c = 0, γ = 17 kN/m³. The load is vertical. Compute the safe bearing capacity using Meyerhof's method. Take F.S. = 3. (7 Marks)** #### Part (a) — Model Answer (7 Marks) **Shape Factors:** Account for the geometry of the foundation. They modify the strip footing (B/L → 0) case to a rectangular/square footing. - $F_{cs} = 1 + (B/L)(N_q/N_c)$ - $F_{qs} = 1 + (B/L)\tan\phi$ - $F_{\gamma s} = 1 - 0.4(B/L)$ **Depth Factors:** Account for soil shearing resistance along the failure surface above the base. If D_f/B ≤ 1: - $F_{cd} = F_{qd} = 1 + 0.4(D_f/B)$ - $F_{\gamma d} = 1.0$ If D_f/B > 1: - $F_{cd} = F_{qd} = 1 + 0.4\tan^{-1}(D_f/B)$ (in radians) - $F_{\gamma d} = 1.0$ **Inclination Factors:** Account for inclined loads (β is angle of load with vertical). - $F_{ci} = F_{qi} = (1 - \beta/90)^2$ - $F_{\gamma i} = (1 - \beta/\phi)^2$ **Significance:** For vertical, strip footings at the surface, all factors = 1.0 and the equation reduces to the basic Terzaghi form. #### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks) **Given:** B = L = 2 m, D_f = 1.0 m, c = 0, φ = 35°, γ = 17 kN/m³, vertical load (β = 0). **Step 1: Bearing Capacity Factors (φ = 35°)** $$N_q = e^{\pi \tan 35°} \cdot \tan^2(45° + 17.5°)$$ $$\tan 35° = 0.7002, \quad \pi \tan 35° = 2.1995, \quad e^{2.1995} = 9.02$$ $$\tan^2(62.5°) = (1.9210)^2 = 3.690$$ $$N_q = 9.02 \times 3.690 = 33.30$$ $$N_{\gamma} = (33.30 - 1) \times \tan(1.4 \times 35°) = 32.30 \times \tan 49° = 32.30 \times 1.1504 = 37.16$$ **Step 2: Shape Factors (B/L = 1)** $$F_{\gamma s} = 1 - 0.4 \times 1 = 0.6$$ **Step 3: Depth Factors (D_f/B = 1.0/2.0 = 0.5 ≤ 1)** $$F_{qd} = 1 + 0.4 \times 0.5 = 1.20$$ $$F_{\gamma d} = 1.0$$ **Step 4: Inclination Factors (β = 0)** All inclination factors = 1.0. **Step 5: Master Equation (c = 0, so first term vanishes)** $$q = 17 \times 1.0 = 17 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$q_{ult} = 17 \times 33.30 \times 1.20 + 0.5 \times 17 \times 2 \times 37.16 \times 0.6 \times 1.0$$ $$= 679.3 + 379.0 = 1058.3 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ **Step 6: Net Safe Bearing Capacity** $$q_{nu} = 1058.3 - 17 = 1041.3 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$q_{ns} = \frac{1041.3}{3} = 347.1 \text{ kN/m}^2$$ $$\boxed{q_{ns} \approx 347 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Don't confuse D_f/B and D_f/B in the depth factor formula** — the ratio is unitless, so no unit conversion needed. > 2. **tan⁻¹ argument must be in radians** for D_f/B > 1 case — convert using (D_f/B × π/180) if calculator is in degree mode. > 3. **For c = 0 case, do not compute N_c**, only the surcharge and self-weight terms are non-zero. Forgetting this wastes 2–3 marks. > 4. **N_γ uses 1.4φ (Meyerhof) not 1.5φ (Vesic)** — using the wrong coefficient will yield wrong numerical answer and cost full marks. > 5. **Always subtract q (= γD_f) to get net** values; many students forget this distinction. > 6. **For square footing, B/L = 1**, so F_γs = 0.6 (not 1.0) — a very common mistake. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Meyerhof (1963)** proposed a unified bearing capacity equation that overcomes Terzaghi's limitations. - [ ] **Master Equation:** $q_{ult} = cN_cF_{cs}F_{cd}F_{ci} + qN_qF_{qs}F_{qd}F_{qi} + 0.5\gamma BN_\gamma F_{\gamma s}F_{\gamma d}F_{\gamma i}$ - [ ] **N_q** = $e^{\pi \tan\phi} \cdot \tan^2(45 + \phi/2)$ — surcharge factor (always positive) - [ ] **N_c** = $(N_q - 1)\cot\phi$ — cohesion factor (use 5.14 for φ = 0) - [ ] **N_γ** = $(N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi)$ — Meyerhof's empirical formula (1.4 not 1.5) - [ ] **Shape factors** modify strip (B/L = 0) to rectangular: F_cs ≥ 1, F_qs ≥ 1, F_γs ≤ 1 - [ ] **Depth factors** bifurcate at D_f/B = 1: linear below 1, arctan above 1 - [ ] **Inclination factors** reduce BC for inclined loads: F_γi = 0 when β = φ - [ ] **Net values** require subtracting γD_f from gross values - [ ] **Eccentric loading** handled via effective width B′ = B − 2e - [ ] **q = γD_f** is the surcharge at the base of the footing - [ ] **Failure surface** in Meyerhof's model is a **logarithmic spiral** rising to the ground surface - [ ] **Default F.S.** for bearing capacity design = **3** (IS code convention) - [ ] For **φ = 0 (pure clay)**: N_q = 1, N_c = 5.14, N_γ = 0 - [ ] **B/L ratio** for shape factors: B is the smaller dimension, L is the larger - [ ] **For water table correction** (KTU Module 2 extension): use modified γ and q - [ ] **Meyerhof's method is preferred** for footings subjected to inclined/eccentric loads (e.g., retaining walls, bridge piers) - [ ] **Always remember the units**: c in kN/m², γ in kN/m³, B, L, D_f in meters → q_ult in kN/m²

topics.title: Numerical Problem, Uplift Capacity of Single Piles and Group of Piles in Clay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Numerical Problem, Uplift Capacity of Single Piles and Group of Piles in Clay # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Uplift (Tension) Capacity of Piles The **uplift capacity** (or **tension capacity**) of a pile is defined as the maximum tensile load that a pile foundation can resist before being pulled out of the ground. It is the load applied in the **opposite direction** to the normal compressive working load, and is encountered in structures subjected to significant overturning moments or tensile forces. Formally, for a pile embedded in **cohesive soil (clay)**, the ultimate uplift capacity $Q_u$ is the sum of two resisting components: $$Q_u = Q_s + W_p$$ Where: - $Q_s$ = **Shaft Resistance** (skin friction acting downwards when pile is pulled up) - $W_p$ = **Weight of the pile itself** (acting downwards, opposing uplift) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECET635, Module 2):** > In saturated clay, the analysis is performed in terms of **total stress** using the **α-method** (adhesion factor). The effective stress method ($\beta$ and $\lambda$ methods) is **not preferred** for clays under short-term (end-of-construction) loading, since excess pore water pressure dominates. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Anchor in Mud" Intuition Imagine you push a smooth wooden stick deep into a bowl of thick, sticky wet clay (like clay used for pottery). Now try to pull it straight out. - The clay grips the sides of the stick due to its **stickiness (cohesion, $c_u$)**. - The harder you pull, the more the clay resists. - The longer and thicker the stick, the more gripping surface is available, and the harder it is to pull out. - This resistance is the **shaft friction (skin friction)**. Now imagine a flagpole anchored in the ground. Wind blowing on the flag creates a **lifting force**. The pole is held down not only by friction along its length but also by its own **weight**. This is exactly the mechanism of an uplift pile. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Difference from Compressive Capacity:** > In **compression**, the pile tip also contributes **end-bearing resistance ($Q_b$)**. In **uplift**, there is **no tip resistance** — the tip cannot "push" against anything. Therefore, uplift capacity is *always less* than compressive capacity for the same pile in identical soil. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Symbols (KTU Convention) | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit | |---|---|---| | $Q_u$ | Ultimate uplift capacity | kN | | $Q_a$ | Allowable (safe) uplift capacity | kN | | $c_u$ | Undrained shear strength of clay | kN/m² or kPa | | $\alpha$ | Adhesion factor (dimensionless) | — | | $D$ | Pile diameter (for circular pile) | m | | $L$ | Embedded length of pile | m | | $A_s$ | Surface area of pile shaft = $\pi D L$ | m² | | $W_p$ | Weight of pile | kN | | $\gamma_c$ | Unit weight of pile material | kN/m³ | | $F.S.$ | Factor of Safety | 2.5 to 3.0 (typical) | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Breakdown ### Step 1 — Identify the Soil Type Clay soils are analyzed using **total stress (α-method)** under undrained conditions because: - Cohesion is the dominant strength parameter. - Pore pressure response is critical during rapid loading. - Drained parameters ($c'$, $\phi'$) are less relevant for short-term uplift. ### Step 2 — Compute the Shaft Friction (Adhesion) Force The skin friction developed along the pile shaft in clay is governed by the **undrained adhesion** $\alpha \cdot c_u$, where $\alpha$ is a reduction factor accounting for the fact that pile-soil adhesion is **less than the soil's own shear strength** (due to remolding, installation disturbance, and progressive failure). $$Q_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u \cdot A_s$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Pile Self-Weight For a circular concrete pile: $$W_p = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L \cdot \gamma_c$$ For a square pile of side $B$: $$W_p = B^2 \cdot L \cdot \gamma_c$$ ### Step 4 — Apply Factor of Safety The allowable (safe) uplift capacity is: $$Q_a = \frac{Q_u}{F.S.} = \frac{\alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u \cdot A_s + W_p}{F.S.}$$ A typical **F.S. = 2.5 to 3.0** is adopted for uplift piles in clay as per IS 2911 (Part 1). ### Step 5 — Special Case: Belled (Under-reamed) Piles For piles with an enlarged base (bell), an **additional uplift resistance** is mobilized by **soil suction** and **shear** along a vertical surface projected from the bell edge: $$Q_{u,\text{bell}} = \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u \cdot A_s + c_u \cdot A_b' + W_p$$ Where $A_b'$ is the projected area of the bell on a vertical plane (annular area for a circular belled pile). ## 2.2 Why α is Always Less Than 1.0 | Reason | Explanation | |---|---| | **Progressive failure** | Peak strength is not mobilized simultaneously along full length. | | **Installation remolding** | Driving or boring disturbs the soil around the shaft. | | **Tensile vs Shear failure** | Tension failure surface is different from compression; friction angle contribution is reduced. | | **Pore pressure effects** | During uplift, negative pore pressures (suction) develop that are not sustained long-term. | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q_u = \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u \cdot A_s + W_p$ | Ultimate uplift capacity (single pile, clay) | All standard numericals | | 2 | $A_s = \pi D L$ | Surface area of circular pile shaft | Single pile, uniform $D$ | | 3 | $A_s = 4 B L$ | Surface area of square pile shaft | Square pile, uniform $B$ | | 4 | $W_p = (\pi/4) D^2 L \gamma_c$ | Weight of circular concrete pile | Gravity contribution | | 5 | $W_p = B^2 L \gamma_c$ | Weight of square concrete pile | Gravity contribution | | 6 | $Q_a = Q_u / F.S.$ | Allowable (safe) capacity | Design checks | | 7 | $Q_{ug} = \eta \cdot n \cdot Q_u$ | Group uplift capacity (with efficiency $\eta$) | Pile group analysis | | 8 | $Q_{ug} = L_g \cdot B_g \cdot c_u \cdot N_c + 2 D_g (L_g + B_g) \alpha c_u + W_g$ | Block failure (clay group) | When pile spacing is small | | 9 | $N_c = 9$ (for $L_g/D_g \geq 4$) | Terzaghi's bearing capacity factor (clay) | Block uplift (uses $N_c$ reversed) | | 10 | $\alpha = 0.4$ to $1.0$ (for $c_u \leq 100$ kPa) | Standard range as per IS 2911 | Adhesion selection | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Reminder (KTU Valuation):** Always use the **weighted average** $c_u$ if $c_u$ varies with depth. Mention $\bar{c}_u$ clearly in your solution. Examiners specifically look for this distinction. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Why Uplift Capacity Matters | |---|---| | **Transmission towers** | Wind on cables creates net uplift on legs. | | **Offshore platforms** | Wave buoyancy pulls legs upward. | | **Tall chimneys & towers** | Overturning moment lifts one side. | | **Anchored retaining walls** | Tie-backs anchored in clay rely on uplift. | | **Tension foundations for solar trackers** | Modern renewable energy structures. | | **Buoyant foundations (basements)** | Groundwater uplift on buried structures. | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Ultimate Uplift Capacity of a Single Pile in Clay Starting from the fundamental equilibrium of a pile under pure tensile load $T$ applied at the pile head, with the pile embedded to depth $L$ in saturated clay of undrained shear strength $c_u$. ### Step 1 — Free Body Diagram Analysis When the pile is on the verge of being pulled out, two resisting forces act **downward** on the pile: 1. The shear stress (adhesion) mobilized along the cylindrical pile-soil interface, acting downward. 2. The self-weight of the pile acting downward. For equilibrium at the point of incipient failure: $$T_u = \int_0^L (\alpha \cdot c_u(z)) \cdot (\pi D) \, dz + W_p$$ ### Step 2 — Simplification for Uniform Soil If $c_u$ is **constant with depth**, the integration becomes trivial: $$T_u = \alpha \cdot c_u \cdot (\pi D) \cdot L + W_p = \alpha \cdot c_u \cdot A_s + W_p$$ ### Step 3 — Weighted Average for Layered Clay If $c_u$ varies with depth in distinct layers $1, 2, 3 \ldots n$ with thicknesses $L_1, L_2, L_3 \ldots L_n$: $$T_u = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i \cdot c_{u,i} \cdot (\pi D \cdot L_i) + W_p$$ The **weighted average** $c_u$: $$\bar{c}_u = \frac{\sum c_{u,i} \cdot L_i}{\sum L_i}$$ ### Step 4 — Reduction Due to Tension Failure (α vs α_u) For uplift (tension) piles in clay, research (IS 2911 Part 1, Section 5.5) recommends using a **reduced α value** compared to compressive piles. A widely used reduction: $$\alpha_{u} \approx 0.75 \cdot \alpha_{c} \quad \text{(for uplift, conservative)}$$ > [!NOTE] > Many KTU problems use $\alpha$ as directly given in the problem statement. Always use the value specified in the question — do **not** apply extra reduction unless explicitly instructed. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Single Pile Uplift in Clay ### Problem Statement (Standard KTU Pattern) > A **circular concrete pile** of **diameter 0.5 m** is **driven 12 m** into a **soft saturated clay** with **average undrained shear strength $\bar{c}_u$ = 30 kPa**. The **adhesion factor $\alpha$ = 0.8**. The **unit weight of reinforced concrete** is **24 kN/m³**. Determine the **ultimate** and **allowable uplift capacity** using **F.S. = 3**. ### Step 1 — Compute the Surface Area of Pile Shaft $$A_s = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.5 \times 12 = 6\pi \approx 18.8496 \text{ m}^2$$ ### Step 2 — Compute the Shaft Adhesion (Skin Friction Component) $$Q_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u \cdot A_s = 0.8 \times 30 \times 18.8496$$ $$Q_s = 24 \times 18.8496 = 452.39 \text{ kN}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Self-Weight of the Pile Volume of pile: $$V = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.5)^2 \times 12 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.25 \times 12 = 0.75\pi$$ $$V = 0.75 \times 3.1416 = 2.3562 \text{ m}^3$$ Weight of pile: $$W_p = V \cdot \gamma_c = 2.3562 \times 24 = 56.55 \text{ kN}$$ ### Step 4 — Compute the Ultimate Uplift Capacity $$Q_u = Q_s + W_p = 452.39 + 56.55 = 508.94 \text{ kN}$$ ### Step 5 — Compute the Allowable (Safe) Uplift Capacity $$Q_a = \frac{Q_u}{F.S.} = \frac{508.94}{3.0} = 169.65 \text{ kN}$$ ### Final Answer Box $$\boxed{Q_u \approx 508.94 \text{ kN} \quad ; \quad Q_a \approx 169.65 \text{ kN}}$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — Pile Group Uplift in Clay (Block Failure) ### Problem Statement > A **pile group** consists of **9 piles** (3 × 3 arrangement) of **diameter 0.5 m**, **length 12 m**, **center-to-center spacing s = 1.5 m** (3D), installed in **clay with $\bar{c}_u$ = 30 kPa** and **$\alpha$ = 0.8**. Compute the **ultimate uplift capacity of the group** by: (a) **Individual pile method**, (b) **Block failure method**, and use the **lower** for safe design. Take $\gamma_c$ = 24 kN/m³. ### Step 1 — Group Geometry Number of piles, $n = 9$ Group dimensions: $L_g = B_g = (3-1) \times 1.5 + 0.5 = 3.5 \text{ m}$ Group depth: $D_g = L = 12 \text{ m}$ Total weight of all piles: $W_{pg} = n \cdot W_p = 9 \times 56.55 = 508.95 \text{ kN}$ ### Step 2 (a) — Individual Pile Method $$Q_{ug,\text{ind}} = n \cdot Q_u = 9 \times 508.94 = 4580.46 \text{ kN}$$ ### Step 2 (b) — Block Failure Method For a closely spaced pile group, the **soil block** containing the piles may fail as a single unit. The block uplift resistance is the sum of: (i) Shear resistance along the **vertical surface** of the block (4 sides): $$Q_{s,\text{block}} = 2 D_g (L_g + B_g) \cdot \alpha \cdot c_u$$ $$Q_{s,\text{block}} = 2 \times 12 \times (3.5 + 3.5) \times 0.8 \times 30$$ $$Q_{s,\text{block}} = 2 \times 12 \times 7 \times 0.8 \times 30 = 4032 \text{ kN}$$ (ii) **No tip bearing** in uplift — so no $N_c$ term (or $N_c$ contribution is zero for tension). (iii) Weight of the **soil + pile block**: Weight of soil in block: $W_s = L_g \cdot B_g \cdot D_g \cdot \gamma_{\text{soil}}$ Weight of piles: $W_{pg} = 508.95 \text{ kN}$ (computed above) Assuming $\gamma_{\text{clay}} \approx 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$: $$W_s = 3.5 \times 3.5 \times 12 \times 18 = 2646 \text{ kN}$$ $$W_g = W_s + W_{pg} = 2646 + 508.95 = 3154.95 \text{ kN}$$ (iv) Total block uplift capacity: $$Q_{ug,\text{block}} = Q_{s,\text{block}} + W_g = 4032 + 3154.95 = 7186.95 \text{ kN}$$ ### Step 3 — Governing Value (Lower of the Two) $$\boxed{Q_{ug} = \min(Q_{ug,\text{ind}}, \ Q_{ug,\text{block}}) = \min(4580.46, \ 7186.95) = 4580.46 \text{ kN}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Concept:** The **lower** of the two methods is always the design value. For **closely spaced piles in clay**, block failure often governs (since shear area is smaller than sum of individual shafts at low $s/D$). For **wider spacings** ($s/D \geq 6$), individual pile failure governs. ## 3.4 Group Efficiency in Uplift The **uplift group efficiency** $\eta_u$ is defined as: $$\eta_u = \frac{Q_{ug}}{n \cdot Q_u}$$ For our example: $$\eta_u = \frac{4580.46}{9 \times 508.94} = \frac{4580.46}{4580.46} = 1.00$$ This means the group acts as the sum of individual piles. As spacing reduces, block failure starts to govern and $\eta_u$ may **drop below 1.0** (or the block value may govern directly). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Uplift Capacity Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Uplift Capacity Calculation in Clay] --> B{Is the pile a
single pile or a group?} B -->|Single Pile| C[Compute Shaft Adhesion
Qs = alpha x cu x As] C --> D[Compute Pile Self-Weight
Wp = V x gamma_c] D --> E[Compute Ultimate Capacity
Qu = Qs + Wp] E --> F[Apply Factor of Safety
Qa = Qu / F.S.] F --> G[Final: Safe Uplift Capacity Qa] B -->|Pile Group| H{Compute BOTH methods} H --> I[Method 1: Individual Pile
Qug_ind = n x Qu] H --> J[Method 2: Block Failure
Qug_block = 2Dg Lg+Bg alpha cu + Wg] I --> K[Take MINIMUM of two] J --> K K --> L[Apply Factor of Safety
Qa_group = Qug / F.S.] L --> M[Final: Safe Group Uplift Capacity] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Force Components on an Uplift Pile in Clay ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Applied Tensile Load T
at Pile Head] -->|Acts UPWARD| PILE PILE[Pile Body
Length L in Clay] PILE -->|Shaft Adhesion
alpha x cu x As| FRIC[Resists Uplift
Acts DOWNWARD] PILE -->|Self-Weight Wp| WT[Resists Uplift
Acts DOWNWARD] FRIC --> EQ[Force Equilibrium
at Failure] WT --> EQ EQ --> RESULT[Tu = alpha x cu x As + Wp] ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Single vs Block Group Failure Modes ```mermaid flowchart TD G[Pile Group in Clay] --> SP{Spacing s/D} SP -->|s/D less than 3| BLK[Block Failure Mode] SP -->|s/D between 3 and 6| BOTH[Both Modes Possible] SP -->|s/D greater than 6| IND[Individual Pile Failure] BLK --> BLKD[Block:
Shear on perimeter 2Dg Lg+Bg
+ Block Weight Wg] BOTH --> BOTHD[Compute BOTH
Take Lower] IND --> INDD[Individual:
Sum of n piles
+ Total Pile Weight] ``` ## 4.4 Text-Based Schematic — Pile Group Plan and Elevation ``` PLAN VIEW (3 x 3 Pile Group, s = 1.5 m): + - - - - - - - - - - - - - + | P P P | | | | P P P | | | | P P P | | | + - - - - - - - - - - - - - + L_g = B_g = 3.5 m ELEVATION VIEW (Block Failure Surface): Tension T applied | v ======|======= Ground Surface | | | | P | P P | <- Piles | | | | P | P P | | | | | P | P P | | | | ================= Pile Tip (no end bearing in uplift) D_g = 12 m | v Clay Layer (c_u = 30 kPa) Block Shear Surface: 2 x D_g x (L_g + B_g) ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 (3 Marks) — Conceptual **Q:** Differentiate between the **ultimate uplift capacity** and the **allowable uplift capacity** of a pile in clay. Why is **F.S. = 2.5 to 3.0** typically adopted for uplift piles in clay? **Model Answer:** The **ultimate uplift capacity ($Q_u$)** is the maximum tensile load at which the pile-soil system fails, computed as: $$Q_u = \alpha \bar{c}_u A_s + W_p$$ The **allowable uplift capacity ($Q_a$)** is the safe working load obtained by dividing $Q_u$ by a Factor of Safety: $$Q_a = \frac{Q_u}{F.S.}$$ A higher F.S. (2.5–3.0) is used for uplift piles because: (i) failure is **catastrophic** (no warning like progressive settlement), (ii) the load direction is **less predictable** (e.g., wind, wave, seismic), and (iii) the **α-factor itself is empirical** and inherently less reliable than compressive pile data. > **Marks Distribution:** Definition of $Q_u$ (1), Definition of $Q_a$ (1), Reasons for higher F.S. (1). ### Question A2 (3 Marks) — Conceptual **Q:** Why is **end-bearing resistance ignored** in computing the uplift capacity of a pile, whereas it is a major component in compressive capacity? **Model Answer:** In **compression**, the pile tip pushes against the soil below, mobilizing the full **end-bearing resistance** $Q_b = c_u N_c A_b$ (in clay). In **uplift**, the pile is pulled **upward** — there is **no soil below the tip** to resist the upward motion. The tip cannot "push against" the soil; instead, it would simply **separate** from the underlying soil. Hence, end-bearing contributes **zero resistance** in pure tension piles. > **Marks Distribution:** Compression mechanism (1), Uplift mechanism (1), Conclusion that tip resistance is zero in uplift (1). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Numerical Focus **(a) [7 Marks]** A **circular concrete pile** of **diameter 0.45 m** and **length 10 m** is driven into **soft clay** with **$\bar{c}_u$ = 25 kPa** and **α = 0.9**. The unit weight of concrete is **24 kN/m³**. Determine the **ultimate uplift capacity** and **allowable uplift capacity** with **F.S. = 2.5**. #### Model Solution for (a): **Step 1 — Surface Area of Pile Shaft:** $$A_s = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.45 \times 10 = 4.5\pi = 14.137 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Shaft Adhesion Force:** $$Q_s = \alpha \bar{c}_u A_s = 0.9 \times 25 \times 14.137 = 318.09 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3 — Pile Self-Weight:** $$V = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.45)^2 \times 10 = 1.5904 \text{ m}^3$$ $$W_p = V \gamma_c = 1.5904 \times 24 = 38.17 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4 — Ultimate Uplift Capacity:** $$Q_u = Q_s + W_p = 318.09 + 38.17 = 356.26 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 5 — Allowable Uplift Capacity:** $$Q_a = \frac{Q_u}{F.S.} = \frac{356.26}{2.5} = 142.50 \text{ kN}$$ $$\boxed{Q_u = 356.26 \text{ kN} \quad ; \quad Q_a = 142.50 \text{ kN}}$$ --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A **pile group** of **4 piles** (2 × 2) with **diameter 0.45 m**, **length 10 m**, **spacing s = 1.35 m (3D)** is installed in the **same clay** as in part (a). Compute the **ultimate uplift capacity of the group** by: (i) **Individual pile method**, and (ii) **Block failure method**. Take $\gamma_{\text{clay}}$ = 18 kN/m³, $\gamma_c$ = 24 kN/m³. Use the lower value. #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1 — Group Geometry:** - Number of piles: $n = 4$ - Group dimensions: $L_g = B_g = (2-1) \times 1.35 + 0.45 = 1.80 \text{ m}$ - Group depth: $D_g = 10 \text{ m}$ **Step 2 (i) — Individual Pile Method:** From part (a): $Q_u = 356.26$ kN per pile. $$Q_{ug,\text{ind}} = n \cdot Q_u = 4 \times 356.26 = 1425.04 \text{ kN}$$ Total pile weight: $W_{pg} = 4 \times 38.17 = 152.68$ kN (already included in each $Q_u$). **Step 3 (ii) — Block Failure Method:** Block shaft resistance: $$Q_{s,\text{block}} = 2 D_g (L_g + B_g) \alpha c_u = 2 \times 10 \times (1.80 + 1.80) \times 0.9 \times 25$$ $$Q_{s,\text{block}} = 2 \times 10 \times 3.6 \times 0.9 \times 25 = 1620 \text{ kN}$$ Block weight (soil + piles): $$W_s = L_g B_g D_g \gamma_{\text{clay}} = 1.80 \times 1.80 \times 10 \times 18 = 583.20 \text{ kN}$$ $$W_{pg} = 152.68 \text{ kN}$$ $$W_g = 583.20 + 152.68 = 735.88 \text{ kN}$$ Total block uplift: $$Q_{ug,\text{block}} = Q_{s,\text{block}} + W_g = 1620 + 735.88 = 2355.88 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4 — Governing Value:** $$Q_{ug} = \min(1425.04, \ 2355.88) = 1425.04 \text{ kN}$$ $$\boxed{Q_{ug} = 1425.04 \text{ kN} \quad \text{(Individual pile method governs)}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice (Conceptual + Numerical) **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the **two failure modes** of a pile group under **uplift loading** in clay. Under what conditions does each mode govern? #### Model Solution for (a): **Mode 1 — Individual Pile Failure (Pile-by-Pile Pull-out):** - Each pile acts independently, pulled out with its own cylindrical soil plug. - Resistance = sum of individual shaft friction + individual pile weights. - **Governs when** pile spacing $s/D \geq 6$ (i.e., piles do not interact through soil). **Mode 2 — Block Failure (Group Block Pull-out):** - The entire group, together with the soil enclosed between the piles, moves upward as a single block. - Failure surface is a **vertical cylindrical/prismatic surface** at the outer perimeter of the group. - Resistance = shear along block perimeter + weight of (soil block + piles). - **Governs when** pile spacing $s/D \leq 3$ (i.e., soil between piles moves with the group). **Decision Rule:** The **lower** of the two computed capacities is the actual group uplift capacity. > **Marks Distribution:** Sketch (2), Mode 1 description (2), Mode 2 description (2), Governing condition (1). --- **(b) [7 Marks]** An **under-reamed (belled) pile** has the following data: **shaft diameter 0.5 m**, **bell diameter 1.0 m**, **shaft length 8 m**, **bell depth from ground = 9 m**, installed in **clay with $\bar{c}_u$ = 40 kPa** and **α = 0.75**. Unit weight of concrete = 24 kN/m³. Compute the **ultimate uplift capacity** (ignore tip suction). #### Model Solution for (b): **Step 1 — Pile Shaft Volume (cylindrical part):** $$V_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.5)^2 \times 8 = 1.5708 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 2 — Bell Volume (frustum, approximated as cylinder of bell diameter over bell height):** Bell height $h_b = 9 - 8 = 1$ m, bell diameter $D_b = 1.0$ m: $$V_{\text{bell}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (1.0)^2 \times 1 = 0.7854 \text{ m}^3$$ **Step 3 — Total Pile Volume and Weight:** $$V_p = 1.5708 + 0.7854 = 2.3562 \text{ m}^3$$ $$W_p = 2.3562 \times 24 = 56.55 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4 — Shaft Adhesion Force:** $$A_s = \pi D L = \pi \times 0.5 \times 8 = 12.566 \text{ m}^2$$ $$Q_s = \alpha \bar{c}_u A_s = 0.75 \times 40 \times 12.566 = 376.99 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 5 — Bell Uplift Resistance (Projected Shear Area):** The bell mobilizes shear on the **projected vertical annular area** around the bell: $$A_b' = \frac{\pi}{4} (D_b^2 - D^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} (1.0^2 - 0.5^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.75 = 0.589 \text{ m}^2$$ $$Q_{\text{bell}} = c_u \cdot A_b' = 40 \times 0.589 = 23.56 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 6 — Total Ultimate Uplift Capacity:** $$Q_u = Q_s + Q_{\text{bell}} + W_p = 376.99 + 23.56 + 56.55 = 457.10 \text{ kN}$$ $$\boxed{Q_u \approx 457.10 \text{ kN}}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in Uplift Capacity Numerical Problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting $W_p$ (Pile Self-Weight):** Many students compute only $\alpha \bar{c}_u A_s$ and forget the gravity term. **Penalty: 2 to 3 marks.** > 2. **Wrong Area Formula:** Using $A = \pi D^2$ (cross-sectional) instead of $A_s = \pi D L$ (shaft surface). **Penalty: 2 marks.** > 3. **Using $\pi$ = 3.14 instead of 3.1416:** Numerical answers vary by 0.05%. Examiners usually allow ±1% tolerance. > 4. **In Group problems, computing only ONE method:** Always show **both** individual and block failure methods. **Penalty: Up to 5 marks.** > 5. **Using $Q_b$ (end-bearing) in uplift:** This is a **conceptual error** — no end-bearing in uplift. **Penalty: 1 to 2 marks.** > 6. **Wrong F.S. application:** F.S. is applied to **$Q_u$**, not to $Q_s$ alone. **Penalty: 1 mark.** > 7. **Not specifying units (kN vs kPa):** Always write the unit with the numerical answer. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Quick-Recall Bullets - **Uplift Capacity Formula (Single Pile, Clay):** $Q_u = \alpha \bar{c}_u A_s + W_p$ - **Pile Self-Weight MUST be added** — it is the only "gravity" resisting uplift apart from shaft adhesion. - **No end-bearing in uplift** — the tip cannot resist upward motion. - **α-method (total stress)** is used for clay; $\alpha$ typically ranges from **0.4 to 1.0** (lower $c_u$ → higher $\alpha$). - **Layered Clay:** Use weighted average $\bar{c}_u$ for the whole length. - **F.S. for uplift in clay** = 2.5 to 3.0 (higher than compression, which uses 2.5 to 3.0 for sand and 2.0 to 2.5 for clay). - **Pile Group — Two Failure Modes:** (a) **Individual pile** (sum of n piles) — governs at large $s/D$. (b) **Block failure** (shear on block perimeter + block weight) — governs at small $s/D$. - **Design value = MIN** of the two computed group capacities. - **Block failure shaft resistance:** $2 D_g (L_g + B_g) \alpha c_u$ (vertical perimeter shear). - **Block failure weight:** $L_g B_g D_g \gamma_{\text{soil}} + n W_p$ (soil + pile weights). - **Belled piles:** Additional resistance from shear on projected bell area $A_b' = (\pi/4)(D_b^2 - D^2)$. - **Group efficiency $\eta_u = Q_{ug} / (n \cdot Q_u)$** — can exceed 1.0 (for sand) but is generally close to 1.0 for clay in uplift. - **Spacings to remember:** $s = 3D$ is the typical KTU-given spacing, leading to **individual pile failure** usually governing. - **Default material unit weights:** $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³ (concrete), $\gamma_{\text{clay}} = 17$ to 19 kN/m³ (use 18 if not given). - **Standard test references:** IS 2911 (Part 1) – 2010, IS 2911 (Part 3) for under-reamed piles. - **Always check** whether the question is asking for **single pile** or **pile group** — this decides whether block failure is needed. ### High-Yield Diagram Reminder ``` UPLIFT PILE FORCES T (up) applied | ============= <-- Ground Level | PILE | | | Shaft adhesion (alpha x cu x As) acts DOWN | | Pile weight Wp acts DOWN | | ============ <-- Pile Tip (no end-bearing in uplift) v No resistance from tip ``` ### Final Examiner's Tip > In KTU 2024 Scheme ESE, when asked "compute the uplift capacity," the examiner expects you to: > 1. State the formula with proper units. > 2. Substitute with units shown. > 3. Show intermediate steps (not just final numbers). > 4. Provide a **boxed final answer with units** (e.g., **$Q_u = 508.94$ kN**). > 5. For groups — show **both methods** and the **selection of minimum**. Master this structure, and you will secure full marks on this topic consistently.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bearing Capacity — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultimate Bearing Capacity ($q_u$)** of a shallow foundation is defined as the **maximum gross pressure** that the soil can support at the base of the foundation without undergoing **shear failure** and without imposing settlements intolerable to the structure.

Mathematically, the **Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity** is expressed as:

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f$$

where:
- $q_{nu}$ = Net ultimate bearing capacity (kN/m² or kPa)
- $q_u$ = Ultimate bearing capacity (gross)
- $\gamma$ = Unit weight of soil above the base of foundation
- $D_f$ = Depth of foundation

> [!NOTE]
> **Allowable Bearing Capacity ($q_a$)** is obtained by dividing $q_u$ (or $q_{nu}$) by a **Factor of Safety (FOS)**, typically **3.0** for general shear failure conditions (as per IS 6403:1981, reaffirmed 2002).

$$q_a = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{FS} + \gamma D_f \quad \text{or} \quad q_a = \dfrac{q_u}{FS}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Toothpick in Cake" Intuition

Imagine pushing a **flat wooden disc** slowly down onto the surface of a **soft cake** versus a **firm cake**:

- On a **soft cake** (loose sand) → the disc sinks in easily, and the cake surface **bulges upward** around the edges gradually. This is **Local Shear Failure**.
- On a **firm cake** (dense sand/stiff clay) → the disc suddenly tilts, and the cake **cracks and heaves** sharply on one side, often accompanied by an audible sound. This is **General Shear Failure**.
- In a **medium-firm cake** (medium soil) → the failure is sudden, but the surface heave is less pronounced. This is **Punching Shear Failure**.

**In foundation engineering**, these three failure modes dictate which bearing capacity equation (with reduced or full shear strength parameters) should be used.

## 1.3 Modes of Shear Failure (Vesic's Classification)

| Failure Mode | Soil Type | Governing Parameter |
|--------------|-----------|---------------------|
| **General Shear** | Dense sand, stiff clay | Full $c$ and $\phi$ |
| **Local Shear** | Medium sand, medium clay | Modified $c' = \frac{2}{3}c$, $\phi' = \tan^{-1}(\frac{2}{3}\tan\phi)$ |
| **Punching Shear** | Loose sand, soft clay | Significantly reduced parameters |

## 1.4 Why Numerical Problems Dominate Module 2

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2 of PECET635 (Advanced Foundation Engineering) concentrates on **analytical computation of bearing capacity** using classical theories (Terzaghi, Meyerhof, Hansen, Vesic, IS Code). Since these are **computational design problems** that form 14-mark questions in End Semester Examinations (ESE), mastering numerical problems is **non-negotiable** for securing high module marks.
