# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Bearing Capacity Theories

The classical bearing capacity equations can be categorized as:

1. **Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Theory (1943)** — Strip, square, circular foundations
2. **Meyerhof's Bearing Capacity Theory (1963)** — Shape, depth, inclination factors
3. **Hansen's Bearing Capacity Theory (1970)** — Comprehensive correction factors
4. **Vesic's Bearing Capacity Theory (1973)** — Modified Hansen's with adjusted factors
5. **IS Code Method (IS 6403:1981)** — Empirical Indian Standard approach
6. **Skempton's Method (1951)** — For purely cohesive soils ($\phi = 0$)

## 2.2 Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Equation

Terzaghi (1943) was the first to provide a comprehensive theoretical solution. For a **strip footing** under general shear failure:

$$q_u = c N_c + q N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$$

For **square and circular footings**, Terzaghi modified the equation as:

$$q_u = 1.3 c N_c + q N_q + 0.4 \gamma B N_\gamma \quad \text{(Square)}$$

$$q_u = 1.3 c N_c + q N_q + 0.3 \gamma B N_\gamma \quad \text{(Circular)}$$

**Where:**
- $c$ = Cohesion of soil (kN/m²)
- $q = \gamma D_f$ = Surcharge at foundation level (kN/m²)
- $\gamma$ = Unit weight of soil below the base of foundation (kN/m³)
- $B$ = Width (or diameter) of foundation (m)
- $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ = Terzaghi's bearing capacity factors (functions of $\phi$)

## 2.3 Meyerhof's Bearing Capacity Equation (General Form)

The most commonly used **general bearing capacity equation** is:

$$q_u = c N_c s_c d_c i_c + q N_q s_q d_q i_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma i_\gamma$$

**Where:**
- $N_c, N_q, N_\gamma$ = Meyerhof's bearing capacity factors
- $s_c, s_q, s_\gamma$ = Shape factors
- $d_c, d_q, d_\gamma$ = Depth factors
- $i_c, i_q, i_\gamma$ = Inclination factors (for inclined loads)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section — it contains every formula needed to solve Module 2 numerical problems.**

### Table 2.A — Bearing Capacity Factors

| Theory | $N_c$ | $N_q$ | $N_\gamma$ | Notes |
|--------|-------|-------|------------|-------|
| **Terzaghi** | $(N_q - 1)\cot\phi$ | $\dfrac{e^{(3\pi/4 - \phi/2)\tan\phi}}{2\cos^2(45^\circ + \phi/2)}$ | $\dfrac{(N_q - 1)\tan\phi}{2}$ (approx.) | Original 1943 theory |
| **Meyerhof** | $(N_q - 1)\cot\phi$ | $e^{\pi \tan\phi} \tan^2(45^\circ + \phi/2)$ | $(N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi)$ | Most widely used |
| **Hansen** | $(N_q - 1)\cot\phi$ | $e^{\pi \tan\phi} \tan^2(45^\circ + \phi/2)$ | $1.5(N_q - 1)\tan\phi$ | Comprehensive |
| **Vesic** | $(N_q - 1)\cot\phi$ | $e^{\pi \tan\phi} \tan^2(45^\circ + \phi/2)$ | $2(N_q + 1)\tan\phi$ | Modern practice |
| **IS 6403** | $5.7$ (for $\phi = 0$) | $1.0$ (for $\phi = 0$) | $0.0$ (for $\phi = 0$) | Empirical charts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Meyerhof's $N_\gamma$ formula** most commonly used in KTU exams: $N_\gamma = (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi)$

### Table 2.B — Shape Factors (Meyerhof/Hansen)

| Factor | Strip ($L/B = \infty$) | Square/Rectangular | Formula (Rectangular) |
|--------|----------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| $s_c$ | 1.0 | 1.3 (square) | $1 + \dfrac{N_q}{N_c} \cdot \dfrac{B}{L}$ |
| $s_q$ | 1.0 | 1.0 (square) | $1 + \dfrac{B}{L}\tan\phi$ |
| $s_\gamma$ | 1.0 | 0.8 (square) | $1 - 0.4 \dfrac{B}{L}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Use 1.3, 1.0, 0.8 for square footings; 1.0, 1.0, 1.0 for strip footings.**

### Table 2.C — Depth Factors (Meyerhof/Hansen)

For $\dfrac{D_f}{B} \le 1$:

$$d_c = 1 + 0.4 \dfrac{D_f}{B}$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2\tan\phi(1 - \sin\phi)^2 \cdot \dfrac{D_f}{B}$$

$$d_\gamma = 1.0$$

For $\dfrac{D_f}{B} > 1$:

$$d_c = 1 + 0.4 \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{D_f}{B}\right)$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2\tan\phi(1 - \sin\phi)^2 \cdot \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{D_f}{B}\right)$$

$$d_\gamma = 1.0$$

### Table 2.D — Skempton's Bearing Capacity Factors (Purely Cohesive Soils, $\phi = 0$)

| Condition | $N_c$ |
|-----------|-------|
| Strip footing, $D_f/B = 0$ | $5.14$ (Prandtl-Reissner exact) |
| Strip footing, $D_f/B \le 2.5$ | $5.14 \left(1 + 0.2 \dfrac{B}{L}\right)\left(1 + 0.2 \dfrac{D_f}{B}\right)$ capped at $6.2$ |
| Square footing | $6.2$ |
| Circular footing | $6.0$ |

## 2.5 Influence of Water Table — Critical Correction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests water table corrections. **Three scenarios** must be considered:

**Scenario 1:** Water table lies between base and a depth $B$ below base at distance $d_w$ from base.

- Substitute $\gamma' = \gamma_{sub} + \dfrac{d_w}{B}(\gamma - \gamma_{sub})$ in the third term
- Effective surcharge: $q' = (D_f - z_{w1})\gamma + z_{w1}\gamma'$

**Scenario 2:** Water table at base of foundation (i.e., $z_{w1} = 0$).

- Use $\gamma_{sub}$ in third term
- Surcharge $q' = (D_f - z_{w1})\gamma$

**Scenario 3:** Water table at ground surface ($z_{w1} = D_f$).

- Both surcharge and third term use $\gamma'$
- $q' = 0$ and $\gamma = \gamma_{sub}$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Critical Bearing Capacity Concern |
|-------------|-----------------------------------|
| **High-rise buildings** | Square/rectangular footings with deep basements ($D_f/B > 1$) |
| **Bridge piers** | Inclined loads due to wind/seismic action |
| **Retaining walls** | Eccentric loading from lateral earth pressure |
| **Offshore platforms** | Combined vertical, horizontal, and moment loading |
| **Towers & chimneys** | Small $B/L$ ratios, sensitivity to tilt |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix (Worked Numerical Problems)

## 3.1 PROBLEM 1 — Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity (Strip Footing, General Shear)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**
> A strip footing **1.5 m wide** is founded at a depth of **1.0 m** in a soil with unit weight $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$, cohesion $c = 25 \text{ kN/m}^2$, and angle of internal friction $\phi = 30^\circ$. Determine the **ultimate bearing capacity** using Terzaghi's theory assuming general shear failure.

### Given Data
- $B = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- $D_f = 1.0 \text{ m}$
- $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- $c = 25 \text{ kN/m}^2$
- $\phi = 30^\circ$

### Step 1: Look up Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Factors for $\phi = 30^\circ$

For $\phi = 30^\circ$ (Standard table values):

$$N_c = 30.14, \quad N_q = 18.40, \quad N_\gamma = 22.40$$

### Step 2: Calculate Surcharge ($q$)

$$q = \gamma D_f = 18 \times 1.0 = 18 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 3: Apply Terzaghi's Strip Footing Equation

$$q_u = c N_c + q N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$$

Substituting:

$$q_u = (25)(30.14) + (18)(18.40) + (0.5)(18)(1.5)(22.40)$$

Computing each term:

$$q_u = 753.5 + 331.2 + 302.4$$

$$\boxed{q_u = 1387.1 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

### Step 4: Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 1387.1 - 18 = 1369.1 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 5: Allowable Bearing Capacity (FS = 3)

$$q_a = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{FS} = \dfrac{1369.1}{3} = 456.37 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q_a = 456.37 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

---

## 3.2 PROBLEM 2 — Meyerhof's Method with Shape & Depth Factors (Square Footing)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> A square footing **2.5 m × 2.5 m** is placed at a depth of **1.8 m** in a sandy soil with $\phi = 35^\circ$, $c = 0$, and $\gamma = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$. Calculate the ultimate and safe bearing capacity using Meyerhof's theory. Take **FOS = 3**.

### Given Data
- $B = L = 2.5 \text{ m}$
- $D_f = 1.8 \text{ m}$
- $c = 0$, $\phi = 35^\circ$
- $\gamma = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- FS = 3

### Step 1: Calculate Meyerhof's Bearing Capacity Factors for $\phi = 35^\circ$

$$N_q = e^{\pi \tan 35^\circ} \cdot \tan^2(45^\circ + 35^\circ/2)$$

Computing $\tan 35^\circ = 0.7002$:

$$N_q = e^{3.1416 \times 0.7002} \cdot \tan^2(62.5^\circ)$$

$$N_q = e^{2.1995} \cdot (1.92098)^2 = 9.0183 \times 3.690 = 33.30$$

$$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi = (33.30 - 1)\cot 35^\circ = 32.30 \times 1.4281 = 46.13$$

$$N_\gamma = (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi) = (33.30 - 1)\tan(49^\circ) = 32.30 \times 1.1504 = 37.16$$

### Step 2: Calculate Shape Factors (Square: $B/L = 1$)

$$s_c = 1 + \dfrac{N_q}{N_c} \cdot \dfrac{B}{L} = 1 + \dfrac{33.30}{46.13} \cdot 1 = 1 + 0.7219 = 1.722$$

$$s_q = 1 + \dfrac{B}{L}\tan\phi = 1 + 1 \cdot 0.7002 = 1.700$$

$$s_\gamma = 1 - 0.4 \cdot \dfrac{B}{L} = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6$$

### Step 3: Calculate Depth Factors

For $D_f/B = 1.8/2.5 = 0.72 \le 1$:

$$d_c = 1 + 0.4 \times 0.72 = 1.288$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2\tan 35^\circ(1 - \sin 35^\circ)^2 \times 0.72$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2(0.7002)(1 - 0.5736)^2 \times 0.72$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2(0.7002)(0.4264)^2 \times 0.72$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2(0.7002)(0.1818) \times 0.72 = 1 + 0.1834 = 1.183$$

$$d_\gamma = 1.0$$

### Step 4: Substitute in Meyerhof's Equation

Since $c = 0$, first and second terms reduce:

$$q_u = q N_q s_q d_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma$$

Computing $q = \gamma D_f = 19 \times 1.8 = 34.2 \text{ kN/m}^2$:

**Term 2 (Surcharge term):**

$$q N_q s_q d_q = 34.2 \times 33.30 \times 1.700 \times 1.183$$

$$= 34.2 \times 33.30 \times 2.0111 = 34.2 \times 66.97 = 2290.4 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Term 3 (Self-weight term):**

$$0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma = 0.5 \times 19 \times 2.5 \times 37.16 \times 0.6 \times 1.0$$

$$= 0.5 \times 19 \times 2.5 \times 22.30 = 529.6 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 5: Final Ultimate Bearing Capacity

$$q_u = 0 + 2290.4 + 529.6 = 2820.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q_u = 2820.0 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

### Step 6: Safe Bearing Capacity

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 2820.0 - 34.2 = 2785.8 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$q_a = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{FS} + \gamma D_f = \dfrac{2785.8}{3} + 34.2 = 928.6 + 34.2$$

$$\boxed{q_a = 962.8 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

---

## 3.3 PROBLEM 3 — Water Table Correction (KTU's Favourite!)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**
> A strip footing **2.0 m wide** rests at a depth of **1.5 m** in a soil with $\gamma = 17.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $\gamma_{sub} = 9.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $c = 30 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $\phi = 25^\circ$. The water table is located at a depth of **0.75 m** below the ground surface. Calculate the **net ultimate bearing capacity** using Terzaghi's theory.

### Given Data
- $B = 2.0 \text{ m}$, $D_f = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- $\gamma = 17.5$, $\gamma_{sub} = 9.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$
- $c = 30 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $\phi = 25^\circ$
- Water table at $z_{w1} = D_f - 0.75 = 0.75 \text{ m}$ below the base

### Step 1: Terzaghi's Factors for $\phi = 25^\circ$

$$N_c = 25.13, \quad N_q = 12.72, \quad N_\gamma = 9.01$$

### Step 2: Effective Surcharge ($q'$)

Water table is at 0.75 m below ground surface, i.e., 0.75 m above the base:

$$q' = (D_f - z_{w1})\gamma + z_{w1}\gamma' = (1.5 - 0.75)(17.5) + (0.75)(9.5)$$

$$q' = 0.75 \times 17.5 + 0.75 \times 9.5 = 13.125 + 7.125 = 20.25 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 3: Effective Unit Weight Below Base

Distance of water table below base: $d_w = 0.75 \text{ m}$

$$\bar{\gamma} = \gamma' + \dfrac{d_w}{B}(\gamma - \gamma_{sub}) = 9.5 + \dfrac{0.75}{2.0}(17.5 - 9.5)$$

$$\bar{\gamma} = 9.5 + 0.375 \times 8.0 = 9.5 + 3.0 = 12.5 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

### Step 4: Apply Modified Terzaghi's Equation

$$q_u = c N_c + q' N_q + 0.5 \bar{\gamma} B N_\gamma$$

$$q_u = (30)(25.13) + (20.25)(12.72) + (0.5)(12.5)(2.0)(9.01)$$

$$q_u = 753.9 + 257.58 + 112.625$$

$$\boxed{q_u = 1124.1 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

### Step 5: Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 1124.1 - (17.5)(1.5) = 1124.1 - 26.25$$

$$\boxed{q_{nu} = 1097.85 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

---

## 3.4 PROBLEM 4 — Skempton's Method for Clay (Purely Cohesive Soil)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
> A square footing **3.0 m × 3.0 m** is to be placed at **1.5 m depth** in a saturated clay with $c_u = 80 \text{ kN/m}^2$ and $\phi_u = 0$. Calculate the safe bearing capacity using Skempton's method. Take **FOS = 3**.

### Given Data
- $B = L = 3.0 \text{ m}$, $D_f = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- $c_u = 80 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $\phi_u = 0$
- Square footing

### Step 1: Skempton's $N_c$ for Square Footing

For a **square footing on purely cohesive soil**:

$$N_c = 6.2 \quad \text{(constant for } D_f/B \le 2.5 \text{)}$$

### Step 2: Ultimate Bearing Capacity

For $\phi = 0$, $N_q = 1.0$ and $N_\gamma = 0$:

$$q_u = 1.3 c_u N_c + \gamma D_f N_q = 1.3(80)(6.2) + (1.5)(18)(1.0)$$

$$q_u = 644.8 + 27.0 = 671.8 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 3: Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 671.8 - 27.0 = 644.8 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 4: Safe Bearing Capacity

$$q_a = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{FS} = \dfrac{644.8}{3} = 214.93 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q_a \approx 215 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

---

## 3.5 PROBLEM 5 — Eccentrically Loaded Foundation (Effective Width Method)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
> A rectangular footing **3.0 m × 2.0 m** carries a vertical load of **1200 kN** and a moment of **240 kN·m** about the axis parallel to the longer side. The depth of foundation is **1.5 m**. The soil has $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $c = 0$, $\phi = 30^\circ$. Determine the **factor of safety** against bearing failure using Meyerhof's effective width method.

### Given Data
- $B = 2.0 \text{ m}$ (width, perpendicular to eccentricity)
- $L = 3.0 \text{ m}$ (length, parallel to eccentricity direction)
- $P = 1200 \text{ kN}$, $M = 240 \text{ kN·m}$
- $D_f = 1.5 \text{ m}$
- $\gamma = 18 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $\phi = 30^\circ$, $c = 0$

### Step 1: Calculate Eccentricity

$$e = \dfrac{M}{P} = \dfrac{240}{1200} = 0.2 \text{ m}$$

**Check:** $e \le B/6 = 2.0/6 = 0.333$ m ✓ (No tension, effective width concept valid)

### Step 2: Effective Width

$$B' = B - 2e = 2.0 - 2(0.2) = 1.6 \text{ m}$$

$$L' = L = 3.0 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3: Effective Area

$$A' = B' \times L' = 1.6 \times 3.0 = 4.8 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 4: Meyerhof's Factors for $\phi = 30^\circ$

$$N_q = 18.40, \quad N_\gamma = (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4 \times 30^\circ) = 17.40 \times \tan(42^\circ) = 17.40 \times 0.9004 = 15.67$$

### Step 5: Shape Factors (for $B'/L' = 1.6/3.0 = 0.533$)

$$s_q = 1 + \dfrac{B'}{L'}\tan\phi = 1 + 0.533 \times 0.5774 = 1.308$$

$$s_\gamma = 1 - 0.4 \cdot \dfrac{B'}{L'} = 1 - 0.4(0.533) = 0.787$$

### Step 6: Effective Stress at Base

$$q' = \gamma D_f = 18 \times 1.5 = 27 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 7: Ultimate Bearing Capacity (using $B'$)

$$q_u = q' N_q s_q d_q + 0.5 \gamma B' N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma$$

Assuming $d_q = d_\gamma = 1$ (approximation):

$$q_u = (27)(18.40)(1.308)(1) + 0.5(18)(1.6)(15.67)(0.787)(1)$$

$$q_u = 649.5 + 177.6 = 827.1 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

### Step 8: Ultimate Load Capacity

$$Q_u = q_u \times A' = 827.1 \times 4.8 = 3970 \text{ kN}$$

### Step 9: Factor of Safety

$$FS = \dfrac{Q_u}{P} = \dfrac{3970}{1200} = 3.31$$

$$\boxed{FS \approx 3.31 \text{ (Safe against bearing failure)}}$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Failure Mechanism Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Foundation Load Applied] --> B[Stress Increases Below Base]
    B --> C{Soil Density?}
    C -->|Dense Sand / Stiff Clay| D[General Shear Failure]
    C -->|Medium Soil| E[Local Shear Failure]
    C -->|Loose Sand / Soft Clay| F[Punching Shear Failure]
    D --> D1[Sudden Heave on Sides]
    D --> D2[Distinct Failure Surface]
    D --> D3[Use Full c and phi]
    E --> E1[Gradual Compression]
    E --> E2[Partial Failure Surface]
    E --> E3[Use Modified c' and phi']
    F --> F1[Foundation Punches Down]
    F --> F2[No Visible Heave]
    F --> F3[Use Significantly Reduced Parameters]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Bearing Capacity Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Input: B, Df, c, phi, gamma, FS, Water Table] --> CF[Compute Bearing Capacity Factors Nc, Nq, Ngamma]
    CF --> SF[Apply Shape Factors sc, sq, s_gamma]
    SF --> DF[Apply Depth Factors dc, dq, d_gamma]
    DF --> IF[Apply Inclination Factors ic, iq, i_gamma]
    IF --> WT[Apply Water Table Corrections]
    WT --> AGG[Aggregate All Three Terms]
    AGG --> QU[Compute Ultimate Bearing Capacity qu]
    QU --> QNU[Compute Net Ultimate qnu = qu - gamma Df]
    QNU --> QA[Compute Safe Bearing Capacity qa = qnu / FS]
    QA --> OUTPUT[Output Design Recommendation]
```

## 4.3 Decision Matrix for Water Table Correction

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WT[Water Table Position?] --> Z1{Position 1: Below Base at depth dw}
    WT --> Z2{Position 2: At Base Level}
    WT --> Z3{Position 3: Above Base within Df}
    WT --> Z4{Position 4: At Ground Surface}
    Z1 --> C1[Use gammabar in 3rd term and q in 2nd term]
    Z2 --> C2[Use gamma_sub in 3rd term, q from above]
    Z3 --> C3[Use effective q and gamma_sub in 3rd term]
    Z4 --> C4[Use gamma_sub throughout, q=0]
```

## 4.4 Eccentric Loading — Effective Width Concept

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UN[Original Footing B x L]
        UA[Total Area A = B * L]
    end
    UN --> ECC[Eccentricity e = M / P]
    ECC --> CK{e <= B/6 ?}
    CK -->|Yes| EW[Effective Width B prime = B - 2e]
    CK -->|No| ERR[Unacceptable - Redesign Footing]
    EW --> EA[Effective Area A prime = B prime x L]
    EA --> AP[Apply Meyerhof with B prime and A prime]
    AP --> RES[Result: Reduced Ultimate Capacity]
```

## 4.5 Comparison of Failure Surfaces (Schematic Description)

```
                GENERAL SHEAR              LOCAL SHEAR           PUNCHING SHEAR
               (Dense Sand)              (Medium Sand)          (Loose Sand)
                  │                         │                        │
   Surface ───────┤                    ─────┤                  ─────┤
                  │                         │                        │
       ╱╲         │                ╱─ ─ ─╲  │               (no      │
      ╱  ╲        │               ╱       ╲ │               visible  │
     ╱    ╲       │              ╱    ╱╲   ╲│               heave)   │
    ╱      ╲      │             ╱    ╱  ╲   │                        │
   ╱________╲     │            ╱____╱    ╲__│                        │
                  │                         │                        │
   F = Pult        │           F = ½ Pult   │            F = ⅓ Pult  │
   (Maximum)       │           (Reduced)    │            (Minimum)   │
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the terms: (i) Net ultimate bearing capacity, (ii) Safe bearing capacity, and (iii) Factor of safety. How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**

> (i) **Net ultimate bearing capacity ($q_{nu}$)** is the maximum net pressure (i.e., pressure in excess of overburden pressure $\gamma D_f$) that the soil can sustain without shear failure.
>
> $$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f$$
>
> (ii) **Safe/Allowable bearing capacity ($q_a$)** is the maximum pressure that the soil can carry safely without risk of shear failure and with settlements within tolerable limits.
>
> $$q_a = \dfrac{q_u}{F} \quad \text{or} \quad q_a = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{F} + \gamma D_f$$
>
> (iii) **Factor of safety (FS)** is the ratio of ultimate load to the working/safe load on the foundation.
>
> $$FS = \dfrac{q_u}{q_a}$$
>
> Typical values of FS: **2.5 to 3.0** for Terzaghi/Meyerhof, **3.0** for IS Code.

---

### Question A2: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is the significance of bearing capacity factors $N_c$, $N_q$, and $N_\gamma$? State how they are related to the angle of internal friction $\phi$.**

**Model Answer:**

> The bearing capacity factors $N_c$, $N_q$, and $N_\gamma$ are **dimensionless coefficients** that quantify the contribution of soil cohesion, surcharge, and self-weight respectively to the ultimate bearing capacity.
>
> - $N_c$ → Contribution of **cohesion** ($c N_c$)
> - $N_q$ → Contribution of **surcharge** ($q N_q$)
> - $N_\gamma$ → Contribution of **soil self-weight** ($0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$)
>
> They are **functions of $\phi$ only** (for a given theory). General relationship:
>
> $$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$$
>
> As $\phi$ increases from $0°$ to $45°$, $N_c$, $N_q$, $N_\gamma$ all increase **non-linearly** (exponentially for $N_q$). For purely cohesive soil ($\phi = 0$), $N_q = 1$ and $N_\gamma = 0$.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### 🔷 Question Choice A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2]**
> A square footing **3.0 m × 3.0 m** is to be constructed at a depth of **2.0 m** in a soil with the following properties: $\gamma = 19 \text{ kN/m}^3$, $c = 15 \text{ kN/m}^2$, $\phi = 25°$. The water table is at a depth of **1.0 m** below the base of the foundation. Compute:
>
> **(a)** The **net ultimate bearing capacity** using Terzaghi's method. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The **safe bearing capacity** and **net safe bearing capacity** with FOS = 3. Also check the suitability of the footing for a column load of **1500 kN**. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a): Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity

**Given:**
- $B = L = 3.0$ m, $D_f = 2.0$ m
- $\gamma = 19$ kN/m³, $c = 15$ kN/m², $\phi = 25°$
- Water table is 1.0 m **below** the base of foundation
- $\gamma_{sub} = 19 - 9.81 = 9.19 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (assuming standard)

**Step 1: Terzaghi's factors for $\phi = 25°$**

$$N_c = 25.13, \quad N_q = 12.72, \quad N_\gamma = 9.01$$

**Step 2: Surcharge ($q$)**

Since water table is below the base, the surcharge at the base is not affected by it:

$$q = \gamma D_f = 19 \times 2.0 = 38 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Effective unit weight below base**

Water table is at $d_w = 1.0$ m below the base (and $B = 3.0$ m), so:

$$\bar{\gamma} = \gamma' + \dfrac{d_w}{B}(\gamma - \gamma_{sub}) = 9.19 + \dfrac{1.0}{3.0}(19 - 9.19)$$

$$\bar{\gamma} = 9.19 + 0.333 \times 9.81 = 9.19 + 3.27 = 12.46 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**Step 4: Apply Terzaghi's equation for square footing**

$$q_u = 1.3 c N_c + q N_q + 0.4 \bar{\gamma} B N_\gamma$$

$$q_u = 1.3 \times 15 \times 25.13 + 38 \times 12.72 + 0.4 \times 12.46 \times 3.0 \times 9.01$$

$$q_u = 490.04 + 483.36 + 134.71 = 1108.11 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5: Net ultimate bearing capacity**

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 1108.11 - 38 = 1070.11 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q_{nu} = 1070.11 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

#### Solution to Part (b): Safe Bearing Capacity & Footing Suitability

**Step 1: Safe bearing capacity**

$$q_{ns} = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{FS} = \dfrac{1070.11}{3} = 356.7 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 2: Net safe bearing capacity (including overburden)**

$$q_{sa} = q_{ns} + \gamma D_f = 356.7 + 38 = 394.7 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 3: Actual pressure under footing for 1500 kN load**

$$q_{actual} = \dfrac{P}{A} + \gamma D_f = \dfrac{1500}{3 \times 3} + 38 = 166.67 + 38 = 204.67 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4: Check safety**

$$FS_{actual} = \dfrac{q_u \times A}{P} = \dfrac{1108.11 \times 9}{1500} = 6.65$$

Since $q_{actual} = 204.67 < q_{sa} = 394.7 \text{ kN/m}^2$, the **footing is SAFE** and the design is conservative.

$$\boxed{\text{Foundation is safe; } FS_{actual} = 6.65 > 3 \text{ (acceptable)}}$$

---

### 🔷 Question Choice B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper 1]**
> A **rectangular footing 4.0 m × 2.5 m** is founded at a depth of **2.0 m** in a c-$\phi$ soil with $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, $c = 25$ kN/m², $\phi = 30°$. The footing is subjected to a **vertical load of 1800 kN** and a **moment of 200 kN·m** about the axis parallel to the 4.0 m side.
>
> **(a)** Compute the **ultimate bearing capacity** using Meyerhof's theory, accounting for the eccentricity. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Determine the **factor of safety** against bearing failure. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a): Ultimate Bearing Capacity

**Given:**
- $B = 2.5$ m (perpendicular to eccentricity direction)
- $L = 4.0$ m (parallel to eccentricity direction)
- $D_f = 2.0$ m, $P = 1800$ kN, $M = 200$ kN·m
- $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, $c = 25$ kN/m², $\phi = 30°$

**Step 1: Compute eccentricity**

$$e = \dfrac{M}{P} = \dfrac{200}{1800} = 0.111 \text{ m}$$

**Check:** $e = 0.111 < B/6 = 0.417$ m ✓

**Step 2: Effective dimensions**

$$B' = B - 2e = 2.5 - 2(0.111) = 2.5 - 0.222 = 2.278 \text{ m}$$

$$L' = L = 4.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Meyerhof's bearing capacity factors for $\phi = 30°$**

$$N_q = 18.40$$
$$N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot 30° = 17.40 \times 1.7321 = 30.14$$
$$N_\gamma = (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4 \times 30°) = 17.40 \times \tan(42°) = 17.40 \times 0.9004 = 15.67$$

**Step 4: Shape factors for $B'/L' = 2.278/4.0 = 0.5695$**

$$s_c = 1 + \dfrac{N_q}{N_c} \cdot \dfrac{B'}{L'} = 1 + \dfrac{18.40}{30.14} \times 0.5695 = 1 + 0.6104 \times 0.5695 = 1.348$$

$$s_q = 1 + \dfrac{B'}{L'}\tan\phi = 1 + 0.5695 \times 0.5774 = 1.329$$

$$s_\gamma = 1 - 0.4 \dfrac{B'}{L'} = 1 - 0.4(0.5695) = 1 - 0.228 = 0.772$$

**Step 5: Depth factors for $D_f/B = 2.0/2.5 = 0.8$**

$$d_c = 1 + 0.4(0.8) = 1.32$$

$$d_q = 1 + 2\tan 30°(1 - \sin 30°)^2 \times 0.8$$
$$= 1 + 2(0.5774)(0.25)(0.8) = 1 + 0.231 = 1.231$$

$$d_\gamma = 1.0$$

**Step 6: Surcharge**

$$q = \gamma D_f = 18 \times 2.0 = 36 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 7: Compute three terms of Meyerhof's equation**

**Term 1 (Cohesion term):**

$$c N_c s_c d_c = 25 \times 30.14 \times 1.348 \times 1.32 = 1341.7 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Term 2 (Surcharge term):**

$$q N_q s_q d_q = 36 \times 18.40 \times 1.329 \times 1.231 = 1082.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Term 3 (Self-weight term):**

$$0.5 \gamma B' N_\gamma s_\gamma d_\gamma = 0.5 \times 18 \times 2.278 \times 15.67 \times 0.772 \times 1.0$$
$$= 0.5 \times 18 \times 2.278 \times 12.097 = 248.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 8: Ultimate Bearing Capacity**

$$q_u = 1341.7 + 1082.0 + 248.0 = 2671.7 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{q_u = 2671.7 \text{ kN/m}^2}$$

#### Solution to Part (b): Factor of Safety

**Step 1: Ultimate load capacity**

$$Q_u = q_u \times A' = 2671.7 \times (2.278 \times 4.0) = 2671.7 \times 9.112 = 24345 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Factor of Safety**

$$FS = \dfrac{Q_u}{P} = \dfrac{24345}{1800} = 13.53$$

$$\boxed{FS = 13.53 \text{ (extremely safe — footing may be oversized)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Most common mistake (2-3 marks lost):** Forgetting to apply **all three corrections** — shape ($s$), depth ($d$), and effective width ($B'$) — simultaneously. Students often use $B$ instead of $B'$ in the self-weight term.
> 2. **Second pitfall (1-2 marks lost):** Using **Meyerhof's $N_\gamma$ formula incorrectly**. Two versions exist: $N_\gamma = (N_q-1)\tan(1.4\phi)$ (Meyerhof) vs $N_\gamma = 1.5(N_q-1)\tan\phi$ (Hansen). **Use Meyerhof's form** unless the problem specifically mentions Hansen.
> 3. **Third pitfall:** Not verifying the eccentricity check $e \le B/6$. If $e > B/6$, the entire effective width concept fails and a different analysis is needed.
> 4. **Marks lost in unit errors:** Always keep **kN/m² (kPa)** consistent throughout. When multiplying $c \times N_c$, the result is in kN/m².
> 5. **Common error in water table problems:** Using $\gamma$ instead of $\gamma'$ (submerged unit weight) in the surcharge term when the water table is at or near the base.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 Numerical Problems**

- **Bearing Capacity Factor Formulas (must memorize):**
  - $N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \tan^2(45° + \phi/2)$
  - $N_c = (N_q - 1)\cot\phi$
  - $N_\gamma = (N_q - 1)\tan(1.4\phi)$ [Meyerhof]
  - $N_\gamma = 1.5(N_q - 1)\tan\phi$ [Hansen]
  - $N_\gamma = 2(N_q + 1)\tan\phi$ [Vesic]

- **For $\phi = 0$ (purely cohesive):** $N_q = 1$, $N_\gamma = 0$, $N_c = 5.14$ (strip) or $6.2$ (square) or $6.0$ (circular)

- **Terzaghi's equation is the simplest** — only $c$, $q$, $\gamma B$ terms multiplied by factors. **No shape/depth corrections** built in (you have to multiply by 1.3, 1.0, 0.4 manually for square footings).

- **Meyerhof's equation is the most general** — uses $s_c, s_q, s_\gamma$ (shape) and $d_c, d_q, d_\gamma$ (depth) factors.

- **Water table corrections:** Three scenarios — water table at base, above base, or below base. Always use $\gamma'$ (submerged) below water table.

- **Eccentric loading:** Use **effective width $B' = B - 2e$** and **effective area $A' = B' \times L'$**.

- **Inclined loading:** Use **inclination factors** $i_c, i_q, i_\gamma < 1$.

- **Factor of Safety (typical):**
  - **3.0** for general shear failure (Terzaghi)
  - **2.5 to 3.0** for local shear failure
  - **3.0 to 4.0** for punching shear failure

- **Standard bearing capacity factor values to remember:**

  | $\phi$ | $N_c$ | $N_q$ | $N_\gamma$ (Meyerhof) |
  |--------|-------|-------|------------------------|
  | 0°     | 5.14  | 1.0   | 0.0                    |
  | 10°    | 8.35  | 2.47  | 1.10                   |
  | 20°    | 14.83 | 6.40  | 5.27                   |
  | 25°    | 25.13 | 12.72 | 9.01                   |
  | 30°    | 30.14 | 18.40 | 15.67                  |
  | 35°    | 46.13 | 33.30 | 37.16                  |
  | 40°    | 75.31 | 64.20 | 78.40                  |
  | 45°    | 134.86| 134.86| 196.0                  |

- **Numerical problem strategy:**
  1. Identify soil type (c-$\phi$ or purely cohesive)
  2. Select appropriate theory (Terzaghi for simple, Meyerhof for general)
  3. Look up or compute bearing capacity factors
  4. Apply shape and depth corrections (for Meyerhof)
  5. Apply water table correction (if applicable)
  6. Compute $q_u$, $q_{nu}$, $q_a$

- **Final answer units:** Always express in **kN/m²** (or kPa). Use **3 significant figures** unless otherwise specified.

> **End of Module 2 — Numerical Problems Notes**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Under-Reamed Piles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Under-Reamed Piles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

An **Under-Reamed Pile** is a special type of bored cast-in-situ concrete pile that possesses one or more **bulbs** (enlarged cavities) formed at specific intervals along its straight shaft by means of an under-reamer. The enlarged bulb diameter is typically **2 to 3 times** the shaft diameter, while bulb spacing is generally **1.25 to 1.5 times** the bulb diameter. The design and construction of these piles in India is governed by **IS 2911 (Part III) – 1980** (Reaffirmed 2010).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECET635 Module 3):**
> Under-reamed piles are an essential topic for expansive and black cotton soils. Students must master the concept of bulb formation, load transfer mechanics (end-bearing through bulbs + shaft friction), spacing criteria (≥ 2D for group action, ≥ 1.25D_b for bulb spacing), and IS 2911 Part III specifications.

**Key Nomenclature (IS 2911 Part III):**

| Symbol | Description | Typical Value |
|--------|-------------|---------------|
| $D$ | Shaft diameter | **200 mm to 500 mm** |
| $D_b$ | Bulb diameter | **2D to 3D** (typically **2.5D**) |
| $L$ | Pile depth | **3 m to 8 m** (typical) |
| $S_b$ | Centre-to-centre bulb spacing | **1.25 $D_b$ to 1.5 $D_b$** |
| $N_b$ | Number of bulbs | **1 to 3** (single, double, triple) |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine hammering a plain cylindrical stick into soft ground – it will offer some resistance through skin friction but may pull out easily. Now imagine a stick with one or more **mushroom-shaped knobs** along its length, like a chicory root (chicorium). The bulbs act like **anchors** that lock the pile into the surrounding soil.

**Real-world analogy:** Think of an under-reamed pile like a **clothes hook** driven into a plaster wall. The straight shank provides friction, but the expanded head (bulb) prevents it from being pulled out. The bulb does the heavy lifting of providing bearing resistance, while the shaft transfers the load down via skin friction.

**Why use them?** In expansive soils like **black cotton soil** (found extensively in Central India – Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh, Gujarat, Karnataka), the soil swells when wet and shrinks when dry. A straight pile in such soil can heave up and down seasonally, damaging the structure. Under-reamed piles are anchored deep enough below the active swelling zone to remain stable year-round.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** Under-reamed piles are NOT used for very deep foundations or for very soft clays. They shine in **medium-stiff to stiff clays**, **expansive soils**, and where **moderate loads (50–500 kN)** need to be transferred within **3–8 m depth**.

---

### 1.3 Components of an Under-Reamed Pile

1. **Pile Cap** – Distributes superstructure load to the pile group.
2. **Pile Shaft** – Straight cylindrical bored section of diameter $D$.
3. **Bulb(s)** – Enlarged conical/cylindrical cavities providing end-bearing resistance.
4. **Pile Tip / Enlarged Base** – The bottom-most bulb that transfers the maximum load to soil.

### 1.4 Classification of Under-Reamed Piles

**(a) Based on Number of Bulbs:**
- **Single-bulb pile** (most common) – One bulb at the base.
- **Double-bulb pile** – Two bulbs, one above the other, used for higher loads.
- **Multi-bulb pile** – Three or more bulbs (rare, used in very soft strata).

**(b) Based on Load Transfer Mechanism:**
- **End-bearing type** – Bulb rests on a hard stratum; bulb carries >70% load.
- **Friction type** – Skin friction dominates; bulb provides additional anchorage.

**(c) Based on Construction:**
- **Bored cast-in-situ** – Hole bored, bulb reamed, concrete poured.
- **Driven precast** – Precast units with pre-formed bulbs driven into the ground (less common).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Load Transfer Mechanism – "The How and Why"

The total load capacity of an under-reamed pile is the sum of **three distinct resistance components**:

1. **End-Bearing at the Bottom Bulb** ($Q_b$) – The bulb acts as a deep footing resting on soil. The bulb's projected area is much larger than the shaft, so end-bearing is significantly enhanced.

2. **Skin Friction along the Shaft** ($Q_s$) – Adhesion between concrete shaft and surrounding soil. The shaft contributes substantially in clays due to cohesion.

3. **Shear Cones Above Each Bulb** ($Q_{bulb}$) – When a bulb tries to fail, the soil above it mobilizes in a cone shape. The weight of this soil cone + the shear strength along the cone's slanted surface provides additional uplift resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual "Why":** Why doesn't the soil above the bulb just fall into the pile? Because the bulb's enlarged diameter is greater than the shaft – it physically interlocks with the soil mass. This is the principle of **positive anchorage**.

### 2.2 Governing Code Provisions (IS 2911 Part III)

**Minimum Spacing of Piles in a Group:**
$$S_{min} = 2D \text{ (centre-to-centre)}$$

**Minimum Bulb Spacing (centre-to-centre):**
$$S_b = 1.25 \, D_b \text{ to } 1.5 \, D_b$$

**Minimum Depth of First Bulb Below Ground Level:**
$$D_{first} \geq 1.5 \, D_b$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** The minimum spacing ensures that **stress bulbs** (isobars) of adjacent piles do not overlap, preventing group efficiency loss. If piles are too close, soil between them fails as a block rather than individual piles.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $Q_u = Q_b + Q_s + Q_{bulb}$ | Ultimate load capacity (compression) | Total design load |
| 2 | $Q_b = A_b \cdot q_b = A_b \cdot c \cdot N_c$ | End-bearing at bottom bulb (clay) | Bulb on clay stratum |
| 3 | $A_b = \frac{\pi}{4} D_b^2$ | Projected bulb area | Geometry |
| 4 | $Q_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot A_s$ | Skin friction (adhesion method) | Cohesive soils |
| 5 | $A_s = \pi \cdot D \cdot L_s$ | Shaft surface area (lateral) | Geometry |
| 6 | $Q_{uplift} = W_p + Q_s + Q_{bulb,shear}$ | Uplift capacity | Expansive soils |
| 7 | $N_c = 9$ | Bearing capacity factor (deep, $\phi=0$) | $\phi = 0$ analysis (Skempton) |
| 8 | $Q_{allowable} = \frac{Q_u}{F.S.}$ | Safe load | $F.S. = 2.5$ to $3.0$ |
| 9 | $S_{min} = 2D$ | Group spacing | Group design |
| 10 | $S_b = 1.25 D_b$ to $1.5 D_b$ | Bulb spacing | Multi-bulb design |
| 11 | $D_b = 2D$ to $3D$ | Bulb diameter | Standard practice |
| 12 | $\eta_g = \frac{Q_g}{n \cdot Q_s}$ | Group efficiency | Group analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $\alpha$ = adhesion factor (0.4 to 0.9 for clays, typically **0.5** for bored piles), $\bar{c}$ = average undrained cohesion along shaft, $A_s$ = shaft-soil contact area, $W_p$ = effective weight of pile, $L_s$ = length of shaft in contact with soil, $F.S.$ = factor of safety.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Rural Housing in Black Cotton Soil Regions** – Foundation for houses, schools, hospitals in central India.
2. **Transmission Line Towers** – Anchor foundations for EHV towers subjected to uplift forces.
3. **Bridges & Culverts** – Substructure in expansive soil belts.
4. **Tanks & Silos** – Moderate-load industrial structures.
5. **Coastal Areas** – Resist uplift due to fluctuating water tables.
6. **Seismic Zones** – Provide additional anchorage in liquefiable or soft strata.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Ultimate Load Capacity of Single Under-Reamed Pile (Compression)

**Given:** A single-bulb under-reamed pile of shaft diameter $D$, bulb diameter $D_b$, shaft length $L$, with the bulb embedded in a clay layer of undrained cohesion $c$.

**Step 1: Identify all load components.**

The pile transfers load to the soil through three mechanisms:
- **End-bearing at the bottom bulb** ($Q_b$)
- **Skin friction along the shaft** ($Q_s$)
- **Bulb shear cone resistance** (negligible in pure compression loading, but included in uplift)

For pure **compression**, the dominant components are $Q_b$ and $Q_s$:
$$Q_u = Q_b + Q_s$$

**Step 2: Formulate the end-bearing capacity at the bulb.**

The bulb acts as a deep circular footing. Using the general bearing capacity equation with the assumption $\phi = 0$ (undrained clay), surcharge and self-weight terms vanish (since $c$ dominates and $N_q = 1, N_\gamma = 0$ for $\phi=0$):
$$q_b = c \cdot N_c$$

The end-bearing force is:
$$Q_b = A_b \cdot q_b = A_b \cdot c \cdot N_c$$

where $A_b$ is the projected bulb area:
$$A_b = \frac{\pi}{4} D_b^2$$

For a **deep foundation** with $L/D_b \geq 4$ (which is usually satisfied for under-reamed piles), Skempton's $N_c$ value is:
$$N_c = 9$$

So,
$$Q_b = \frac{\pi}{4} D_b^2 \cdot c \cdot 9 = \frac{9\pi c D_b^2}{4}$$

**Step 3: Formulate the skin friction (adhesion) along the shaft.**

For a bored pile in clay, skin friction is computed using the **alpha ($\alpha$) method**:
$$Q_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot A_s$$

The shaft contact area is the lateral surface of the cylindrical bore:
$$A_s = \pi D L_s$$

where $L_s$ is the effective length of the shaft contributing to friction. For a single-bulb pile, the bulb region (top + bottom of bulb) typically does not contribute to shaft friction in the simplified analysis, so $L_s = L - D_b$ approximately.

Substituting:
$$Q_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot \pi D L_s$$

**Step 4: Combine both components to get ultimate load capacity.**

$$\boxed{Q_u = \frac{9\pi c D_b^2}{4} + \alpha \bar{c} \pi D L_s}$$

**Step 5: Apply factor of safety to obtain allowable load.**

$$Q_{allowable} = \frac{Q_u}{F.S.}$$

with $F.S. = 2.5$ (IS code recommendation for individual pile load test validation) and $F.S. = 3.0$ (when validated only by static formula without load test).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Uplift Capacity of Under-Reamed Pile

**Given:** Single-bulb under-reamed pile subjected to tensile (uplift) load – typical scenario for transmission tower foundations or expansive soil heave.

**Step 1: Identify the resistance components during uplift.**

When the pile tries to pull **upward**, the following resist:
1. **Self-weight of the pile** ($W_p$) – Concrete weight minus buoyancy.
2. **Skin friction along the shaft** – Reverses in direction (acts downward, resisting uplift).
3. **Shear cone resistance above the bulb** – This is the *crucial* contribution of the bulb. Without the bulb, the pile would have no anchorage.

$$Q_{u,up} = W_p + Q_s + Q_{cone}$$

**Step 2: Compute the pile self-weight (effective).**

$$W_p = \left(\frac{\pi D^2}{4} L + \frac{\pi D_b^2}{4} D_b \right) \cdot \gamma_c - V_{displaced} \cdot \gamma_w$$

where $\gamma_c$ = unit weight of reinforced concrete (≈ **24 kN/m³**), and $V_{displaced}$ is the volume of soil displaced (equal to the pile volume for bored piles).

**Step 3: Compute the shear cone resistance above the bulb.**

The bulb, when pulled upward, mobilizes a truncated cone of soil above it. The cone has:
- Base diameter = $D_b$ (bulb diameter)
- Top diameter = $D_b + 2L_c \tan\phi$ (where $L_c$ is the cone height)
- Typical cone angle: $\beta = 30°$ to $45°$ with vertical (IS 2911 recommends $\beta = 30°$ for $\phi=0$ analysis, but uses $\beta$ based on actual $\phi$ for general soils).

For **cohesionless soil** ($\phi$ soil, $c=0$):
$$Q_{cone} = W_{cone} \cdot \tan\phi + \gamma_{soil} \cdot V_{cone} \cdot \tan\phi$$

Wait – this needs careful treatment. Let me use the **general shear cone formula**:

The shear resistance along the cone's slanted surface (length $l_c$) is:
$$Q_{cone} = (\pi/4) (D_b + 2 L_c \tan\beta)^2 \cdot c \cdot N_c' \text{ (clay)}$$

For simplicity, IS 2911 Part III Section 5 gives:
$$Q_{cone} = \frac{\pi}{4} (D_b^2 - D^2) \cdot c \cdot N_c + \pi D L_s c \text{ (simplified, clay)}$$

Let me re-derive cleanly for the most common case (clay, $\phi=0$):

For a **clay** stratum, the failure surface above the bulb is approximately cylindrical (since $\phi = 0$, no cone inclination is mobilized by friction). The shear resistance is the **adhesion along the vertical cylindrical surface** of soil above the bulb, of diameter $D_b$ and length equal to the height of the soil column:

$$Q_{cone,clay} = c_a \cdot \pi D_b L_{c,shear}$$

where $L_{c,shear}$ is the length over which the soil column can sustain shear (typically = depth of overburden above bulb).

For the **simplified IS Code Method**, the ultimate uplift capacity is:

$$\boxed{Q_{u,up} = W_p + \alpha \bar{c} \pi D L_s + c_a \pi D_b L_{c}}$$

**Step 4: Apply factor of safety.**

$$Q_{a,up} = \frac{Q_{u,up}}{F.S.}$$

with $F.S. \geq 2.5$ (IS 2911 Part III recommendation for uplift).

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Design a single under-reamed pile for the following conditions:
- Depth of pile $L = 6$ m
- Shaft diameter $D = 300$ mm = 0.3 m
- Bulb diameter $D_b = 750$ mm = 0.75 m (i.e., $D_b = 2.5D$)
- Soil: clay with $\bar{c} = 80$ kN/m², $\alpha = 0.5$
- Bulb embedment in clay: $L_b = 4$ m below ground
- Effective shaft length: $L_s = 6 - 0.75 = 5.25$ m
- $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³, $F.S. = 3.0$

**Solution:**

**Step 1: End-bearing capacity at the bulb.**

$$A_b = \frac{\pi}{4} D_b^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.75)^2 = 0.4418 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_b = A_b \cdot c \cdot N_c = 0.4418 \times 80 \times 9 = 318.1 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Skin friction along the shaft.**

$$A_s = \pi D L_s = \pi \times 0.3 \times 5.25 = 4.948 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_s = \alpha \bar{c} A_s = 0.5 \times 80 \times 4.948 = 197.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Ultimate load capacity.**

$$Q_u = Q_b + Q_s = 318.1 + 197.9 = 516.0 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Allowable load.**

$$Q_{allow} = \frac{Q_u}{F.S.} = \frac{516.0}{3.0} = 172.0 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Check minimum bulb spacing criterion (for future multi-bulb extension).**
$$S_b \geq 1.25 D_b = 1.25 \times 0.75 = 0.94 \text{ m} \quad \checkmark \text{ (Satisfied for single-bulb case)}$$

**Result:** The pile can safely carry **172 kN**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Students often forget to subtract the bulb height from the shaft length when computing skin friction. The bulb does NOT contribute to skin friction in the standard analysis – it contributes only via end-bearing. Be careful with the geometry!

---

### 3.4 Group Efficiency Calculation

For a group of $n$ piles:

$$Q_{g,ult} = n \cdot Q_{u,individual} \cdot \eta_g$$

The **group efficiency** $\eta_g$ depends on pile spacing:
- For $S = 2D$: $\eta_g \approx 0.7$ to $0.8$ (clay)
- For $S = 3D$: $\eta_g \approx 0.9$ to $1.0$
- For $S \geq 4D$: $\eta_g \approx 1.0$ (isolated pile behaviour)

For very closely spaced piles in clay, **block failure** must be checked:

$$Q_{block} = 2D (L)(B+L) c + \gamma D L^2 \text{ (simplified block formula)}$$

where $B$ = group width, $L$ = group length.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**Define an under-reamed pile and state its suitability for expansive soils.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **under-reamed pile** is a bored cast-in-situ concrete pile provided with one or more **enlarged bulbs** along its straight shaft. The bulb diameter is typically **2 to 3 times** the shaft diameter, and is formed using a special under-reaming tool.

**Suitability for expansive soils (1.5 marks):** Expansive soils (e.g., black cotton soil) undergo large volumetric changes with moisture variation – they swell when wet and shrink when dry. A plain pile in such soil would be lifted by heave. Under-reamed piles are anchored deep below the **active zone** (depth of seasonal moisture change, typically 1.5–3 m) by means of bulbs that rest on a stable stratum. The bulbs provide positive anchorage against uplift forces caused by swelling, while the shaft transfers compression loads through skin friction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**List any three advantages of under-reamed piles over straight bored piles.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Higher load capacity** – The bulb's enlarged area provides significant end-bearing resistance in addition to skin friction, increasing total capacity by **30% to 50%** over straight piles of the same length.

2. **Better anchorage against uplift** – The bulb's enlarged diameter provides positive anchorage through the soil cone above it, making it ideal for foundations subjected to tensile loads (transmission towers, expansive soil heave).

3. **Economical** – For moderate loads (50–500 kN) at shallow depths (3–8 m), under-reamed piles eliminate the need for deep, large-diameter straight piles, reducing concrete and boring costs.

*(Optional 4th point: Simple construction equipment – only a tripod and under-reamer tool, no heavy rigs required.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction procedure of a single-bulb under-reamed pile. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single under-reamed pile has the following data:
- Shaft diameter $D = 0.4$ m
- Bulb diameter $D_b = 1.0$ m
- Length of pile $L = 6$ m
- Average cohesion $\bar{c} = 100$ kN/m²
- Adhesion factor $\alpha = 0.5$
- Unit weight of concrete $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³

Compute the safe load on the pile using a factor of safety of **3.0**. Take $N_c = 9$ for clay. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Construction Procedure (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Boring the Hole (1 mark)**
A tripod pile frame is set up. A **boring tool** (auger or bailer) is used to bore a vertical hole of diameter $D$ (300–500 mm) to the design depth. The bore is kept open temporarily using a **casing pipe** if the soil is loose.

**Step 2: Lowering the Under-reamer (1.5 marks)**
A special **under-reaming tool** (a collapsible cutter that expands when rotated) is lowered into the bore on a **tremie pipe / boring rod**. The tool's expanded diameter determines the bulb diameter $D_b$.

**Step 3: Forming the Bulb (1.5 marks)**
The tool is rotated and slowly raised, cutting an **inverted truncated cone** of soil above the tool position, forming the bulb cavity. The bulb diameter is verified using a **sizing tool** before concrete placement.

**Step 4: Reinforcement and Concreting (2 marks)**
A **reinforcement cage** (typically 4 to 6 longitudinal bars of 12–16 mm dia with 8 mm helical binders at 150 mm pitch) is lowered into the bore. **Concrete of grade M20 to M30** is then poured using a **tremie pipe** to avoid segregation. The casing is gradually withdrawn.

**Step 5: Curing and Cap (1 mark)**
The pile is cured for at least **7 to 14 days**. A **pile cap** is cast on top to transfer superstructure loads.

> *(Sketch should show: ground level, casing, shaft, bulb, reinforcement cage, and concrete pour. Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$D = 0.4$ m, $D_b = 1.0$ m, $L = 6$ m, $\bar{c} = 100$ kN/m², $\alpha = 0.5$, $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³, $F.S. = 3.0$, $N_c = 9$

**Step 1: End-bearing at bulb (3 marks).**

$$A_b = \frac{\pi}{4} D_b^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (1.0)^2 = 0.7854 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_b = A_b \cdot c \cdot N_c = 0.7854 \times 100 \times 9 = 706.86 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Skin friction along shaft (2.5 marks).**

Effective shaft length (excluding bulb):
$$L_s = L - D_b = 6 - 1.0 = 5.0 \text{ m}$$

$$A_s = \pi D L_s = \pi \times 0.4 \times 5.0 = 6.283 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_s = \alpha \bar{c} A_s = 0.5 \times 100 \times 6.283 = 314.16 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Ultimate and safe load (1.5 marks).**

$$Q_u = Q_b + Q_s = 706.86 + 314.16 = 1021.02 \text{ kN}$$

$$Q_{allow} = \frac{Q_u}{F.S.} = \frac{1021.02}{3.0} = \boxed{340.34 \text{ kN}}$$

**Verification of bulb spacing criterion:** Single bulb – no spacing check needed. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of under-reamed piles. State the IS code provisions regarding minimum spacing of piles and bulbs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An under-reamed pile is subjected to an uplift load. The following data are given:
- Shaft diameter $D = 0.45$ m, Bulb diameter $D_b = 1.1$ m, Pile length $L = 5$ m
- Unit weight of concrete $\gamma_c = 25$ kN/m³
- Effective unit weight of soil above bulb $\gamma_{sub} = 10$ kN/m³
- Average cohesion $\bar{c} = 60$ kN/m², adhesion factor $\alpha = 0.6$, $c_a = 0.7 \bar{c}$

Determine the **ultimate uplift capacity** of the pile. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Advantages & Limitations + IS Code (7 marks):**

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. Higher load capacity per unit length.
2. Provides anchorage against uplift – suitable for expansive soils and tension foundations.
3. Simple equipment – no heavy rig needed.
4. Economical for 3–8 m depth.
5. Bulb can be inspected before concreting.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
1. Not suitable for **very soft clays** (bulb may not form cleanly).
2. Limited to **moderate depths** (typically ≤ 15 m).
3. **Groundwater** inflow complicates bulb formation.
4. Quality control is critical (bulb geometry must be verified).
5. Not effective in **rock** or dense gravel.

**IS 2911 (Part III) Provisions (2.5 marks):**
- **Pile-to-pile spacing** in a group: minimum **2D** (centre-to-centre), recommended **2.5D to 3D** for clay.
- **Bulb-to-bulb spacing** in multi-bulb piles: minimum **1.25 $D_b$**, ideally **1.5 $D_b$**.
- **First bulb depth**: minimum **1.5 $D_b$** below ground level or below the scour depth / active zone, whichever is greater.
- **Bulb diameter**: **2D to 3D** (preferably **2.5D**).
- **Minimum shaft diameter**: **200 mm** (for safe concreting).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution for Uplift (7 marks):**

**Given:** $D = 0.45$ m, $D_b = 1.1$ m, $L = 5$ m, $\gamma_c = 25$ kN/m³, $\gamma_{sub} = 10$ kN/m³, $\bar{c} = 60$ kN/m², $\alpha = 0.6$, $c_a = 0.7\bar{c} = 42$ kN/m².

**Step 1: Effective self-weight of pile (2 marks).**

Volume of shaft:
$$V_s = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.45)^2 (5) = 0.7952 \text{ m}^3$$

Volume of bulb (approx as cylinder of dia $D_b$ and height $D_b$):
$$V_b = \frac{\pi}{4} D_b^2 \cdot D_b = \frac{\pi}{4} (1.1)^2 (1.1) = 1.0455 \text{ m}^3$$

Total concrete volume: $V = 0.7952 + 1.0455 = 1.8407$ m³

Self-weight: $W_p = V \cdot \gamma_c = 1.8407 \times 25 = 46.02$ kN

Buoyancy correction: pile displaces same volume of soil
$$W_{buoy} = V \cdot \gamma_{sub} = 1.8407 \times 10 = 18.41 \text{ kN}$$

$$W_{p,eff} = W_p - W_{buoy} = 46.02 - 18.41 = 27.61 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Skin friction (1.5 marks).**

$$A_s = \pi D (L - D_b) = \pi \times 0.45 \times (5 - 1.1) = \pi \times 0.45 \times 3.9 = 5.513 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_s = \alpha \bar{c} A_s = 0.6 \times 60 \times 5.513 = 198.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Shear cone above bulb (2.5 marks).**

Height of soil cone above bulb: conservatively take as full pile length above bulb, $L_c = L - D_b = 3.9$ m.
$$Q_{cone} = c_a \cdot \pi D_b L_c = 42 \times \pi \times 1.1 \times 3.9 = 566.0 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Ultimate uplift capacity (1 mark).**

$$Q_{u,up} = W_{p,eff} + Q_s + Q_{cone} = 27.61 + 198.5 + 566.0 = \boxed{792.1 \text{ kN}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract bulb height from shaft length** when computing $A_s$. The bulb does NOT contribute to skin friction in the standard analysis. Use $L_s = L - D_b$, not $L_s = L$.
>
> 2. **Using $N_c = 6$ instead of $N_c = 9$** for deep foundations. Students often use the **shallow footing** value. For piles with $L/D_b \geq 4$, Skempton's $N_c = 9$ is correct.
>
> 3. **Conflating upward and downward friction.** In compression, shaft friction acts *downward* (resisting downward load). In uplift, it acts *downward* on the pile, but the failure surface is *different*. Do not mix the two.
>
> 4. **Ignoring IS 2911 Part III spacing rules.** Even if the numerical problem doesn't ask for spacing, examiners often award 1–2 marks for "stating the spacing criteria." Always mention it in design-type questions.
>
> 5. **Not including the bulb's self-weight in uplift calculation.** A common omission that leads to underestimating $Q_{u,up}$.
>
> 6. **Using diameter in mm without converting to m.** A pile with $D = 400$ mm and $c = 100$ kN/m² needs $D = 0.4$ m in formulas. Unit mismatch costs full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision – Master These Points Before Exam:**

- [ ] **Definition:** Bored cast-in-situ pile with enlarged bulb(s); governed by **IS 2911 Part III (1980)**.
- [ ] **Bulb diameter:** $D_b = 2D$ to $3D$ (usually **$2.5D$**).
- [ ] **Bulb spacing (multi-bulb):** $S_b \geq 1.25 D_b$ to $1.5 D_b$.
- [ ] **Pile spacing (group):** $S \geq 2D$ (centre-to-centre).
- [ ] **First bulb depth:** $\geq 1.5 D_b$ below ground.
- [ ] **Typical depth range:** 3 m to 8 m (max ~15 m).
- [ ] **Typical diameter range:** 200 mm to 500 mm shaft.
- [ ] **Load capacity:** $Q_u = Q_b + Q_s$ (compression), with $Q_b = A_b \cdot c \cdot N_c$, $N_c = 9$.
- [ ] **Skin friction:** $Q_s = \alpha \bar{c} A_s$, with $\alpha \approx 0.4$ to $0.6$ for bored piles in clay.
- [ ] **Uplift capacity:** $Q_{u,up} = W_p + Q_s + Q_{cone}$, with $Q_{cone}$ being the key bulb contribution.
- [ ] **Factor of safety:** $F.S. = 2.5$ (with load test), $3.0$ (formula only).
- [ ] **Best suited for:** Expansive clays (black cotton soil), medium-stiff clay, uplift-prone foundations.
- [ ] **Avoid in:** Very soft clay, dense gravel, rock, very deep foundations (>15 m), high water table with loose sand.
- [ ] **Advantage over straight piles:** Higher capacity + uplift anchorage + economy at moderate depths.
- [ ] **Bulb formation tool:** Under-reamer (collapsible cutter) – verified for size before concreting.
- [ ] **Construction method:** Bored → Under-ream → Verify → Reinforce → Tremie concrete → Cure → Cap.
- [ ] **Concrete grade:** Minimum **M20** (IS 2911 Part III); M25/M30 preferred in aggressive environments.
- [ ] **Cover to reinforcement:** Minimum **50 mm** for piles in soil, **75 mm** in aggressive/water environments.
- [ ] **Load test:** Initial load test on **0.5%** of piles (min 2); routine test on **1% to 2%**.

**Exam Mnemonic – "BULB-SAFE":**
- **B**ulb diameter = 2.5D
- **U**nder-reamer forms bulb
- **L**oad test required
- **B**earing capacity factor $N_c = 9$ (deep)
- **S**pacing: 2D for piles, 1.25 $D_b$ for bulbs
- **A**dhesion factor $\alpha = 0.5$ typical
- **F**actor of safety = 3.0 (formula only)
- **E**xpansive soil primary application

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ultimate Load Carrying Capacity in Sand and Clay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — ULTIMATE LOAD CARRYING CAPACITY IN SAND AND CLAY

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultimate Bearing Capacity (q_u or q_ult):** The maximum gross pressure that the soil can sustain at the base of the foundation without undergoing shear failure, as defined in **IS 6403:1981 (Reaffirmed 2002)** and the classic theories of Terzaghi (1943), Meyerhof (1963), Hansen (1970), and Vesić (1973).

The **Ultimate Load Carrying Capacity** of a foundation is the limiting load per unit area at which the supporting soil mass fails in shear. The classical analysis treats two extreme soil conditions:

| Soil Type | Dominant Shear Strength Parameter | Special Theoretical Treatment |
|---|---|---|
| **Cohesionless Soils (Sand)** | Internal Friction Angle (φ) | Use of N_γ, N_q factors; no cohesion term |
| **Cohesive Soils (Clay)** | Undrained Cohesion (c_u) | Skempton's equation; special N_c values |

The **General Bearing Capacity Equation** (Meyerhof–Hansen–Vesić formulation) is:

$$
q_u = c\,N_c\,S_c\,d_c\,i_c \;+\; q\,(N_q - 1)\,S_q\,d_q\,i_q \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma\,S_\gamma\,d_\gamma\,i_\gamma
$$

where **q = γ · D_f** is the overburden pressure, **B** is the footing width, and **S, d, i** are shape, depth, and inclination factors respectively.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ice Cream Scoop" Model

Imagine pressing a flat-bottomed **ice cream scoop** vertically down into two different tubs:

- **In a tub of dry sand (cohesionless):** The scoop cannot penetrate easily. The sand grains must *ride up* over each other, generating frictional resistance. The deeper you push, the harder it gets because of the overlying sand's weight. This is the role of **N_q** and **N_γ**.

- **In a tub of wet clay (cohesive):** The clay sticks to the scoop. The resistance is mainly the *stickiness* (cohesion) of the clay, not friction. The overlying clay weight matters less because clay is nearly incompressible. This is why the **N_c** factor dominates for clay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** Sand resists by **friction + overburden weight**; Clay resists by **cohesion (stickiness)**. The two require fundamentally different equations at the limit state, though the same general framework is used in practice.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> The **bearing capacity factors** N_c, N_q, N_γ are *purely functions of the soil friction angle φ* (degrees). For φ = 0 (pure clay), **N_c = 5.14** (Terzaghi) or **N_c = 6.0 to 9.0** (Skempton, varying with D_f/B). For sand (φ = 30° to 45°), **N_q ranges from 18.4 to 134.9**, and **N_γ ranges from 22.4 to 271.9**.

The standard values used by KTU examiners and recommended by IS 6403 are tabulated in Section 2.4.

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Theory (1943) — The Foundation of All Modern Methods

Karl Terzaghi (1943) developed the first comprehensive theory for shallow foundations. He considered a **strip footing** (length L → ∞) and assumed the soil above the base is replaced by an equivalent surcharge **q = γ D_f**.

### Key Assumptions of Terzaghi's Theory:

1. The footing is of **infinite length (strip footing)** — plane strain condition.
2. The **base of the footing is rough**, and the soil above the base is replaced by a uniform surcharge **q = γD_f**.
3. The shear strength of soil follows the **Mohr-Coulomb criterion**: τ_f = c + σ tan φ.
4. The failure zones are pre-defined as in Figure 4.1 (Section 4):
   - **Zone I (Active wedge):** Triangular wedge beneath the footing (inclined at φ with horizontal).
   - **Zone II (Radial shear / Prandtl zone):** Log-spiral curve.
   - **Zone III (Passive wedge):** Triangular wedge at the ground surface (inclined at 45° - φ/2 with horizontal).
5. The **footing depth is ignored** in the failure mechanism (this is the most criticized assumption).
6. The surcharge **q = γ D_f** is approximated as a uniform load at the footing base level.

### Terzaghi's Three Failure Cases:

| Case | Soil Type | Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **General shear failure** | Dense sand / Stiff clay | Full classical equation |
| **Local shear failure** | Medium sand / Firm clay | Modified c' = 2c/3, φ' = tan⁻¹(2 tan φ/3) |
| **Punching shear failure** | Loose sand / Soft clay | Not covered by classical equation |

## 2.2 Terzaghi's Bearing Capacity Equations

### For Strip Footing (General Shear):

$$
q_u \;=\; c\,N_c \;+\; q\,N_q \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma
$$

### For Square Footing (B = L):

$$
q_u \;=\; 1.3\,c\,N_c \;+\; q\,N_q \;+\; 0.4\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma
$$

### For Circular Footing (Diameter B):

$$
q_u \;=\; 1.3\,c\,N_c \;+\; q\,N_q \;+\; 0.3\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** For **square and circular** footings, multiply the **N_γ term by 0.4 or 0.3** respectively, and the **N_c term by 1.3**. The N_q term remains unchanged.

## 2.3 Evolution Beyond Terzaghi: Meyerhof, Hansen, and Vesić

The Terzaghi equation was refined by three key researchers to overcome its limitations (only strip footing, neglects D_f, no inclination, no shape correction for general B/L ratios).

### A. Meyerhof's Bearing Capacity Equation (1963)

Meyerhof (1963) introduced **shape, depth, and inclination factors**, making the equation applicable to **all foundation geometries**:

$$
q_u \;=\; c\,N_c\,S_c\,d_c\,i_c \;+\; q\,N_q\,S_q\,d_q\,i_q \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma\,S_\gamma\,d_\gamma\,i_\gamma
$$

He also recognized the **effect of footing depth D_f** within the failure mechanism itself, not merely as a surcharge.

### B. Hansen's Bearing Capacity Equation (1970)

Hansen (1970) introduced the **base inclination factors b_c, b_q, b_γ** and **ground inclination factors g_c, g_q, g_γ**, in addition to Meyerhof's S, d, i factors:

$$
q_u \;=\; c\,N_c\,S_c\,d_c\,i_c\,b_c\,g_c \;+\; q\,N_q\,S_q\,d_q\,i_q\,b_q\,g_q \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma\,S_\gamma\,d_\gamma\,i_\gamma\,b_\gamma\,g_\gamma
$$

> This is the **most complete general bearing capacity equation** used in KTU examinations.

### C. Vesić's Bearing Capacity Equation (1973)

Vesić (1973) used the same general form as Meyerhof but with **revised shape factors** and recommended a slightly different N_γ formulation. Vesić's N_c and N_q are identical to those of Terzaghi and Meyerhof.

## 2.4 Bearing Capacity Factors (Friction Angle Functions)

$$
N_q \;=\; e^{\pi \tan\phi}\,\tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

$$
N_c \;=\; (N_q - 1)\,\cot\phi
$$

$$
N_\gamma \;=\; 2\,(N_q + 1)\,\tan\phi \quad \text{(Vesić, 1973 — KTU recommended)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 6403:1981 uses the same N_c and N_q as Terzaghi/Meyerhof**, but for **N_γ, IS 6403 adopts the Brinch-Hansen (1970) formulation:** N_γ = 1.5 (N_q − 1) tan φ. This is the most commonly tested value in KTU university exams.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ultimate Bearing Capacity (Strip, Terzaghi)** | $q_u = c N_c + q N_q + 0.5 \gamma B N_\gamma$ | $q = \gamma D_f$ |
| 2 | **Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity** | $q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f$ | Subtract overburden |
| 3 | **Net Safe Bearing Capacity (with FS)** | $q_{ns} = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{F_s}$ | F_s = 3 (usual, KTU standard) |
| 4 | **Safe Bearing Capacity (Gross)** | $q_{s} = q_{ns} + \gamma D_f$ | Add overburden back |
| 5 | **N_q** | $N_q = e^{\pi \tan\phi} \tan^2(45^\circ + \phi/2)$ | Function of φ only |
| 6 | **N_c** | $N_c = (N_q - 1) \cot\phi$ | Function of φ only |
| 7 | **N_γ (IS 6403)** | $N_\gamma = 1.5 (N_q - 1) \tan\phi$ | Recommended for KTU |
| 8 | **N_γ (Vesić)** | $N_\gamma = 2 (N_q + 1) \tan\phi$ | Used in some KTU problems |
| 9 | **Shape Factor S_c (IS 6403)** | $S_c = 1 + 0.2 (B/L)$ | Continuous footing: 1.0 |
| 10 | **Shape Factor S_q (IS 6403)** | $S_q = 1 + 0.2 (B/L)$ | Same as S_c |
| 11 | **Shape Factor S_γ (IS 6403)** | $S_\gamma = 1 - 0.4 (B/L)$ | Square: 0.6 |
| 12 | **Depth Factor d_c (IS 6403, D_f ≤ B)** | $d_c = 1 + 0.2 (D_f/B)$ | For D_f/B > 1, use Hansen |
| 13 | **Depth Factor d_q (IS 6403, D_f ≤ B)** | $d_q = 1 + 0.1 (D_f/B)$ | D_f in m, B in m |
| 14 | **Depth Factor d_γ (IS 6403)** | $d_\gamma = 1.0$ | Always 1.0 (IS 6403) |
| 15 | **Inclination Factor i_c (Hansen)** | $i_c = \left(1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{90^\circ}\right)^2$ | α = load inclination from vertical |
| 16 | **Inclination Factor i_q (Hansen)** | $i_q = \left(1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{90^\circ}\right)^2$ | Same as i_c for φ > 0 |
| 17 | **Inclination Factor i_γ (Hansen)** | $i_\gamma = \left(1 - \dfrac{\alpha}{\phi}\right)^2$ | Special for friction |
| 18 | **Skempton's N_c (Surface, Clay)** | $N_c = 5.14$ | D_f/B = 0 |
| 19 | **Skempton's N_c (D_f/B ≤ 2.5)** | $N_c = 5.14 \left(1 + 0.2 \dfrac{D_f}{B}\right)$ | Rectangular |
| 20 | **Skempton's N_c (D_f/B > 2.5)** | $N_c = 5.14 \left(1 + 0.2 \cdot 2.5\right) = 6.71$ | Capped, rectangular |
| 21 | **Water Table Correction — Surcharge term** | $q' = \gamma D_1 + \gamma' D_2$ | D_1 above WT, D_2 below WT |
| 22 | **Water Table Correction — N_γ term** | Effective B' = B − 2 × (WT dist from edge) | Reduces B if WT within B depth |

> [!NOTE]
> **Water Table Distance Notation:** When the water table is at a distance **D_w** from the ground surface, the effective unit weight above WT is **γ** (moist) and below is **γ' = γ_sat − γ_w** (buoyant).

## 2.6 Special Case: Ultimate Bearing Capacity in CLAY (φ = 0 Analysis)

For **saturated clay under undrained conditions (φ_u = 0)**, the bearing capacity equation simplifies dramatically.

### Step 1: Identify the foundation geometry.

### Step 2: Apply Skempton's Equation:

For a **rectangular footing** at depth D_f:

$$
N_c \;=\; 5.14 \left(1 + 0.2 \dfrac{D_f}{B}\right) \quad \text{for} \; 0 \le \dfrac{D_f}{B} \le 2.5
$$

For **strip footings** (B/L → 0):

$$
N_c \;=\; 5.14 \left(1 + 0.2 \dfrac{D_f}{B}\right) \quad \text{but limited to } 5.14 \text{ at surface}
$$

For **circular and square footings** (B/L = 1):

$$
N_c \;=\; 6.2 \left(1 + 0.2 \dfrac{D_f}{B}\right) \quad \text{(capped at 9.0 for D_f/B ≥ 2.5)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Skempton correction is critical for KTU clay problems** because students frequently use N_c = 5.14 (Terzaghi's strip value) for *all* shapes and *all* depths, which causes major mark loss.

### Final Bearing Capacity for Clay (φ = 0):

$$
q_u \;=\; c_u\,N_c
$$

This is **extremely simple** because the N_q and N_γ terms vanish (since φ = 0 ⇒ N_q = 1, N_γ = 0).

## 2.7 Special Case: Ultimate Bearing Capacity in SAND (c = 0 Analysis)

For **cohesionless sand (c' = 0)**, the equation simplifies to:

$$
q_u \;=\; q\,N_q\,S_q\,d_q\,i_q \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma\,S_\gamma\,d_\gamma\,i_\gamma
$$

The **N_c term vanishes** because c = 0. The sand's resistance comes entirely from:
1. **Overburden friction** (N_q term, scaled by q = γD_f)
2. **Self-weight of failing wedge** (N_γ term, scaled by 0.5γB)

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget to apply the water table correction in sand. If the WT is above the base of the footing, use **γ' (buoyant)** for the soil below WT, and reduce **B** if WT is within the influence zone of B below the footing.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> The ultimate bearing capacity calculation is the **backbone of every shallow foundation design** — from residential buildings on strip footings to bridge piers on large rectangular pads. IS 6403 (the Indian Standard used across Kerala) is mandatory for any structure taller than 3 stories in KTU-mandated designs. The methods of Hansen and Vesić are used for offshore foundations, transmission tower footings, and heavily inclined/loaded foundations where the simple Terzaghi equation fails.

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the General Bearing Capacity Equation (Conceptual Outline)

The classical derivation begins with Terzaghi's **assumption of three failure zones** beneath a strip footing (see Section 4 for diagram). The total bearing capacity is the **superposition of three contributions**:

1. **Cohesion contribution** — frictional resistance generated by the soil's own cohesion in zone II.
2. **Surcharge contribution** — resistance from the overburden soil weight on the passive wedge.
3. **Self-weight contribution** — resistance from the weight of soil in the failing wedge itself.

Because the failure surface is complex (composed of straight lines and a log-spiral), the closed-form integration is **mathematically intractable**. The classical derivation uses **limit equilibrium of wedge I** combined with **Prandtl's log-spiral solution** for zone II.

### Step-by-Step Derivation of Terzaghi's Strip Equation:

**Step 1: Geometry of Wedge I (Active Zone)**

Wedge I is an isosceles triangle with:
- Base angle = φ (with horizontal)
- Height = (B/2) tan φ
- Base width = B/2 (from footing edge to centerline)

The wedge is treated as a **Rankine active zone**, where the major principal stress is vertical and equals the contact pressure (q_u).

**Step 2: Equivalent Surcharge q on Wedge I's Outer Faces**

Since the soil above the base is replaced by an equivalent surcharge:

$$
\sigma_v = q_u
$$

The horizontal stress on the vertical face of wedge I is:

$$
\sigma_{h1} \;=\; \sigma_v \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right) \;=\; q_u \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Wedge I is in Equilibrium Under Three Sets of Forces**

- Vertical: q_u (footing pressure) acting downward on the inclined top
- Horizontal: σ_h1 acting inward on the vertical sides
- Weight: W = (1/2) · (B/2) · (B/2) tan φ · γ = γB² tan φ / 8
- Shear and normal forces on the inclined face AB (inclined at φ with horizontal)

**Step 4: Apply the Mohr-Coulomb Criterion on Face AB**

On the inclined face, the shear strength must be mobilized fully:

$$
\tau_f \;=\; c + \sigma_n \tan\phi
$$

**Step 5: Vertical Equilibrium of Wedge I**

Resolving all forces vertically:

$$
q_u \cdot \dfrac{B}{2} \;=\; 2 \cdot \dfrac{c \cdot B}{2 \cos\phi} \cdot \cos\phi + \sigma_{h1} \cdot B + W - \text{(surcharge effect)}
$$

After integration along the log-spiral of zone II and applying the **Prandtl–Reissner surcharge correction**, the closed-form result is:

$$
q_u \;=\; c\,N_c \;+\; q\,N_q \;+\; 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma
$$

where the bearing capacity factors are functions of φ only (as given in Section 2.4).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Example — Sand Under a Square Footing (IS 6403)

> **Problem Statement:** A square footing of size **2 m × 2 m** is placed at a depth of **1.5 m** below the ground surface. The soil is **medium-dense sand** with **φ = 35°**, **c = 0**, **γ = 18 kN/m³**, and the **water table is at 1.0 m** below the ground surface. Determine the **net ultimate bearing capacity** using **IS 6403:1981**. Use a **factor of safety F = 3**.

### Step 1: List Known Data

- Footing type: Square, B = L = 2 m
- Depth of footing: D_f = 1.5 m
- Water table depth from ground: D_w = 1.0 m
- Soil properties: φ = 35°, c = 0, γ = 18 kN/m³ (above WT), γ_sat = 20 kN/m³, γ_w = 9.81 kN/m³
- Factor of safety: F = 3

### Step 2: Compute Bearing Capacity Factors for φ = 35°

**N_q:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_q &= e^{\pi \tan 35^\circ} \cdot \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \dfrac{35^\circ}{2}\right) \\[4pt]
&= e^{\pi \cdot 0.7002} \cdot \tan^2(62.5^\circ) \\[4pt]
&= e^{2.1995} \cdot (1.92098)^2 \\[4pt]
&= 9.0172 \cdot 3.6901 \\[4pt]
&= 33.30
\end{aligned}
$$

**N_c:**

$$
N_c = (N_q - 1) \cot\phi = (33.30 - 1) \cot 35^\circ = 32.30 \cdot 1.4281 = 46.12
$$

**N_γ (IS 6403):**

$$
N_\gamma = 1.5 (N_q - 1) \tan\phi = 1.5 \cdot 32.30 \cdot 0.7002 = 33.93
$$

### Step 3: Compute Shape Factors (IS 6403, B/L = 1)

$$
S_c \;=\; 1 + 0.2 \cdot \dfrac{B}{L} \;=\; 1 + 0.2 \cdot 1 \;=\; 1.2
$$

$$
S_q \;=\; 1 + 0.2 \cdot \dfrac{B}{L} \;=\; 1.2
$$

$$
S_\gamma \;=\; 1 - 0.4 \cdot \dfrac{B}{L} \;=\; 1 - 0.4 \cdot 1 \;=\; 0.6
$$

### Step 4: Compute Depth Factors (IS 6403, D_f/B = 1.5/2 = 0.75 ≤ 1)

$$
d_c \;=\; 1 + 0.2 \cdot \dfrac{D_f}{B} \;=\; 1 + 0.2 \cdot 0.75 \;=\; 1.15
$$

$$
d_q \;=\; 1 + 0.1 \cdot \dfrac{D_f}{B} \;=\; 1 + 0.1 \cdot 0.75 \;=\; 1.075
$$

$$
d_\gamma \;=\; 1.0
$$

### Step 5: Water Table Correction

The WT is at 1.0 m, footing base is at 1.5 m. The distance from footing base to WT = **0.5 m above the base**. The total depth below WT = **1.0 m (from WT to base)**.

**For the surcharge term (q):**

The overburden at the base has two layers:
- 1.0 m above WT: γ = 18 kN/m³
- 0.5 m below WT: γ' = γ_sat − γ_w = 20 − 9.81 = 10.19 kN/m³

Effective overburden:

$$
q_{eff} = (1.0 \cdot 18) + (0.5 \cdot 10.19) = 18 + 5.095 = 23.095 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**For the N_γ term:**

The WT is at 0.5 m above the footing base (i.e., within B = 2 m depth below footing). The effective B' is reduced:

$$
B' = B - 2 \cdot (\text{WT distance above base}) \cdot \tan 60^\circ
$$

But IS 6403 uses a simpler convention: if WT is within B below the base, the effective B is reduced proportionally:

$$
B' = B \cdot \dfrac{\text{distance from base to WT below}}{\text{distance from base to influence zone}}
$$

For simplicity, we apply the **IS 6403 water table correction factor** for N_γ:

If WT is at a distance **D_w' = 0.5 m** above the base (so D_w' = B/2 above), we use the recommended effective width reduction:

Effective B' for sand:

$$
B' = B \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{D_{w,\text{above}}}{B}\right) \cdot \text{correction}
$$

For our case, the **standard IS 6403 procedure** gives:

$$
\gamma_{avg} = \dfrac{18 \cdot 1.0 + 10.19 \cdot 0.5}{1.5} = \dfrac{18 + 5.095}{1.5} = 15.40 \text{ kN/m}^3
$$

The term 0.5γB is computed as:

$$
0.5 \cdot \gamma_{avg} \cdot B' = 0.5 \cdot 15.40 \cdot 2 = 15.40 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

### Step 6: Apply the General Equation (c = 0, no inclination)

$$
q_u = c N_c S_c d_c + q_{eff} (N_q - 1) S_q d_q + 0.5 \gamma B' N_\gamma S_\gamma d_\gamma
$$

Since **c = 0**, the first term vanishes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= 0 + 23.095 \cdot (33.30 - 1) \cdot 1.2 \cdot 1.075 + 15.40 \cdot 33.93 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 1.0 \\[4pt]
&= 23.095 \cdot 32.30 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 1.075 + 15.40 \cdot 33.93 \cdot 0.6 \\[4pt]
&= 23.095 \cdot 32.30 \cdot 1.29 + 313.22 \\[4pt]
&= 962.21 + 313.22 \\[4pt]
&= 1275.43 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Compute Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity

$$
q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma_{avg} D_f = 1275.43 - 15.40 \cdot 1.5 = 1275.43 - 23.10 = 1252.33 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

### Step 8: Compute Net Safe Bearing Capacity

$$
q_{ns} = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{F} = \dfrac{1252.33}{3} = 417.44 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

### Step 9: Final Summary Table

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| N_q | 33.30 | — |
| N_c | 46.12 | — |
| N_γ | 33.93 | — |
| S_c, S_q | 1.2 | — |
| S_γ | 0.6 | — |
| d_c | 1.15 | — |
| d_q | 1.075 | — |
| d_γ | 1.0 | — |
| q_eff | 23.10 | kN/m² |
| **q_u** | **1275.43** | **kN/m²** |
| **q_nu** | **1252.33** | **kN/m²** |
| **q_ns (F=3)** | **417.44** | **kN/m²** |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Theory Selection Decision Tree

| If the Soil Is... | And Footing Is... | Then Use... | And Modify... |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure clay (φ = 0)** | Strip | Skempton: q_u = 5.14 c_u | Multiply by (1 + 0.2 D_f/B) |
| **Pure clay (φ = 0)** | Square / Circular | Skempton: q_u = 6.2 c_u (surface) | Use D_f/B correction, cap at 9.0 |
| **Pure clay (φ = 0)** | Rectangular | Skempton: N_c = 5.14(1 + 0.2 D_f/B) | Cap at 6.71 for D_f/B > 2.5 |
| **Sand (c = 0)** | Strip | Terzaghi: q_u = q N_q + 0.5γB N_γ | No shape factor needed |
| **Sand (c = 0)** | Square / Rectangular | IS 6403: full general equation | Apply S_q = 1.2, S_γ = 0.6 |
| **c-φ soil** | Any shape | General equation (Hansen/IS 6403) | All three terms + all factors |
| **Two-layer soil** | Any | Sevaoglu & Raju / Vesic punching | Use weaker layer or weighted avg |
| **Inclined load** | Any | Hansen's i factors | Apply i_c, i_q, i_γ |
| **Sloping ground** | Any | Hansen's g factors | Apply g_c, g_q, g_γ |
