# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block Architecture of Bearing Capacity Theories

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Soil Properties c, phi, gamma] --> B{Failure Theory?}
    B -- Terzaghi 1943 --> C1[Strip Only — 3 zones]
    B -- Meyerhof 1963 --> C2[General Shape — S factors]
    B -- Hansen 1970 --> C3[Inclined load + Sloping ground]
    B -- Vesić 1973 --> C4[Refined N_gamma]
    B -- IS 6403 1981 --> C5[Indian Standard — modified Hansen]
    B -- Skempton 1951 --> C6[Pure Clay only]
    C1 --> D[Compute N_c, N_q, N_gamma from phi]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    C6 --> D
    D --> E{Shape Correction?}
    E -- Yes --> F1[Apply S_c, S_q, S_gamma]
    E -- No --> G[Skip shape]
    F1 --> H{Depth Correction?}
    G --> H
    H -- Yes --> J1[Apply d_c, d_q, d_gamma]
    H -- No --> K[Skip depth]
    J1 --> L{Inclination Correction?}
    K --> L
    L -- Yes --> M1[Apply i_c, i_q, i_gamma]
    L -- No --> N[Skip inclination]
    M1 --> O[Water Table Correction]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Final q_u = cNcScdcic + qNqSqdqiq + 0.5 gamma B N_gamma S_gamma d_gamma i_gamma]
    P --> Q[Subtract overburden to get q_nu]
    Q --> R[Divide by F = 3 to get q_ns]
    R --> S[Output: Safe Bearing Capacity]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Terzaghi's Three Failure Zones

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Ground Surface — surcharge q = gamma D_f] -.-> A2[Base of Footing]
    A2 --> B1[Zone I — Active Wedge, angle phi to horizontal]
    B1 --> B2[Zone II — Prandtl Radial Shear, log-spiral curve]
    B2 --> B3[Zone III — Passive Wedge, angle 45 minus phi/2]
    subgraph Below_Footing
    B1
    B2
    B3
    end
    A2 -- Footing width B --> X[Centerline of Footing]
    B3 -- Emerges at surface --> Y[Heave on Ground Surface]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Sand vs Clay Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify Soil Type] --> B{Is c = 0?}
    B -- Yes Sand --> C[Use N_q and N_gamma only]
    B -- No Clay --> D{Is phi = 0?}
    D -- Yes Pure Clay --> E[Use Skempton N_c equation]
    D -- No c-phi Soil --> F[Use all three N_c N_q N_gamma]
    C --> G[Check WT in influence zone]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H{WT above base?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use gamma sub for surcharge, reduce B for N_gamma]
    H -- No --> J[Use full gamma and B]
    I --> K[Final q_u calculation]
    J --> K
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Water Table Position Effects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Foundation at Depth D_f] --> B[Water Table at D_w]
    B -- D_w = 0 at GS --> C[Surcharge term uses gamma sub only]
    B -- 0 less than D_w less than D_f --> D[Surcharge term: split into gamma and gamma sub]
    B -- D_w = D_f at base --> E[Surcharge term: full gamma above, gamma sub below at base]
    B -- D_w greater than D_f below base --> F[Full gamma for surcharge, check B reduction]
    C --> G[N_gamma term: full reduction if WT in B zone]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute final q_u with effective parameters]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The flowcharts above describe the **logical procedure** for computing bearing capacity. The physical failure zones (Section 4.2) are best understood from textbook illustrations — refer to **Das's Principles of Foundation Engineering** or **B.C. Punmia's Soil Mechanics** for the classic figures.

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define Ultimate Bearing Capacity. State the general bearing capacity equation as per IS 6403.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Ultimate Bearing Capacity (q_u):** It is the maximum gross pressure that the soil can support at the base of the foundation without undergoing shear failure.
>
> The **IS 6403:1981 general bearing capacity equation** is:
>
> $$
> q_u = c\,N_c\,S_c\,d_c + q\,(N_q - 1)\,S_q\,d_q + 0.5\,\gamma\,B\,N_\gamma\,S_\gamma\,d_\gamma
> $$
>
> where **c** = cohesion, **q = γD_f** = overburden, **B** = width, **γ** = unit weight, and **S, d** are shape and depth factors respectively.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q2. Distinguish between the bearing capacity factors used for sand and clay as per Skempton's theory.**

**Model Answer:**

> **For Sand (c = 0):** The bearing capacity factors N_q and N_γ are functions of φ. Typical values for φ = 30° are N_q = 18.4, N_γ = 22.4.
>
> **For Clay (φ = 0):** Skempton proposed that **N_c depends on both foundation shape and D_f/B ratio**:
> - Strip footing: N_c = 5.14 (surface), increasing with depth
> - Square/circular: N_c = 6.2 (surface), capping at 9.0
>
> The key difference: in clay, **N_c dominates** and the friction factors (N_q, N_γ) vanish because φ = 0.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**A square footing of size 2.5 m × 2.5 m is founded at a depth of 2.0 m in a soil with c = 20 kN/m², φ = 25°, and γ = 18 kN/m³. The water table is at 3.0 m below the ground surface. Determine the safe bearing capacity using IS 6403. Take F = 3.**

#### Part (a) — Compute Bearing Capacity Factors and Shape/Depth Factors (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Bearing capacity factors for φ = 25°**

$$
N_q = e^{\pi \tan 25°} \cdot \tan^2(57.5°) = e^{1.4661} \cdot 2.4637 = 12.72
$$

$$
N_c = (N_q - 1) \cot\phi = (12.72 - 1) \cdot 2.1445 = 25.13
$$

$$
N_\gamma = 1.5 (N_q - 1) \tan\phi = 1.5 \cdot 11.72 \cdot 0.4663 = 8.20
$$

**Step 2: Shape factors (B/L = 1)**

$$
S_c = 1 + 0.2 = 1.2, \quad S_q = 1.2, \quad S_\gamma = 1 - 0.4 = 0.6
$$

**Step 3: Depth factors (D_f/B = 2.0/2.5 = 0.8)**

$$
d_c = 1 + 0.2(0.8) = 1.16, \quad d_q = 1 + 0.1(0.8) = 1.08, \quad d_\gamma = 1.0
$$

**Step 4: Water table check**

WT is at 3.0 m, base is at 2.0 m. So WT is **1.0 m below the base** — **no water table correction needed** for either term.

#### Part (b) — Compute Ultimate and Safe Bearing Capacity (7 Marks)

**Step 5: Overburden pressure**

$$
q = \gamma D_f = 18 \cdot 2.0 = 36 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 6: Apply the IS 6403 equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_u &= 20 \cdot 25.13 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 1.16 + 36 \cdot 11.72 \cdot 1.2 \cdot 1.08 + 0.5 \cdot 18 \cdot 2.5 \cdot 8.20 \cdot 0.6 \cdot 1.0 \\
&= 700.42 + 547.14 + 110.70 \\
&= 1358.26 \text{ kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Net ultimate bearing capacity**

$$
q_{nu} = 1358.26 - 36 = 1322.26 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 8: Net safe bearing capacity (F = 3)**

$$
q_{ns} = \dfrac{1322.26}{3} = 440.75 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 9: Gross safe bearing capacity**

$$
q_s = q_{ns} + \gamma D_f = 440.75 + 36 = 476.75 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

| Result | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| q_u | 1358.26 | kN/m² |
| q_nu | 1322.26 | kN/m² |
| **q_s (gross)** | **476.75** | **kN/m²** |

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**A strip footing 2 m wide is to be placed at a depth of 1.5 m in saturated clay with c_u = 50 kN/m² and φ_u = 0. The water table is at the ground surface. Determine the safe bearing capacity using Skempton's method. Take F = 3.**

#### Part (a) — Apply Skempton's Equation for a Strip Footing (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify the conditions**

- Soil: Saturated clay, c_u = 50 kN/m², φ_u = 0
- Footing: Strip, B = 2 m, D_f = 1.5 m
- Water table at GS — no overburden effective stress, so **q = 0** for the analysis (since γ ≈ 0 saturated above base)

**Step 2: Compute D_f/B ratio**

$$
\dfrac{D_f}{B} = \dfrac{1.5}{2} = 0.75
$$

**Step 3: Compute Skempton's N_c for a strip footing**

$$
N_c = 5.14 \left(1 + 0.2 \cdot \dfrac{D_f}{B}\right) = 5.14 \cdot (1 + 0.2 \cdot 0.75) = 5.14 \cdot 1.15 = 5.911
$$

**Step 4: Compute ultimate bearing capacity (φ = 0 ⇒ N_q = 1, N_γ = 0)**

$$
q_u = c_u \cdot N_c = 50 \cdot 5.911 = 295.55 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

#### Part (b) — Compute Net and Safe Bearing Capacity (7 Marks)

**Step 5: Net ultimate bearing capacity**

For the overburden, we need the saturated unit weight of clay. Assume γ_sat = 19 kN/m³. The water table is at GS, so the overburden at the base is fully submerged:

$$
q = (\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w) \cdot D_f = (19 - 9.81) \cdot 1.5 = 9.19 \cdot 1.5 = 13.785 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

Wait — IS 6403 uses the **effective overburden** at the base. With WT at GS:

$$
q_{eff} = \gamma' \cdot D_f = (19 - 9.81) \cdot 1.5 = 13.785 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 6: Net ultimate bearing capacity**

$$
q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma' D_f = 295.55 - 13.785 = 281.77 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 7: Net safe bearing capacity**

$$
q_{ns} = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{F} = \dfrac{281.77}{3} = 93.92 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 8: Gross safe bearing capacity**

$$
q_s = q_{ns} + \gamma' D_f = 93.92 + 13.785 = 107.71 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

| Result | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| N_c (Skempton) | 5.911 | — |
| q_u | 295.55 | kN/m² |
| q_nu | 281.77 | kN/m² |
| **q_s (gross)** | **107.71** | **kN/m²** |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 CRITICAL MARK-LOSS ZONES — KTU VALUATION KEY (Dec 2023)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting water table correction in sand problems:** If WT is within B depth below the base, you MUST use γ' (submerged) for the N_γ term. Missing this costs **2–3 marks**.
>
> 2. **Using N_c = 5.14 for square/circular footings in clay:** Skempton's N_c for square is **6.2 at surface**, not 5.14. Failure to use the shape-corrected value is a **3-mark deduction**.
>
> 3. **Not using (N_q − 1) instead of N_q in IS 6403:** IS 6403 uses the **net surcharge** effect. The N_q term in IS 6403 is **q(N_q − 1)**, not q·N_q. This is a common trap and costs **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Confusing Terzaghi's and Hansen's N_γ:** Terzaghi uses N_γ = (N_q − 1) tan(1.4φ). IS 6403 uses N_γ = 1.5(N_q − 1) tan φ. Using the wrong formula gives wrong answers.
>
> 5. **Forgetting d_γ = 1.0 in IS 6403:** Many students compute d_γ = 1 + 0.1(D_f/B), but **IS 6403 specifies d_γ = 1.0 always** for shallow foundations. This is a **1-mark loss**.
>
> 6. **Not subtracting overburden when computing q_nu:** q_nu = q_u − γD_f, not just q_u. The factor of safety applies to the **net** ultimate capacity.
>
> 7. **Mixing up gross and net safe bearing capacity:** The question will ask for one or the other. **q_s = q_ns + γD_f**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> ✅ **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
> - N_q = e^(π tan φ) · tan²(45° + φ/2)
> - N_c = (N_q − 1) cot φ
> - N_γ (IS 6403) = 1.5(N_q − 1) tan φ
> - q_u = cN_cS_cd_c + q(N_q−1)S_qd_q + 0.5γBN_γS_γd_γ
> - q_nu = q_u − γD_f
> - q_ns = q_nu / F
> - q_s = q_ns + γD_f

> ✅ **Standard Shape Factors (IS 6403):**
> - S_c = S_q = 1 + 0.2(B/L)
> - S_γ = 1 − 0.4(B/L)

> ✅ **Standard Depth Factors (IS 6403, D_f/B ≤ 1):**
> - d_c = 1 + 0.2(D_f/B)
> - d_q = 1 + 0.1(D_f/B)
> - d_γ = 1.0

> ✅ **Skempton's N_c (Clay, φ = 0):**
> - Strip: N_c = 5.14(1 + 0.2 D_f/B), capped at 5.14 (D_f=0) to higher values
> - Square: N_c = 6.2(1 + 0.2 D_f/B), capped at 9.0
> - Rectangular: Interpolate between strip and square

> ✅ **Water Table Rules:**
> - If WT is at depth z above base, the overburden at the base uses γ above WT and γ' below WT
> - If WT is within B depth below the base, use B' = B for N_γ term
> - If WT is at or above the base, use γ' for the N_γ term

> ✅ **Critical Numerical Values to Remember:**
> - For φ = 0°: N_c = 5.14, N_q = 1, N_γ = 0
> - For φ = 30°: N_c ≈ 30.14, N_q ≈ 18.4, N_γ ≈ 22.4 (IS) or 30.2 (Vesić)
> - For φ = 35°: N_c ≈ 46.12, N_q ≈ 33.30, N_γ ≈ 33.93 (IS)
> - For φ = 45°: N_c ≈ 133.87, N_q ≈ 134.87, N_γ ≈ 134.85 (IS)

> ✅ **Theories Hierarchy (from oldest to most complete):**
> - **Terzaghi (1943):** Strip only, surcharge only, no D_f effect
> - **Meyerhof (1963):** Added S, d, i factors
> - **Hansen (1970):** Added base & ground inclination (b, g factors)
> - **Vesić (1973):** Refined N_γ formula
> - **IS 6403 (1981):** Indian adaptation of Hansen

> ✅ **Common Factor of Safety (F) Values (KTU Standard):**
> - F = 3 for ordinary buildings
> - F = 2.5 to 4 for bridges and heavy structures
> - F = 4 to 6 for important structures / weak soils

> ✅ **Definition Traps in KTU Viva:**
> - "Net bearing capacity" = **gross capacity − overburden** (NOT minus weight of soil alone)
> - "Safe bearing pressure" = **net safe bearing capacity + overburden** (i.e., the actual allowable stress)
> - "Local shear" modifies **c' = 2c/3, φ' = tan⁻¹(2 tan φ/3)** — easy marks question

> ✅ **Quick Decision Shortcut:**
> - If c = 0 (sand): N_c term vanishes, focus on N_q and N_γ
> - If φ = 0 (clay): N_q and N_γ vanish, use Skempton's N_c only
> - If both c and φ exist: full equation with all three terms
> - If WT above base: use γ' for submergence
> - If WT within B below base: reduce effective B

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Considerations as per IS, IS Formula - Single and Double Bulb
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Considerations as per IS, IS Formula - Single and Double Bulb

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (IS 2911 Terminology)

An **Under-Reamed Pile** is a bored, cast-in-situ concrete pile having one or more bulbs (enlargements) formed by enlarging the bore hole at the pile tip or at intermediate depths. These bulbs act as anchors and significantly increase both the **bearing capacity** and **uplift resistance** of the pile.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS Code Reference:** The design and construction of under-reamed piles in India is governed by **IS 2911 (Part 3) : 1980** (Reaffirmed 2010) – *Code of Practice for Design and Construction of Pile Foundations – Part 3: Under-Reamed Piles*. The first revision was published in 1980, and the code provides both **Single Bulb** and **Double Bulb** configurations.

### 1.2 Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Symbol | Typical Range (IS 2911) |
|---|---|---|
| Pile shaft diameter | $D$ | 200 mm – 500 mm |
| Bulb diameter | $D_u$ | $2.5D$ to $3.0D$ |
| Bulb height | $H_b$ | $1.25D$ to $1.5D$ |
| Spacing between bulbs (double bulb) | $L_b$ | $1.25D_u$ to $1.5D_u$ |
| Length of pile | $L$ | 3 m – 10 m (typical) |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Tree Root" Principle:**
> Imagine a long bamboo stick pushed vertically into soft soil. It resists downward force poorly because the skin friction along its smooth shaft is the only resistance. Now, imagine that same stick has a **bulb (a round swelling)** at the bottom — exactly like the root ball of a tree. The bulb cannot be pulled up easily because soil has to be sheared around its enlarged perimeter. Push down on it, and the bulb presses against a much larger soil area below.
>
> In **double-bulb** piles, it's like having **two roots stacked** at different depths, multiplying both the bearing and uplift capacity. This is precisely how under-reamed piles work — the bulbs are mechanically formed "anchors" embedded in competent bearing strata.

### 1.4 Why Under-Reamed Piles? (Design Motivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - Ideal for **expansive (black cotton) soils** where swelling/shrinkage creates uplift forces.
> - Used in **soft clays and loose sands** to mobilize higher end-bearing.
> - Economical alternative to large-diameter bored piles or deep foundations.
> - Excellent for **tension/uplift loads** (e.g., transmission towers, overhead tanks, machine foundations subjected to uplift).

---

### 1.5 Single Bulb vs. Double Bulb — When to Use

> [!NOTE]
> - **Single Bulb:** Used when pile length is short (≤ 5 m) and the bulb can be positioned in a firm bearing stratum. Common in stiff clays at shallow depths.
> - **Double Bulb:** Used when pile length is greater (> 5 m) and additional anchorage is required against uplift. The upper bulb helps resist swelling pressures, while the lower bulb carries structural load.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometry Specifications (IS 2911 Part 3, Cl. 5.1)

The IS code prescribes strict geometric constraints for the bulb:

**Bulb Diameter:**

$$D_u = 2.5\,D \quad \text{to} \quad 3.0\,D$$

**Bulb Height (Vertical Height of the Enlargement):**

$$H_b = 1.25\,D \quad \text{to} \quad 1.5\,D$$

**Spacing Between Bulbs (Double Bulb Only):**

$$L_b = 1.25\,D_u \quad \text{to} \quad 1.5\,D_u$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The upper limit of $L_b$ must not exceed **2.0 $D_u$** as per IS recommendations, otherwise the soil between the bulbs is not properly confined and the two bulbs act independently rather than cooperatively.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Design Logic

The design of an under-reamed pile follows this logical sequence:

1. **Establish subsoil profile** through borehole data (SPT-N values, soil type, water table).
2. **Decide pile diameter ($D$)** based on structural load and pile length.
3. **Decide number of bulbs** (1 or 2) based on uplift requirement and soil stratification.
4. **Calculate bulb dimensions** ($D_u$ and $H_b$) from IS ratios.
5. **Compute ultimate bearing capacity** ($Q_u$) using IS formulas.
6. **Compute ultimate uplift capacity** ($Q_{up}$) — critical for expansive soils.
7. **Apply factor of safety** (F.S. = 2.5 for compressive, 3.0 for uplift) to arrive at **safe load**.
8. **Check settlement** and structural capacity of pile shaft.

### 2.3 IS Formula — Ultimate Bearing Capacity (Compressive Load)

The IS 2911 (Part 3) formula for the **ultimate load capacity** of an under-reamed pile is:

$$Q_u = Q_{eb} + Q_{sf}$$

Where:
- $Q_{eb}$ = End-bearing component at the lowest bulb
- $Q_{sf}$ = Skin friction along the pile shaft (including the cylindrical surface above the bulb)

> [!NOTE]
> **A key distinction from conventional piles:** In an under-reamed pile, **end-bearing is computed over the cross-sectional area of the BULB ($A_u$)**, not the shaft ($A_p$). The annular area between the bulb and shaft does not contribute to end-bearing because it is already in contact with soil.

#### 2.3.1 In Cohesive Soils (Clay)

For the **lowest bulb** resting on/in clay:

$$Q_{eb} = A_u \cdot N_c \cdot c_u + A_p \cdot c_u$$

Where:
- $A_u = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_u^2$ = cross-sectional area of the bulb
- $A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ = cross-sectional area of the pile shaft
- $N_c$ = bearing capacity factor (= **9.0** for $\phi = 0$ as per IS, often taken as 6 to 9 for clays)
- $c_u$ = undrained cohesion of soil at bulb level

For the **additional bulb(s) in double-bulb piles**, the bearing capacity is computed over the **annular area** between the bulb and the shaft (because the soil column above the bulb is sheared through):

$$Q_{eb,\text{upper}} = (A_u - A_p) \cdot c_u \quad \text{(cohesive soil)}$$

The skin friction along the shaft:

$$Q_{sf} = (\alpha \cdot \bar{c}) \cdot A_s$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = adhesion factor (typically **0.4 to 0.8** for clays)
- $\bar{c}$ = average cohesion along shaft
- $A_s = \pi D L$ = surface area of pile shaft

#### 2.3.2 In Cohesionless Soils (Sand)

For the lowest bulb in sand:

$$Q_{eb} = A_u \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{2} D_u \cdot \gamma \cdot N_\gamma \cdot F_\gamma + \gamma \cdot D_f \cdot N_q \cdot F_q \right)$$

And:

$$Q_{eb,\text{upper}} = (A_u - A_p) \cdot \gamma \cdot D_f \cdot N_q \cdot F_q$$

Skin friction in sand:

$$Q_{sf} = \dfrac{1}{2} K \cdot \bar{\gamma} \cdot L^2 \cdot \pi D \cdot \tan \delta$$

Where $\delta$ = soil-pile interface friction angle (typically $\frac{2}{3}\phi$).

### 2.4 IS Formula — Ultimate Uplift Capacity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most exam-critical formula** for Module 3. Uplift capacity is the primary reason for using under-reamed piles in expansive soils.

**Ultimate Uplift Capacity ($Q_{u,\text{up}}$):**

$$Q_{u,\text{up}} = W_p + W_s + Q_{sf,\text{up}} + Q_{b,\text{up}}$$

Where:
- $W_p$ = self-weight of pile
- $W_s$ = weight of soil enclosed within the bulb
- $Q_{sf,\text{up}}$ = uplift skin friction
- $Q_{b,\text{up}}$ = **uplift resistance of the bulb** — the dominant term

**Uplift Resistance of a Single Bulb (Cohesive Soil):**

$$Q_{b,\text{up}} = A_u \cdot N_c \cdot c_u + A_p \cdot c_u$$

(Same form as end-bearing, but mobilized in tension.)

**Uplift Resistance of a Single Bulb (Cohesionless Soil):**

$$Q_{b,\text{up}} = A_u \cdot \gamma \cdot D_f \cdot N_q \cdot F_q + W_s'$$

Where $W_s'$ is the effective weight of the soil cone/cylinder above the bulb.

### 2.5 Factor of Safety (As Recommended by IS 2911)

| Load Condition | Minimum F.S. |
|---|---|
| **Compressive load (static)** | **2.5** |
| **Compressive load (static + dynamic/seismic)** | **3.0** |
| **Uplift / Tension load** | **3.0** |
| **Lateral load** | **2.5** |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bulb diameter | $D_u = (2.5 \text{ to } 3.0)\,D$ | mm | IS 2911 (Part 3) |
| 2 | Bulb height | $H_b = (1.25 \text{ to } 1.5)\,D$ | mm | IS 2911 (Part 3) |
| 3 | Bulb spacing (double bulb) | $L_b = (1.25 \text{ to } 1.5)\,D_u$ | mm | IS 2911 (Part 3) |
| 4 | Area of bulb | $A_u = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D_u^2$ | m² | Cross-section |
| 5 | Area of shaft | $A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | m² | Cross-section |
| 6 | Annular area | $A_a = A_u - A_p$ | m² | Between bulb & shaft |
| 7 | End-bearing (clay) | $Q_{eb} = A_u N_c c_u + A_p c_u$ | kN | Lowest bulb |
| 8 | End-bearing (sand) | $Q_{eb} = A_u \left(\tfrac{1}{2} D_u \gamma N_\gamma F_\gamma + \gamma D_f N_q F_q\right)$ | kN | Lowest bulb |
| 9 | Skin friction (clay) | $Q_{sf} = \alpha \bar{c} \pi D L$ | kN | $\alpha$ = 0.4–0.8 |
| 10 | Skin friction (sand) | $Q_{sf} = \tfrac{1}{2} K \bar{\gamma} L^2 \pi D \tan\delta$ | kN | $\delta \approx \tfrac{2}{3}\phi$ |
| 11 | Uplift resistance (bulb, clay) | $Q_{b,\text{up}} = A_u N_c c_u + A_p c_u$ | kN | Dominant term |
| 12 | Uplift resistance (bulb, sand) | $Q_{b,\text{up}} = A_u \gamma D_f N_q F_q + W_s'$ | kN | Includes soil weight |
| 13 | Safe load | $Q_{\text{safe}} = Q_u / F.S.$ | kN | F.S. = 2.5 / 3.0 |
| 14 | Min. clear cover | 40 mm (pile cap), 50 mm (marine) | mm | IS 2911 |
| 15 | Min. concrete grade | M 20 (static), M 25 (dynamic) | — | IS 2911 Cl. 6.2 |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Transmission & Telecom Towers:** Subjected to large overturning moments → high uplift at one corner.
> - **Overhead Water Tanks:** Wind-induced uplift, especially on cylindrical column supports.
> - **Building Foundations in Black Cotton Soil:** Heave and settlement mitigation.
> - **Machine Foundations:** Dynamic loads with tension components.
> - **Bridge Piers & Abutments:** Scour-prone alluvial deposits.
> - **Solar Panel Mounting Structures in Soft Soils:** Lightweight superstructure, large uplift.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bulb Area and Annular Area

Starting from the geometric definition of a circular bulb of diameter $D_u$ and a shaft of diameter $D$:

**Step 1:** Cross-sectional area of the bulb (full circle):

$$A_u = \frac{\pi}{4}\,D_u^2$$

**Step 2:** Cross-sectional area of the pile shaft (smaller circle):

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4}\,D^2$$

**Step 3:** The annular (ring-shaped) area between the bulb and the shaft, which is the actual soil area that must be sheared for an upper bulb in a double-bulb configuration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_a &= A_u - A_p \\[4pt]
A_a &= \frac{\pi}{4}\,D_u^2 - \frac{\pi}{4}\,D^2 \\[4pt]
A_a &= \frac{\pi}{4}\,\left(D_u^2 - D^2\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting the IS ratio $D_u = 2.5\,D$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_a &= \frac{\pi}{4}\left[(2.5D)^2 - D^2\right] \\[4pt]
A_a &= \frac{\pi}{4}\left[6.25D^2 - D^2\right] \\[4pt]
A_a &= \frac{\pi}{4}\,(5.25)\,D^2 \\[4pt]
A_a &= 1.3125\,\pi D^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Comparing to shaft area $A_p = 0.25\pi D^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{A_a}{A_p} &= \frac{1.3125\,\pi D^2}{0.25\,\pi D^2} = 5.25
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Even with the minimum IS ratio ($D_u = 2.5D$), the **annular area of the bulb is 5.25 times the shaft area**. This dramatically amplifies the bearing/uplift capacity, which is why under-reamed piles are highly efficient in soft soils.

### 3.2 Derivation: Ultimate Bearing Capacity of a Single-Bulb Pile in Clay

**Step 1:** Sum the two contributing components:

$$Q_u = Q_{eb} + Q_{sf}$$

**Step 2:** End-bearing on the lowest bulb — soil fails in general shear with $N_c = 9$ (Skempton / IS 2911 for $\phi = 0$):

$$Q_{eb} = A_u \cdot N_c \cdot c_u = \left(\frac{\pi}{4} D_u^2\right)(9)(c_u)$$

**Step 3:** Skin friction along the cylindrical shaft (no contribution from the bulb's curved surface per IS, as the bulb is "passive" and only mobilizes bearing):

$$Q_{sf} = \alpha \cdot \bar{c} \cdot \pi D L$$

**Step 4:** Total ultimate capacity:

$$
Q_u = \frac{9\pi}{4}\,D_u^2\,c_u + \alpha\,\bar{c}\,\pi D L
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $D_u = 2.5D$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_u &= \frac{9\pi}{4}(2.5D)^2 c_u + \alpha \bar{c} \pi D L \\[4pt]
Q_u &= \frac{9\pi}{4}(6.25 D^2) c_u + \alpha \bar{c} \pi D L \\[4pt]
Q_u &= 14.0625\,\pi D^2 c_u + \alpha \bar{c} \pi D L \\[4pt]
Q_u &= \pi D \left(14.0625\,D c_u + \alpha \bar{c} L\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Ultimate Bearing Capacity of a Double-Bulb Pile in Clay

For a double-bulb under-reamed pile of total length $L$ in clay, with bulbs at depths $L_1$ (upper) and $L_2$ (lower, $L_2 > L_1$):

**Step 1:** Lower bulb end-bearing (on the bulb face):

$$Q_{eb,\text{lower}} = A_u\,N_c\,c_{u,2}$$

**Step 2:** Upper bulb — the bulb acts as an **anchor**. Soil above the upper bulb fails in shear along a cylindrical surface of diameter $D_u$ extending from the bulb up to ground level. The shear resistance is computed over the **annular area of the bulb** multiplied by the cohesion (this is the IS treatment):

$$Q_{eb,\text{upper}} = A_a \cdot c_{u,1}$$

where $c_{u,1}$ is the cohesion at the upper bulb level. (The $N_c$ factor is conservatively omitted for the upper bulb in the IS formulation because the failure mode is different from classical end-bearing.)

**Step 3:** Total ultimate capacity:

$$
Q_u = A_u\,N_c\,c_{u,2} + A_a\,c_{u,1} + \alpha\,\bar{c}\,\pi D L
$$

### 3.4 Derivation: Ultimate Uplift Capacity (Single Bulb in Clay)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU university exams for Module 3.

**Step 1:** Sum the four uplift components:

$$Q_{u,\text{up}} = W_p + Q_{sf,\text{up}} + Q_{b,\text{up}}$$

**Step 2:** The bulb uplift resistance — soil is sheared along a cylindrical surface of diameter $D_u$ above the bulb. Using $N_c = 9$ for $\phi = 0$ (IS convention):

$$Q_{b,\text{up}} = A_u\,N_c\,c_u = \frac{\pi}{4} D_u^2 (9) c_u$$

**Step 3:** Self-weight of pile (concrete density $\gamma_c \approx 24$ kN/m³):

$$W_p = A_p \cdot L \cdot \gamma_c = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L (24)$$

**Step 4:** Uplift skin friction (acts downward, resisting pile pullout):

$$Q_{sf,\text{up}} = \alpha \bar{c} \pi D L$$

**Step 5:** Combine:

$$
Q_{u,\text{up}} = \frac{9\pi}{4} D_u^2 c_u + \alpha \bar{c} \pi D L + \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L (24)
$$

### 3.5 Derivation: Critical Spacing Between Bulbs (Double Bulb)

The IS code mandates that the bulb spacing $L_b$ must ensure that **stress bulbs of the two bulbs overlap**, creating a composite stress zone. The stress influence depth beneath a bulb is approximately $1.5 D_u$ (Boussinesq for circular loaded area). For overlap:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_b &\leq 1.5\,D_u \quad \text{(lower bound for overlap)} \\[4pt]
L_b &\geq 1.25\,D_u \quad \text{(upper bound to avoid stress shadow)} \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad 1.25\,D_u &\leq L_b \leq 1.5\,D_u
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If $L_b > 2.0 D_u$, the bulbs behave as **two isolated piles**, and the composite action is lost. This is why IS 2911 caps the spacing.

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Single vs. Double Bulb

| Design Parameter | Single Bulb | Double Bulb |
|---|---|---|
| Number of bulbs | 1 (at pile tip) | 2 (one at tip, one above) |
| Bearing capacity | Lower (no upper anchor) | Higher by factor of $(A_u N_c c_{u,2} + A_a c_{u,1})$ |
| Uplift capacity | Limited to one bulb + shaft | Significantly higher (2 bulbs + soil weights) |
| Typical pile length | ≤ 5 m | 5 m – 10 m |
| Construction complexity | Simple | Requires careful sequential boring |
| Cost | Lower | ~30–40% higher |
| Suitability in expansive soil | Moderate (heave protection) | Excellent (double anchorage) |
| IS formula form | $Q_u = A_u N_c c_u + A_p c_u + \alpha \bar{c} \pi D L$ | $Q_u = A_u N_c c_{u,2} + A_a c_{u,1} + \alpha \bar{c} \pi D L$ |

### 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A single-bulb under-reamed pile is to be installed in a clay deposit with average undrained cohesion $c_u = 80$ kN/m². Pile diameter $D = 300$ mm, bulb diameter $D_u = 2.5D = 750$ mm, pile length $L = 6$ m, adhesion factor $\alpha = 0.6$. Determine the **safe load** on the pile. Use F.S. = 2.5.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_u &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.750)^2 = 0.4418 \text{ m}^2 \\[4pt]
A_p &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.300)^2 = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2 \\[4pt]
Q_{eb} &= A_u N_c c_u + A_p c_u = (0.4418)(9)(80) + (0.0707)(80) \\[4pt]
&= 318.10 + 5.66 = 323.76 \text{ kN} \\[4pt]
Q_{sf} &= \alpha \bar{c} \pi D L = (0.6)(80)(\pi)(0.300)(6) = 271.43 \text{ kN} \\[4pt]
Q_u &= 323.76 + 271.43 = 595.19 \text{ kN} \\[4pt]
Q_{\text{safe}} &= \frac{Q_u}{F.S.} = \frac{595.19}{2.5} = \boxed{238.08 \text{ kN}}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Single-Bulb Under-Reamed Pile

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Ground Surface] --> B[Pile Cap or Column Base]
    B --> C[Pile Shaft - Cylindrical]
    C --> D[Pile Shaft continues through soil]
    D --> E[Bulb Enlargement - D_u]
    E --> F[Pile Tip - Sealed with Concrete]
    F --> G[Bearing Stratum]

    H[Forces Acting] --> I[Compressive Load Q down]
    H --> J[Uplift Load Q_up up]
    H --> K[Side Friction Q_sf along shaft]
    H --> L[End Bearing Q_eb at bulb base]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Double-Bulb Under-Reamed Pile

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Ground Surface] --> B[Pile Cap]
    B --> C[Upper Pile Shaft Segment]
    C --> D[Upper Bulb - D_u at depth L1]
    D --> E[Inter-bulb Shaft Segment]
    E --> F[Lower Bulb - D_u at depth L2]
    F --> G[Pile Tip]
    G --> H[Bearing Stratum]

    I[Spacing Requirement] --> J[L_b between 1.25 D_u and 1.5 D_u]
    K[Uplift Resistance Sources] --> L[Upper bulb anchor]
    K --> M[Lower bulb anchor]
    K --> N[Soil weight between bulbs]
    K --> O[Self weight of pile]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: IS Design Procedure for Under-Reamed Piles

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Subsoil Investigation] --> B[Determine c_u phi and N values at each stratum]
    B --> C{Select pile diameter D}
    C --> D[Structural load requirement]
    C --> E[Length L based on bearing stratum]
    D --> F[Choose bulb count: 1 or 2]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Single or Double Bulb}
    G -->|Single| H[Set D_u = 2.5D to 3D]
    G -->|Double| I[Set D_u for both bulbs and L_b per IS]
    H --> J[Compute A_u and A_p]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute Q_eb at lowest bulb]
    K --> L[Compute Q_sf along shaft]
    L --> M[Compute Q_u total]
    M --> N[Compute Uplift Capacity Q_u_up]
    N --> O[Apply F.S.]
    O --> P[Check settlement and structural capacity]
    P --> Q[Safe Load Output]
```

### 4.4 ASCII Schematic: Geometry of Bulb

```
                 GROUND SURFACE
                 |
                 |  (Pile cap / column connection)
                 |
                 |
        |        |        |
        |        |        |  ← Pile shaft (D)
        |        |        |
        |        |        |
        |   ___________    |
        |  |           |   |  ← Upper bulb (D_u)   [Double bulb only]
        |  |           |   |
        |   ‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾    |
        |        |        |
        |        |        |  ← Inter-bulb spacing (L_b)
        |        |        |
        |   ___________    |
        |  |           |   |  ← Lower bulb (D_u)
        |  |           |   |
        |   ‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾    |
        |        |        |
        |        |        |  ← Pile shaft tip
        |________|________|
              BEARING STRATUM
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is an under-reamed pile? How is the bulb diameter related to the pile shaft diameter as per IS 2911 (Part 3)?*

**Model Answer:**

An **under-reamed pile** is a bored cast-in-situ concrete pile with one or more bulbs (enlargements) formed near its tip to increase its load-carrying and uplift capacity.

As per **IS 2911 (Part 3)**, the bulb diameter $D_u$ is related to the pile shaft diameter $D$ as:

$$D_u = 2.5\,D \quad \text{to} \quad 3.0\,D$$

Typically, $D_u = 2.5D$ is adopted for double-bulb piles and $D_u = 3D$ for single-bulb piles in stiff clays. The bulb height is $1.25D$ to $1.5D$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the spacing requirement between bulbs in a double-bulb under-reamed pile as per IS code.*

**Model Answer:**

As per **IS 2911 (Part 3)**, the centre-to-centre spacing between two bulbs in a double-bulb pile is:

$$L_b = 1.25\,D_u \quad \text{to} \quad 1.5\,D_u$$

with a maximum allowable spacing of **$2.0\,D_u$**. If the spacing exceeds $2.0 D_u$, the two bulbs act independently and the composite anchorage effect is lost, defeating the purpose of the double-bulb design.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **IS 2911 (Part 3) design considerations** for an under-reamed pile in clay. Discuss the geometry of single and double bulb configurations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **double-bulb under-reamed pile** is proposed in a clayey soil with the following data:

- Pile diameter, $D = 400$ mm
- Bulb diameter, $D_u = 2.5D = 1000$ mm
- Pile length, $L = 8$ m
- Upper bulb depth, $L_1 = 4$ m, cohesion at upper bulb, $c_{u,1} = 60$ kN/m²
- Lower bulb depth, $L_2 = 8$ m, cohesion at lower bulb, $c_{u,2} = 100$ kN/m²
- Average cohesion along shaft, $\bar{c} = 70$ kN/m²
- Adhesion factor, $\alpha = 0.5$
- Factor of safety = 2.5
- $N_c = 9$

Compute the **safe load** on the pile. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Design Considerations (7 Marks):**
- Definition and IS code reference (IS 2911 Part 3).
- Geometric constraints: $D_u = 2.5D$ to $3D$; $H_b = 1.25D$ to $1.5D$; $L_b = 1.25 D_u$ to $1.5 D_u$ (max $2.0 D_u$).
- Load transfer mechanism: end-bearing at bulb + skin friction along shaft.
- Distinction between lower bulb (end-bearing on $A_u$) and upper bulb (anchorage on $A_a$).
- Factor of safety guidelines.
- Suitability for expansive soils and uplift-prone structures.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_u &= \frac{\pi}{4}(1.000)^2 = 0.7854 \text{ m}^2 \\[4pt]
A_p &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.400)^2 = 0.1257 \text{ m}^2 \\[4pt]
A_a &= A_u - A_p = 0.7854 - 0.1257 = 0.6597 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**End-bearing at lower bulb:**

$$Q_{eb,\text{lower}} = A_u N_c c_{u,2} = (0.7854)(9)(100) = 706.86 \text{ kN}$$

**Anchorage of upper bulb (using annular area, no $N_c$ for upper bulb in IS formulation):**

$$Q_{eb,\text{upper}} = A_a\,c_{u,1} = (0.6597)(60) = 39.58 \text{ kN}$$

**Skin friction along shaft:**

$$Q_{sf} = \alpha \bar{c} \pi D L = (0.5)(70)(\pi)(0.400)(8) = 351.86 \text{ kN}$$

**Total ultimate capacity:**

$$Q_u = 706.86 + 39.58 + 351.86 = 1098.30 \text{ kN}$$

**Safe load:**

$$Q_{\text{safe}} = \frac{Q_u}{F.S.} = \frac{1098.30}{2.5} = \boxed{439.32 \text{ kN}}$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the **ultimate uplift capacity** of an under-reamed pile in clay as per IS 2911. Explain why the bulb contributes the dominant share of uplift resistance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **single-bulb under-reamed pile** in clay is subjected to an uplift load. Given:
- $D = 300$ mm, $D_u = 2.5D = 750$ mm
- Pile length $L = 5$ m
- Undrained cohesion $c_u = 90$ kN/m²
- Adhesion factor $\alpha = 0.5$
- Unit weight of concrete $\gamma_c = 24$ kN/m³

Determine the **ultimate uplift capacity** and the **safe uplift load** (F.S. = 3.0). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theoretical Discussion (7 Marks):**
- Components of uplift capacity: bulb resistance + skin friction (downward) + self-weight of pile.
- Bulb resistance dominates because $A_u$ is 6.25 to 9 times $A_p$.
- IS formula derivation with $N_c = 9$.
- Importance in expansive (black cotton) soils.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_u &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.750)^2 = 0.4418 \text{ m}^2 \\[4pt]
A_p &= \frac{\pi}{4}(0.300)^2 = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Bulb uplift resistance:**

$$Q_{b,\text{up}} = A_u N_c c_u = (0.4418)(9)(90) = 357.86 \text{ kN}$$

**Uplift skin friction (downward, resisting pullout):**

$$Q_{sf,\text{up}} = \alpha c_u \pi D L = (0.5)(90)(\pi)(0.300)(5) = 212.06 \text{ kN}$$

**Self-weight of pile:**

$$W_p = A_p L \gamma_c = (0.0707)(5)(24) = 8.48 \text{ kN}$$

**Ultimate uplift capacity:**

$$Q_{u,\text{up}} = 357.86 + 212.06 + 8.48 = 578.40 \text{ kN}$$

**Safe uplift load:**

$$Q_{\text{safe,up}} = \frac{578.40}{3.0} = \boxed{192.80 \text{ kN}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Under-Reamed Pile Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing $A_u$ and $A_p$:** Many students compute end-bearing over the shaft area $A_p$ instead of the bulb area $A_u$. This underestimates capacity by 6–9 times — a major mistake costing up to **3–4 marks**.
> 2. **Using $N_c$ for the upper bulb:** In a double-bulb pile, IS 2911 does **not** apply $N_c$ to the upper bulb; the resistance is taken as $A_a \cdot c_u$ only. Adding $N_c$ inflates the answer incorrectly.
> 3. **Wrong F.S. for uplift:** IS mandates **F.S. = 3.0** for uplift, not 2.5. Examiners specifically check this.
> 4. **Forgetting self-weight in uplift:** The pile's self-weight is small but mandatory in the formula. Omitting it costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Spacing violation:** A common conceptual error is stating $L_b = D_u$ (too close) or $L_b = 3D_u$ (too far). The IS range must be quoted correctly.
> 6. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing mm and m in area calculations. Always convert diameters to **meters** before squaring.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Under-Reamed Piles (IS 2911 Part 3):**
> - ✅ Under-reamed pile = bored cast-in-situ pile with **bulb(s)** for higher bearing + uplift capacity.
> - ✅ **IS 2911 (Part 3) : 1980** (reaffirmed 2010) is the governing code.
> - ✅ $D_u = 2.5D$ to $3.0D$; $H_b = 1.25D$ to $1.5D$.
> - ✅ $L_b = 1.25 D_u$ to $1.5 D_u$ (max $2.0 D_u$) for double-bulb piles.
> - ✅ **Single bulb** → one bulb at pile tip; **Double bulb** → one at tip + one at mid-depth.
> - ✅ End-bearing on bulb: $Q_{eb} = A_u N_c c_u$ (clay) or IS sand formula.
> - ✅ Upper bulb in double-bulb: resistance = $A_a \cdot c_u$ (no $N_c$).
> - ✅ Uplift capacity formula: $Q_{u,\text{up}} = Q_{b,\text{up}} + Q_{sf,\text{up}} + W_p$.
> - ✅ **F.S. = 2.5** for compression (static); **3.0** for uplift and dynamic.
> - ✅ $N_c = 9$ (Skempton) for $\phi = 0$ clay as per IS convention.
> - ✅ Adhesion factor $\alpha = 0.4$ to $0.8$ for clays; $\delta \approx \tfrac{2}{3}\phi$ for sands.
> - ✅ Minimum concrete grade: **M 20** (static), **M 25** (dynamic/lateral loads).
> - ✅ Minimum clear cover: **40 mm** (normal), **50 mm** (marine/coastal).
> - ✅ Bulbs are best suited for **expansive soils**, **soft clays**, and **uplift-loaded** structures.
> - ✅ The annular area $A_a = A_u - A_p$ is the key design parameter for the **upper bulb's anchorage** action.
> - ✅ Never confuse **end-bearing** (uses $A_u$ and $N_c$) with **upper-bulb anchorage** (uses $A_a$ without $N_c$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Numerical Problems, Drilled Piers (Straight Shafted and Belled) in Clay
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Numerical Problems, Drilled Piers (Straight Shafted and Belled) in Clay

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Drilled Pier (Caisson)**: A deep foundation element constructed by drilling a cylindrical shaft into the ground, placing a reinforcement cage, and filling the shaft with concrete. When constructed in clay, the load transfer mechanism is governed by the **α-method (total stress approach)** for shaft resistance and **Skempton's bearing capacity factor** for end bearing.

A **Straight Shafted Drilled Pier** is one where the diameter of the pier remains constant from the ground surface to its tip.

A **Belled Drilled Pier** is one where the bottom of the shaft is enlarged (belled) into a truncated cone shape to mobilize a larger end-bearing area, provided the founding stratum is stiff clay or rock capable of supporting the bell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Net Ultimate Bearing Capacity** of a drilled pier in clay is the sum of the **net shaft resistance (skin friction)** plus the **net end bearing resistance** at the tip, both of which are evaluated using the **undrained shear strength (cᵤ)** of the clay.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine pushing a **smooth steel rod** vertically into a tub of soft butter:
- The **side surfaces** of the rod develop a *sticky* resistance as the butter grips the rod — this is **shaft friction (Qₛ)**.
- The **flat bottom** of the rod pushes against the butter, creating a *pressure bulge* beneath — this is **end bearing (Qₚ)**.

Now, if you weld a **wider metal disc** to the bottom of the rod before pushing it in, the bottom area increases, and so does the end-bearing capacity. This disc is the **bell**. The total capacity = sticky sides + push on the disc.

> The **drilled pier** works the same way: concrete shaft gripped by clay (shaft friction) + concrete base pushing the clay (end bearing). For a **belled pier**, the base is enlarged, dramatically increasing end bearing.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Unit weight of concrete (γc) ≈ 24 kN/m³** (often taken as 23.5–25 kN/m³).
- **Factor of Safety (FS)**: **2.5 to 3.0** for compression (KTU board value = **2.5** for individual pier; **3.0** for group/est. load).
- **Skempton's Nc** for Φ = 0 (undrained):
  - **Nc = 6.0** for end bearing on **belled pier** (shallow rupture, local shear in clay).
  - **Nc = 9.0** for end bearing on **straight shafted pier** (deep punching shear).
  - Nc = 6 × (1 + 0.2B/L) for rectangular; for circular, ≈ 6.2 for B/L = 1.
- **Adhesion factor α** (Tomlinson): α = 0.5 (for cᵤ ≤ 24 kPa), reducing to 0.36 (for cᵤ = 72 kPa), interpolated linearly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanism

#### A. Shaft Resistance (Skin Friction) in Clay — α Method
The total stress (α) method is preferred for clays because installation disturbance and stress relief cause the effective stress approach to under-predict capacity in drilled piers.

1. The clay's undrained strength (cᵤ) is determined (UDS, VST, or correlations from SPT-N).
2. The adhesion factor α is read from the Tomlinson chart (or formula) based on cᵤ.
3. Average cᵤ along the shaft depth is computed: $\bar{c}_u$.
4. Unit shaft friction: $f_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u$ (kPa).
5. Total shaft resistance: $Q_s = f_s \cdot A_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L$ (kN).

#### B. End Bearing Resistance in Clay — Skempton's Theory
For Φ = 0 (undrained) conditions, the net ultimate end bearing is:

$$Q_p = c_u \cdot N_c \cdot A_p$$

where:
- $A_p = (\pi/4) \cdot D^2$ for straight shafted piers.
- $A_p = (\pi/4) \cdot D_b^2$ for belled piers (use bell diameter $D_b$).
- $N_c = 9$ for straight shafted (deep failure surface — Skempton, 1951).
- $N_c = 6$ for belled pier (because the bell is wider than the shaft, the failure becomes shallower, similar to a shallow footing on clay).

#### C. Why Nc Changes from 9 (straight) to 6 (belled) — The Physical Reason
- For a **straight shafted pier** (D/L ≤ 1), the failure surface is forced deep, punching into the clay — Nc = 9.
- For a **belled pier**, the bell creates a localized bearing failure similar to a shallow circular footing on the surface of clay — Nc drops to 6.
- This drop is the *trade-off* the engineer accepts in exchange for a **larger base area (A_b)**.

#### D. Net vs. Gross Capacity
- **Gross**: $Q_{ult,gross} = Q_s + Q_p + W_p$ (where $W_p$ = weight of pier).
- **Net** (used in practice, to compare with applied load): $Q_{ult,net} = Q_s + Q_p - W_p$.
- KTU standard practice: Report **net ultimate** and apply FS to obtain **net allowable** = $Q_{all,net} = Q_{ult,net}/FS$.

#### E. Bell Geometry Limits (Critical — Frequently Tested)
The bell angle (β, measured from vertical) must satisfy:
- **β ≤ 30°** (i.e., 1H : 2V slope) — typical conservative limit.
- For some codes: **β ≤ 45°** maximum.
- Bell height: $h_b = (D_b - D_s)/(2 \tan\beta)$.
- For β = 30°: $h_b = 0.866 \cdot (D_b - D_s)$.

> [!NOTE]
> The bell cannot be excessively steep; otherwise, the **concrete cannot be placed properly** and **stress concentrations** cause the bell shoulder to spall off. KTU examiners often deduct marks if β is not checked.

#### F. Suitability of Belled Piers
- ✔ Stiff to hard clay (cᵤ > 100 kPa) above the bell.
- ✔ Depth to founding stratum < 25 m (practical limit for hand excavation).
- ✘ Soft clay above the bell → bell cannot be excavated cleanly.
- ✘ Water table above bell tip → requires casing or slurry, costly.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|------|---------|
| 1 | Unit shaft friction | $f_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u$ | kPa | α-method (total stress) |
| 2 | Total shaft resistance | $Q_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L$ | kN | $\bar{c}_u$ = avg. cᵤ along shaft |
| 3 | End bearing (straight) | $Q_p = c_{u,base} \cdot 9 \cdot (\pi/4) D^2$ | kN | Skempton, Nc=9 |
| 4 | End bearing (belled) | $Q_p = c_{u,base} \cdot 6 \cdot (\pi/4) D_b^2$ | kN | Nc=6 (bell as shallow footing) |
| 5 | Adhesion factor α | α = 0.5 (cᵤ ≤ 24 kPa); α = 0.36 (cᵤ = 72 kPa); linear interp. | – | Tomlinson (1971) |
| 6 | Net ultimate capacity | $Q_{u,net} = Q_s + Q_p - W_p$ | kN | $W_p$ = weight of pier |
| 7 | Net allowable capacity | $Q_{a,net} = Q_{u,net}/FS$ | kN | FS = 2.5 (comp.); 3.0 (uplift) |
| 8 | Bell height | $h_b = (D_b - D_s)/(2 \tan\beta)$ | m | β = bell angle from vertical |
| 9 | Bell check | $\tan\beta \leq 1/\sqrt{3}$ (β ≤ 30°) | – | Conservative geometry |
| 10 | Pier weight | $W_p = \gamma_c \cdot V_{pier}$ | kN | $V_{pier}$ = volume |
| 11 | Average cᵤ | $\bar{c}_u = (\sum c_{u,i} \cdot L_i)/L$ | kPa | Weighted by layer thickness |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Bridge piers in riverbeds** (deep stiff clay) → belled piers transfer column loads to dense stratum.
- **High-rise buildings in coastal Kerala (Cochin, Alappuzha clay)** → straight shafted drilled piers with cased construction.
- **Transmission tower foundations** → belled piers resist uplift (cone method) and compression.
- **Under-reamed piles (Indian variant)** — same principle as belled piers; mandatory in expansive black cotton soils.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Complete Worked Example 1 — Straight Shafted Pier in Clay

**Problem Statement**:
A straight shafted drilled pier has diameter **D = 1.2 m** and is embedded to a depth **L = 15 m** in a uniform clay deposit with undrained shear strength **cᵤ = 80 kPa** and unit weight **γ = 18 kN/m³**. The groundwater table is at 3 m depth. Determine the **net ultimate axial capacity** and the **net allowable load** using **FS = 2.5**.

**Step 1 — Identify the adhesion factor α (Tomlinson chart)**:
For cᵤ = 80 kPa (slightly above the 72 kPa reference), use linear interpolation:

$$\alpha = 0.36 + \left[\frac{0.5 - 0.36}{24 - 72}\right] \cdot (80 - 72) = 0.36 + \left[\frac{0.14}{-48}\right] \cdot 8$$

$$= 0.36 - 0.0233 = 0.337$$

> **Logic**: The factor α is the inverse function of cᵤ — softer clays mobilize higher adhesion ratio; stiffer clays have lower α because of brittle interface behavior.

**Step 2 — Unit shaft friction**:

$$f_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u = 0.337 \cdot 80 = 26.96 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Total shaft resistance**:

$$Q_s = f_s \cdot A_s = f_s \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L = 26.96 \cdot \pi \cdot 1.2 \cdot 15$$

$$= 26.96 \cdot 56.549 = 1524.6 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — End bearing resistance** (straight shafted → Nc = 9):

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (1.2)^2 = 1.131 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_p = c_{u,base} \cdot N_c \cdot A_p = 80 \cdot 9 \cdot 1.131 = 814.3 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Pier self-weight** (concrete γc = 24 kN/m³):

$$W_p = \gamma_c \cdot V_p = 24 \cdot A_p \cdot L = 24 \cdot 1.131 \cdot 15 = 407.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Net ultimate capacity**:

$$Q_{u,net} = Q_s + Q_p - W_p = 1524.6 + 814.3 - 407.2 = 1931.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 — Net allowable capacity**:

$$Q_{a,net} = \frac{Q_{u,net}}{FS} = \frac{1931.7}{2.5} = 772.7 \text{ kN}$$

> **Final Answer**: **Qₐ,ₙₑₜ ≈ 773 kN**

---

### 3.2 Complete Worked Example 2 — Belled Pier in Clay

**Problem Statement**:
A belled drilled pier is founded at **L = 12 m** in stiff clay. Shaft diameter **Dₛ = 1.0 m**, bell diameter **D_b = 2.5 m**, and bell angle **β = 30°**. Average cᵤ along shaft = **60 kPa**, cᵤ at bell base = **120 kPa**. Determine net ultimate and allowable capacity (FS = 2.5).

**Step 1 — Adhesion factor α for cᵤ = 60 kPa** (linear interp. between 24 and 72):

$$\alpha = 0.36 + \left[\frac{0.5 - 0.36}{24 - 72}\right] \cdot (60 - 72) = 0.36 + \left[\frac{0.14}{-48}\right] \cdot (-12)$$

$$= 0.36 + 0.035 = 0.395$$

**Step 2 — Unit shaft friction**:

$$f_s = \alpha \cdot \bar{c}_u = 0.395 \cdot 60 = 23.7 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Shaft resistance** (along the cylindrical part only, not the bell):

$$Q_s = 23.7 \cdot \pi \cdot 1.0 \cdot 12 = 893.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Verify bell geometry**:

$$h_b = \frac{D_b - D_s}{2 \tan\beta} = \frac{2.5 - 1.0}{2 \cdot \tan 30°} = \frac{1.5}{2 \cdot 0.5774} = 1.299 \text{ m} \approx 1.3 \text{ m}$$

✔ **Geometry OK** (β = 30° ≤ 30° limit, full mobilization possible).

**Step 5 — End bearing resistance** (belled → Nc = 6):

$$A_b = \frac{\pi}{4} D_b^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (2.5)^2 = 4.909 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_p = c_{u,base} \cdot 6 \cdot A_b = 120 \cdot 6 \cdot 4.909 = 3534.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Pier weight** (shaft cylinder + truncated cone bell):

Volume of shaft cylinder: $V_s = (\pi/4)(1.0)^2 \cdot 12 = 9.425 \text{ m}^3$.

Volume of bell (truncated cone, h_b = 1.3 m):
$$V_b = \frac{\pi \cdot h_b}{3} \left(R_s^2 + R_s R_b + R_b^2\right) = \frac{\pi \cdot 1.3}{3} \left(0.5^2 + 0.5 \cdot 1.25 + 1.25^2\right)$$

$$= \frac{1.361}{3} (0.25 + 0.625 + 1.5625) = 0.4537 \cdot 2.4375 = 1.106 \text{ m}^3$$

$$V_{total} = 9.425 + 1.106 = 10.531 \text{ m}^3$$

$$W_p = 24 \cdot 10.531 = 252.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 — Net ultimate capacity**:

$$Q_{u,net} = Q_s + Q_p - W_p = 893.4 + 3534.4 - 252.7 = 4175.1 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 8 — Net allowable capacity**:

$$Q_{a,net} = \frac{4175.1}{2.5} = 1670.0 \text{ kN}$$

> **Final Answer**: **Qₐ,ₙₑₜ ≈ 1670 kN** — a **2.16× increase** over the straight shafted case for the same shaft length, demonstrating the value of the bell.

---

### 3.3 Complete Worked Example 3 — Comparison & Multi-Layer Clay (Advanced)

**Problem Statement**:
A straight shafted pier, **D = 1.5 m, L = 20 m**, passes through 3 clay layers:
- Layer 1 (0–6 m): cᵤ = 30 kPa
- Layer 2 (6–14 m): cᵤ = 70 kPa
- Layer 3 (14–20 m): cᵤ = 110 kPa (bearing stratum)

Determine net ultimate capacity. Take FS = 2.5.

**Step 1 — Adhesion factor for each layer**:

Layer 1: cᵤ = 30 kPa → between 24 and 72, interpolate:
$$\alpha_1 = 0.36 + \left(\frac{0.5 - 0.36}{24 - 72}\right) \cdot (30 - 24) = 0.36 + \left(\frac{0.14}{-48}\right) \cdot 6 = 0.36 - 0.0175 = 0.3425$$

Layer 2: cᵤ = 70 kPa:
$$\alpha_2 = 0.36 + \left(\frac{0.14}{-48}\right) \cdot (70 - 72) = 0.36 + 0.00583 = 0.3658$$

Layer 3: cᵤ = 110 kPa (> 72 kPa) → use $\alpha_3 = 0.36$ (extrapolate as constant beyond 72 kPa; Tomlinson chart plateau).

**Step 2 — Shaft resistance layer-wise**:

$$A_s = \pi \cdot D = \pi \cdot 1.5 = 4.712 \text{ m}^2/\text{m (per metre depth)}$$

$$Q_{s1} = \alpha_1 \cdot c_{u1} \cdot \pi D \cdot L_1 = 0.3425 \cdot 30 \cdot 4.712 \cdot 6 = 290.5 \text{ kN}$$

$$Q_{s2} = 0.3658 \cdot 70 \cdot 4.712 \cdot 8 = 965.2 \text{ kN}$$

$$Q_{s3} = 0.36 \cdot 110 \cdot 4.712 \cdot 6 = 1120.0 \text{ kN}$$

$$Q_{s,total} = 290.5 + 965.2 + 1120.0 = 2375.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — End bearing** (cᵤ at base = 110 kPa, Nc = 9):

$$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4}(1.5)^2 = 1.767 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Q_p = 110 \cdot 9 \cdot 1.767 = 1749.3 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Pier weight**:

$$W_p = 24 \cdot 1.767 \cdot 20 = 848.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Net ultimate and allowable**:

$$Q_{u,net} = 2375.7 + 1749.3 - 848.2 = 3276.8 \text{ kN}$$

$$Q_{a,net} = \frac{3276.8}{2.5} = 1310.7 \text{ kN}$$

> **Final Answer**: **Qₐ,ₙₑₜ ≈ 1311 kN**

---

### 3.4 Comparative Summary Table — When Does a Bell Pay Off?

| Parameter | Straight (Ex. 1) | Belled (Ex. 2) | Belled Advantage |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|------------------|
| Shaft length (m) | 15 | 12 | Shorter, less excavation |
| Shaft diam (m) | 1.2 | 1.0 | – |
| Bell diam (m) | – | 2.5 | **+108% base area** |
| Qₛ (kN) | 1524.6 | 893.4 | Straight > Belled (longer shaft) |
| Qₚ (kN) | 814.3 | 3534.4 | **Belled is 4.3× higher** |
| Net ult (kN) | 1931.7 | 4175.1 | **Belled 2.16× higher** |
| Allowable (kN) | 772.7 | 1670.0 | **Belled 2.16× higher** |

### 4.4 Failure Surface Schematic (Conceptual — Described in Text)

For a **straight shafted pier**, the failure surface extends from the pier base *upwards and sideways* in a deep, near-cylindrical shear zone — justifying **Nc = 9**.

For a **belled pier**, the failure surface is **shallow and confined near the bell base** (Prandtl-type general shear for a circular footing on clay) — justifying **Nc = 6**.
