| 2.06 | 203.59 | 1000.41 | 1204.00 | 1200 | **Match!** |

**Step 10 — Root ≈ 2.06 m. Adopt B = 2.10 m** (rounded up to next practical dimension in multiples of 0.05 m).

**Step 11 — Verification of the adopted size:**

$$\begin{aligned}
q_{applied} &= \dfrac{1200}{2.10^2} = \dfrac{1200}{4.41} = 272.11 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \\
q_{ns} &= 235.82 + 23.28 \times 2.10 = 235.82 + 48.89 = 284.71 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \\
\text{Check: } q_{applied} &= 272.11 < 284.71 = q_{ns} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

**The footing is safe. Provide B = 2.10 m × 2.10 m.**

---

### Part (c) — Effect of Increasing Depth to 2.5 m

**Step 12 — Recompute q_u with D_f = 2.5 m.**  
Only the 2nd term changes:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{2nd term: } \gamma D_f N_q &= 18 \times 2.5 \times 12.7 = 45.0 \times 12.7 = 571.50 \;\text{kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
q_u &= 391.56 + 571.50 + 69.84 B = 963.06 + 69.84 B \;\text{kN/m}^2 \\
q_{nu} &= 963.06 + 69.84 B - 18 \times 2.5 = 963.06 + 69.84 B - 45.0 = 918.06 + 69.84 B \\
q_{ns} &= \dfrac{918.06 + 69.84 B}{3.0} = 306.02 + 23.28 B
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 13 — Solve new cubic:**

$$\begin{aligned}
1200 &= B^2 (306.02 + 23.28 B) \\
23.28\,B^3 + 306.02\,B^2 - 1200 &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

| Trial B (m) | 23.28 B³ | 306.02 B² | Sum | Target = 1200 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1.7 | 114.45 | 884.40 | 998.85 | Too low |
| 1.8 | 135.77 | 991.50 | 1127.27 | Too low |
| 1.9 | 159.69 | 1104.73 | 1264.42 | Slightly high |
| 1.85 | 147.36 | 1047.50 | 1194.86 | Just low |
| 1.86 | 149.88 | 1058.81 | 1208.69 | **Match!** |

**New footing side B₂ ≈ 1.86 m. Adopt B = 1.90 m.**

**Step 14 — Compute the percentage reduction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\%\text{ reduction} &= \dfrac{B_1 - B_2}{B_1} \times 100 = \dfrac{2.10 - 1.90}{2.10} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{0.20}{2.10} \times 100 \approx 9.52\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** By increasing D_f from 1.5 m to 2.5 m, the footing size dropped by **9.5 %**. In volume terms, the **concrete saving is ≈ 18 %** (B² scaling). This shows **why architects often push the plinth deeper** on weak soils — it pays off in foundation economy.

---

## 3.2 Supplementary Analytical Check — Local Shear Failure

Since the soil has φ = 25° < 29°, the failure mode could be **local shear** (loose soil). Re-check using modified parameters:

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi_m &= \tan^{-1}(0.67 \tan 25°) = \tan^{-1}(0.67 \times 0.4663) = \tan^{-1}(0.3124) \approx 17.36° \\
c_m &= 0.67 \times 12 = 8.04 \;\text{kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

For φ_m = 17.36°, interpolating from the table → N_c ≈ 13.0, N_q ≈ 4.6, N_γ ≈ 2.4 (approx).  
The reduced q_u (local shear) is much lower — therefore **general shear governs** when c > 0 and φ is reasonable. Use **general shear values** for design unless the soil is **very loose (N < 10)**.

---

## 3.3 Final Design Summary Table

| Parameter | Case 1: D_f = 1.5 m | Case 2: D_f = 2.5 m |
|---|:---:|:---:|
| q_u (kN/m²) | 734.46 + 69.84 B | 963.06 + 69.84 B |
| q_{ns} (kN/m²) | 235.82 + 23.28 B | 306.02 + 23.28 B |
| Required B (m) | 2.06 | 1.86 |
| Adopted B (m) | **2.10** | **1.90** |
| Actual pressure (kN/m²) | 272.11 | 332.40 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Depth of Embedment Design Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Collect Soil and Load Data] --> B[Soil Parameters: c, phi, gamma, GWT Depth]
    A --> C[Structural Load: P, Column Layout]
    B --> D[Select Footing Shape: Strip, Square, Circular, Rectangular]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Read Bearing Capacity Factors: Nc, Nq, Ngamma from phi]
    E --> F[Compute Ultimate Bearing Capacity qu using Terzaghi Shape Equation]
    F --> G[Compute Net Ultimate Capacity: qnu = qu minus gamma Df]
    G --> H[Apply Factor of Safety: qns = qnu divided by FOS]
    H --> I[Equate Contact Pressure: P over A = qns]
    I --> J[Solve for B: Quadratic for Strip, Cubic for Square]
    J --> K{B within Practical Range?}
    K -->|No| L[Iterate: Adjust Df or FOS and Repeat]
    L --> D
    K -->|Yes| M[Adopt B: Round to Nearest 0.05 m]
    M --> N[Check Local Shear: phi less than 29 degree, use phi_m c_m]
    N --> O{Local Shear qns OK?}
    O -->|No| L
    O -->|Yes| P[Verify Minimum Df: IS 1080, Frost, Scour, GWT]
    P --> Q[Final Design: Footing Size B x B and Depth Df]
    Q --> R[End]
```

## 4.2 Concept Map — How D_f Influences Bearing Capacity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Depth of Embedment Df] --> B[Surcharge term gamma Df Nq]
    A --> C[Overburden Removal: qnu = qu minus gamma Df]
    A --> D[Minimum Depth Code: IS 1080]
    A --> E[Skempton depth factor: 1 plus 0.2 Df over B]
    B --> F[Higher qu]
    C --> G[Lower net applied pressure]
    F --> H[Smaller Footing B]
    G --> H
    D --> I[Safe Foundation]
    E --> I
    H --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** Notice that **D_f appears in 4 different places** in the analysis — the surcharge term, the net conversion, the code-mandated minimum, and Skempton's depth amplification factor. **Forgetting any one of them costs marks.**
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define depth of embedment and list any four factors affecting it.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:** The depth of embedment (D_f) is the vertical distance between the natural ground surface and the base of the foundation. **Four factors affecting D_f are:**
1. Bearing capacity of the soil (higher D_f → higher γD_fN_q term → smaller B).
2. Depth of frost penetration (D_f must be below the frost line).
3. Scour depth in case of bridge foundations in rivers.
4. Position of groundwater table (D_f should be such that GWT does not rise into the footing zone during monsoon).

---

### **Q2. State Terzaghi's bearing capacity equation for a square footing. What is the shape factor for the N_c term in this case?** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:** Terzaghi's bearing capacity equation for a **square footing** is:

$$q_u \;=\; 1.3\,cN_c \;+\; \gamma D_f N_q \;+\; 0.4\,\gamma B N_\gamma$$

The **shape factor for the cohesion (N_c) term is 1.3** (i.e., the cohesion contribution for a square footing is **30 % higher** than that of a strip footing of the same width B).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3. (a)** A strip footing of width 2.0 m is founded at a depth of 1.2 m in a soil with c = 18 kN/m², φ = 20°, and γ = 17.5 kN/m³. Determine the **ultimate, net ultimate, and safe (FOS = 3) net bearing capacities** using Terzaghi's theory. (7 marks)

**Q3. (b)** A square footing has to carry a column load of 1800 kN at a depth of 1.0 m in a c–φ soil with c = 20 kN/m², φ = 30°, and γ = 19 kN/m³. Using FOS = 3, determine the **size of the footing** and verify the design. (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution to Q3(a):**

**Step 1** — Read Terzaghi factors for φ = 20°:

$$N_c = 17.7, \quad N_q = 7.4, \quad N_\gamma = 5.0$$

**Step 2** — Strip footing equation:

$$q_u = cN_c + \gamma D_f N_q + \tfrac{1}{2}\gamma B N_\gamma$$

**Step 3** — Term-by-term:

$$\begin{aligned}
cN_c &= 18 \times 17.7 = 318.60 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \\
\gamma D_f N_q &= 17.5 \times 1.2 \times 7.4 = 21.0 \times 7.4 = 155.40 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \\
\tfrac{1}{2}\gamma B N_\gamma &= 0.5 \times 17.5 \times 2.0 \times 5.0 = 87.50 \;\text{kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4** — Sum up:

$$q_u = 318.60 + 155.40 + 87.50 = 561.50 \;\text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5** — Net ultimate bearing capacity:

$$q_{nu} = q_u - \gamma D_f = 561.50 - (17.5 \times 1.2) = 561.50 - 21.0 = 540.50 \;\text{kN/m}^2$$

**Step 6** — Safe net bearing capacity:

$$q_{ns} = \dfrac{q_{nu}}{FOS} = \dfrac{540.50}{3.0} = 180.17 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \;\;(\text{rounded: } 180.2 \;\text{kN/m}^2)$$

---

#### **Solution to Q3(b):**

**Step 1** — For φ = 30°: N_c = 37.2, N_q = 22.5, N_γ = 19.7

**Step 2** — Square footing equation:

$$q_u = 1.3\,cN_c + \gamma D_f N_q + 0.4\,\gamma B N_\gamma$$

**Step 3** — Constant terms (B-free):

$$\begin{aligned}
1.3\,cN_c &= 1.3 \times 20 \times 37.2 = 26 \times 37.2 = 967.20 \\
\gamma D_f N_q &= 19 \times 1.0 \times 22.5 = 427.50 \\
0.4\,\gamma B N_\gamma &= 0.4 \times 19 \times B \times 19.7 = 149.72\,B
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4** — Form the q_{ns} expression:

$$q_u = 1394.70 + 149.72\,B \quad\Rightarrow\quad q_{ns} = \dfrac{1394.70 + 149.72 B - 19 \times 1.0}{3} = 458.57 + 49.91 B$$

**Step 5** — Equate P/A to q_{ns}:

$$\dfrac{1800}{B^2} = 458.57 + 49.91 B \quad\Rightarrow\quad 49.91 B^3 + 458.57 B^2 - 1800 = 0$$

**Step 6** — Trial and error:

| B (m) | 49.91 B³ | 458.57 B² | Sum | Target 1800 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1.5 | 168.45 | 1031.78 | 1200.23 | Too low |
| 1.7 | 245.13 | 1325.27 | 1570.40 | Too low |
| 1.8 | 291.14 | 1485.77 | 1776.91 | Just low |
| 1.82 | 301.13 | 1519.30 | 1820.43 | **Match** |

**Step 7** — Adopt B = 1.85 m (rounded up):

$$\begin{aligned}
q_{applied} &= 1800 / 1.85^2 = 1800 / 3.4225 = 525.93 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \\
q_{ns} &= 458.57 + 49.91 \times 1.85 = 458.57 + 92.33 = 550.90 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \\
\text{Check: } 525.93 < 550.90 &\Rightarrow \text{ SAFE } \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

---

### **Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Choice**

**Q4. (a)** Explain the procedure to determine the **minimum depth of foundation** as per IS 1080. List the various criteria considered. (7 marks)

**Q4. (b)** A square footing of size 2.5 m × 2.5 m is placed at 1.0 m depth in a soil with c = 25 kN/m², φ = 25°, and γ = 18 kN/m³. Compute the **safe load** the footing can carry with FOS = 3. If the depth is increased to 2.0 m, compute the **percentage increase in safe load capacity**. (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution to Q4(a):**

**Minimum depth of foundation as per IS 1080:1985** is governed by the **most critical of the following criteria** (whichever gives the largest D_f):

1. **Frost penetration depth** — D_f ≥ frost line in cold climates (from climatological data).
2. **Depth of scour** — D_f ≥ anticipated scour depth for bridge piers.
3. **Depth of topsoil / organic soil** — D_f ≥ thickness of weak top layer.
4. **Depth of moisture variation** in expansive soils — D_f ≥ 1.5 m in black cotton soil.
5. **Bearing capacity and settlement** — D_f large enough to give q_{ns} ≥ applied pressure.
6. **Adjacent foundation influence** — D_f below the stress influence zone (≈ 1.5B) of nearby footings.
7. **Practical minimum** — D_f shall not be less than **0.50 m** in non-aggressive soils.

**Adopted D_f = maximum of all the above.**

---

#### **Solution to Q4(b):**

**Step 1** — For φ = 25°: N_c = 25.1, N_q = 12.7, N_γ = 9.7

**Step 2** — Square footing, B = 2.5 m, D_f = 1.0 m:

$$\begin{aligned}
q_u &= 1.3 \times 25 \times 25.1 + 18 \times 1.0 \times 12.7 + 0.4 \times 18 \times 2.5 \times 9.7 \\
&= 815.75 + 228.60 + 174.60 \\
&= 1218.95 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \\
q_{ns} &= \dfrac{1218.95 - 18 \times 1.0}{3} = \dfrac{1200.95}{3} = 400.32 \;\text{kN/m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3** — Safe load for B = 2.5 m:

$$P_1 = q_{ns} \times B^2 = 400.32 \times 2.5^2 = 400.32 \times 6.25 = 2502.0 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 4** — Repeat with D_f = 2.0 m:

$$\begin{aligned}
q_u &= 815.75 + 18 \times 2.0 \times 12.7 + 174.60 \\
&= 815.75 + 457.20 + 174.60 = 1447.55 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \\
q_{ns} &= \dfrac{1447.55 - 18 \times 2.0}{3} = \dfrac{1411.55}{3} = 470.52 \;\text{kN/m}^2 \\
P_2 &= 470.52 \times 6.25 = 2940.75 \;\text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5** — Percentage increase:

$$\% \text{ increase} = \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{P_1} \times 100 = \dfrac{2940.75 - 2502.0}{2502.0} \times 100 \approx 17.54\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract γD_f** in the net bearing capacity (q_{nu} = q_u − γD_f) is the **single most common mistake** — it costs **2 full marks**.
> 2. **Using the wrong shape factors** (e.g., strip factors for a square footing) is the second-most-frequent error.
> 3. **Skipping the local shear check** when φ < 29° loses at least **1 mark** in 14-mark problems.
> 4. **Rounding B downward** instead of upward (always round **up** for safety).
> 5. **Forgetting units** in the final answer — write "m" for dimensions and "kN/m²" for pressure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **D_f is the depth from NGL to the footing base**; it appears in the **surcharge term γD_fN_q** in Terzaghi's equation.
- [x] **q_{nu} = q_u − γD_f** — this removal of overburden is what "net" means.
- [x] **Shape constants** to memorise: Strip → (1, 1, 0.5); Square → (1.3, 1, 0.4); Circular → (1.3, 1, 0.3).
- [x] **Bearing capacity factors** depend **only on φ** — read from the KTU table for φ = 0°, 5°, 10°, … 45°.
- [x] **Equating P/A = q_{ns}** gives a **cubic equation for square footings** and a **quadratic for strip footings** — solved by trial/error or formula.
- [x] **Always round B upward** to the nearest 50 mm.
- [x] **FOS ≥ 3** is the KTU standard for bearing capacity design.
- [x] **Minimum D_f ≥ 0.50 m** (IS 1080) and below frost/scour/organic layers.
- [x] **Local shear** check mandatory when φ < 29° — use φ_m = tan⁻¹(0.67 tan φ), c_m = 0.67 c.
- [x] **Increasing D_f reduces the required B** but not linearly — concrete savings are **proportional to B²**.
- [x] **Skempton's formula** (for pure c-soil, c ≠ 0, φ = 0) gives **q_u = 5c(1 + 0.2B/L)(1 + 0.2D_f/B)** for D_f ≤ B.
- [x] **GWT correction:** use **γ' = γ_sat − γ_w** if GWT is between NGL and footing base.
- [x] **IS 6403 (Indian Standard)** method is an alternative — factors differ from Terzaghi's (e.g., for φ = 30°, IS gives N_γ = 7.70 vs Terzaghi's 19.7). Always state which method is used.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anchored Sheet Pile Walls
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Anchored Sheet Pile Walls

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Anchored Sheet Pile Wall** (also termed a *Tied-Back Sheet Pile Wall*) is a flexible earth-retaining structure in which a row of interlocking steel, timber, or reinforced concrete sheet piles is driven into the ground and mechanically tied back near its top to a *deadman* anchorage, *soil/rock anchors*, or a *wale-and-strut* system. The tie-rod (anchor) provides an additional horizontal restraint that dramatically reduces the lateral deflection, bending moment, and required depth of embedment when compared to a **cantilever sheet pile wall**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic belongs to the cluster *"Design of Flexible Earth-Retaining Structures"*. The two classical design philosophies mandated by the syllabus are the **Free Earth Support Method** and the **Fixed Earth Support (Blum's Equivalent Beam) Method**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fishing Rod" Principle

Imagine holding a long fishing rod vertically in loose sand with one hand at the top and pushing the other end sideways. Without support, the rod bends excessively and tips over — this is a **cantilever wall** resisting purely by *passive resistance* of the embedded sand. Now, tie a string from your upper hand to a stake driven firmly behind you. The string immediately **reduces the bending**, **lowers the tip deflection**, and **lets the rod stand in shallower embedment**. The string is the **anchor tie-rod**, your upper hand is the **wale**, and the stake is the **deadman anchor**. The same physics — equilibrium of moments, forces, and lateral earth pressures — governs anchored sheet piling used in real projects such as dock walls, deep basements, and cofferdams.

## 1.3 When Anchored Walls Are Used

| Situation | Reason for Choosing Anchored Wall |
|---|---|
| Wall height **> 6 m** | Cantilever design becomes uneconomical due to huge bending moments |
| Soft clays / loose sands | Long embedment in cantilever mode is impractical |
| Deep excavations near buildings | Anchor restricts deflection and protects adjacent structures |
| Waterfront structures (quay walls, wharves) | Permanent tie-rods handle surcharge from cranes, cargo, traffic |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Unit weight of water: **γ_w = 9.81 kN/m³** (≈ 10 kN/m³ for quick design)
- Typical steel sheet pile section modulus: **S ≈ 1000 – 3500 cm³/m**
- Standard design codes: **IS 9527, BS 8002, Eurocode 7 (EC7)**
- Active earth-pressure coefficient: **K_a = tan²(45° – φ/2)**
- Passive earth-pressure coefficient: **K_p = tan²(45° + φ/2)**
- Wall friction angle: typically **δ = (2/3) φ** for steel piles in sand

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition Rule (KTU 2024):** Anchored walls are *statically indeterminate to the 1st degree*. The redundancy is resolved by an **assumption about toe fixity** — either the toe is *free to rotate* (Free Earth Support) or it is *perfectly fixed* (Fixed Earth Support / Blum's Method).

## 1.5 Components of an Anchored Sheet Pile Wall

1. **Sheet Piles** — the main retaining skin.
2. **Wale (Waling)** — horizontal beam distributing anchor force along the wall.
3. **Tie-Rod** — tension member, usually at depth **0.3 m to 1.0 m** below the top.
4. **Deadman Anchor** — buried block/continuous strip behind the wall in the *stable* zone.
5. **Sheet Pile Toe** — the bottom portion driven below the dredge line for embedment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's Eye-View:** A good anchored-wall design has the *net pressure diagram* (Active minus Passive) crossing the zero axis **once** — at the *point of contraflexure* — which lies somewhere in the embedded zone.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Modes of Failure & Assumptions

| Method | Toe Condition | Net Pressure Diagram | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Free Earth Support** | Pile is *free to rotate*; passive resistance just balances moments | Drives wall *forward* everywhere in embedment | Cohesionless soil, short embedment |
| **Fixed Earth Support** | Toe is *fully fixed* (no rotation) | Reverses sign (counter-pressure) near the toe | Cohesive soil or where strict deflection control is needed |
| **Blum's Equivalent Beam Method** | Approximates fixed support by an *equivalent simply-supported beam* | Approximation of fixed condition | **Most widely used in practice** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Both classical methods assume: **(i)** Plane-strain conditions, **(ii)** Wall friction δ = 2φ/3, **(iii)** No residual stresses from pile driving, **(iv)** Steady-state groundwater.

## 2.2 Active & Passive Earth Pressure — Quick Recall

For cohesionless (c = 0) soil at depth *z*:

$$\sigma_a = K_a \, \gamma \, z$$

$$\sigma_p = K_p \, \gamma \, z$$

where 

$$K_a = \frac{1 - \sin \phi}{1 + \sin \phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ - \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

$$K_p = \frac{1 + \sin \phi}{1 - \sin \phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

## 2.3 Net Pressure Diagram (The Heart of the Analysis)

Let **H_1** = height of pile above dredge line, **D** = depth of embedment below dredge line, **H = H_1 + D** = total pile length.

**Step A — Above Dredge Line (0 ≤ z ≤ H_1):**
- Back side: σ_a = K_a γ z
- Front side: σ_p = 0 (no soil) or σ_p = K_p γ z if submerged soil
- **Net pressure at dredge line:** p = K_a γ H_1

**Step B — Below Dredge Line (z > H_1):**
- Back side continues: σ_a = K_a γ z
- Front side: σ_p = K_p γ (z − H_1)
- **Net pressure:** p(z) = K_a γ z − K_p γ (z − H_1)
- At z = H_1: p = K_a γ H_1
- As z increases, p **decreases** linearly because K_p > K_a
- **Point of zero net pressure** (Blum's contraflexure) at depth *d* below dredge line:

$$K_a \, \gamma \, (H_1 + d) = K_p \, \gamma \, d$$

$$\boxed{d = \frac{K_a \, H_1}{K_p - K_a}}$$

This single equation is the **most-tested formula** for anchored walls in KTU exams.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM ALERT:** The following 12 formulas appear in **almost every KTU question** on anchored sheet pile walls. Memorize the symbols — examiners often replace γ with γ_sub and add water-pressure components.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Active coefficient | $K_a = \tan^2(45^\circ - \phi/2)$ | – | Always |
| 2 | Passive coefficient | $K_p = \tan^2(45^\circ + \phi/2)$ | – | Always |
| 3 | Net pressure at dredge line | $p_{DL} = K_a \, \gamma \, H_1$ | kN/m² | Free + Fixed |
| 4 | Point of zero net pressure | $d = K_a H_1 / (K_p - K_a)$ | m | Blum's method |
| 5 | Depth of embedment (Blum) | $D = 1.2 \, d$ (no water) | m | Blum's method |
| 6 | Depth of embedment (Blum, with water) | $D = 1.5 \, d$ | m | Blum's method |
| 7 | Depth of embedment (Free Earth, empirical) | $D = 0.2 \, H$ to $0.4 \, H$ | m | Quick check |
| 8 | Anchor force (Blum's, simply supported beam) | Found by $\sum M = 0$ about lower support | kN/m | Direct design |
| 9 | Max bending moment | $M_{max}$ from shear = 0 in upper beam | kN·m/m | Pile section selection |
| 10 | Pressure at toe of fixed wall (Blum) | $\sigma_t = (K_p - K_a) \, \gamma \, (D - d) + p_0$ | kN/m² | To check fixity |
| 11 | Water pressure (hydrostatic) | $p_w = \gamma_w \, h_w$ | kN/m² | When water table present |
| 12 | Anchor location (typical) | $0.3$ to $1.0$ m below top | m | Practical detailing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trick:** The point of contraflexure *d* in Blum's method is *independent* of γ and *γ_sub*! It depends only on K_a, K_p, and H_1.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Anchored sheet pile walls are the *workhorse* of:
- **Port & Harbour Engineering** (quay walls at Cochin, Vizhinjam)
- **Underground Metro Construction** (Delhi, Kochi Metro cut-and-cover)
- **Deep Building Basements** (5+ storeys below grade)
- **Bridge Abutments** for grade-separated interchanges
- **Cofferdams** for pier construction in rivers
- **Slope Stabilization** along highways in hilly terrain (Munnar, Wayanad ghat roads)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 DERIVATION 1 — Free Earth Support Method (Cohesionless Soil, No Water)

**Setup:**
- Pile height above dredge line = H_1
- Embedment depth = D
- Anchor at top
- Total pile length = H = H_1 + D

**Pressure Diagram:**

- Back side (Active): Triangular, zero at top, value K_a γ H at bottom.
- Front side (Passive): Triangular, zero at dredge line, value K_p γ D at bottom.
- Below dredge line, net pressure = (Active) − (Passive).

**Step 1: Total Active Force on Back Side**

$$P_a = \frac{1}{2} \, K_a \, \gamma \, H^2 = \frac{1}{2} \, K_a \, \gamma \, (H_1 + D)^2$$

**Step 2: Total Passive Force on Front Side (below dredge line only)**

$$P_p = \frac{1}{2} \, K_p \, \gamma \, D^2$$

**Step 3: Lever Arms (measured from the top, where anchor is located)**

- Centroid of active triangle (over full height H) is at H/3 from the top:

$$\bar{y}_a = \frac{H}{3} = \frac{H_1 + D}{3}$$

- Centroid of passive triangle (from dredge line to depth D) is at D/3 from its base, i.e., at distance (H_1 + 2D/3) from the top:

$$\bar{y}_p = H_1 + \frac{2D}{3}$$

**Step 4: Sum of Moments About the Anchor (ΣM = 0)**

In the Free Earth Support Method, the toe is *free to rotate* — therefore, the moment of the *active* force about the anchor is *balanced by* the moment of the *passive* force. The anchor takes care of force balance, not moment balance.

$$P_a \cdot \bar{y}_a = P_p \cdot \bar{y}_p$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma (H_1 + D)^2 \cdot \frac{(H_1 + D)}{3} = \frac{1}{2} K_p \gamma D^2 \cdot \left(H_1 + \frac{2D}{3}\right)$$

**Step 5: Simplify**

Cancelling the common factor ½γ from both sides:

$$K_a \, \frac{(H_1 + D)^3}{3} = K_p \, D^2 \left(H_1 + \frac{2D}{3}\right)$$

Multiply both sides by 3:

$$K_a \, (H_1 + D)^3 = 3 K_p \, D^2 H_1 + 2 K_p \, D^3$$

**Step 6: Expand (H_1 + D)³**

$$(H_1 + D)^3 = H_1^3 + 3 H_1^2 D + 3 H_1 D^2 + D^3$$

Substituting:

$$K_a H_1^3 + 3 K_a H_1^2 D + 3 K_a H_1 D^2 + K_a D^3 = 3 K_p D^2 H_1 + 2 K_p D^3$$

**Step 7: Rearrange as a Cubic in D**

$$K_a H_1^3 + 3 K_a H_1^2 D + 3 H_1 D^2 (K_a - K_p) + D^3 (K_a - 2 K_p) = 0$$

This is the **governing cubic** for the Free Earth Support Method. Solve by:
- **Trial-and-error** (preferred for KTU)
- **Newton-Raphson iteration**
- **Graphical/Chart method** (using NAVFAC DM-7 charts)

**Step 8: Anchor Force T (Horizontal Force Equilibrium ΣF_x = 0)**

The total driving force (P_a) must be balanced by the anchor force (T) plus the passive resistance (P_p):

$$P_a = T + P_p$$

$$\boxed{T = P_a - P_p = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma (H_1 + D)^2 - \frac{1}{2} K_p \gamma D^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Once T is found per unit width (kN/m), multiply by the **pile spacing (s)** to get the load on each tie-rod: T_rod = T × s.

**Step 9: Maximum Bending Moment (M_max)**

The maximum bending moment occurs at the **point of zero shear** in the wall, i.e., where the cumulative pressure from the top equals the anchor force T. For triangular pressure distribution above dredge line:

$$T = \frac{1}{2} \, K_a \, \gamma \, z_{max}^2$$

Solving for z_max (the depth of zero shear from the top):

$$z_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{2T}{K_a \, \gamma}}$$

The maximum bending moment is:

$$M_{max} = T \cdot z_{max} - \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma z_{max}^2 \cdot \frac{z_{max}}{3} = \frac{T \cdot z_{max}}{3} = \frac{2T}{3} \sqrt{\frac{2T}{K_a \gamma}}$$

A simpler, oft-used practical formula for triangular active pressure with point load T at top:

$$\boxed{M_{max} = \frac{T \cdot z_{max}}{3}}$$

---

## 3.2 DERIVATION 2 — Blum's Equivalent Beam Method (Cohesionless Soil, No Water)

**Setup:** 
- Anchor at top
- The wall is replaced by a *simply supported beam* of span L = H_1 + d
- Supports: Anchor (top) and *point of zero net pressure* (depth d below dredge line)
- d is given by $d = K_a H_1 / (K_p - K_a)$
- Actual embedment: **D = 1.2 d** (without water) or **D = 1.5 d** (with water)

**Net Pressure Diagram (above point of contraflexure):**

Region 1 (0 to H_1): Net pressure increases linearly from 0 to K_a γ H_1 (active on back only).

Region 2 (H_1 to H_1 + d): Net pressure decreases linearly from K_a γ H_1 to 0 (active minus passive, with passive growing faster).

**Step 1: Total Net Driving Force (F_net) above point of contraflexure**

The pressure diagram is composed of two triangles:

- **Triangle I** (0 to H_1): Area = ½ × K_a γ H_1 × H_1 = ½ K_a γ H_1²
  - Centroid distance from top = (2/3) H_1
- **Triangle II** (H_1 to H_1 + d): Area = ½ × K_a γ H_1 × d
  - Centroid distance from top = H_1 + d/3

$$F_{net} = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1 \, d$$

$$F_{net} = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1 (H_1 + d)$$

**Step 2: Moment of F_net about the Anchor (top)**

$$M_{net} = \left(\frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1^2\right) \cdot \frac{2 H_1}{3} + \left(\frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1 d\right) \cdot \left(H_1 + \frac{d}{3}\right)$$

$$M_{net} = \frac{1}{3} K_a \gamma H_1^3 + \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1^2 d + \frac{1}{6} K_a \gamma H_1 d^2$$

**Step 3: Reaction R at Lower Support (= Shear in Wall at Point of Contraflexure)**

Taking moments about the lower support (depth H_1 + d from top):

$$R \cdot (H_1 + d) = M_{net}$$

$$\boxed{R = \frac{M_{net}}{H_1 + d}}$$

**Step 4: Anchor Force T (Force Equilibrium ΣF_x = 0)**

$$T + R = F_{net}$$

$$\boxed{T = F_{net} - R = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1 (H_1 + d) - R}$$

**Step 5: Maximum Bending Moment in the Wall**

Locate the depth of zero shear in the upper portion:

- At depth z (0 ≤ z ≤ H_1): Shear V(z) = T − ½ K_a γ z²
- Setting V(z) = 0: z = √(2T / (K_a γ))

If this z lies in Region 1, M_max occurs there. If it lies in Region 2, account for the decreasing net pressure. The classical KTU approach is to tabulate V(z) and find where V = 0.

**Step 6: Actual Embedment Depth (Empirical Blum's Correction)**

Since Blum's equivalent beam assumes a *hinge* at the point of contraflexure (which the real fixed-end wall does *not* have), we must drive the pile deeper to develop the *true* fixity:

$$\boxed{D = 1.2 \, d \text{ (no water)}, \quad D = 1.5 \, d \text{ (water present)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Shortcut:** When a question says "no groundwater" use **D = 1.2 d**; if "groundwater table" is mentioned, use **D = 1.5 d**.

---

## 3.3 DERIVATION 3 — Effect of Surcharge (Uniform Load q Behind Wall)

A uniform surcharge q on the back side adds a *rectangular* active pressure of magnitude **K_a × q** acting over the full height of the pile.

**Equivalent additional height:**

$$H_{eq} = \frac{q}{\gamma}$$

**Additional active force on back:**

$$\Delta P_a = K_a \, q \, H = K_a \, q \, (H_1 + D)$$

This surcharge pressure simply adds a rectangle of magnitude K_a q on top of the existing triangular pressure diagram. The moment about the anchor gets an additional term **K_a q H × (H/2)**, and the cubic in D is modified accordingly. KTU questions frequently test this with *traffic load* or *construction surcharge* of **q = 10 to 20 kN/m²**.

---

## 3.4 DERIVATION 4 — Effect of Water Table (Submerged Soil)

If the water table is at the dredge line:
- Above water table: use **γ** (moist)
- Below water table: use **γ' = γ_sat − γ_w** for effective stress on both active and passive sides
- Add a *hydrostatic water pressure* on both sides that **cancels** (it acts equally on both sides of the impermeable pile)

The net effect is to *reduce* the net pressure below the dredge line, but the *water pressure on the wall* still induces bending. For design, the **increased** bending requires a **larger** pile section.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU Nuance:** Although water pressure cancels on either side of the pile, it produces *equal and opposite* pressures on the back and front, generating a *net zero horizontal force*. But the pile still bends because the pressures act on *different lever arms* if the water levels differ.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (For Concept Reinforcement)

**Given:** H_1 = 6 m, φ = 30°, γ = 18 kN/m³, no water, no surcharge.

**Step 1:** K_a = tan²(45° − 15°) = tan²(30°) = (0.577)² = **0.333**
**Step 2:** K_p = tan²(45° + 15°) = tan²(60°) = (1.732)² = **3.000**
**Step 3:** Point of contraflexure depth d = (0.333 × 6)/(3.0 − 0.333) = 1.998/2.667 = **0.749 m**
**Step 4:** Actual embedment D = 1.2 × 0.749 = **0.899 m** ≈ **0.90 m**
**Step 5:** Total length of pile = H_1 + D = 6 + 0.90 = **6.90 m**
**Step 6:** Net driving force F_net = ½ × 0.333 × 18 × 6 × (6 + 0.749) = ½ × 0.333 × 18 × 6 × 6.749 = **121.4 kN/m**
**Step 7:** M_net = ⅓ × 0.333 × 18 × 6³ + ½ × 0.333 × 18 × 6² × 0.749 + ⅙ × 0.333 × 18 × 6 × 0.749²

Calculating term-by-term:
- Term 1: ⅓ × 0.333 × 18 × 216 = 431.6 kN·m/m
- Term 2: ½ × 0.333 × 18 × 36 × 0.749 = 80.84 kN·m/m
- Term 3: ⅙ × 0.333 × 18 × 6 × 0.561 = 3.36 kN·m/m
- **M_net = 515.8 kN·m/m**

**Step 8:** R = 515.8 / 6.749 = **76.4 kN/m**
**Step 9:** T = 121.4 − 76.4 = **45.0 kN/m**
**Step 10:** z_max (where shear = 0) = √(2 × 45.0/(0.333 × 18)) = √(15.0) = **3.87 m** (lies in Region 1, since 3.87 < 6.0)
**Step 11:** M_max = T × z_max / 3 = 45.0 × 3.87 / 3 = **58.1 kN·m/m**

> [!NOTE]
> **Selected Pile Section:** Required section modulus S = M_max / σ_allow = 58.1 × 10⁶ / 175 = **332,000 mm³/m = 332 cm³/m** → choose a Z-type pile with S ≥ 332 cm³/m.

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Anchored Sheet Pile Wall System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Retained Soil Back Side] --> B[Sheet Pile Wall - Vertical Skin]
    C[Anchor Tie Rod - At Top] --> B
    B --> D[Embedment Zone Below Dredge Line]
    D --> E[Passive Earth Resistance]
    B --> F[Wale - Horizontal Load Distributor]
    F --> C
    C --> G[Deadman Anchor - Behind Wall in Stable Zone]
    H[Surcharge Load q - Traffic or Cargo] --> A
    I[Groundwater Table] --> D
    B --> J[Lateral Deflection Controlled at Top]
    G --> K[Pull-out Resistance from Passive Pressure]
```

## 4.2 Pressure Distribution Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[At Top z equals 0] --> A1[Active Pressure equals 0]
    A1 --> A2[At Dredge Line z equals H1]
    A2 --> A3[Active Pressure equals Ka times gamma times H1]
    A3 --> A4[At Depth d below Dredge Line]
    A4 --> A5[Net Pressure equals 0 - Point of Contraflexure]
    A5 --> A6[At Bottom z equals H1 plus D]
    A6 --> A7[Net Pressure equals Ka times gamma times H plus D minus Kp times gamma times D]
    
    B0[Above Dredge Line Region] --> B1[Triangle Increasing 0 to Ka gamma H1]
    B2[Below Dredge Line to Point of Contraflexure] --> B3[Triangle Decreasing Ka gamma H1 to 0]
    B4[Below Point of Contraflexure to Toe] --> B5[Reverse Triangle Negative Net Pressure Passive Dominates]
```

## 4.3 Free Body Force Diagram — Blum's Equivalent Beam

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UPPER_BEAM [Upper Beam - Simply Supported from Top to Depth d below Dredge Line]
        direction LR
        ANCHOR[Anchor Force T at Top - Pulls Wall Back]:::anchor
        REACT[Reaction R at Depth d - Pushes Wall Back]:::support
        NETP[Net Pressure Distribution - Triangular with Peak Ka gamma H1 at Dredge Line]:::load
        NETP --- ANCHOR
        NETP --- REACT
    end
    
    subgraph LOWER_FIXED [Lower Portion - Provides True Fixity]
        direction LR
        FIX[Toe is Fixed - No Rotation Allowed]:::fixed
        REV[Reverse Pressure - Passive Greater than Active]:::reverse
        REV --> FIX
    end
    
    UPPER_BEAM --> LOWER_FIXED
    
    classDef anchor fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef support fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000

```

## 4.4 Design Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start - Define Wall Geometry H1, Soil Properties phi, gamma] --> B{Soil Type?}
    B -->|Cohesionless c equals 0| C[Use Ka and Kp Formulas]
    B -->|Cohesive with phi equals 0| D[Use Skempton Coefficients Nc]
    C --> E{Groundwater Present?}
    E -->|No| F[Use Effective Unit Weight gamma]
    E -->|Yes| G[Use Submerged Unit Weight gamma prime plus Hydrostatic Pressure]
    F --> H{Choose Design Method}
    G --> H
    H -->|Quick| I[Free Earth Support Method]
    H -->|Rigorous| J[Blum Equivalent Beam Method]
    I --> K[Solve Cubic Equation in D]
    J --> L[Find d equals Ka H1 over Kp minus Ka]
    L --> M[Find D equals 1.2 d or 1.5 d with Water]
    K --> N[Compute Anchor Force T]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Compute Maximum Bending Moment Mmax]
    O --> P[Select Pile Section Modulus S equals Mmax over sigma allow]
    P --> Q[Design Tie Rod Diameter using T and Steel Grade]
    Q --> R[Design Deadman Anchor Block]
    R --> S[End - Check Overall Stability]
```

## 4.5 Comparison Matrix — Free vs Fixed Earth Support

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Free Earth Support Method] --> A1[Toe Free to Rotate]
    A1 --> A2[Smaller Embedment D]
    A2 --> A3[Lower Bending Moment]
    A3 --> A4[Lower Construction Cost]
    A4 --> A5[Used for Temporary Works]
    
    B[Fixed Earth Support Blum Method] --> B1[Toe Fully Fixed]
    B1 --> B2[Larger Embedment D equals 1.2 d to 1.5 d]
    B2 --> B3[Reverse Pressure at Toe]
    B3 --> B4[Higher Bending Moment]
    B4 --> B5[Better Deflection Control]
    B5 --> B6[Used for Permanent Structures]
```
