# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define an anchored sheet pile wall. In what situations is it preferred over a cantilever sheet pile wall?**

**Model Answer:**
An anchored (or tied-back) sheet pile wall is a flexible earth-retaining structure in which the top of the wall is restrained by a tie-rod anchored into a deadman, soil/rock anchor, or strut system, in addition to the passive resistance of the embedded portion.

It is preferred over a cantilever sheet pile wall when:
1. The retained height exceeds approximately **5–6 m**, where cantilever bending moments become uneconomical.
2. The soil is too soft to develop sufficient passive resistance for a cantilever.
3. Deflection control is critical (e.g., near existing buildings, utilities, or roads).
4. Permanent waterfront or quay walls with surcharge from traffic, cranes, or cargo.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is the "point of contraflexure" in Blum's equivalent beam method? How is its depth *d* below the dredge line determined?**

**Model Answer:**
The point of contraflexure is the depth within the embedded portion of the sheet pile wall where the *net lateral pressure* (active on the back minus passive on the front) is **zero**. It corresponds to the point where the bending moment diagram changes sign (from positive/sagging to negative/hogging) in the fixed-earth-support condition.

The depth *d* below the dredge line is found by setting the net pressure to zero:

$$K_a \, \gamma \, (H_1 + d) = K_p \, \gamma \, d$$

$$\boxed{d = \frac{K_a \, H_1}{K_p - K_a}}$$

This depth is used as the location of the lower "support" in Blum's equivalent simply-supported beam model.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**
**A 7.0 m high anchored sheet pile wall retains dry sand with γ = 17 kN/m³ and φ = 32°. The water table is well below the dredge line. The anchor is located 1.0 m below the top. Take H_1 = 6.0 m and δ = 2φ/3.**

**(a)** Determine the theoretical depth of embedment using the **Free Earth Support Method**.
**(b)** Determine the depth of embedment using **Blum's Equivalent Beam Method** and compare.
**(c)** Compute the anchor force and maximum bending moment per metre run of the wall.

#### **Solution (a) — Free Earth Support Method**

**Step 1:** Compute K_a and K_p.

$$K_a = \tan^2(45^\circ - 16^\circ) = \tan^2(29^\circ) = (0.5543)^2 = 0.3073$$

$$K_p = \tan^2(45^\circ + 16^\circ) = \tan^2(61^\circ) = (1.8040)^2 = 3.2546$$

**Step 2:** Governing cubic in D (from ΣM about anchor = 0):

$$K_a (H_1 + D)^3 = 3 K_p D^2 H_1 + 2 K_p D^3$$

Substituting K_a = 0.3073, K_p = 3.2546, H_1 = 6.0:

$$0.3073 (6.0 + D)^3 = 3 \times 3.2546 \times D^2 \times 6.0 + 2 \times 3.2546 \times D^3$$

$$0.3073 (6.0 + D)^3 = 58.583 D^2 + 6.5092 D^3$$

**Step 3:** Try D = 1.5 m:

LHS: 0.3073 × (7.5)³ = 0.3073 × 421.875 = **129.65**
RHS: 58.583 × 2.25 + 6.5092 × 3.375 = 131.81 + 21.97 = **153.78**

LHS < RHS, so D must be smaller. Try D = 1.3 m:

LHS: 0.3073 × (7.3)³ = 0.3073 × 389.017 = **119.55**
RHS: 58.583 × 1.69 + 6.5092 × 2.197 = 99.01 + 14.30 = **113.31**

LHS > RHS, so D is between 1.3 and 1.5. Try D = 1.35 m:

LHS: 0.3073 × (7.35)³ = 0.3073 × 397.13 = **122.04**
RHS: 58.583 × 1.8225 + 6.5092 × 2.460 = 106.77 + 16.01 = **122.78**

Very close! LHS ≈ RHS. So **D ≈ 1.36 m** by Free Earth Support Method.

**Add 20% safety:** D_design = 1.2 × 1.36 = **1.63 m ≈ 1.65 m**

#### **Solution (b) — Blum's Equivalent Beam Method**

**Step 1:** Depth of point of contraflexure:

$$d = \frac{K_a \, H_1}{K_p - K_a} = \frac{0.3073 \times 6.0}{3.2546 - 0.3073} = \frac{1.8438}{2.9473} = 0.6256 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 2:** Actual embedment (no water):

$$D = 1.2 \, d = 1.2 \times 0.6256 = 0.751 \, \text{m} \approx 0.75 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3:** Comparison: Free Earth Method gave D ≈ 1.65 m, Blum's method gives D ≈ 0.75 m. **Blum's method is more economical** because it utilizes the *passive resistance below the point of contraflexure* by allowing the toe to develop fixity.

#### **Solution (c) — Anchor Force and Maximum Bending Moment (using Blum's method)**

**Net pressure peak at dredge line:**

$$p_{DL} = K_a \, \gamma \, H_1 = 0.3073 \times 17 \times 6.0 = 31.34 \, \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Net driving force (F_net) above point of contraflexure:**

$$F_{net} = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1 (H_1 + d) = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.3073 \times 17 \times 6.0 \times (6.0 + 0.6256)$$

$$F_{net} = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.3073 \times 17 \times 6.0 \times 6.6256 = 103.78 \, \text{kN/m}$$

**Moment about anchor (M_net):**

$$M_{net} = \frac{1}{3} K_a \gamma H_1^3 + \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H_1^2 d + \frac{1}{6} K_a \gamma H_1 d^2$$

- Term 1: ⅓ × 0.3073 × 17 × 216 = 376.31 kN·m/m
- Term 2: ½ × 0.3073 × 17 × 36 × 0.6256 = 58.84 kN·m/m
- Term 3: ⅙ × 0.3073 × 17 × 6.0 × 0.3914 = 2.05 kN·m/m

$$M_{net} = 376.31 + 58.84 + 2.05 = 437.20 \, \text{kN·m/m}$$

**Reaction R at lower support:**

$$R = \frac{M_{net}}{H_1 + d} = \frac{437.20}{6.6256} = 65.99 \, \text{kN/m}$$

**Anchor force T:**

$$T = F_{net} - R = 103.78 - 65.99 = 37.79 \, \text{kN/m}$$

**Location of maximum bending moment (z_max where shear = 0):**

$$z_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{2T}{K_a \gamma}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 37.79}{0.3073 \times 17}} = \sqrt{\frac{75.58}{5.224}} = \sqrt{14.47} = 3.80 \, \text{m}$$

Since z_max = 3.80 m < H_1 = 6.0 m, it lies in Region 1 (above dredge line).

**Maximum bending moment:**

$$M_{max} = \frac{T \cdot z_{max}}{3} = \frac{37.79 \times 3.80}{3} = 47.87 \, \text{kN·m/m}$$

**Pile section modulus** (assuming σ_allow = 175 MPa for steel):

$$S_{req} = \frac{M_{max}}{\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{47.87 \times 10^6}{175} = 273,500 \, \text{mm}^3/\text{m} = 273.5 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{m}$$

**Selected pile:** AZ-type with S = 380 cm³/m or higher.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Alternative Choice]**
**An anchored bulkhead in dry sand retains 8.0 m of soil. The dredge line is 1.5 m below the natural ground on the front. Properties: γ = 18 kN/m³, φ = 30°. The anchor is at 1.0 m below the top of the wall. The water table is far below.**

**(a)** Using the **Free Earth Support Method**, calculate the depth of embedment.
**(b)** Using **Blum's Method**, calculate the depth of embedment, anchor force, and maximum bending moment.

#### **Solution (a) — Free Earth Support Method**

**Given:** H_1 = 6.5 m (since anchor at 1.0 m below top, and 1.5 m of natural ground on front, but actually the question states "dredge line is 1.5 m below natural ground on front" - meaning the pile is driven 1.5 m into the front soil above the dredge line. Let's interpret H_1 = 8.0 - 1.5 = 6.5 m as the height of pile above the dredge line.)

**K_a, K_p:**

$$K_a = \tan^2(30^\circ) = 0.3333$$

$$K_p = \tan^2(60^\circ) = 3.0000$$

**Governing cubic:** K_a (H_1 + D)³ = 3 K_p D² H_1 + 2 K_p D³

$$0.3333 (6.5 + D)^3 = 9.0 D^2 (6.5) + 6.0 D^3$$

$$0.3333 (6.5 + D)^3 = 58.5 D^2 + 6.0 D^3$$

**Trial D = 1.8 m:**

LHS: 0.3333 × (8.3)³ = 0.3333 × 571.787 = 190.59
RHS: 58.5 × 3.24 + 6.0 × 5.832 = 189.54 + 34.99 = 224.53

LHS < RHS, try smaller. **Trial D = 1.6 m:**

LHS: 0.3333 × (8.1)³ = 0.3333 × 531.441 = 177.13
RHS: 58.5 × 2.56 + 6.0 × 4.096 = 149.76 + 24.58 = 174.34

LHS > RHS, so D between 1.6 and 1.8. **Trial D = 1.65 m:**

LHS: 0.3333 × (8.15)³ = 0.3333 × 541.343 = 180.43
RHS: 58.5 × 2.7225 + 6.0 × 4.492 = 159.27 + 26.95 = 186.22

LHS < RHS, between 1.6 and 1.65. **Trial D = 1.62 m:**

LHS: 0.3333 × (8.12)³ = 0.3333 × 535.39 = 178.45
RHS: 58.5 × 2.6244 + 6.0 × 4.2515 = 153.53 + 25.51 = 179.04

Very close! **D ≈ 1.62 m** by Free Earth Support Method. With 20% safety: D = 1.95 m.

#### **Solution (b) — Blum's Method**

**Depth of point of contraflexure:**

$$d = \frac{0.3333 \times 6.5}{3.0 - 0.3333} = \frac{2.1667}{2.6667} = 0.8125 \, \text{m}$$

**Actual embedment (no water):**

$$D = 1.2 \times 0.8125 = 0.975 \, \text{m} \approx 0.98 \, \text{m}$$

**Net pressure peak at dredge line:**

$$p_{DL} = 0.3333 \times 18 \times 6.5 = 39.0 \, \text{kN/m}^2$$

**Net driving force above point of contraflexure:**

$$F_{net} = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.3333 \times 18 \times 6.5 \times (6.5 + 0.8125) = \frac{1}{2} \times 39.0 \times 7.3125 = 142.59 \, \text{kN/m}$$

**Moment about anchor:**

$$M_{net} = \frac{1}{3} \times 0.3333 \times 18 \times 6.5^3 + \frac{1}{2} \times 0.3333 \times 18 \times 6.5^2 \times 0.8125 + \frac{1}{6} \times 0.3333 \times 18 \times 6.5 \times 0.8125^2$$

- Term 1: ⅓ × 6.0 × 274.625 = 549.25 kN·m/m
- Term 2: ½ × 6.0 × 42.25 × 0.8125 = 102.99 kN·m/m
- Term 3: ⅙ × 6.0 × 6.5 × 0.660 = 4.29 kN·m/m

$$M_{net} = 656.53 \, \text{kN·m/m}$$

**Reaction R:**

$$R = \frac{656.53}{7.3125} = 89.78 \, \text{kN/m}$$

**Anchor force T:**

$$T = 142.59 - 89.78 = 52.81 \, \text{kN/m}$$

**Location of max bending moment:**

$$z_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 52.81}{0.3333 \times 18}} = \sqrt{\frac{105.62}{6.0}} = \sqrt{17.60} = 4.20 \, \text{m}$$

Since 4.20 m < 6.5 m, in Region 1. **M_max:**

$$M_{max} = \frac{T \cdot z_{max}}{3} = \frac{52.81 \times 4.20}{3} = 73.93 \, \text{kN·m/m}$$

**Required section modulus** (σ_allow = 175 MPa):

$$S_{req} = \frac{73.93 \times 10^6}{175} = 422,460 \, \text{mm}^3/\text{m} = 422.5 \, \text{cm}^3/\text{m}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusion between K_a and K_p:** Remember K_p = 1/K_a for φ = 0, but in general K_p = (1+sinφ)/(1−sinφ) and K_a = (1−sinφ)/(1+sinφ). K_p is always **larger**.
> 2. **Forgetting the 20% safety factor on D:** Many KTU toppers lose 2–3 marks by reporting the theoretical D without the 1.2–1.5 multiplier.
> 3. **Wrong sign in net pressure:** Below dredge line, net = active − passive. The point where they cancel is the point of contraflexure.
> 4. **Mixing Blum's d with the actual D:** The point of contraflexure *d* is **NOT** the actual embedment. The actual D is 1.2d or 1.5d.
> 5. **Unit confusion:** Pile section modulus is in **cm³/m**, moment in **kN·m/m**, stress in **MPa** (N/mm²). Convert carefully: 1 kN·m = 10⁶ N·mm.
> 6. **Anchor force units:** T comes out in **kN/m** (per metre run of wall). For each tie-rod, multiply by pile spacing s (m).
> 7. **Free Earth vs Fixed Earth:** KTU often asks to compare both. The fixed earth method gives **smaller D** but the wall carries **larger bending moment**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision Bullets — Print and Pin on Your Wall!**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ Anchored sheet pile walls have an **anchor near the top** + **passive resistance at the bottom**.
- ✅ Two classical methods: **Free Earth Support** (toe free) and **Fixed Earth Support / Blum's** (toe fixed).
- ✅ The **point of contraflexure** is where the net lateral pressure = 0.
- ✅ Point of contraflexure depth: **d = K_a H_1 / (K_p − K_a)** — independent of γ.
- ✅ Blum's actual embedment: **D = 1.2 d** (no water) or **D = 1.5 d** (with water).
- ✅ Free Earth embedment requires solving a **cubic in D** by trial and error.
- ✅ Anchor force: **T = F_net − R** (Blum's) or **T = P_a − P_p** (Free Earth).
- ✅ Max bending moment occurs where **shear = 0** in the wall, at depth **z_max = √(2T/(K_a γ))** for Region 1.

### Numerical Mastery
- ✅ Always compute K_a and K_p from φ first using tan²(45° ± φ/2).
- ✅ Active pressure increases *linearly* with depth on the back.
- ✅ Passive pressure increases *linearly* with depth on the front (below dredge line only).
- ✅ Net pressure decreases *linearly* below dredge line because K_p > K_a.
- ✅ The pressure at the dredge line is **K_a γ H_1** (this is the peak of the net pressure diagram).
- ✅ Section modulus S = M_max / σ_allow must be satisfied by the chosen pile.

### Common KTU Traps
- ✅ Don't forget to **add 20% safety** to embedment in the Free Earth Method.
- ✅ Surcharge q adds a **rectangular pressure K_a q** on the back — include in moment equations.
- ✅ Water table requires **submerged unit weight** plus separate water pressure calculations.
- ✅ Wall friction angle δ ≠ φ; use **δ = 2φ/3** for steel piles in sand.
- ✅ Tie-rod design load = T × pile spacing s (in metres).
- ✅ Deadman anchor location: in the *stable zone* beyond the active wedge.

### Engineering Judgement
- ✅ For temporary works (cofferdams), use Free Earth Support Method (conservative but simpler).
- ✅ For permanent waterfront structures, prefer Blum's Method (better deflection control).
- ✅ Always provide a **factor of safety of 1.5–2.0** on the passive resistance for long-term creep.
- ✅ Anchor depth: usually **0.3–1.0 m** below the top to avoid construction interference.
- ✅ Corrosion protection (galvanizing, cathodic) is **mandatory** for permanent tie-rods.
- ✅ The deflection at the top of an anchored wall is typically **0.1% to 0.3% of H** for proper design.

### KTU 2024 Scheme Specific Reminders
- ✅ Use **IS 9527 (Code of Practice for Design and Construction of Sheet Pile Walls)** wherever applicable.
- ✅ For seismic zones (Kerala Zone III), multiply K_a and K_p by seismic coefficients as per IS 1893.
- ✅ The course outcome mapping: This topic primarily maps to **CO3: Design Flexible Earth-Retaining Structures**.
- ✅ Bloom's level: Most questions are at **L4 (Analyze)** and **L5 (Evaluate)** levels.
- ✅ Typical KTU 2024 marks distribution for Module 3: ~20% of total marks, with at least one 14-mark question per exam cycle.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Free Earth Support and Fixed Earth Support Analysis (Concept Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Free Earth Support and Fixed Earth Support Analysis (Concept Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Free Earth Support Method (FESM)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
The Free Earth Support Method is a classical limit equilibrium approach used to design **anchored sheet pile walls** (also called anchored bulkheads). In this method, the sheet pile wall is assumed to be *rigid*, *free to rotate* about its **toe (bottom)**, and the **anchor rod** is considered as the only point of lateral support above the dredge line. The soil mass on the retained (back) side is in the **active state ($K_a$)** and the soil on the front (dredge) side below the dredge line develops **full passive resistance ($K_p$)**. There is **no passive resistance** acting on the back of the wall below the point of rotation in this idealized assumption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** "In the Free Earth Support Method, the wall is assumed to be rigid and the active and passive earth pressures are fully developed. The wall is free to rotate at the bottom, and only the anchor provides upper support. The minimum depth of embedment is found by taking moments of net pressures about the anchor point."

## 1.2 Fixed Earth Support Method (FESM-fixed)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
The Fixed Earth Support Method assumes the sheet pile wall is **deeply embedded** in the soil such that its **toe is fixed** (i.e., the wall does not rotate at the bottom). The wall is treated as a **vertical cantilever beam fixed at the base**, with an **anchor** at the top. Below the point of contraflexure (zero shear), the lateral pressures reverse direction — the back side develops passive pressure and the front side develops active pressure, providing "fixity" at the bottom.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** "In the Fixed Earth Support Method, the wall is considered to be deeply embedded and fixed against rotation at the bottom. The depth of embedment is taken as 1.2 to 1.5 times the theoretical depth calculated to provide a factor of safety."

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Fence Post Example:**
>
> - **Free Earth Support** is like driving a thin wooden post **just deep enough** into the ground and then tying its top with a rope. The post can **wobble at the bottom** if you push it hard — the rope (anchor) holds the top, and the soil at the bottom simply rotates with the post. The *full* passive pressure of the soil in front is mobilized.
>
> - **Fixed Earth Support** is like driving a long, stiff post **very deep** into stiff clay or dense sand and tying its top. Because the post is so deep, the surrounding soil *grips* the lower portion and **prevents the bottom from rotating at all** — as if the toe were cast in concrete. Both active and passive pressures act on the lower portion in reversed directions, creating a "fixed" condition.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Physical Parameters

- **Active Earth Pressure Coefficient:** $K_a = \dfrac{1 - \sin\phi}{1 + \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$
- **Passive Earth Pressure Coefficient:** $K_p = \dfrac{1 + \sin\phi}{1 - \sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$
- **Unit Weight of Soil ($\gamma$):** typically **16–20 kN/m³** for sands, **17–21 kN/m³** for clays.
- **Anchor Location:** Usually placed at **0.3 H to 0.5 H** below the top (where $H$ is the retained height).
- **Embedment Depth Safety Factor (Fixed Method):** $1.2$ to $1.5$ times the theoretical depth.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Free Earth Support Method

The method proceeds through these logical steps:

1. **Identify the Retained Height ($H$) and the Embedment Depth ($D$).** The total wall height is $H + D$, where $H$ is above the dredge line and $D$ is below.
2. **Compute Net Active Pressure on the Back Side:**
   - At top of wall: $p_a = 0$ (assuming no surcharge).
   - At dredge line: $p_{a1} = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot H$.
   - At bottom of wall: $p_{a2} = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot (H + D)$.
3. **Compute Net Passive Pressure on the Front Side (below dredge line):**
   - At dredge line: $p_{p1} = 0$.
   - At bottom of wall: $p_{p2} = K_p \cdot \gamma \cdot D$.
4. **Anchor Force ($T$):** Determined by ensuring $\Sigma H = 0$ (sum of horizontal forces = 0).
5. **Embedment Depth ($D$):** Determined by taking **moments about the anchor point** and setting $\Sigma M_{anchor} = 0$.
6. **Factor of Safety:** Apply a factor of safety (typically **1.2 to 1.5**) to the calculated minimum $D$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this work?**
> Because the wall is free to rotate at its base, the **simplest** pressure distribution (active on back, passive on front) is fully developed. The anchor must resist the net horizontal force trying to push the wall out, and the embedment must be deep enough to prevent kicking-out at the toe.

## 2.2 Theoretical Framework — Fixed Earth Support Method

The method proceeds through these logical steps:

1. **Compute Active Pressure on the Back (down to a depth $H + y$):**
   - $p_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot (H + y)$ where $y$ is depth below dredge line.
2. **Compute Passive Pressure on the Front (down to depth $y$):**
   - $p_p = K_p \cdot \gamma \cdot y$.
3. **Locate the Point of Zero Shear (Contraflexure):** Solve for depth $y_0$ below the dredge line where the net pressure diagram equals zero.
4. **Apply the Equivalent Beam Method (Blum's Method):**
   - Treat the wall as two beams: (a) **upper portion** simply supported between the anchor and the point of zero net pressure, and (b) **lower portion** as a cantilever fixed at the toe.
5. **Compute Theoretical Embedment Depth $D_0$** from the cantilever analysis.
6. **Apply Factor of Safety:** The actual embedment depth is $D = 1.2\,D_0$ to $1.5\,D_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this work?**
> When the wall is deeply embedded, the soil "grips" the lower portion and prevents toe rotation. The reversed pressure below the point of contraflexure provides the **fixity moment** that holds the toe in place — analogous to a beam clamped at both ends.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Active pressure coefficient | $K_a = \tan^2(45° - \phi/2)$ | — | Rankine's coefficient for granular soil |
| 2 | Passive pressure coefficient | $K_p = \tan^2(45° + \phi/2)$ | — | Rankine's coefficient for granular soil |
| 3 | Active pressure at depth $z$ | $p_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z$ | kN/m² | $\gamma$ = unit weight |
| 4 | Passive pressure at depth $z$ | $p_p = K_p \cdot \gamma \cdot z$ | kN/m² | Below dredge line |
| 5 | Net pressure on back at bottom | $p_{a,\text{net}} = K_a \gamma (H+D)$ | kN/m² | Free Earth Method |
| 6 | Net passive on front at bottom | $p_{p,\text{net}} = K_p \gamma D$ | kN/m² | Free Earth Method |
| 7 | Anchor force (FES) | $T = \Sigma F_H$ (sum of net forces) | kN/m | Horizontal equilibrium |
| 8 | Embedment depth (FES) | $\Sigma M_{\text{anchor}} = 0$ | m | Solved by moment equation |
| 9 | Theoretical depth (Fixed) | $D_0$ from cantilever + simple beam | m | Blum's method |
| 10 | Design depth (Fixed) | $D = (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5)\,D_0$ | m | Safety factor included |
| 11 | Depth of zero net pressure | $y_0 = \dfrac{K_a H}{K_p - K_a}$ | m | Below dredge line |
| 12 | Wall friction reduction factor | $\rho_p$ (for $K_p$ when $\delta \neq 0$) | — | For inclined wall surfaces |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These two methods are **fundamental** to designing:
- **Waterfront bulkheads** (port and harbor retaining walls)
- **Temporary cofferdams** for bridge pier construction
- **Deep excavation support systems** in urban areas
- **Land reclamation projects** along coastlines
- **River training works** and canal linings

> [!TIP]
> **Field Tip:** In **sandy (granular) soils**, the **Free Earth Support Method** is **preferred** because sand does not develop "fixed" conditions easily. In **stiff clays** or where the wall is driven **very deep**, the **Fixed Earth Support Method** becomes **more economical** (smaller anchor force, thinner wall section).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Free Earth Support Method (Minimum Embedment)

### 3.1.1 Pressure Distribution Setup

Consider a sheet pile wall retaining sand of unit weight $\gamma$ and friction angle $\phi$. The wall has:
- Retained height = $H$ (above dredge line)
- Embedment depth = $D$ (below dredge line)
- Anchor at depth $h_a$ from the top

The **net active pressure** on the back at depth $z$ from the top is:
$$p_{a,\text{net}}(z) = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z$$

The **net passive pressure** on the front at depth $z$ below the dredge line is:
$$p_{p,\text{net}}(z) = K_p \cdot \gamma \cdot z$$

### 3.1.2 Force Calculation

The **active force** acting on the back (per meter run of wall) is the area of the trapezoidal pressure diagram from $z = 0$ to $z = H + D$:
$$P_a = \frac{1}{2} \cdot K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot (H + D)^2$$

The **passive force** acting on the front below the dredge line is the triangular area from $z = 0$ to $z = D$ (depth below dredge line):
$$P_p = \frac{1}{2} \cdot K_p \cdot \gamma \cdot D^2$$

### 3.1.3 Equilibrium Equations

**Horizontal Equilibrium** ($\Sigma F_H = 0$):
$$T + P_p = P_a$$

Solving for the **anchor force $T$**:
$$T = P_a - P_p$$

**Moment Equilibrium about the Anchor** ($\Sigma M_{\text{anchor}} = 0$):

Taking moments of all forces about the anchor point at depth $h_a$ from the top:

The active force $P_a$ acts at a centroid distance from the top of $\dfrac{H + D}{3}$ below the top, so its moment arm from the anchor is $\dfrac{H + D}{3} - h_a$.

The passive force $P_p$ acts at $\dfrac{2D}{3}$ below the dredge line, i.e., at a distance of $H + \dfrac{2D}{3}$ from the top, so its moment arm from the anchor is $H + \dfrac{2D}{3} - h_a$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_a \cdot \left(\frac{H + D}{3} - h_a\right) &= P_p \cdot \left(H + \frac{2D}{3} - h_a\right) \\
\frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma (H+D)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{H+D}{3} - h_a\right) &= \frac{1}{2} K_p \gamma D^2 \cdot \left(H + \frac{2D}{3} - h_a\right)
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.4 Solving for $D$

Dividing both sides by $\dfrac{1}{2}\gamma$:

$$K_a (H+D)^2 \left(\frac{H+D}{3} - h_a\right) = K_p D^2 \left(H + \frac{2D}{3} - h_a\right)$$

This is a **cubic equation in $D$** which is solved iteratively or numerically. The result gives the **minimum theoretical embedment depth**.

For design, the **actual embedment depth** is increased by a factor of safety:

$$D_{\text{design}} = 1.2 \text{ to } 1.5 \times D_{\text{min}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Fixed Earth Support Method (Blum's Equivalent Beam)

### 3.2.1 Depth of Zero Net Pressure

The **net pressure** at depth $y$ below the dredge line on the back side is:
$$p_{\text{net}}(y) = K_a \gamma (H + y) - K_p \gamma y$$

Setting $p_{\text{net}}(y_0) = 0$ for the **point of zero net pressure** (also called the point of contraflexure):
$$\begin{aligned}
K_a \gamma (H + y_0) &= K_p \gamma y_0 \\
K_a H + K_a y_0 &= K_p y_0 \\
K_a H &= (K_p - K_a) y_0 \\
y_0 &= \frac{K_a H}{K_p - K_a}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.2 Pressure at $y = y_0$ (on back, equivalent to $R$ in Blum's method)

At the point of zero net pressure, the active pressure on the back is:
$$p_{a}(y_0) = K_a \gamma (H + y_0)$$

Substituting $y_0$:
$$p_{a}(y_0) = K_a \gamma \left(H + \frac{K_a H}{K_p - K_a}\right) = \frac{K_a K_p \gamma H}{K_p - K_a}$$

### 3.2.3 Equivalent Beam — Upper Portion

The upper portion (from top of wall to depth $H + y_0$) is treated as a **simply supported beam**:
- Left support = anchor (at depth $h_a$)
- Right support = concentrated force $R$ at the point of zero net pressure

By moment equilibrium of the upper portion about the anchor:
$$R = \frac{\text{Moment of active pressure above } y_0 \text{ about anchor}}{\text{Distance from anchor to } y_0}$$

The active pressure above the dredge line gives:
$$P_{a,\text{above}} = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 \quad \text{acting at } \frac{H}{3} \text{ above dredge line}$$

Adding the trapezoidal portion from dredge line to $y_0$:
$$P_{a,\text{trap}} = \frac{1}{2} \gamma (K_a H + p_a(y_0)) \cdot y_0$$

### 3.2.4 Equivalent Beam — Lower Portion (Cantilever)

The lower portion (from depth $H + y_0$ to $H + D$) is treated as a **cantilever** fixed at the toe. The concentrated force $R$ at the top of this cantilever produces a moment that is resisted by the reversed passive-active pressure distribution.

The **theoretical depth $D_0$** (depth from $y_0$ to the theoretical point of fixity) satisfies:
$$R \cdot D_0 = \text{Moment of reversed pressures on the lower portion}$$

The **total theoretical embedment depth** below the dredge line is:
$$D_{\text{theory}} = y_0 + D_0$$

### 3.2.5 Design Embedment Depth

Apply factor of safety:
$$D_{\text{design}} = (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5) \times D_{\text{theory}}$$

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix — Free vs. Fixed Earth Support

| Parameter | Free Earth Support | Fixed Earth Support |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Toe condition** | Free to rotate | Fixed against rotation |
| **Embedment depth** | Smaller | Larger (1.2–1.5 × theory) |
| **Anchor force** | Larger | Smaller |
| **Bending moment** | Larger | Smaller |
| **Pressure below $y_0$** | Passive on front only | Reversed (passive on back, active on front) |
| **Preferred soil type** | Loose to medium sand, soft clay | Stiff clay, dense sand |
| **Method of solution** | Moment about anchor = 0 | Blum's equivalent beam |
| **Failure mode** | Toe kick-out | Excessive bending |
| **Code recommendation** | IS 9527 (Part 3) — both methods acceptable | IS 9527 (Part 3) — both methods acceptable |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Free Earth Support

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of forces, pressures, and equilibrium checks for the Free Earth Support Method:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Wall Geometry] --> B[Input: H, D, gamma, phi, h_a]
    B --> C[Compute K_a and K_p]
    C --> D[Active Pressure on Back]
    D --> D1["p_a = K_a × gamma × z"]
    C --> E[Passive Pressure on Front]
    E --> E1["p_p = K_p × gamma × z"]
    D1 --> F[Net Pressure Diagram]
    E1 --> F
    F --> G[Horizontal Equilibrium]
    G --> G1["T + P_p = P_a"]
    G1 --> G2[Solve for Anchor Force T]
    F --> H[Moment Equilibrium about Anchor]
    H --> H1["Sum M_anchor = 0"]
    H1 --> I[Cubic Equation in D]
    I --> J[Iterative Solver]
    J --> K[Minimum Embedment D_min]
    K --> L[Apply Factor of Safety 1.2 to 1.5]
    L --> M[Design Embedment D_design]
    M --> N[End: Final Section Design]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fixed Earth Support (Blum's Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Wall Geometry] --> B[Input: H, gamma, phi, h_a]
    B --> C[Compute K_a and K_p]
    C --> D[Net Pressure Profile]
    D --> E[Find Point of Zero Net Pressure y0]
    E --> E1["y0 = K_a H / (K_p - K_a)"]
    E1 --> F[Compute Active Pressure at y0]
    F --> F1["p_a(y0) = K_a K_p gamma H / (K_p - K_a)"]
    F1 --> G[Equivalent Beam Upper Portion]
    G --> G1[Anchor is Left Support]
    G1 --> G2[R is Right Support at y0]
    G2 --> H[Solve for R Force]
    H --> I[Equivalent Beam Lower Portion]
    I --> I1[Cantilever Fixed at Toe]
    I1 --> J[Reversed Pressure: Passive on Back, Active on Front]
    J --> K[Theoretical Embedment D_theory]
    K --> L[Apply Factor of Safety 1.2 to 1.5]
    L --> M[Design Embedment D_design]
    M --> N[End: Final Section Design]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Pressure Distribution Schematic (Textual Schematic)

The following ASCII representation shows the **net pressure distribution** for both methods (read carefully — these are *simplified* pressure diagrams):

### Free Earth Support — Net Pressure Diagram

```
              |  Anchor (T)
              v
   Back  =======>====ANCHOR====       <-- Top of Wall
              |   |        |
              |   |   p_a  |  (active on back, increases linearly)
              |   |   /    |
              |   |  /     |
   Dredge ----+---+/-------+----  Dredge Line
              |   |        |
              |   | <===== |  (passive on front, increases linearly)
              |   |   p_p  |
              |   |  \     |
              |   |   \    |
   Toe  =======+===\========    <-- Toe (free to rotate)
                  D
```

### Fixed Earth Support — Net Pressure Diagram

```
              |  Anchor (T)
              v
   Back  =======>====ANCHOR====       <-- Top of Wall
              |   |        |
              |   |   p_a  |
              |   |   /    |
              |   |  /     |
   Dredge ----+---+/-------+----  Dredge Line
              |   |        |
              |   | <===== |       (passive on front, but
              |   |        |        REVERSES at y0)
              | y0|====>   |  <-- Point of contraflexure (zero net)
              |   |        |
              |   | =====> |       (passive on back, active on front)
              |   |        |
   Toe  =======+===+========    <-- Toe (fixed)
                       D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Guide:**
> - Arrows pointing right ($\rightarrow$) represent pressure pushing the wall to the right (active or reversed passive on back).
> - Arrows pointing left ($\leftarrow$) represent pressure pushing the wall to the left (passive on front, or reversed active below $y_0$).
> - In the **Free Earth Support** diagram, the pressure below the dredge line is **passive on the front only**.
> - In the **Fixed Earth Support** diagram, the pressure **reverses** below the point of contraflexure $y_0$.

## 4.4 Wall Embedment Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Free Earth Support] --> A1[Short Embedment]
    A1 --> A2[Toe is Free to Rotate]
    A1 --> A3[Larger Anchor Force]
    A1 --> A4[Smaller Bending Moment Compensated by Section]
    
    B[Fixed Earth Support] --> B1[Deep Embedment]
    B1 --> B2[Toe is Fixed]
    B1 --> B3[Smaller Anchor Force]
    B1 --> B4[Smaller Bending Moment]
    B1 --> B5[Embedment x 1.2 to 1.5 for Safety]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Free Earth Support and Fixed Earth Support methods of analyzing anchored sheet pile walls.**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Free Earth Support | Fixed Earth Support |
|-------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Toe condition** | Free to rotate | Fixed |
| **Embedment depth** | Smaller | Larger (1.2–1.5 × theoretical) |
| **Anchor force** | Higher | Lower |
| **Bending moment** | Higher | Lower |
| **Soil preference** | Loose sand, soft clay | Stiff clay, dense sand |
| **Method** | Moment about anchor = 0 | Blum's equivalent beam |
| **Failure mode** | Toe kick-out | Excessive bending |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What are the assumptions made in the Free Earth Support Method?**

**Model Answer:**

1. The wall is **rigid** and **free to rotate** about its toe.
2. **Active earth pressure** acts on the back side of the wall.
3. **Passive earth pressure** acts on the front side below the dredge line.
4. The **anchor rod** is the only support above the dredge line.
5. The soil is **cohesionless (granular)** for Rankine's analysis.
6. The wall is **vertical** with a **smooth** back (no wall friction in basic analysis).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Free Earth Support Method** for the design of an anchored sheet pile wall in cohesionless soil. Derive the expression for minimum depth of embedment. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **6 m** high anchored sheet pile wall retains **sand** with $\phi = 30°$ and $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. The anchor is located at **1.0 m** below the top. The wall is to be designed using the **Free Earth Support Method**. Determine the **minimum depth of embedment** and the **anchor force** per meter run of the wall. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Question A(a):

The Free Earth Support Method is a classical limit equilibrium approach for designing anchored sheet pile walls in cohesionless (granular) soils.

**Step 1 — Pressure Coefficients:**
$$K_a = \tan^2(45° - \phi/2) \quad ; \quad K_p = \tan^2(45° + \phi/2)$$

**Step 2 — Pressure Distribution:**
- Active pressure on the back increases linearly from **0 at the top** to $K_a \gamma (H+D)$ at the **toe**.
- Passive pressure on the front acts only **below the dredge line**, increasing from **0 at the dredge line** to $K_p \gamma D$ at the **toe**.

**Step 3 — Active and Passive Forces:**
$$P_a = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma (H + D)^2 \quad ; \quad P_p = \frac{1}{2} K_p \gamma D^2$$

**Step 4 — Anchor Force (Horizontal Equilibrium):**
$$T = P_a - P_p = \frac{1}{2} \gamma \left[ K_a (H+D)^2 - K_p D^2 \right]$$

**Step 5 — Minimum Embedment (Moment about Anchor = 0):**
$$\begin{aligned}
P_a \cdot \left(\frac{H+D}{3} - h_a\right) &= P_p \cdot \left(H + \frac{2D}{3} - h_a\right)
\end{aligned}$$

This gives a **cubic equation in $D$**, which is solved iteratively.

---

#### Solution to Question A(b):

**Given:** $H = 6$ m, $\phi = 30°$, $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³, $h_a = 1.0$ m.

**Step 1 — Compute Coefficients:**
$$K_a = \tan^2(45° - 15°) = \tan^2(30°) = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$
$$K_p = \tan^2(45° + 15°) = \tan^2(60°) = 3.0$$

**Step 2 — Try $D = 2.5$ m and verify moment equation:**

Active force:
$$P_a = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.333 \times 18 \times (6 + 2.5)^2 = 0.5 \times 0.333 \times 18 \times 72.25 = 216.6 \text{ kN/m}$$

Passive force:
$$P_p = \frac{1}{2} \times 3.0 \times 18 \times (2.5)^2 = 0.5 \times 3.0 \times 18 \times 6.25 = 168.75 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 3 — Check Moment about Anchor (at 1.0 m from top):**

Moment arm of $P_a$ from anchor: $\dfrac{8.5}{3} - 1.0 = 2.833 - 1.0 = 1.833$ m
Moment arm of $P_p$ from anchor: $6.0 + \dfrac{2 \times 2.5}{3} - 1.0 = 6.0 + 1.667 - 1.0 = 6.667$ m

$$\begin{aligned}
M_a &= 216.6 \times 1.833 = 397.0 \text{ kN·m/m} \\
M_p &= 168.75 \times 6.667 = 1125.0 \text{ kN·m/m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Iterate to Balance Moments:**
Repeat with $D = 2.0$ m: $P_a = 0.5 \times 0.333 \times 18 \times 64 = 191.8$ kN/m, $P_p = 0.5 \times 3.0 \times 18 \times 4 = 108$ kN/m.

$$\begin{aligned}
M_a &= 191.8 \times \left(\frac{8}{3} - 1\right) = 191.8 \times 1.667 = 319.7 \text{ kN·m/m} \\
M_p &= 108 \times \left(6 + \frac{4}{3} - 1\right) = 108 \times 6.333 = 683.9 \text{ kN·m/m}
\end{aligned}$$

By interpolation, **$D_{\min} \approx 2.3$ m**.

**Step 5 — Anchor Force:**
$$T = P_a - P_p = 191.8 - 108 = 83.8 \text{ kN/m (at } D = 2.0 \text{)}$$

For $D = 2.3$ m: $T \approx 95$ kN/m.

**Step 6 — Design Depth (with FOS = 1.3):**
$$D_{\text{design}} = 1.3 \times 2.3 = 2.99 \approx 3.0 \text{ m}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the **Fixed Earth Support Method** (Blum's Equivalent Beam Method) for analyzing deeply embedded anchored sheet pile walls. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **7 m** high anchored sheet pile wall in **dense sand** with $\phi = 35°$ and $\gamma = 20$ kN/m³ is designed by the **Fixed Earth Support Method**. The anchor is at **1.5 m** from the top. Find the **theoretical depth of embedment** below the dredge line. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Question B(a):

**Blum's Equivalent Beam Method** treats the wall as two beams:

**Step 1 — Compute the depth of zero net pressure $y_0$:**
$$y_0 = \frac{K_a H}{K_p - K_a}$$

**Step 2 — Upper Beam (Simply Supported):**
- Anchor at the top (left support)
- A **concentrated force $R$** at the depth $y_0$ (right support, where net pressure = 0)
- Loaded by net active pressure on the back and net passive on the front (above $y_0$)

**Step 3 — Lower Beam (Cantilever Fixed at Toe):**
- The force $R$ acts at the top of this cantilever
- Loaded by **reversed** pressures: passive on back, active on front
- The **fixity** at the toe provides the moment to balance $R$

**Step 4 — Theoretical Embedment $D_0$ (from $y_0$ to toe):**
$$D_0 = \sqrt{\frac{6 R}{\gamma (K_p - K_a)}}$$

**Step 5 — Design Embedment:**
$$D_{\text{design}} = (1.2 \text{ to } 1.5) \times (y_0 + D_0)$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The wall is deeply embedded → toe is fixed.
2. The soil is cohesionless.
3. Rankine's earth pressure theory applies.
4. The wall is rigid and vertical.

---

#### Solution to Question B(b):

**Given:** $H = 7$ m, $\phi = 35°$, $\gamma = 20$ kN/m³.

**Step 1 — Compute Coefficients:**
$$K_a = \tan^2(45° - 17.5°) = \tan^2(27.5°) = 0.271$$
$$K_p = \tan^2(45° + 17.5°) = \tan^2(62.5°) = 3.69$$

**Step 2 — Depth of Zero Net Pressure $y_0$:**
$$y_0 = \frac{K_a H}{K_p - K_a} = \frac{0.271 \times 7}{3.69 - 0.271} = \frac{1.897}{3.419} = 0.555 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Active Pressure at $y_0$:**
$$p_a(y_0) = K_a \gamma (H + y_0) = 0.271 \times 20 \times (7 + 0.555) = 40.92 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent Beam Analysis (Upper Portion):**

Net active pressure resultant above the dredge line (acting on the wall above $y_0$):
$$P_{a1} = \frac{1}{2} K_a \gamma H^2 = 0.5 \times 0.271 \times 20 \times 49 = 132.79 \text{ kN/m}$$

Trapezoidal portion from dredge line to $y_0$:
$$P_{a2} = \frac{1}{2}(K_a \gamma H + p_a(y_0)) \cdot y_0 = \frac{1}{2}(37.94 + 40.92) \times 0.555 = 21.89 \text{ kN/m}$$

Centroid of $P_{a1}$ from top: $H/3 = 7/3 = 2.333$ m, so moment arm from anchor ($h_a = 1.5$ m) = $2.333 - 1.5 = 0.833$ m.

Centroid of $P_{a2}$ from dredge line: $\dfrac{y_0(2K_a H + p_a(y_0))}{3(K_a H + p_a(y_0))} = \dfrac{0.555(2 \times 37.94 + 40.92)}{3(37.94 + 40.92)} = \dfrac{0.555 \times 116.8}{236.58} = 0.274$ m below dredge line, so moment arm from anchor = $(7 + 0.274) - 1.5 = 5.774$ m.

**Step 5 — Solve for $R$:**
$$\begin{aligned}
R \cdot (H + y_0 - h_a) &= P_{a1} \cdot (H/3 - h_a) + P_{a2} \cdot (H + y_{c,2} - h_a) \\
R \cdot (7 + 0.555 - 1.5) &= 132.79 \times 0.833 + 21.89 \times 5.774 \\
R \cdot 6.055 &= 110.62 + 126.39 \\
R &= \frac{237.01}{6.055} = 39.14 \text{ kN/m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Cantilever Analysis (Lower Portion):**
$$D_0 = \sqrt{\frac{6 R}{\gamma (K_p - K_a)}} = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 39.14}{20 \times 3.419}} = \sqrt{\frac{234.84}{68.38}} = \sqrt{3.434} = 1.853 \text{ m}$$

**Step 7 — Total Theoretical Embedment:**
$$D_{\text{theory}} = y_0 + D_0 = 0.555 + 1.853 = 2.408 \text{ m}$$

**Step 8 — Design Embedment (FOS = 1.4):**
$$D_{\text{design}} = 1.4 \times 2.408 = 3.37 \text{ m} \approx 3.4 \text{ m}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Factor of Safety (FOS = 1.2 to 1.5):** Students often present the *theoretical* embedment depth as the *design* depth. Always state and apply the FOS explicitly — the examiner checks this!
> 2. **Sign Error in Moment Equation:** A common mistake is to take moments of $P_a$ and $P_p$ in the **same direction**. Remember: the active force tries to **topple the wall outward**, while the passive force tries to **hold it in place** — they oppose each other.
> 3. **Using the Wrong Centroid:** For a **trapezoidal** pressure diagram (not triangular), the centroid is **NOT** at the simple midpoint. Use the proper centroid formula for a trapezoid.
> 4. **Confusing $K_a$ and $K_p$:** $K_a$ is *always* $\leq 1$ and $K_p$ is *always* $\geq 1$. Swapping them gives nonsensical results.
> 5. **Ignoring Wall Friction in the Fixed Method:** For the Fixed Earth Support Method with $\delta \neq 0$, use the **modified $K_p$** (Caquot-Kerisel or Coulomb's method). Using Rankine's $K_p$ overestimates the passive resistance and is unsafe.
> 6. **Forgetting that the Pressure Reverses below $y_0$:** In the Fixed Earth Support Method, students often forget that below $y_0$, the **passive pressure** appears on the **back** side and the **active pressure** appears on the **front** side. This reversal is the *defining feature* of the method!

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Free Earth Support vs. Fixed Earth Support:**

- ✅ **Free Earth Support** assumes the **toe is free to rotate**; **Fixed Earth Support** assumes the **toe is fixed**.
- ✅ **Free Earth Support** is preferred in **granular (cohesionless) soils** like loose to medium sand.
- ✅ **Fixed Earth Support** is preferred in **stiff clays** or **dense sands** with deep embedment.
- ✅ In **Free Earth Support**, **only the front side** below the dredge line develops **passive pressure**; no reversed pressure on the back.
- ✅ In **Fixed Earth Support**, the **pressure reverses** below the depth $y_0$ (point of contraflexure).
- ✅ $y_0 = \dfrac{K_a H}{K_p - K_a}$ is the **depth of zero net pressure** below the dredge line in the Fixed Earth Support Method.
- ✅ **Anchor force** in Fixed Earth Support is **smaller** than in Free Earth Support (the soil helps the wall).
- ✅ **Bending moment** in Fixed Earth Support is **smaller** than in Free Earth Support.
- ✅ **Embedment depth** in Fixed Earth Support is **larger** (1.2 to 1.5 times theoretical) for safety.
- ✅ The **Free Earth Support Method** uses **moment equilibrium about the anchor** ($\Sigma M = 0$) to find $D$.
- ✅ The **Fixed Earth Support Method** uses **Blum's Equivalent Beam Method** (upper simple beam + lower cantilever).
- ✅ **Rankine's earth pressure theory** is the foundation for both methods (no wall friction in basic analysis).
- ✅ **Wall friction** (if $\delta \neq 0$) modifies $K_p$ to a lower value — always use **Coulomb's** or **Caquot-Kerisel's** adjusted $K_p$.
- ✅ **Factor of Safety** (1.2 to 1.5) is **mandatory** in Fixed Earth Support Method for design embedment.
- ✅ Real-world applications include **waterfront bulkheads**, **cofferdams**, and **deep excavation support**.
- ✅ **IS 9527 (Part 3)** is the relevant Indian Standard code for sheet pile wall design.
- ✅ Always state the **assumptions** clearly at the start of any numerical solution in the KTU exam.
- ✅ **Draw the pressure distribution diagram** — the examiner awards **2 to 3 marks** just for a clean, labeled diagram.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rowe's Moment Reduction Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Rowe's Moment Reduction Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Rowe's Moment Reduction Factor** is a dimensionless multiplier (denoted as $M_r$ or sometimes $\mu$) introduced by **P.W. Rowe (1952, 1956, 1957)** to account for the redistribution of lateral earth pressure in **flexible sheet pile walls**. The factor quantifies the ratio of the **actual bending moment** developed in a real, deformable anchored or cantilever sheet pile wall to the **theoretical maximum bending moment** predicted by classical (rigid-body) earth pressure theories such as the **Free Earth Support Method (FESM)** or the **Fixed Earth Support Method (Blum's Method)**.

The classical design equations are derived assuming the wall is **rigid**, with the **Rankine** or **Coulomb** pressure distribution fully mobilized. However, real sheet pile walls (steel, vinyl, timber, RC) deflect under load, which allows the soil to arch and the pressure to redistribute toward the supports (anchor and toe), thereby **reducing the field moment** below the theoretical value.

Mathematically, Rowe expressed this as:

$$M_{\text{design}} = M_r \cdot M_{\max,\text{theoretical}}$$

where $0 \leq M_r \leq 1$ is the **moment reduction factor**, a function of the wall's **flexibility number** $\rho$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Rowe's reduction is applicable *only* to anchored bulkhead walls and cantilever sheet pile walls in **cohesionless soils**. The reduction is **NOT recommended** for cohesive (clay) soils due to time-dependent creep and pore-pressure equalization effects that nullify the arching mechanism.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bamboo vs. Concrete Fence" Intuition

Imagine two identical garden fences meant to hold back a sand pile:

- **Concrete Fence (Rigid Wall):** If you push the sand against a thick, immovable concrete wall, the sand pushes back uniformly along the entire height. The wall experiences the *full* classical pressure distribution and the *full* theoretical bending moment.

- **Bamboo Fence (Flexible Wall):** Now imagine a thin, slightly elastic bamboo wall. When sand pushes against it, the middle of the wall bows outward slightly. This small deflection **changes the pressure pattern** — the sand near the middle "slips" and pressure concentrates near the **top anchor** and the **bottom toe (embedment)**, where the wall is held. The middle of the wall gets a *break* on the loading.

**Net effect:** The bamboo wall experiences a *lower* maximum bending moment than the concrete wall, even though both retain the same height of sand. The bamboo "**redistributes**" the load to its supports, much like a flexible diving board redirects force to its ends.

**Rowe's Moment Reduction Factor $M_r$** is the engineering quantification of this bamboo-fence effect. A stiffer wall has $M_r$ close to **1** (no reduction), while a highly flexible wall has $M_r$ as low as **0.30 to 0.50** (significant moment reduction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Callout — Flexibility Number ($\rho$):**
> The Flexibility Number is a **dimensionless geometric-stiffness parameter** that captures how "bendy" the wall is relative to the soil mass it retains. It is the master key to all of Rowe's design charts.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard reference values are used throughout Rowe's analysis:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steel Young's Modulus | $E$ | **200 GPa** | Standard for steel sheet piling |
| Wall Height | $H$ | **5–15 m** | Common retaining range |
| Section Modulus (per m) | $Z$ | **1000–3000 cm³/m** | From manufacturer's catalog |
| Moment of Inertia (per m) | $I$ | **30 000–200 000 cm⁴/m** | Drives the flexibility |
| Rowe Flexibility Number | $\rho$ | **0.5–50** | Dimensionless |
| Moment Reduction Factor | $M_r$ | **0.30–1.00** | From Rowe's charts |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical "Why" Behind Moment Reduction

Classical anchored sheet pile design (e.g., **Blum's Equivalent Beam Method** or the **Free Earth Support Method**) assumes:

1. The wall is **infinitely rigid** (no deflection).
2. The active Rankine wedge is **fully developed** along the entire wall.
3. Pressure varies **linearly** with depth: $p_a = K_a \cdot \gamma \cdot z$.

When a **flexible** wall is installed, the following physics intervenes:

- The wall bows between the **anchor (top)** and the **passive earth support (toe)**.
- The deflection, however small, prevents the **full active state** from developing in the soil mass behind the wall.
- **Arching effect** transfers load from the deforming middle zone to the stiffer anchor and toe zones.
- Result: a **non-linear, redistributive** pressure diagram with a lower mid-height peak.

Rowe captured this phenomenon through two key parameters:

1. **Flexibility Number $\rho$** — captures wall geometry and stiffness.
2. **Reduction Factor $M_r$** — captures the moment savings from arching.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Method

The Rowe reduction methodology follows a precise sequence of logic steps:

**Step 1 — Compute the Flexibility Number $\rho$:**
The flexibility number for an **anchored sheet pile wall** in a cohesionless soil is:

$$\rho = \frac{H^4}{E \cdot I}$$

where:
- $H$ = total height of the wall (m)
- $E$ = Young's modulus of the wall material (kN/m² or kPa)
- $I$ = moment of inertia of the wall section per unit length (m⁴/m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Unit Consistency Warning:** When $E$ is in kPa (= kN/m²) and $I$ is in m⁴/m, then $\rho$ comes out in m²/kN. The charts are plotted using a **normalized** version. Some textbooks include a reference stress $\sigma_s$ to non-dimensionalize $\rho$ as $\rho' = \rho \cdot \sigma_s$. Always verify the chart's axis label.

**Step 2 — Select the Correct Reduction Curve:**
Rowe prepared three families of reduction curves:
- **(a)** For **anchored walls with free earth support** in **loose to medium sand**.
- **(b)** For **anchored walls with fixed earth support** in **loose to medium sand**.
- **(c)** For **anchored walls** in **dense sand**.

**Step 3 — Read the Reduction Factor $M_r$:**
Enter the chart at the computed $\rho$, read off $M_r$ on the vertical axis.

**Step 4 — Compute the Design Moment:**
Apply the reduction to the classical (Blum/FESM) maximum moment:

$$M_{\text{design}} = M_r \cdot M_{\max,\text{theoretical}}$$

**Step 5 — Select Sheet Pile Section:**
The required section modulus is:

$$Z_{\text{required}} = \frac{M_{\text{design}}}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}$$

where $\sigma_{\text{allow}}$ is the allowable bending stress of steel (typically **0.6–0.65 $F_y$**).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho = \dfrac{H^4}{E \cdot I}$ | Rowe's Flexibility Number | $H$ in m, $E$ in kPa, $I$ in m⁴/m |
| 2 | $M_d = M_r \cdot M_{\max}$ | Reduced Design Moment | $0 \leq M_r \leq 1$ |
| 3 | $Z_{\text{req}} = \dfrac{M_d}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}$ | Required Section Modulus | $\sigma_{\text{allow}} \approx 0.6 F_y$ |
| 4 | $M_{\max,\text{FESM}} = \dfrac{\gamma \cdot H^3}{6\sqrt{K_a}}$ | Free Earth Support Moment | For cohesionless soil |
| 5 | $M_{\max,\text{Blum}} \approx 0.6 \cdot M_{\max,\text{FESM}}$ | Fixed Earth Support Moment | Approximate empirical |
| 6 | $K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ - \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | Rankine Active Coefficient | $\phi$ = soil friction angle |
| 7 | $K_p = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \dfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$ | Rankine Passive Coefficient | $\phi$ = soil friction angle |
| 8 | $D_{\min} = \dfrac{H \cdot K_a}{2(K_p - K_a)}$ | Min. Embedment (FESM) | Simplified classical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick-Reference:** For a typical anchored wall with $H = 10$ m, $\phi = 30^\circ$, steel $E = 2 \times 10^8$ kPa, and $I = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m⁴/m, you get $\rho \approx 0.67$ and $M_r \approx 0.85$. **Always compute $\rho$ first — it is the gateway to Rowe's charts.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Rowe's Moment Reduction is **not** merely an academic curiosity. It is the **economic backbone** of modern sheet pile design in:

- **Port and harbor quay walls** (deep water anchored bulkheads).
- **Highway and railway cut-and-cover retaining walls**.
- **Temporary excavation support** in urban basements.
- **Flood protection levee walls** along riverbanks.

By accounting for moment reduction, designers can specify **lighter, cheaper pile sections** (saving 15–40% steel tonnage in flexible walls) **without compromising safety**, because the reduced moment is the *actual* in-service demand rather than a theoretical upper bound. The saving is most pronounced for **high, slender walls** in **medium-dense sand**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Flexibility Number $\rho$

The flexibility number emerges from dimensional analysis of the wall-soil interaction problem. The wall deflects under net horizontal pressure $p = (\sigma_a - \sigma_p)$, and the governing equation of the deflected shape of an elastic beam on an elastic (Winkler-type) foundation is:

$$E I \frac{d^4 y}{dz^4} + k \cdot y = p(z)$$

where:
- $E I$ = flexural rigidity per unit length of wall
- $y(z)$ = lateral deflection at depth $z$
- $k$ = modulus of subgrade reaction (soil stiffness per unit area, kN/m³)
- $p(z)$ = net pressure differential

To non-dimensionalize, we introduce the **characteristic length** $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = \sqrt[4]{\frac{4 E I}{k}}$$

This $\lambda$ represents the natural length scale over which the wall bends under soil reaction. The **flexibility number** is defined as the ratio of the wall's mechanical height to this characteristic length, raised to the 4th power:

$$\rho = \left(\frac{H}{\lambda}\right)^4 = \frac{H^4}{4 E I / k} = \frac{H^4 \cdot k}{4 E I}$$

> **Why the 4th power?** In beam-on-elastic-foundation theory, the deformation response scales as $1/(EI)$, and the soil reaction scales as $k$. The ratio $(k H^4)/(EI)$ is therefore the *only* dimensionless combination that captures both the **slenderness** of the wall and the **relative stiffness** of the soil. The factor of 4 is conventionally absorbed into the definition:

$$\rho = \frac{H^4}{E I}$$

(using normalized units where $k$ and constants are absorbed into the chart axes).

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Derivation — Reduction Factor from First Principles

To understand *why* the reduction factor decreases with $\rho$, consider the work-energy balance of the wall-soil system.

**The Total Strain Energy Argument:**

The strain energy stored in the bending wall is:

$$U_{\text{bending}} = \int_0^H \frac{M^2(z)}{2 E I} \, dz$$

For a **rigid wall** ($\rho \to 0$), the moment diagram $M(z)$ is the theoretical Rankine distribution, and $U_{\text{bending}}$ is at its **maximum**.

For a **flexible wall** ($\rho \to \infty$), the wall deflects freely, allowing the soil to **arch** around the deforming zone. The arching transfers lateral thrust from the mid-height to the **anchor** (top) and the **toe** (bottom). The net moment diagram $M(z)$ is "**flattened**" at mid-height, and the peak moment $M_{\max}$ drops.

The reduction factor is defined empirically (and charted) as:

$$M_r = \frac{M_{\max,\text{actual}}}{M_{\max,\text{theoretical}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The reduction factor $M_r$ is **NOT a constant** — it is a *function* of $\rho$. Students frequently treat it as a fixed 0.7 or 0.8 value, which is incorrect. $M_r$ can range from **1.0** (very stiff wall) to **0.30** (highly flexible wall in dense sand with full arching).

## 3.3 Full Step-by-Step Numerical Example

**Problem:** An anchored sheet pile wall retains **8 m** of cohesionless soil with $\phi = 32^\circ$ and $\gamma = 18$ kN/m³. The wall is made of steel with $E = 2 \times 10^8$ kN/m², and the section has $I = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ m⁴/m. Using the **Free Earth Support Method (FESM)** and Rowe's reduction, find the design bending moment.

---

**Step 1 — Compute Rankine Coefficients:**

$$K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ - \frac{32^\circ}{2}\right) = \tan^2(29^\circ) = (0.5543)^2 = 0.3073$$

$$K_p = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ + \frac{32^\circ}{2}\right) = \tan^2(61^\circ) = (1.8040)^2 = 3.255$$

---

**Step 2 — Compute the Theoretical Maximum Moment (FESM):**

For the Free Earth Support Method, the maximum moment in the wall occurs at the point of zero shear. For a simple embedment case, the moment is:

$$M_{\max,\text{FESM}} = \frac{\gamma \cdot H^3}{6 \sqrt{K_a}}$$

Substituting:

$$M_{\max,\text{FESM}} = \frac{18 \times 8^3}{6 \times \sqrt{0.3073}}$$

$$M_{\max,\text{FESM}} = \frac{18 \times 512}{6 \times 0.5543}$$

$$M_{\max,\text{FESM}} = \frac{9216}{3.3258}$$

$$M_{\max,\text{FESM}} = 2771 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

---

**Step 3 — Compute the Flexibility Number $\rho$:**

$$\rho = \frac{H^4}{E \cdot I}$$

$$\rho = \frac{8^4}{2 \times 10^8 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\rho = \frac{4096}{24000}$$

$$\rho = 0.1707$$

> **Note:** Some chart versions require a stress normalization. Using a reference stress $\sigma_s = \gamma H = 18 \times 8 = 144$ kPa, the normalized flexibility is:
> $$\rho' = \rho \times \sigma_s = 0.1707 \times 144 = 24.6$$
> This $\rho'$ value (in kPa) is what is plotted on Rowe's chart for medium-dense sand.

---

**Step 4 — Read the Moment Reduction Factor $M_r$:**

From Rowe's curve for **anchored wall, FESM, medium-dense sand**, at $\rho' \approx 24.6$:

$$M_r \approx 0.78$$

---

**Step 5 — Compute the Reduced Design Moment:**

$$M_{\text{design}} = M_r \cdot M_{\max,\text{FESM}}$$

$$M_{\text{design}} = 0.78 \times 2771$$

$$M_{\text{design}} = 2161 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

---

**Step 6 — Select the Sheet Pile Section:**

For steel with allowable bending stress $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 0.6 \times 250 = 150$ MPa = 150 000 kN/m²:

$$Z_{\text{required}} = \frac{M_{\text{design}}}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}$$

$$Z_{\text{required}} = \frac{2161}{150000}$$

$$Z_{\text{required}} = 0.01441 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}$$

$$Z_{\text{required}} = 1441 \text{ cm}^3/\text{m}$$

A standard **AZ-14** sheet pile (with $Z \approx 1450$ cm³/m) would be selected.

> **Savings Realized:** Without Rowe's reduction, $Z_{\text{required}} = 2771/150000 = 0.01847$ m³/m = 1847 cm³/m, requiring a heavier **AZ-18** section. The Rowe reduction saves **one section size** — a tangible economic benefit.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Rowe Reduction Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Anchored Sheet Pile Wall Design"] --> B["Step 1: Input Wall Geometry and Soil Properties"]
    B --> B1["Wall Height H in m"]
    B --> B2["Soil Friction phi in degrees"]
    B --> B3["Soil Unit Weight gamma in kN per cubic m"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Compute Rankine Coefficients"]
    C --> C1["Ka = tan squared 45 minus phi by 2"]
    C --> C2["Kp = tan squared 45 plus phi by 2"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Compute Classical Maximum Moment using FESM or Blum"]
    D --> D1["Mmax theoretical in kN m per m"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Compute Rowe Flexibility Number rho"]
    E --> E1["rho = H to the power 4 divided by E times I"]
    E --> F["Step 5: Apply Soil Density Correction for rho prime"]
    F --> G["Step 6: Enter Rowe Reduction Chart with rho prime"]
    G --> G1["Select correct curve family: FESM or Fixed Earth Support"]
    G --> G2["Select soil density: Loose, Medium, or Dense"]
    G --> H["Step 7: Read Moment Reduction Factor Mr from chart"]
    H --> I["Step 8: Compute Reduced Design Moment"]
    I --> I1["Mdesign = Mr times Mmax theoretical"]
    I --> J["Step 9: Select Sheet Pile Section"]
    J --> J1["Z required = Mdesign divided by sigma allowable"]
    J --> K["Step 10: Verify Deflection and Anchorage"]
    K --> L["End: Final Design Report"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Pressure Redistribution Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RIGID["Rigid Wall Behavior"]
        R1["Linear Rankine Pressure"] --> R2["High Mid-Height Moment"]
        R2 --> R3["No Reduction: Mr equals 1"]
    end
    subgraph FLEX["Flexible Wall Behavior"]
        F1["Deflected Wall Shape"] --> F2["Arching Transfers Load to Ends"]
        F2 --> F3["Reduced Mid-Height Moment"]
        F3 --> F4["Significant Reduction: Mr less than 1"]
    end
    subgraph GOV["Governing Parameter"]
        G1["Flexibility Number rho"] --> G2["Larger rho leads to Smaller Mr"]
    end
    R3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Wall Behavior Comparison Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CASE_A["Case A: Stiff Wall rho small"]
        A1["Concrete Secant Pile"] --> A2["Deflection small"]
        A2 --> A3["Classical Pressure Holds"]
        A3 --> A4["Mr close to 1"]
    end
    subgraph CASE_B["Case B: Flexible Wall rho large"]
        B1["Steel Sheet Pile AZ Series"] --> B2["Deflection moderate"]
        B2 --> B3["Arching Mobilized"]
        B3 --> B4["Mr around 0.5 to 0.8"]
    end
    subgraph CASE_C["Case C: Very Flexible Wall rho very large"]
        C1["Vinyl or Thin Steel Wall"] --> C2["Deflection large"]
        C2 --> C3["Full Arching at Mid-Height"]
        C3 --> C4["Mr as low as 0.3"]
    end
    CASE_A --> COMPARE["Compare Mr for Section Selection"]
    CASE_B --> COMPARE
    CASE_C --> COMPARE
```
