<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Rowe's Moment Reduction Factor. State the condition under which Rowe's reduction is NOT applicable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Rowe's Moment Reduction Factor ($M_r$) is defined as the ratio of the **actual maximum bending moment** developed in a flexible anchored sheet pile wall to the **theoretical maximum bending moment** computed by classical methods such as the Free Earth Support Method (FESM) or the Fixed Earth Support (Blum) Method. It is a function of the wall's **Flexibility Number** $\rho = H^4 / (E \cdot I)$.

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for $\rho$ expression, 1 mark for non-applicability condition)**

Rowe's reduction is **NOT applicable** to:
- Cohesive (clay) soils, due to time-dependent consolidation and creep.
- Rigid walls (very low $\rho$).
- Walls with significant surcharge loads, where arching is disrupted.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is Rowe's Flexibility Number? Write its mathematical expression and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Rowe's Flexibility Number $\rho$ is a **dimensionless parameter** that quantifies the relative flexibility of a sheet pile wall with respect to the surrounding soil. **(1 mark)**

Mathematically:
$$\rho = \frac{H^4}{E \cdot I}$$

**(1 mark)**

**Physical Significance:** A high $\rho$ indicates a **slender, flexible** wall that deflects significantly under load, allowing the soil to arch and reducing the bending moment. A low $\rho$ indicates a **stiff, rigid** wall that approximates classical rigid-body behaviour. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 3, Q4]`

**(a)** A 9 m high anchored sheet pile wall retains dry cohesionless soil with $\phi = 30^\circ$ and $\gamma = 17$ kN/m³. The wall material is steel with $E = 2 \times 10^8$ kN/m², and the section has $I = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m⁴/m. The reference stress for Rowe's chart is $\sigma_s = \gamma H$.
**(i)** Compute the theoretical maximum bending moment using the Free Earth Support Method. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Compute the Flexibility Number $\rho$ and the normalized value $\rho' = \rho \cdot \sigma_s$. **(2 marks)**
**(iii)** From Rowe's chart for FESM in medium-dense sand, if $M_r = 0.75$, compute the reduced design moment. **(2 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the difference in pressure distribution and bending moment diagram between a **rigid** and a **flexible** anchored sheet pile wall. Comment on the role of **soil arching**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a)(i) — Theoretical Maximum Moment (FESM):**

Rankine active coefficient:

$$K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45^\circ - \frac{30^\circ}{2}\right) = \tan^2(30^\circ) = \left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)^2 = 0.3333$$

Theoretical moment (FESM):

$$M_{\max,\text{FESM}} = \frac{\gamma \cdot H^3}{6 \sqrt{K_a}} = \frac{17 \times 9^3}{6 \times \sqrt{0.3333}}$$

$$M_{\max,\text{FESM}} = \frac{17 \times 729}{6 \times 0.5774} = \frac{12393}{3.4641}$$

$$M_{\max,\text{FESM}} = 3577.6 \text{ kN·m/m} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)}$$

**Part (a)(ii) — Flexibility Number:**

$$\rho = \frac{H^4}{E \cdot I} = \frac{9^4}{2 \times 10^8 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{6561}{30000} = 0.2187$$

Reference stress: $\sigma_s = 17 \times 9 = 153$ kPa

$$\rho' = \rho \cdot \sigma_s = 0.2187 \times 153 = 33.46 \text{ kPa} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Part (a)(iii) — Reduced Design Moment:**

$$M_{\text{design}} = M_r \cdot M_{\max,\text{FESM}} = 0.75 \times 3577.6 = 2683.2 \text{ kN·m/m} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Part (b) — Rigid vs. Flexible Wall Behaviour:**

In a **rigid wall**, the wall does not deflect appreciably. The full Rankine active wedge develops behind the wall, and the lateral pressure increases linearly with depth, producing a triangular pressure distribution. The bending moment diagram is a cubic curve with the maximum moment near the lower third of the wall height. **(3 marks)**

In a **flexible wall**, the wall bows between the anchor and the toe. The deflection prevents the full active state in the central region, and **soil arching** redistributes the lateral thrust toward the **top anchor** and the **bottom passive zone**. The pressure diagram becomes **non-linear** with reduced mid-height intensity, and the bending moment diagram shows a **flattened peak**. **(3 marks)**

**Soil arching** is the load-transfer mechanism wherein soil grains "lock" against each other and bridge across the deforming zone, redirecting stress around the flexible middle to the stiffer boundaries. This is the physical basis of Rowe's moment reduction, and it is captured mathematically through the moment reduction factor $M_r < 1$. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 3, Q5]`

**(a)** What is a sheet pile wall? Differentiate between a **cantilever** and an **anchored** sheet pile wall. **(4 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss Rowe's method for the design of anchored sheet pile walls in cohesionless soils. Clearly bring out the role of the **flexibility number** and the **moment reduction factor**. **(10 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Sheet Pile Wall Basics:**

A sheet pile wall is a **continuous vertical wall** made by driving interlocking sheets of steel, vinyl, timber, or precast concrete into the ground, primarily used to retain soil or water. **(1 mark)**

| Feature | Cantilever Sheet Pile Wall | Anchored Sheet Pile Wall |
|---|---|---|
| Support | Self-supporting via embedment only | Supported by ground anchor or tie-back at top |
| Height Limit | Up to ~5–6 m (economical) | Up to 15–20 m or more |
| Deflection | Large, especially at top | Small, controlled by anchor |
| Design Method | Cantilever (fixed-free) beam theory | FESM or Fixed Earth Support + Rowe reduction |
| Typical Use | River training, low-headwater cofferdams | Quay walls, deep excavations, flood walls |

**(3 marks for differentiation)**

**Part (b) — Rowe's Method in Detail:**

Rowe's method is a **semi-empirical refinement** of classical anchored wall design, developed to bridge the gap between **theoretical rigid-wall moments** and **observed in-service moments** in flexible walls. The method consists of the following elements:

**(i) Flexibility Number $\rho$:**
$$\rho = \frac{H^4}{E \cdot I}$$
This captures the relative stiffness of the wall. **(2 marks)**

**(ii) Flexibility Number Correction:**
For comparison with Rowe's experimental charts, the parameter is normalized as $\rho' = \rho \cdot \sigma_s$, where $\sigma_s$ is a characteristic stress (often $\gamma H$ or a representative overburden stress). **(2 marks)**

**(iii) Moment Reduction Factor $M_r$:**
Using the appropriate Rowe chart (chosen by soil density and support condition), $M_r$ is read directly. The chart shows that **$M_r$ decreases as $\rho'$ increases**, with the steepest reduction in **dense cohesionless soils** where arching is most efficient. **(3 marks)**

**(iv) Design Moment Calculation:**
$$M_d = M_r \cdot M_{\max,\text{theoretical}}$$
The reduced design moment is then used to select the pile section via:
$$Z_{\text{req}} = \frac{M_d}{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}$$
**(2 marks)**

**(v) Economic Impact and Limitations:**
The Rowe method allows 15–40% savings in sheet pile tonnage for typical flexible walls. However, it is **not applicable to cohesive soils**, walls with high surcharge loads, or walls where deflection is restricted by adjacent structures. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Treating $M_r$ as a constant (0.7 or 0.8).** It is a *function* of $\rho$. Examiners explicitly check whether you have referenced the flexibility number in your answer. **(–1 to –2 marks)**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to normalize $\rho$.** The raw $H^4/(EI)$ has units of m²/kN. Chart inputs require a dimensionless or stress-normalized form. Mixing units in $\rho$ is a top-3 reason for full-mark denial. **(–2 marks)**
>
> 3. **Applying Rowe's reduction in clay.** This is a **conceptual disqualifier**. Rowe's method is for **cohesionless** soils only. Writing a full numerical answer in a clay-soil problem will earn zero. **(–3 marks)**
>
> 4. **Confusing FESM and Fixed Earth Support.** The two methods use different reduction curves. Using the wrong chart family leads to an incorrect $M_r$. **(–2 marks)**
>
> 5. **Ignoring the allowable stress check.** Section modulus is the deliverable; forgetting $Z_{\text{req}} = M_d / \sigma_{\text{allow}}$ is a frequent oversight. **(–1 mark)**
>
> 6. **Forgetting to state that $M_r$ comes from a chart/curve.** The reduction factor is not a closed-form equation — it is **read from a graph**. Examiners expect a clear statement to this effect. **(–1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Rowe's Moment Reduction Factor**

- **Definition:** $M_r = M_{\max,\text{actual}} / M_{\max,\text{theoretical}}$, always $0 \leq M_r \leq 1$.
- **Flexibility Number:** $\rho = H^4 / (E \cdot I)$ — the master input parameter.
- **Normalized Form:** $\rho' = \rho \cdot \sigma_s$ (where $\sigma_s \approx \gamma H$ for cohesionless soil).
- **Applicability:** Cohesionless soils ONLY. NEVER apply to clay.
- **Source of $M_r$:** Always read from a **chart/curve**, never from a closed-form equation.
- **Chart Selection:** Match the curve family to (i) FESM vs. Fixed Earth Support, and (ii) soil density (loose / medium / dense).
- **Trend:** As $\rho'$ increases, $M_r$ decreases (more flexible wall → more moment savings).
- **Design Equation:** $M_d = M_r \cdot M_{\max,\text{FESM or Blum}}$.
- **Section Selection:** $Z_{\text{req}} = M_d / \sigma_{\text{allow}}$, with $\sigma_{\text{allow}} \approx 0.6 F_y$ for steel.
- **Savings Potential:** 15–40% reduction in required section modulus for typical flexible walls.
- **Physical Cause:** Soil arching redistributes lateral pressure from the deflecting mid-height to the anchor and toe.
- **Limitation 1:** Not for cohesive soils.
- **Limitation 2:** Not for walls with restricted deflection (e.g., next to sensitive structures).
- **Limitation 3:** Assumes uniform, drained, free-draining cohesionless soil.
- **Historical Note:** Developed by P.W. Rowe (1952–1957) based on large-scale model tests and field observations.
- **Units Vigilance:** $H$ in m, $E$ in kPa, $I$ in m⁴/m → $\rho$ in m²/kN; multiply by $\sigma_s$ in kPa to obtain dimensionless-like $\rho'$.
- **Default Fallback:** If $\rho$ is very small ($\rho' < 5$), the wall is essentially rigid → use $M_r = 1$ and proceed with classical design.
- **Default Fallback:** If $\rho$ is very large ($\rho' > 200$), the wall is extremely flexible → $M_r$ asymptotes to a lower bound (≈ 0.30 in dense sand).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lateral Load Test on Vertical Piles, Details of Broms' Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Lateral Load Test on Vertical Piles, Details of Broms' Method # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Lateral Load Test on Vertical Piles — Formal Definition The **Lateral Load Test** is an *in-situ* full-scale loading test performed on a vertical pile (or pile group) to determine its **ultimate lateral capacity (H_u)** and **load-deflection (H–δ) behaviour** when subjected to a horizontal force at or near the pile head. The test is conducted as per **IS 2911 (Part 4) : 1985 (Reaffirmed 2010)** — *Code of Practice for Design and Construction of Pile Foundations — Part 4: Load Test on Piles*. > [!NOTE] > **Purpose of the Test (KTU 2024 Highlight)** > 1. To verify the **design lateral capacity** of a single pile under field conditions. > 2. To obtain the **H–δ curve** for estimating deflections at working loads. > 3. To calibrate analytical models (e.g., **Broms' Method**, *p-y curves*) with actual field response. > 4. To determine the **group efficiency** under lateral loading. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Skewer in Sand" > [!TIP] > **Imagine pushing a wooden skewer sideways through a tub of butter (cohesive) versus a tub of dry sand (cohesionless).** > > - In **clay (cohesive)**, the butter resists the skewer with a *constant squeezing force* along its entire embedded length — resistance does **not** grow with depth. This mimics Broms' assumption of **p_u = 9 c_u B** (constant ultimate reaction). > > - In **sand (cohesionless)**, the deeper the skewer goes, the *stronger* the surrounding sand pushes back. Resistance **grows linearly with depth** because the overburden pressure (γ·z) increases — mirroring Broms' **p_u = 3 K_p B γ z**. > > - The **head condition** is like the skewer's top: held *free* (it can swivel — "free-head") or *clamped* (rigid cap — "fixed-head"). The way the skewer breaks or rotates depends entirely on this top condition. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted) | Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Minimum test load duration per increment** | **≥ 30 minutes** (or till rate of settlement ≤ 0.1 mm/hr) | Stabilization criterion per IS 2911 | | **Failure deflection criterion** | **12 mm** (or 5 % of pile diameter **B**, whichever is less) | Defines H_u from test data | | **Load increment** | **10 % – 15 % of estimated H_u** | Standard stepwise loading | | **Unloading decrements** | **3 to 5 equal stages** | Recovery measurement | | **Reaction gap** | **≥ 5 × B** from test pile | To avoid soil-pile interaction | | **Anchor pile capacity** | **≥ 1.25 × H_u** of test pile | Safety margin | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner Expectation:** Always quote the **deflection criterion (12 mm or 5 % B)** when interpreting lateral load test results — this is the most common *two-mark* recall question in ESE. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Broms' Method — Conceptual Framework **Broms (1964)** developed closed-form analytical solutions for the ultimate lateral capacity of rigid and flexible piles. The method assumes: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Assumptions of Broms' Method** > 1. The **ultimate soil reaction per unit length (p_u)** is fully mobilized along the failure zone. > 2. The pile is **rigid** (short pile) or **flexible** (long pile with plastic hinge). > 3. Soil behaviour is **undrained (clay)** or **drained (sand)**. > 4. Soil reaction distribution is **idealized** (constant for clay, triangular for sand). > 5. The pile head is either **free** (rotation allowed) or **fixed** (rotation restrained). ## 2.2 Soil Reaction Models ### (a) Cohesive Soil (Clay, c–φ = 0, undrained) The ultimate lateral resistance per unit length of pile (Broms, 1964): $$p_u = 9 \, c_u \, B$$ where **c_u = undrained cohesion (kN/m²)** and **B = pile diameter (m)**. > The factor **9** comes from Rankine passive pressure coefficient **N_p = 9** for c–φ = 0 soil (Stas & Kulhawy, 1984; Brinch Hansen, 1961). ### (b) Cohesionless Soil (Sand, c = 0, drained) The ultimate lateral resistance per unit length of pile: $$p_u = 3 \, K_p \, B \, \gamma \, z$$ where **K_p = Rankine passive coefficient = tan²(45° + φ/2)**, **γ = unit weight (kN/m³)**, and **z = depth (m)**. ## 2.3 Pile Classification (Short vs Long) The classification is governed by the **Critical Length L_c** — the pile length beyond which the lateral capacity cannot be increased by adding more embedment depth. ### For Cohesive Soil - **Free-head pile:** $$L_c = 3B \left( \frac{M_y}{9 \, c_u \, B^3} + 0.5 \right)$$ - **Fixed-head pile:** $$L_c = 2.5B \left( \frac{M_y}{9 \, c_u \, B^3} + 0.5 \right)$$ ### For Cohesionless Soil $$L_c = \left( \frac{2 M_y}{B \gamma K_p} \right)^{1/3} \quad \text{(free-head)}$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Consolidated Table) > [!NOTE] > **The following table is the single most important revision tool for ESE — memorize the four key cases for cohesive and cohesionless soils.** | **Case** | **Soil Type** | **Pile Type** | **Ultimate Lateral Load (H_u)** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Clay (c–φ = 0) | Free-head, **Short** | $H_u = 9 \, c_u \, B \, (L - 1.5B)$ | | 2 | Clay (c–φ = 0) | Free-head, **Long** | $H_u = M_y \, / \, (e + 1.5B)$ | | 3 | Clay (c–φ = 0) | Fixed-head, **Short** | $H_u = 9 \, c_u \, B \, (L - 0.5B)$ | | 4 | Clay (c–φ = 0) | Fixed-head, **Long** | $H_u = 2 M_y \, / \, B$ | | 5 | Sand (c = 0) | Free-head, **Short** | $H_u = 0.5 \, \gamma \, B \, L^2 \, K_p \, / \, (1 + 2e/L)$ | | 6 | Sand (c = 0) | Free-head, **Long** | $H_u = M_y \, / \, (e + f)$ with $f = 0.82 \sqrt{M_y / (B \gamma K_p)}$ | | 7 | Sand (c = 0) | Fixed-head, **Short** | $H_u = 1.5 \, \gamma \, B \, L^2 \, K_p$ | | 8 | Sand (c = 0) | Fixed-head, **Long** | $H_u = 2 M_y \, / \, B$ | Where: - **M_y** = Yield moment of the pile section (kN·m) - **e** = Eccentricity of lateral load above ground (m) - **f** = Depth from ground to point of maximum moment in sand - **B** = Pile width/diameter (m) - **L** = Embedded length of pile (m) - **γ** = Unit weight of soil (kN/m³) - **K_p** = Rankine passive earth pressure coefficient ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Offshore wind turbine foundations** — monopiles are designed using Broms' method for sand (long free-head) and *p-y* curves for refined analysis. - **Bridge piers and abutments** — pile bents under braking/wind loads are designed via Broms' closed-form. - **Transmission line tower foundations** — *fixed-head* short pile case dominates in stiff clays. - **Seismic design** — initial sizing of piles under cyclic lateral loads uses Broms' static ultimate capacity as a baseline. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Lateral Load Test — Interpretation of H–δ Curve The lateral load test data is plotted as **Lateral Load (H, kN)** vs **Lateral Deflection (δ, mm)**. The ultimate lateral load is determined by **any one** of the following criteria (KTU/IS 2911 conventions): > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Standard Criteria for H_u from Test Data** > 1. **Deflection Criterion** — Load corresponding to δ = **12 mm** or δ = 0.05B (whichever is less). > 2. **Tangent Method** — Draw two tangents to the initial and final linear portions of the H–δ curve; H_u is the load at the point of intersection. > 3. **Logarithmic Method (Davisson's analogy)** — On a log H vs log δ plot, the point of change in slope defines H_u. ## 3.2 Derivation: Broms' Formula for Free-Head Short Pile in Clay ### Problem Setup - A rigid pile of length **L** and diameter **B** is embedded in clay of undrained cohesion **c_u**. - Lateral load **H** is applied at height **e** above ground level. - Soil reaction: **p_u = 9 c_u B** (constant with depth). - The pile rotates as a rigid body about a point at depth **f = 1.5B** from the ground surface (Broms' hinge depth for cohesive soil). ### Step 1 — Free Body & Force Equilibrium The total horizontal soil resistance above the hinge point (length = L − 1.5B) must equal the applied lateral load H: $$\begin{aligned} H_u &= \int_{1.5B}^{L} p_u \, dz \\ H_u &= \int_{1.5B}^{L} 9 \, c_u \, B \, dz \\ H_u &= 9 \, c_u \, B \, [\,z\,]_{1.5B}^{L} \\ H_u &= 9 \, c_u \, B \, (L - 1.5B) \end{aligned}$$ This is the **closed-form Broms' formula for free-head short pile in clay**. ### Step 2 — Moment Equilibrium Verification (Why f = 1.5B?) For a free-head pile, the **point of zero shear** (and hence the location of maximum moment) occurs at depth **1.5B** in clay. This is derived by integrating the constant pressure and locating the centroid of the soil reaction diagram: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Shear at depth } z: & \quad V(z) = H - 9 c_u B \cdot z \\ \text{Setting } V(z) = 0: & \quad H = 9 c_u B \cdot z \\ \text{But } H = 9 c_u B (L - 1.5B): & \quad 9 c_u B (L - 1.5B) = 9 c_u B \cdot z \\ \Rightarrow & \quad z = L - 1.5B \end{aligned}$$ For the **maximum moment** point (where shear = 0 and soil reaction reverses), Broms showed the location is **1.5B below ground** for the **upper hinge** (the additional 1.5B accounts for the point of contraflexure in the deflected shape of a rigid pile). ### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) > **Given:** B = 0.4 m, L = 12 m, c_u = 50 kN/m², e = 0.5 m. Pile is free-head. **Step 3a — Compute H_u:** $$\begin{aligned} H_u &= 9 \times 50 \times 0.4 \times (12 - 1.5 \times 0.4) \\ H_u &= 180 \times (12 - 0.6) \\ H_u &= 180 \times 11.4 \\ H_u &= 2052 \text{ kN} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3b — Check if Long Pile Applies:** Assume **M_y = 250 kN·m** for a reinforced concrete pile. Compute L_c: $$\begin{aligned} L_c &= 3B \left( \frac{M_y}{9 c_u B^3} + 0.5 \right) \\ L_c &= 3 \times 0.4 \times \left( \frac{250}{9 \times 50 \times 0.4^3} + 0.5 \right) \\ L_c &= 1.2 \times \left( \frac{250}{288} + 0.5 \right) \\ L_c &= 1.2 \times (0.868 + 0.5) \\ L_c &= 1.2 \times 1.368 \\ L_c &= 1.64 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$ Since **L = 12 m ≫ L_c = 1.64 m**, the pile is **LONG**. The lateral capacity is governed by **plastic hinge formation** (Step 4). **Step 3c — Compute H_u as Long Pile:** $$\begin{aligned} H_u &= \frac{M_y}{e + 1.5B} \\ H_u &= \frac{250}{0.5 + 1.5 \times 0.4} \\ H_u &= \frac{250}{0.5 + 0.6} \\ H_u &= \frac{250}{1.1} \\ H_u &= 227.27 \text{ kN} \end{aligned}$$ > [!TIP] > **Why the Long-Pile Capacity (227 kN) is Far Less than Short-Pile (2052 kN):** > In a long flexible pile, **only the upper 1.5B to 2.0B zone** mobilizes soil reaction before the pile yields. Below this, the pile simply bends — the deep soil is never engaged. The **design is governed by structural yielding**, not soil strength. ## 3.3 Derivation: Broms' Formula for Free-Head Short Pile in Sand ### Setup - Sand with friction angle **φ**, unit weight **γ**. - Soil reaction: **p_u = 3 K_p B γ z** (linear with depth). - Rigid pile rotates about a point at depth **f**; failure by rigid-body rotation. ### Step 1 — Total Force Above Rotation Point (depth 0 to f) $$\begin{aligned} F_1 &= \int_{0}^{f} 3 K_p B \gamma z \, dz = 1.5 K_p B \gamma f^2 \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 2 — Total Force Below Rotation Point (depth f to L) $$\begin{aligned} F_2 &= \int_{f}^{L} 3 K_p B \gamma z \, dz = 1.5 K_p B \gamma (L^2 - f^2) \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 3 — Horizontal Force Equilibrium (F_1 = F_2) $$\begin{aligned} 1.5 K_p B \gamma f^2 &= 1.5 K_p B \gamma (L^2 - f^2) \\ f^2 &= L^2 - f^2 \\ 2 f^2 &= L^2 \\ f &= \frac{L}{\sqrt{2}} \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4 — Moment Equilibrium About Rotation Point $$\begin{aligned} H_u \cdot \left(e + \frac{L}{\sqrt{2}}\right) &= \int_{f}^{L} 3 K_p B \gamma z (z - f) \, dz - \int_{0}^{f} 3 K_p B \gamma z (f - z) \, dz \\ H_u &= \frac{0.5 \gamma B L^3 K_p}{e + L/\sqrt{2}} \quad \text{(after algebraic simplification)} \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The 1/√2 factor (≈ 0.707) appearing in the denominator is a unique signature of Broms' sand analysis** and is frequently tested as a "find-the-error" type question. ### Step 5 — Special Case: e = 0 (Load at Ground Level) $$\begin{aligned} H_u &= \frac{0.5 \gamma B L^3 K_p}{0 + L/\sqrt{2}} \\ H_u &= \frac{0.5 \gamma B L^2 K_p}{1/\sqrt{2}} \\ H_u &= 0.707 \, \gamma \, B \, L^2 \, K_p \end{aligned}$$ > [!TIP] > **Quick Comparison:** For **fixed-head** short pile in sand, the coefficient becomes **1.5** instead of **0.707** — a **~ 2.12× increase in capacity**. This shows why pile cap restraint is so effective. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart Logic | **Decision Point** | **Question** | **If YES →** | **If NO →** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Is soil cohesive (c–φ = 0)? | Use p_u = 9 c_u B | Use p_u = 3 K_p B γ z | | 2 | Is pile head restrained (fixed)? | Use fixed-head formula | Use free-head formula | | 3 | Is L < L_c (rigid pile)? | Use *short-pile* formula | Use *long-pile* formula | | 4 | Does structural M_y < soil demand? | Capacity = structural | Capacity = soil-controlled | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Lateral Load Test — Field Setup (IS 2911 Part 4) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Ground_Surface["GROUND SURFACE"] D1["Dial Gauge 1
Back of Test Pile"]:::gauge D2["Dial Gauge 2
Front of Test Pile"]:::gauge RBP["Reaction Beam
Steel W-section"]:::steel TP["TEST PILE
B diameter"]:::testPile end subgraph Below_Ground["SOIL MEDIUM"] EP1["Embedded
Length L"]:::embed S1["Surrounding
Soil"]:::soil end AP1["Anchor Pile 1"]:::anchor AP2["Anchor Pile 2"]:::anchor HJ["Hydraulic Jack"]:::jack AP3["Anchor Pile 3"]:::anchor AP4["Anchor Pile 4"]:::anchor D1 --- TP D2 --- TP RBP --- TP AP1 --- RBP AP2 --- RBP HJ --- TP AP3 --- RBP AP4 --- RBP TP --- EP1 EP1 --- S1 classDef gauge fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 classDef steel fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000 classDef testPile fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF classDef embed fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,color:#000 classDef soil fill:#DEB887,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 classDef anchor fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef jack fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The **hydraulic jack (HJ)** pushes the test pile (TP) sideways while being resisted by **four anchor piles (AP1–AP4)** via a **reaction beam (RBP)**. Two **dial gauges (D1, D2)** record deflection at the pile head. ## 4.2 Broms' Method — Classification & Selection Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A["START: Pile Subjected to
Lateral Load H"]:::start A --> B{"Soil Type?"}:::decision B -- "Clay (c-phi = 0)" --> C1["p_u = 9 c_u B
constant with depth"]:::clay B -- "Sand (c = 0)" --> C2["p_u = 3 K_p B gamma z
linear with depth"]:::sand C1 --> D1{"Head Condition?"}:::decision C2 --> D2{"Head Condition?"}:::decision D1 -- "Free Head" --> E1{"L vs L_c?"}:::decision D1 -- "Fixed Head" --> E2{"L vs L_c?"}:::decision D2 -- "Free Head" --> E3{"L vs L_c?"}:::decision D2 -- "Fixed Head" --> E4{"L vs L_c?"}:::decision E1 -- "L less than L_c
Short Pile" --> F1["H_u = 9 c_u B times L minus 1.5B"]:::formula E1 -- "L greater than L_c
Long Pile" --> F2["H_u = M_y divided by e plus 1.5B"]:::formula E2 -- "L less than L_c
Short Pile" --> F3["H_u = 9 c_u B times L minus 0.5B"]:::formula E2 -- "L greater than L_c
Long Pile" --> F4["H_u = 2 M_y divided by B"]:::formula E3 -- "L less than L_c
Short Pile" --> F5["H_u = 0.707 gamma B L squared K_p
for e = 0"]:::formula E3 -- "L greater than L_c
Long Pile" --> F6["H_u = M_y divided by e plus f"]:::formula E4 -- "L less than L_c
Short Pile" --> F7["H_u = 1.5 gamma B L squared K_p"]:::formula E4 -- "L greater than L_c
Long Pile" --> F8["H_u = 2 M_y divided by B"]:::formula classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef clay fill:#4682B4,stroke:#00008B,color:#FFF classDef sand fill:#F4A460,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000 classDef formula fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 H–δ Curve — Interpretation Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Curve["LOAD vs DEFLECTION CURVE"] O["Origin 0,0"]:::pt A["A: Initial Linear
Elastic Zone"]:::pt B["B: Curved Transition
Plastic Zone"]:::pt C["C: Failure Point
delta equals 12 mm OR 0.05B"]:::pt D["D: Ultimate Zone
H_u plateau"]:::pt end O --> A A --> B B --> C C --> D T1["Tangent 1:
Initial Slope"]:::tangent T2["Tangent 2:
Final Slope"]:::tangent X["Intersection
equals H_u"]:::intersect T1 -.-> X T2 -.-> X classDef pt fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef tangent fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef intersect fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Two tangents drawn to the initial elastic portion (slope = pile stiffness) and the final plastic portion intersect at the **ultimate lateral load H_u**. The deflection criterion (12 mm) is also marked at point **C** for IS 2911 compliance. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **List the essential components of the lateral load test setup on a vertical pile as per IS 2911 (Part 4). What is the standard failure deflection criterion?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The essential components of a lateral load test setup per **IS 2911 (Part 4) : 1985** are: 1. **Test pile** instrumented at the head with **two dial gauges** (one on the load side, one opposite) to measure lateral deflection (δ) — 1 mark. 2. **At least two (preferably four) anchor/reaction piles** placed at a minimum spacing of **5B** from the test pile, with the reaction beam connecting them. A **hydraulic jack** applies the lateral load from the test pile to the reaction beam — 1 mark. 3. **Standard failure criterion:** Ultimate lateral load **H_u** is the load corresponding to a **lateral deflection of 12 mm** or **5 % of the pile diameter (B)**, whichever is less — 1 mark. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "load at which deflection becomes asymptotic" as the only criterion. IS 2911 explicitly uses the **12 mm / 5 % B** deflection limit; mentioning the asymptotic criterion alone is incomplete. --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **State Broms' assumption regarding the ultimate lateral soil reaction (p_u) for (a) cohesive soil and (b) cohesionless soil.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **(a) Cohesive Soil (Clay, undrained, c–φ = 0)** — 1.5 marks: Broms (1964) assumed the ultimate lateral soil reaction per unit length of pile to be **constant with depth**, given by: $$p_u = 9 \, c_u \, B$$ where **c_u = undrained cohesion (kN/m²)** and **B = pile diameter (m)**. The factor 9 represents Rankine's passive earth pressure coefficient for c–φ = 0 soil. **(b) Cohesionless Soil (Sand, drained, c = 0)** — 1.5 marks: Broms assumed p_u to **vary linearly with depth** as: $$p_u = 3 \, K_p \, B \, \gamma \, z$$ where **K_p = tan²(45° + φ/2)** is Rankine's passive coefficient, **γ = unit weight of sand**, and **z = depth below ground level**. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper] **(a)** Derive Broms' expression for the ultimate lateral load capacity of a **free-head, short pile in cohesive soil**. State all assumptions clearly. (7 marks) **(b)** A concrete pile of **diameter 0.5 m** and **length 14 m** is driven into a soft clay deposit with **c_u = 30 kN/m²**. The pile is free-headed, and the lateral load is applied at the **ground surface (e = 0)**. The yield moment of the pile section is **M_y = 400 kN·m**. Compute the ultimate lateral capacity using Broms' method. (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)** **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. The pile behaves as a **rigid body** rotating about a hinge at depth 1.5B. 2. The ultimate soil reaction is **p_u = 9 c_u B** (constant with depth). 3. The soil is **saturated, undrained** (φ = 0, c = c_u). 4. The lateral load H is applied at height **e** above the ground surface. **Derivation (5 marks):** For a free-head short pile in clay, the pile rotates as a rigid body. The hinge (point of zero shear / maximum moment) forms at depth **f = 1.5B** below the ground surface. The total passive resistance of the soil above the hinge is: $$\begin{aligned} F_{passive} &= \int_{1.5B}^{L} p_u \, dz \\ &= \int_{1.5B}^{L} 9 \, c_u \, B \, dz \\ &= 9 \, c_u \, B \, (L - 1.5B) \end{aligned}$$ Equating this to the applied lateral load H_u: $$\boxed{H_u = 9 \, c_u \, B \, (L - 1.5B)}$$ This is the **Broms' (1964) ultimate lateral capacity for a free-head, short, rigid pile in cohesive soil**. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)** **Given:** B = 0.5 m, L = 14 m, c_u = 30 kN/m², e = 0, M_y = 400 kN·m. **Step 1 — Check if Pile is Short or Long (2 marks):** Compute critical length for free-head pile in clay: $$\begin{aligned} L_c &= 3B \left( \frac{M_y}{9 c_u B^3} + 0.5 \right) \\ L_c &= 3 \times 0.5 \times \left( \frac{400}{9 \times 30 \times 0.5^3} + 0.5 \right) \\ L_c &= 1.5 \times \left( \frac{400}{337.5} + 0.5 \right) \\ L_c &= 1.5 \times (1.185 + 0.5) \\ L_c &= 1.5 \times 1.685 \\ L_c &= 2.53 \text{ m} \end{aligned}$$ Since **L = 14 m ≫ L_c = 2.53 m**, the pile is a **LONG PILE**. **Step 2 — Apply Long-Pile Formula (3 marks):** For free-head long pile in clay: $$\begin{aligned} H_u &= \frac{M_y}{e + 1.5B} \\ H_u &= \frac{400}{0 + 1.5 \times 0.5} \\ H_u &= \frac{400}{0.75} \\ H_u &= 533.33 \text{ kN} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Verification Using Short-Pile Formula (2 marks):** $$\begin{aligned} H_{u,\text{short}} &= 9 \times 30 \times 0.5 \times (14 - 1.5 \times 0.5) \\ &= 135 \times 13.25 \\ &= 1788.75 \text{ kN} \end{aligned}$$ The **short-pile capacity (1788.75 kN) is much higher** than the long-pile capacity (533.33 kN), confirming that the **structural yield moment M_y** — not the soil strength — governs the design. **Final Answer (highlighted):** > $$\boxed{H_u = 533.33 \text{ kN} \text{ (governed by long-pile/structural condition)}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper] **(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **procedure for conducting a lateral load test on a single vertical pile** as per IS 2911 (Part 4). (7 marks) **(b)** A **free-head short pile** of **B = 0.45 m** and **L = 6 m** is installed in **loose sand** with **φ = 30°** and **γ = 17 kN/m³**. Using Broms' method, determine the **ultimate lateral capacity**. (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)** **Test Setup (3 marks):** The lateral load test setup comprises: - A **test pile** with its head at the proposed cut-off level. - **Two (or four) anchor/reaction piles** at a minimum spacing of **5B** from the test pile, with **tension members** (Dywidag bars or cables) connecting to a **reaction beam**. - A **hydraulic jack** placed horizontally between the test pile and the reaction beam. - **Two dial gauges** (0.01 mm accuracy) at the pile head — one on the loading face, one opposite — both on **independent reference beams** to avoid vibration errors. **Loading Procedure (2 marks):** - Apply the load in **increments of 10 % to 15 % of the estimated H_u** (typically 1/10th to 1/15th). - Each increment is maintained until the **rate of lateral deflection becomes ≤ 0.1 mm/hr** (or for a **minimum of 30 minutes**). - Record deflection at **0, 5, 10, 15, 30 min** and at 30-min intervals thereafter. - Continue loading up to **failure or 1.5 × design load**, whichever is earlier. - **Unload** in 3–5 equal decrements, recording recovery for the same durations. **Interpretation of Results (2 marks):** - Plot **H vs δ** curve. - Determine **H_u** using the **deflection criterion (12 mm or 5 % B)** or the **tangent intersection method** (whichever governs). - Also plot **H vs log(δ)** for additional verification. - Calculate the **secant stiffness k = H/δ at 50 % of H_u** for use in structural analysis. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)** **Given:** Free-head, short pile, B = 0.45 m, L = 6 m, φ = 30°, γ = 17 kN/m³, e = 0 (assumed at ground level). **Step 1 — Compute K_p (1 mark):** $$\begin{aligned} K_p &= \tan^2\left(45° + \frac{\phi}{2}\right) \\ K_p &= \tan^2(60°) \\ K_p &= (1.732)^2 = 3.0 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Broms' Formula for Free-Head Short Pile in Sand (4 marks):** For e = 0, Broms' formula simplifies to: $$\begin{aligned} H_u &= \frac{0.5 \, \gamma \, B \, L^2 \, K_p}{1 + 2e/L} \\ H_u &= 0.707 \, \gamma \, B \, L^2 \, K_p \quad (\text{since } e = 0) \\ H_u &= 0.707 \times 17 \times 0.45 \times (6)^2 \times 3.0 \\ H_u &= 0.707 \times 17 \times 0.45 \times 36 \times 3.0 \\ H_u &= 0.707 \times 826.2 \\ H_u &= 584.1 \text{ kN} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Verification (2 marks):** Cross-check using the general formula: $$\begin{aligned} H_u &= \frac{0.5 \, \gamma \, B \, L^3 \, K_p}{e + L/\sqrt{2}} \\ H_u &= \frac{0.5 \times 17 \times 0.45 \times 216 \times 3.0}{0 + 6/1.414} \\ H_u &= \frac{2478.6}{4.243} \\ H_u &= 584.2 \text{ kN} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned}$$ > $$\boxed{H_u \approx 584 \text{ kN}}$$ --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in ESE** > > 1. **Wrong Hinge Depth:** Students often write **f = 1.5D** for both cohesive and cohesionless soils. The depth **1.5B** is specific to **clay**. For **sand**, the rotation point is at **L/√2 ≈ 0.707 L**, not 1.5B. > > 2. **Confusing Short vs Long Pile for Sand:** In **sand**, the short-pile case is when the **full passive resistance** is mobilized. In **long piles**, the hinge forms at depth **f = 0.82 √(M_y / (B γ K_p))** — students frequently write 1.5B here too, which is a **3-mark error**. > > 3. **Forgetting the 0.707 vs 1.5 coefficient:** For free-head vs fixed-head short piles in sand, the H_u ratio is exactly **0.707 : 1.5 = 1 : 2.12**. A common error is writing the same formula for both head conditions. > > 4. **Sign convention in IS 2911:** The 12 mm deflection is **permitted for a working load test**. For an **ultimate load test**, the criterion is **5 % B or 12 mm whichever is less**. Always specify which test you are interpreting. > > 5. **M_y in wrong units:** The yield moment **M_y** must be in **kN·m**, not kN·cm. A unit mismatch silently inflates the answer by **100×**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU 2024 Prep)** - [ ] **Lateral Load Test** is conducted as per **IS 2911 Part 4**; failure criterion is **12 mm OR 5 % B**, whichever is less. - [ ] Test requires **≥ 2 reaction piles** at **≥ 5B spacing**, jack between test pile and reaction beam, **2 dial gauges** on independent reference beams. - [ ] Load increments: **10–15 % of H_u**; each increment held for **30 min minimum** or till deflection rate ≤ 0.1 mm/hr. - [ ] **Broms' method** uses **2 idealisations**: constant p_u = 9 c_u B (clay), linear p_u = 3 K_p B γ z (sand). - [ ] **Hinge depth in clay = 1.5B**; in **sand (short pile) = L/√2**. - [ ] **Short pile** in clay (free head): **H_u = 9 c_u B (L − 1.5B)**. - [ ] **Long pile** in clay (free head): **H_u = M_y / (e + 1.5B)**. - [ ] **Short pile** in sand (free head): **H_u = 0.707 γ B L² K_p** (for e = 0). - [ ] **Short pile** in sand (fixed head): **H_u = 1.5 γ B L² K_p** (2.12× the free-head value). - [ ] **Critical length L_c** determines short vs long pile — always check before applying any formula. - [ ] **Fixed-head** long pile in either soil: **H_u = 2 M_y / B**. - [ ] **Always check units**: B in m, c_u in kN/m², γ in kN/m³, M_y in kN·m, H_u in kN. - [ ] **Tangent intersection method** is a graphical alternative to the deflection criterion for determining H_u from the H–δ curve. - [ ] For **seismic design**, Broms' static H_u serves as the **lower-bound** baseline; refined analysis uses *p-y curves* (e.g., Matlock 1970, API 2010). - [ ] **Real-world use cases**: offshore monopiles, bridge pier pile bents, transmission tower foundations, harbour quay walls, sign gantry foundations.

topics.title: Chart for Estimating the Resistance of Short and Long Piles in Clayey Soils, Chart for Estimating the Lateral Deflection at Ground Level for Piles in Clayey Soils Under Working Loads Given by Broms, Chart for Estimating the Ultimate Lateral Resistance of Short and Long Piles in Sandy Soils and Chart for Estimating the Lateral Deflection at Ground Level for Piles in Clayey Soils Under Working Loads Given by Broms, Numerical Problems Using Broms' Charts Alone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET635 - ADVANCED FOUNDATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chart for Estimating the Resistance of Short and Long Piles in Clayey Soils, Chart for Estimating the Lateral Deflection at Ground Level for Piles in Clayey Soils Under Working Loads Given by Broms, Chart for Estimating the Ultimate Lateral Resistance of Short and Long Piles in Sandy Soils and Chart for Estimating the Lateral Deflection at Ground Level for Piles in Clayey Soils Under Working Loads Given by Broms, Numerical Problems Using Broms' Charts Alone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Broms' Method — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Broms' Method (1964-65)** is a closed-form, limit-state design methodology for the analysis of **axially and laterally loaded single piles** in **cohesive (clay)** and **cohesionless (sand)** soils. The method utilizes a set of **dimensionless design charts** that relate a normalized ultimate resistance (or deflection) to a normalized pile geometry (L/B) for both **"short" (rigid)** and **"long" (flexible)** pile classifications.

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically demands mastery of four families of charts:
1. Vertical ultimate resistance in **clay** (short & long).
2. Lateral deflection at ground level in **clay** under working loads.
3. Ultimate lateral resistance in **sand** (short & long).
4. Lateral deflection at ground level in **sand** under working loads.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Fishing Rod in Mud vs. Sand":**
> Imagine pushing a thin rod horizontally into wet clay (mud) versus into loose beach sand.
> - In **clay**, the clay squeezes and flows around the rod uniformly; the resistance builds up like squeezing a wet sponge — it is governed by a **constant undrained cohesion (cu)** at all depths.
> - In **sand**, the sand's resistance grows with depth because the grains become heavier and interlock more; it is governed by an **overburden-dependent friction (γ·z·Kp)**.
> - A **short (rigid) pile** is like a short chopstick — it rotates as a unit. A **long (flexible) pile** is like a long fishing rod — it bends and develops a *plastic hinge*, so adding more length beyond a critical value adds **no extra capacity**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Nc\* = 9** — Skempton's bearing capacity factor for piles in clay (clay failure mechanism, Nc* ranges from 6 to 9; Broms used the upper bound **9**).
> - **Critical Length (Lc) = 3·B to 4·B** — in clay, beyond which the pile behaves as "long".
> - **Coefficient of Lateral Earth Pressure (Kp) = tan²(45° + φ/2)** — Rankine passive coefficient for sand.
> - **α (adhesion factor)** for clay = 1.0 (Broms' conservative upper bound for short piles, drops to 0 for long piles).
> - **Characteristic Length (T) = (E·I / η·h)^(1/5)** for sand (linearly increasing subgrade reaction).
> - **Characteristic Length (T) = (E·I / E_s)^(1/4)** for clay (constant subgrade reaction, Winkler).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pile Classification Logic (Broms' Concept)

> [!NOTE]
> **"Short vs. Long" Pile:**
> The classification depends on the **relative stiffness** of the pile-soil system, expressed via the **characteristic length T**:
> - **Short (rigid) pile:** L < 2·T (for sand) or L < 1.5·T (for clay).
> - **Long (flexible) pile:** L ≥ 2·T (for sand) or L ≥ 1.5·T (for clay).
> In a **short pile**, the failure is by **rigid body rotation**; in a **long pile**, the failure is by **plastic hinge formation** at a depth where bending moment is maximum.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 1: Master Formula Sheet for Broms' Charts (All in SI units)**

| **Loading Type** | **Soil** | **Pile Type** | **Head Condition** | **Ultimate Resistance / Deflection Formula** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Vertical (Qu)** | Clay | Short (L ≤ Lc) | — | $\dfrac{Q_u}{c_u B^2} = 9 + \alpha \cdot \dfrac{L}{B}$ (with α = 1.0 per Broms) |
| **Vertical (Qu)** | Clay | Long (L > Lc) | — | $\dfrac{Q_u}{c_u B^2} = 9$ (constant; shaft friction is *not* counted) |
| **Vertical (Qu)** | Sand | Short | — | $\dfrac{Q_u}{\gamma' B L^2}$ = function of φ (read from chart, parabolic) |
| **Vertical (Qu)** | Sand | Long | — | $\dfrac{Q_u}{\gamma' B L^2}$ = constant (function of φ only) |
| **Lateral (Hu)** | Clay | Short | Free | $H_u = 9 c_u B (L + 1.5 B)$ |
| **Lateral (Hu)** | Clay | Long | Free | $H_u = 9 c_u B (1.5 B) = 13.5\,c_u B^2$ |
| **Lateral (Hu)** | Clay | Short | Fixed | $H_u = 9 c_u B (L + 1.5 B)$ (same as free for short) |
| **Lateral (Hu)** | Clay | Long | Fixed | $H_u = 2 \cdot 9 c_u B (1.5 B) = 27\,c_u B^2$ |
| **Lateral (Hu)** | Sand | Short | Free | $H_u = 0.5\,K_p \gamma' B L^2$ (Rankine passive wedge) |
| **Lateral (Hu)** | Sand | Long | Free | $H_u = 1.5\,M_p / T$ (controlled by plastic hinge) |
| **Lateral (Hu)** | Sand | Long | Fixed | $H_u = 2.0\,M_p / T$ |
| **Lateral Defl. (y)** | Clay | — | Free | $y = H_o T^3 / (E_m I)$, where $T = (EI/E_m)^{1/4}$ |
| **Lateral Defl. (y)** | Sand | — | Free | $y = 2.4\,H_o T^3 / (EI)$, where $T = (EI/\eta h)^{1/5}$ |
| **Broms' Reduction** | Clay | Long (vertical) | — | Shaft friction is **completely ignored** for long piles (loose fit) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used in the table:**
> - $c_u$ = undrained shear strength of clay (kPa)
> - $B$ = pile diameter or width (m)
> - $L$ = embedded pile length (m)
> - $\gamma'$ = effective unit weight of sand (kN/m³)
> - $K_p$ = Rankine passive earth pressure coefficient
> - $M_p$ = plastic moment capacity of pile section (kN·m)
> - $E_m$ = horizontal subgrade modulus for clay (kN/m³ or kPa/m)
> - $\eta h$ = constant of horizontal subgrade reaction for sand (kN/m³)
> - $H_o$ = applied working lateral load (kN)
> - $y$ = lateral deflection at ground level (m)
> - $EI$ = flexural rigidity of pile (kN·m²)

### 2.3 Chart-by-Chart Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chart 1 — Vertical Resistance in Clay (Short & Long):**
> - **X-axis:** L/B (dimensionless length ratio).
> - **Y-axis:** $Q_u / (c_u B^2)$ (dimensionless capacity).
> - **Short-pile curve:** Linear, $Y = 9 + (L/B)$, because the entire shaft friction is mobilized in addition to the end-bearing (9·c·Ap).
> - **Long-pile curve:** Horizontal, $Y = 9$, because relative pile-soil displacement needed to mobilize full shaft friction is *not* achievable — the pile "plugs" itself with soil and only end-bearing works.
> - **Transition:** Occurs at L/B ≈ 3 to 4 (Broms assumed Lc = 3B for square; for circular piles, Lc = 3.75D).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chart 2 — Lateral Deflection at GL for Piles in Clay (Working Load):**
> - **X-axis:** L/T (ratio of embedded length to characteristic length).
> - **Y-axis:** $y \cdot E_m \cdot I / (H_o \cdot T^3)$ — dimensionless deflection.
> - **Free-head curve:** Rises and asymptotes to a maximum.
> - **Fixed-head curve:** Lower deflection (typically half of free-head for the same L/T).
> - At working load, the chart uses $H_o = H_u / F.S.$ (factor of safety usually 2.5 or 3.0).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chart 3 — Ultimate Lateral Resistance in Sand (Short & Long):**
> - **X-axis:** L/B.
> - **Y-axis:** $H_u / (\gamma' B^3 L)$ for short piles, and a different normalization involving $M_p$ for long piles.
> - **Curves parameterized by φ** (e.g., 25°, 30°, 35°, 40°, 45°).
> - **Short-pile curve:** Parabolic, because resistance is Rankine passive $H_u = 0.5 K_p \gamma' B L^2$.
> - **Long-pile curve:** Depends on plastic moment $M_p$ and characteristic length $T = (EI/\eta h)^{1/5}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chart 4 — Lateral Deflection at GL for Piles in Sand (Working Load):**
> - **X-axis:** L/T (where $T = (EI/\eta h)^{1/5}$).
> - **Y-axis:** Dimensionless deflection factor.
> - Used to compute ground-line deflection under working lateral load for serviceability design.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Offshore wind turbine foundations** — heavily rely on Broms for lateral capacity under wind/wave loads.
- **Bridge piers** — lateral deflection checks under ship-impact and seismic loads.
- **High-rise building piles** — for combined axial + lateral (seismic/wind) design.
- **Sign boards, transmission towers** — tall, slender structures with significant lateral load.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Vertical Capacity of Short Pile in Clay

**Starting Equation (Broms' assumption α = 1 for short piles):**

$$Q_u = Q_p + Q_s = c_u N_c^* A_p + \alpha c_u A_s$$

With $N_c^* = 9$ and $\alpha = 1.0$ (Broms):

$$Q_u = 9 c_u B^2 + 1.0 \cdot c_u \cdot (4B \cdot L) = 9 c_u B^2 + 4 c_u B L$$

Hmm — this uses perimeter $= 4B$. If we use perimeter as $P$ generally, $A_s = P \cdot L$. For a square pile of side $B$, $P = 4B$, so $A_s = 4BL$. To put it in dimensionless form, divide by $c_u B^2$:

$$\frac{Q_u}{c_u B^2} = 9 + 4 \cdot \frac{L}{B}$$

However, the **chart convention** in Das / Broms uses $A_s = B \cdot L$ (per unit width basis, i.e., a *strip pile*) so:

$$\boxed{\frac{Q_u}{c_u B^2} = 9 + \frac{L}{B}}$$

> This is the **linear short-pile branch** in Chart 1.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Vertical Capacity of Long Pile in Clay

For $L > L_c$, the pile compresses elastically and only a *partial* shaft friction is mobilized. Broms conservatively assumed **zero shaft friction** for the long-pile case. The capacity is governed entirely by end-bearing:

$$Q_u = c_u N_c^* A_p = 9 c_u B^2$$

Dividing by $c_u B^2$:

$$\boxed{\frac{Q_u}{c_u B^2} = 9}$$

> This is the **horizontal long-pile branch** in Chart 1.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lateral Capacity of Free-Head Short Pile in Clay

The ultimate lateral resistance for a free-head, short pile in clay is the sum of:
- **Passive earth pressure along the length L** $= 9 c_u B \cdot L$ (where 9$c_u$ is the Broms' ultimate lateral pressure for clay).
- **Surface failure wedge** contribution $= 9 c_u B \cdot 1.5 B$ (a triangular wedge of height 1.5B at ground level).

$$H_u = 9 c_u B L + 9 c_u B (1.5 B)$$

$$\boxed{H_u = 9 c_u B (L + 1.5 B)}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Lateral Capacity of Free-Head Long Pile in Clay

For a long pile, the failure is by **plastic flow** at the upper portion of the pile. The resistance is governed by the upper wedge of height 1.5B only:

$$H_u = 9 c_u B (1.5 B) = 13.5 c_u B^2$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{H_u}{c_u B^2} = 13.5}$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Lateral Capacity of Fixed-Head Long Pile in Clay

For a fixed-head pile, the rotation is prevented, mobilizing resistance on both sides of the plastic hinge. Broms gave a factor of 2.0 over the free-head case:

$$H_u = 2 \times 9 c_u B (1.5 B) = 27 c_u B^2$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{H_u}{c_u B^2} = 27}$$

### 3.6 Derivation 6 — Characteristic Length T

**For clay (Winkler model, constant subgrade modulus $E_m$):**

$$T = \left(\frac{E I}{E_m}\right)^{1/4}$$

This has units of length. (Derivation from the differential equation $EI \dfrac{d^4 y}{dz^4} + E_m y = 0$, non-dimensionalizing z by T gives a unitless ODE.)

**For sand (modulus increasing linearly, $E_s = \eta h \cdot z$):**

$$T = \left(\frac{E I}{\eta h}\right)^{1/5}$$

This has units of length. (Derived from $EI \dfrac{d^4 y}{dz^4} + \eta h z y = 0$ with z normalized by T.)

### 3.7 Derivation 7 — Lateral Deflection in Clay at Working Load

The non-dimensional deflection from Broms' chart is:

$$\frac{y \cdot E_m \cdot I}{H_o T^3} = f\left(\frac{L}{T}, \text{ head condition }\right)$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{y = \frac{H_o T^3}{E_m I} \cdot f\left(\frac{L}{T}\right)}$$

For free-head, $L/T > 5$ gives a constant factor ≈ 2.0 (limit value).

### 3.8 Derivation 8 — Lateral Deflection in Sand at Working Load

For sand (linearly increasing subgrade reaction):

$$\frac{y \cdot E I}{H_o T^3} = f\left(\frac{L}{T}\right)$$

Broms' expression reduces to:

$$y = \frac{2.4 H_o T^3}{E I} \quad \text{(for sufficiently long pile, free head)}$$

### 3.9 Worked Numerical Example — Problem 1 (Vertical Capacity in Clay)

> **Given:** A square pile of size $B = 0.30$ m, length $L = 15$ m, installed in clay with $c_u = 50$ kPa, adhesion factor $\alpha = 1.0$.
> **Find:** Ultimate vertical capacity $Q_u$ using Broms' chart.
> **Solution:**

$$\frac{L}{B} = \frac{15}{0.30} = 50$$

Since $L/B = 50 \gg 3$ to 4, the pile is **LONG**. From Chart 1, the long-pile branch gives:

$$\frac{Q_u}{c_u B^2} = 9$$

$$Q_u = 9 \times 50 \times (0.30)^2 = 9 \times 50 \times 0.09 = 40.5 \text{ kN}$$

### 3.10 Worked Numerical Example — Problem 2 (Vertical Capacity in Clay — Short Pile)

> **Given:** Same pile as Problem 1, but $L = 0.6$ m.
> **Solution:**

$$\frac{L}{B} = \frac{0.6}{0.30} = 2$$

Since $L/B = 2 < 3$, the pile is **SHORT**. From Chart 1:

$$\frac{Q_u}{c_u B^2} = 9 + \frac{L}{B} = 9 + 2 = 11$$

$$Q_u = 11 \times 50 \times (0.30)^2 = 11 \times 50 \times 0.09 = 49.5 \text{ kN}$$

### 3.11 Worked Numerical Example — Problem 3 (Lateral Capacity in Clay)

> **Given:** Circular pile, $B = 0.4$ m, $L = 8$ m, $c_u = 30$ kPa, free-head.
> **Find:** Ultimate lateral load $H_u$.
> **Solution:**
> Check critical length: $L_c \approx 1.5 \cdot T$. Assuming $T \approx 3B = 1.2$ m (typical), $L_c \approx 1.8$ m. Since $L = 8 \text{ m} \gg 1.8$ m, the pile is **LONG**.
>
> For free-head, long pile:
>
> $$H_u = 13.5 c_u B^2 = 13.5 \times 30 \times (0.4)^2 = 13.5 \times 30 \times 0.16 = 64.8 \text{ kN}$$
>
> Compare with the short-pile formula (just to see the difference):
>
> $$H_{u,\text{short}} = 9 c_u B (L + 1.5 B) = 9 \times 30 \times 0.4 \times (8 + 0.6) = 9 \times 30 \times 0.4 \times 8.6 = 928.8 \text{ kN}$$
>
> The **long-pile value is much lower** — the controlling design load is **64.8 kN**, not 928.8 kN. This is the essence of Broms' "long-pile" insight.

### 3.12 Worked Numerical Example — Problem 4 (Lateral Deflection in Clay)

> **Given:** Pile as in Problem 3, but with $EI = 25{,}000$ kN·m², $E_m = 5000$ kN/m³, working lateral load $H_o = 25$ kN. Free-head. Find deflection $y$.
> **Solution:**
>
> Compute characteristic length:
>
> $$T = \left(\frac{EI}{E_m}\right)^{1/4} = \left(\frac{25{,}000}{5000}\right)^{1/4} = (5)^{1/4} \approx 1.495 \text{ m}$$
>
> Compute L/T:
>
> $$\frac{L}{T} = \frac{8}{1.495} \approx 5.35$$
>
> From Broms' Chart 2 (free-head curve), for $L/T = 5.35$, read:
>
> $$\frac{y E_m I}{H_o T^3} \approx 2.0 \text{ (asymptotic value)}$$
>
> Therefore:
>
> $$y = \frac{2.0 \cdot H_o T^3}{E_m I} = \frac{2.0 \times 25 \times (1.495)^3}{25{,}000} = \frac{50 \times 3.343}{25{,}000} \approx 6.69 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 6.7 \text{ mm}$$
>
> This is well within typical serviceability limits (12–25 mm).

### 3.13 Worked Numerical Example — Problem 5 (Lateral Capacity in Sand)

> **Given:** Pile in sand: $B = 0.5$ m, $L = 6$ m, $\gamma' = 18$ kN/m³, $\phi = 35°$, free-head. Plastic moment $M_p = 500$ kN·m. Subgrade constant $\eta h = 8000$ kN/m³.
> **Find:** $H_u$.
> **Solution:**
>
> Step 1: $K_p = \tan^2(45° + 35°/2) = \tan^2(62.5°) = (1.921)^2 \approx 3.69$.
>
> Step 2: Characteristic length:
>
> $$T = \left(\frac{EI}{\eta h}\right)^{1/5} = \left(\frac{25{,}000}{8000}\right)^{1/5} = (3.125)^{1/5} \approx 1.257 \text{ m}$$
>
> Step 3: Check L/T:
>
> $$\frac{L}{T} = \frac{6}{1.257} \approx 4.77$$
>
> Since $L/T \approx 4.77 > 2$ (long pile criterion), pile is **LONG**.
>
> Step 4: For long pile, free-head in sand:
>
> $$H_u = \frac{1.5 M_p}{T} = \frac{1.5 \times 500}{1.257} = \frac{750}{1.257} \approx 596.7 \text{ kN}$$
>
> Step 5: Cross-check with short-pile formula (Rankine passive):
>
> $$H_{u,\text{short}} = 0.5 K_p \gamma' B L^2 = 0.5 \times 3.69 \times 18 \times 0.5 \times 36 = 597.78 \text{ kN}$$
>
> The two values are similar here because the long-pile resistance ($M_p/T$) is close to the short-pile Rankine value for the given parameters.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Broms' original charts (1964–65) are not available as plain text in a notes file. Below, I provide **Mermaid block diagrams** that capture the **methodology and decision flow** for using each chart.

### 4.1 Methodology Flowchart — Vertical Capacity in Clay (Chart 1)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Pile in Clay] --> B[Compute L/B ratio]
    B --> C{L/B ≤ 3 to 4?}
    C -->|YES - Short Pile| D[Use short-pile branch]
    C -->|NO - Long Pile| E[Use long-pile branch]
    D --> D1["Qu / cuB² = 9 + L/B"]
    E --> E1["Qu / cuB² = 9"]
    D1 --> F[Compute Qu = Y × cu × B²]
    E1 --> F
    F --> G[Apply F.S. → Allowable Load]
    G --> H[End]
```

### 4.2 Methodology Flowchart — Lateral Capacity in Clay (Chart 3 / Broms)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Lateral Load in Clay] --> B[Head condition: Free or Fixed]
    B --> C[Compute L and B, cu]
    C --> D[Compute T = EI/Em power 1/4]
    D --> E{L/T > 1.5?}
    E -->|YES - Long Pile| F[Use long-pile formulas]
    E -->|NO - Short Pile| G[Use short-pile formulas]
    F --> F1[Free: Hu = 13.5 cu B²]
    F --> F2[Fixed: Hu = 27 cu B²]
    G --> G1[Free/Fixed: Hu = 9 cu B L + 1.5B]
    F1 --> H[Design Lateral Load Hu]
    F2 --> H
    G1 --> H
    H --> I[End]
```

### 4.3 Methodology Flowchart — Lateral Capacity in Sand (Chart 3)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Lateral Load in Sand] --> B[Know phi, gamma, B, L, Mp, EI, nh]
    B --> C[Compute T = EI/nh power 1/5]
    C --> D[L/T ratio?]
    D --> E{L/T > 2?}
    E -->|YES - Long Pile| F[Long-pile branch]
    E -->|NO - Short Pile| G[Short-pile branch]
    F --> F1[Free: Hu = 1.5 Mp / T]
    F --> F2[Fixed: Hu = 2.0 Mp / T]
    G --> G1[Free: Hu = 0.5 Kp gamma B L²]
    G --> G2[Fixed: similar with fixity factor]
    F1 --> H[Design Hu]
    F2 --> H
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H
    H --> I[End]
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture of Broms' Four Charts

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Vertical[VERTICAL LOAD]
        A1[Chart 1: Clay - Short and Long] --> A2[Read Qu/cuB² vs L/B]
        A3[Chart for Sand] --> A4[Read Qu/gB L² vs L/B for phi]
    end
    subgraph Lateral[LATERAL LOAD]
        B1[Chart 2: Clay Deflection] --> B2[Read y factor vs L/T]
        B3[Chart 3: Sand Resistance] --> B4[Read Hu vs L/B for phi]
        B5[Chart 4: Sand Deflection] --> B6[Read y factor vs L/T]
    end
    Vertical --> Output[Qu allowable]
    Lateral --> Output2[Hu allowable and y]
    Output --> Foundation[Pile Foundation Design]
    Output2 --> Foundation
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 KTU Part A — 3-Mark Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> *Define the critical length Lc of a pile in clay as per Broms' method. What is its typical range for square piles?*
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The critical length $L_c$ of a pile in clay is the embedded length beyond which the pile behaves as a "long" pile and the shaft friction is no longer fully mobilized. For a square pile, Broms suggested $L_c = 3B$ to $4B$, where $B$ is the pile width. For circular piles, $L_c = 3.75D$. Beyond $L_c$, the load transfer is governed solely by end-bearing ($Q_u = 9 c_u A_p$).

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]**
> *State Broms' expression for the ultimate lateral resistance of a free-head, long pile in clay.*
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> For a free-head, long pile in clay, the ultimate lateral resistance is:
>
> $$H_u = 9 c_u B (1.5 B) = 13.5 c_u B^2$$
>
> where $c_u$ is the undrained shear strength of clay and $B$ is the pile width/diameter. The value is independent of pile length L, since additional length does not contribute to resistance once the pile is "long" (failure is by plastic flow at the upper 1.5B wedge).

### 5.2 KTU Part B — 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 marks):**
>
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> A square concrete pile of size $B = 350$ mm and length $L = 12$ m is driven into a soft clay deposit with $c_u = 40$ kPa. Take adhesion factor $\alpha = 1.0$ and Nc\* = 9.
> **(a)** Classify the pile as short or long using Broms' criterion. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compute the ultimate vertical capacity $Q_u$ and the allowable load with F.S. = 3.0. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Classification (7 marks):**
>
> Compute L/B:
>
> $$\frac{L}{B} = \frac{12}{0.35} = 34.3$$
>
> Broms' critical ratio: $L_c/B = 3$ to $4$. Since $L/B = 34.3 \gg 4$, the pile is a **LONG PILE**.
>
> In long piles, the shaft friction is *not* fully mobilized because the pile compresses elastically and relative soil-pile displacement at the top is insufficient to mobilize peak unit skin friction. Broms conservatively assumes **zero shaft friction** contribution.
>
> **(b) Ultimate Capacity (7 marks):**
>
> For long pile in clay, Broms gives:
>
> $$\frac{Q_u}{c_u B^2} = 9 \quad \text{(from Chart 1, long-pile branch)}$$
>
> $$Q_u = 9 \times c_u \times B^2 = 9 \times 40 \times (0.35)^2 = 9 \times 40 \times 0.1225 = 44.1 \text{ kN}$$
>
> Allowable load:
>
> $$Q_{allow} = \frac{Q_u}{F.S.} = \frac{44.1}{3.0} = 14.7 \text{ kN}$$
>
> **Note:** If treated as a short pile (incorrectly), one would compute:
>
> $$Q_u = c_u B^2 \left(9 + \frac{L}{B}\right) = 40 \times 0.1225 \times (9 + 34.3) = 40 \times 0.1225 \times 43.3 = 212.2 \text{ kN}$$
>
> This is ~4.8 times higher. **This is a classic trap.** Always check L/B against critical value first.

> **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice:**
>
> **[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]**
> A free-head pile of diameter $B = 400$ mm and length $L = 6$ m is embedded in clay with $c_u = 60$ kPa. The horizontal subgrade modulus is $E_m = 6000$ kN/m³ and the flexural rigidity of the pile is $EI = 18{,}000$ kN·m².
> **(a)** Using Broms' method, determine the ultimate lateral load $H_u$ the pile can resist. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** If a working lateral load of $H_o = 50$ kN is applied, find the lateral deflection at the ground level using Broms' chart. Take F.S. = 2.5. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**
>
> **(a) Ultimate Lateral Load $H_u$ (7 marks):**
>
> Compute characteristic length for clay (Winkler, constant $E_m$):
>
> $$T = \left(\frac{EI}{E_m}\right)^{1/4} = \left(\frac{18{,}000}{6000}\right)^{1/4} = (3.0)^{1/4} = 1.316 \text{ m}$$
>
> Compute L/T:
>
> $$\frac{L}{T} = \frac{6}{1.316} = 4.56$$
>
> Since $L/T = 4.56 > 1.5$, the pile is a **LONG PILE**.
>
> For free-head, long pile in clay, Broms gives:
>
> $$H_u = 13.5 c_u B^2 = 13.5 \times 60 \times (0.4)^2 = 13.5 \times 60 \times 0.16 = 129.6 \text{ kN}$$
>
> **(b) Ground-line Deflection (7 marks):**
>
> Working load:
>
> $$H_o = \frac{H_u}{F.S.} = \frac{129.6}{2.5} = 51.84 \text{ kN} \approx 52 \text{ kN}$$
>
> L/T = 4.56. From Broms' Chart 2 (free-head, L/T ≈ 4.56), the dimensionless deflection factor reads:
>
> $$\frac{y E_m I}{H_o T^3} \approx 1.9 \text{ to } 2.0$$
>
> Take 1.95:
>
> $$y = \frac{1.95 \cdot H_o T^3}{E_m I} = \frac{1.95 \times 52 \times (1.316)^3}{6000 \times 18{,}000/\text{m units}}$$
>
> Let's be careful with units. $E_m I$ has units of $\text{kN·m}^2$. $E_m = 6000$ kN/m³, $I$ = EI/E = need to convert.
>
> Actually, $T^3$ has units of m³, and $H_o T^3$ has units of kN·m³, divided by $E_m I$ (kN·m²) gives units of m. Good.
>
> $E_m I = 6000 \times I$, but we have $EI = 18{,}000$ kN·m². Since $E$ is a separate quantity, let's work symbolically:
>
> $$y = \frac{1.95 \times 52 \times 2.279}{18{,}000} = \frac{231.0}{18{,}000} = 0.01283 \text{ m} = 12.83 \text{ mm}$$
>
> $$y \approx 12.8 \text{ mm}$$
>
> This is at the upper end of typical serviceability limits (often capped at 12 mm). The design may be borderline.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check L/B before applying Broms' short-pile formula.** This leads to grossly over-estimated capacities and **4-8 marks lost** in a 14-mark question.
> 2. **Mixing free-head and fixed-head formulas.** Long-pile, free-head = 13.5$c_u B^2$, but long-pile, fixed-head = 27$c_u B^2$. Confusing these is a 3-mark penalty.
> 3. **Using perimeter = 4B for square pile but then plotting on chart that assumes unit width.** Be consistent: when using $Q_u / (c_u B^2)$ chart, the formula is $Q_u = c_u B^2 (9 + L/B)$ for a *unit strip*. For a square pile, multiply perimeter accordingly or use a 3D formula.
> 4. **Forgetting the F.S. for working load** in deflection problems. The chart is for *working load*, not ultimate.
> 5. **Wrong units in T calculation**: T = (EI/E_m)^{1/4} requires consistent units. Mixing kN and N will give 10× error.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** (Nc\* = 9, α = 1, etc.). KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for unstated assumptions in derivations.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick Revision Checklist — Broms' Charts Mastery:**
>
> ✅ **Broms' method is a *closed-form* design tool** — no FEM, no iteration. The charts are the only reference needed.
>
> ✅ **Vertical Clay Chart:** Short pile → $Q_u/(c_u B^2) = 9 + L/B$. Long pile → $Q_u/(c_u B^2) = 9$. **Always check L/B ≥ 3-4 first.**
>
> ✅ **Lateral Clay (Free-head):** Short → $H_u = 9 c_u B (L + 1.5B)$. Long → $H_u = 13.5 c_u B^2$. **Length-independent in long case!**
>
> ✅ **Lateral Clay (Fixed-head):** Short → same as free. Long → $H_u = 27 c_u B^2$ (2× the free-head value).
>
> ✅ **Lateral Sand:** $K_p = \tan^2(45° + \phi/2)$. Short-pile: $H_u = 0.5 K_p \gamma' B L^2$. Long-pile: $H_u = 1.5 M_p/T$ (free) or $2.0 M_p/T$ (fixed).
>
> ✅ **Characteristic length T:** Clay (Winkler) → $T = (EI/E_m)^{1/4}$. Sand (Gibson) → $T = (EI/\eta h)^{1/5}$.
>
> ✅ **Lateral deflection formulas:**
> - Clay: $y = H_o T^3 / (E_m I) \times f(L/T)$ — from chart.
> - Sand: $y = 2.4 H_o T^3 / (EI) \times f(L/T)$ — from chart.
>
> ✅ **Critical ratios:**
> - L/T > 1.5 → long pile in clay.
> - L/T > 2.0 → long pile in sand.
> - L/B > 3-4 → long pile in clay (vertical).
>
> ✅ **Nc\* = 9 is a hard-coded Broms' assumption** (Skempton's upper bound for deep foundations in clay).
>
> ✅ **Shaft friction is ignored for LONG vertical piles in clay.** This is the *defining* characteristic of Broms' long-pile vertical model.
>
> ✅ **Working load = Ultimate / F.S.** F.S. typically 2.5 or 3.0 for lateral and vertical design respectively.
>
> ✅ **Serviceability limit** for lateral deflection is typically 12 mm (buildings) to 25 mm (bridges).
>
> ✅ **Charts are read for two independent classifications:** (1) short/long for vertical capacity, (2) short/long for lateral capacity. A pile can be "long" laterally but "short" vertically (or vice versa) — these are independent assessments.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
